n230012:
tn Heb вАЬThe vision of Isaiah son of Amoz, which he saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem, in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, [and] Hezekiah, kings of Judah.вАЭ
sn IsaiahвАЩs prophetic career probably began in the final year of UzziahвАЩs reign (ca. 740 b.c., see Isa 6:1) and extended into the later years of HezekiahвАЩs reign, which ended in 686 b.c.
n230013: sn The personified heavens and earth are summoned to GodвАЩs courtroom as witnesses against GodвАЩs covenant people. Long before this Moses warned the people that the heavens and earth would be watching their actions (see Deut 4:26; 30:19; 31:28; 32:1).
n230014:
tn Or вАЬsonsвАЭ (NAB, NASB).
sn вАЬFatherвАЭ and вАЬsonвАЭ occur as common terms in ancient Near Eastern treaties and covenants, delineating the suzerain and vassal as participants in the covenant relationship. The prophet uses these terms, the reference to heavens and earth as witnesses, and allusions to deuteronomic covenant curses (1:7-9, 19-20) to set his prophecy firmly against the backdrop of IsraelвАЩs covenantal relationship with Yahweh.
n230015: sn The normal word pair for giving birth to and raising children is вАП„Щ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„УвАО (yalad, вАЬto give birth toвАЭ) and вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„У÷Ј„ЬвАО (gadal, вАЬto grow, raiseвАЭ). The pair вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„У÷Ј„ЬвАО and вАП„®„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (rum, вАЬto raise upвАЭ) probably occur here to highlight the fact that Yahweh made something important of Israel (cf. R. Mosis, TDOT 2:403).
n230016: sn Against the backdrop of YahwehвАЩs care for his chosen people, IsraelвАЩs rebellion represents abhorrent treachery. The conjunction prefixed to a nonverbal element highlights the sad contrast between YahwehвАЩs compassionate care for His people and IsraelвАЩs thankless rebellion.
n230017: sn To rebel carries the idea of вАЬcovenant treachery.вАЭ Although an act of вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„©÷Ј„Б„ҐвАО (pesha њ, вАЬrebellionвАЭ) often signifies a breach of the law, the legal offense also represents a violation of an existing covenantal relationship (E. Carpenter and M. Grisanti, NIDOTTE 3:707).
n230018: tn Heb вАЬand the donkey the feeding trough of its owner.вАЭ The verb in the first line does double duty in the parallelism.
n230019: tn Although both verbs have no object, the parallelism suggests that Israel fails to recognize the Lord as the one who provides for their needs. In both clauses, the placement of вАЬIsraelвАЭ and вАЬmy peopleвАЭ at the head of the clause focuses the readerвАЩs attention on the rebellious nation (C. van der Merwe, J. Naud√©, J. Kroeze, A Biblical Hebrew Reference Grammar, 346-47).
n2300110: sn Having summoned the witnesses and announced the LordвАЩs accusation against Israel, Isaiah mourns the nationвАЩs impending doom. The third person references to the Lord in the second half of the verse suggest that the quotation from the Lord (cf. vv. 2-3) has concluded.
n2300111: tn Or вАЬsonsвАЭ (NASB). The prophet contrasts four terms of privilegeвАФnation, people, offspring, childrenвАФwith four terms that depict IsraelвАЩs sinful condition in IsaiahвАЩs dayвАФsinful, evil, wrong, wicked (see J. A. Motyer, The Prophecy of Isaiah, 43).
n2300112: sn Holy One of Israel is one of IsaiahвАЩs favorite divine titles for God. It pictures the Lord as the sovereign king who rules over his covenant people and exercises moral authority over them.
n2300113: tn Heb вАЬthey are estranged backward.вАЭ The LXX omits this statement, which presents syntactical problems and seems to be outside the synonymous parallelistic structure of the verse.
n2300114: sn In vv. 5-9 Isaiah addresses the battered nation (5-8) and speaks as their representative (9).
n2300115: tn Heb вАЬWhy are you still beaten? [Why] do you continue rebellion?вАЭ The rhetorical questions express the prophetвАЩs disbelief over IsraelвАЩs apparent masochism and obsession with sin. The interrogative construction in the first line does double duty in the parallelism. H. Wildberger (Isaiah, 1:18) offers another alternative by translating the two statements with one question: вАЬWhy do you still wish to be struck that you persist in revolt?вАЭ
n2300116: tn Heb вАЬall the head is illвАЭ; NRSV вАЬthe whole head is sickвАЭ; CEV вАЬYour head is badly bruised.вАЭ
n2300117: tn Heb вАЬthere is not in it healthвАЭ; NAB вАЬthere is no sound spot.вАЭ
n2300120: sn This verse describes wounds like those one would receive in battle. These wounds are comprehensive and without remedy.
n2300121: tn Heb вАЬAs for your land, before you foreigners are devouring it.вАЭ
n2300122: tn Heb вАЬand [there is] devastation like an overthrow by foreigners.вАЭ The comparative preposition вАП„ЫвАО (kaf, вАЬlike, asвАЭ) has here the rhetorical nuance, вАЬin every way like.вАЭ The point is that the land has all the earmarks of a destructive foreign invasion because that is what has indeed happened. One could paraphrase, вАЬit is desolate as it can only be when foreigners destroy.вАЭ On this use of the preposition in general, see GKC 376 ¬І118.x. Many also prefer to emend вАЬforeignersвАЭ here to вАЬSodom,вАЭ though there is no external attestation for such a reading in the mss or ancient versions. Such an emendation finds support from the following context (vv. 9-10) and usage of the preceding noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ф÷∞„§÷µ÷Љ„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (mahpekhah, вАЬoverthrowвАЭ). In its five other uses, this noun is associated with the destruction of Sodom. If one accepts the emendation, then one might translate, вАЬthe devastation resembles the destruction of Sodom.вАЭ
n2300123: tn Heb вАЬdaughter of ZionвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV). The genitive is appositional, identifying precisely which daughter is in view. By picturing Zion as a daughter, the prophet emphasizes her helplessness and vulnerability before the enemy.
n2300124: tn Heb вАЬlike a city besieged.вАЭ Unlike the preceding two comparisons, which are purely metaphorical, this third one identifies the reality of IsraelвАЩs condition. In this case the comparative preposition, as in v. 7b, has the force, вАЬin every way like,вАЭ indicating that all the earmarks of a siege are visible because that is indeed what is taking place. The verb form in MT is Qal passive participle of вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (natsar, вАЬguardвАЭ), but since this verb is not often used of a siege (see BDB 666 s.v. I вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО), some prefer to repoint the form as a Niphal participle from II вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„®вАО (tsur, вАЬbesiegeвАЭ). However, the latter is not attested elsewhere in the Niphal (see BDB 848 s.v. II вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„®вАО).
n2300125: tn Traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hosts.вАЭ The title pictures God as the sovereign king who has at his disposal a multitude of attendants, messengers, and warriors to do his bidding. In some contexts, like this one, the military dimension of his rulership is highlighted. In this case, the title pictures him as one who leads armies into battle against his enemies.
n2300126: tc The translation assumes that вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ШвАО (kim њat, вАЬquickly,вАЭ literally, вАЬlike a littleвАЭ) goes with what follows, contrary to the MT accents, which take it with what precedes. In this case, one could translate the preceding line, вАЬIf the Lord who commands armies had not left us a few survivors.вАЭ If вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ШвАО goes with the preceding line (following the MT accents), this expression highlights the idea that there would only be a few survivors (H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:20; H. Zobel, TDOT 8:456). Israel would not be almost like Sodom but exactly like Sodom.
n2300127: sn Building on the simile of v. 9, the prophet sarcastically addresses the leaders and people of Jerusalem as if they were leaders and residents of ancient Sodom and Gomorrah. The sarcasm is appropriate, for if the judgment is comparable to SodomвАЩs, that must mean that the sin which prompted the judgment is comparable as well.
n2300128: tn Heb вАЬto the instruction of our God.вАЭ In this context, which is highly accusatory and threatening, вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah, вАЬlaw, instructionвАЭ) does not refer to mere teaching, but to corrective teaching and rebuke.
n2300129:
tn Heb вАЬWhy to me the multitude of your sacrifices?вАЭ The sarcastic rhetorical question suggests that their many sacrifices are of no importance to the Lord. This phrase answers the possible objection that an Israelite could raise in response to GodвАЩs indictment: вАЬBut we are offering the sacrifices you commanded!вАЭ
sn In this section the Lord refutes a potential objection that his sinful people might offer in their defense. He has charged them with rebellion (vv. 2-3), but they might respond that they have brought him many sacrifices. So he points out that he requires justice in society first and foremost, not empty ritual.
n2300130: tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„В„С÷Ј„ҐвАО (sava њ, вАЬbe satisfied, fullвАЭ) is often used of eating and/or drinking oneвАЩs fill, to have had fully enough and want no more. See BDB 959 s.v. вАП„©÷Є„В„С÷Ј„ҐвАО. In some cases it means to have had more than enough of something and to want to not have any more (cf. Prov 25:17). The word picture builds on the Near Eastern viewpoint of sacrifices as food for the deity. God essentially says, вАЬenough of that already;вАЭ what he wants is not more of that.
n2300131: sn In the chiastic structure of the verse, the verbs at the beginning and end highlight GodвАЩs displeasure, while the heaping up of references to animals, fat, and blood in the middle lines hints at why God wants no more of their sacrifices. They have, as it were, piled the food on his table and he needs no more.
n2300132: tn Heb вАЬWhen you come to appear before me, who requires this from your hand, trampling of my courtyards?вАЭ The rhetorical question sarcastically makes the point that God does not require this parade of livestock. The verb вАЬtrampleвАЭ probably refers to the eager worshipers and their sacrificial animals walking around in the temple area.
n2300133: tn Or вАЬworthlessвАЭ (NASB, NCV, CEV); KJV, ASV вАЬvain.вАЭ
n2300134: sn Notice some of the other practices that Yahweh regards as вАЬdetestableвАЭ: homosexuality (Lev 18:22-30; 20:13), idolatry (Deut 7:25; 13:15), human sacrifice (Deut 12:31), eating ritually unclean animals (Deut 14:3-8), sacrificing defective animals (Deut 17:1), engaging in occult activities (Deut 18:9-14), and practicing ritual prostitution (1 Kgs 14:23).
n2300135: tn Heb вАЬsin and assemblyвАЭ (these two nouns probably represent a hendiadys). The point is that their attempts at worship are unacceptable to God because the peopleвАЩs everyday actions in the socio-economic realm prove they have no genuine devotion to God (see vv. 16-17).
n2300136: tn Heb вАЬI close my eyes from you.вАЭ
n2300137: sn This does not just refer to the blood of sacrificial animals, but also the blood, as it were, of their innocent victims. By depriving the poor and destitute of proper legal recourse and adequate access to the economic system, the oppressors have, for all intents and purposes, вАЬkilledвАЭ their victims.
n2300138: sn Having demonstrated the peopleвАЩs guilt, the Lord calls them to repentance, which will involve concrete action in the socio-economic realm, not mere emotion.
n2300139: sn This phrase refers to IsraelвАЩs covenant treachery (cf. Deut 28:10; Jer 4:4; 21:12; 23:2, 22; 25:5; 26:3; 44:22; Hos 9:15; Ps 28:4). In general, the noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ь÷µ„Щ„Ы÷ґ„ЭвАО (ma њalleykhem) can simply be a reference to deeds, whether good or bad. However, Isaiah always uses it with a negative connotation (cf. 3:8, 10).
n2300140: tn The precise meaning of this line is uncertain. The translation assumes an emendation of вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„•вАО (khamots, вАЬoppressor [?]вАЭ) to вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю„Х÷Љ„•вАО (khamuts, вАЬoppressedвАЭ), a passive participle from II вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„•вАО (khamats, вАЬoppressвАЭ; HALOT 329 s.v. II вАП„Ч„Ю„•вАО) and takes the verb II вАП„Р÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„®вАО ( Њashar) in the sense of вАЬmake happyвАЭ (the delocutive Piel, meaning вАЬcall/pronounce happy,вАЭ is metonymic here, referring to actually effecting happiness). The parallelism favors this interpretation, for the next two lines speak of positive actions on behalf of the destitute. The other option is to retain the MT pointing and translate, вАЬset right the oppressor,вАЭ but the nuance вАЬset rightвАЭ is not clearly attested elsewhere for the verb I вАП„Р„©„Б„®вАО. This verb does appear as a participle in Isa 3:12 and 9:16 with the meaning вАЬto lead or guide.вАЭ If it can mean to вАЬleadвАЭ or вАЬrebuke/redirectвАЭ in this verse, the prophet could be contrasting this appeal for societal reformation (v. 17c) with a command to reorder their personal lives (v. 17a-b). J. A. Motyer (The Prophecy of Isaiah, 47) suggests that these three statements (v. 17a-c) provide вАЬthe contrast between the two ends of imperfect society, the oppressor and the needy, the one inflicting and the other suffering the hurt. Isaiah looks for a transformed society wherever it needs transforming.вАЭ
n2300141: tn This word refers to a woman who has lost her husband, by death or divorce. The orphan and widow are often mentioned in the OT as epitomizing the helpless and impoverished who have been left without the necessities of life due to the loss of a family provider.
n2300142: sn The Lord concludes his case against Israel by offering them the opportunity to be forgiven and by setting before them the alternatives of renewed blessing (as a reward for repentance) and final judgment (as punishment for persistence in sin).
n2300143: tn Traditionally, вАЬlet us reason together,вАЭ but the context suggests a judicial nuance. The Lord is giving the nation its options for the future.
n2300144: tn The imperfects must be translated as modal (indicating capability or possibility) to bring out the conditional nature of the offer. This purification will only occur if the people repent and change their ways.
n2300145: tn The imperfects must be translated as modal (indicating capability or possibility) to bring out the conditional nature of the offer. This purification will only occur if the people repent and change their ways.
n2300146: tn Heb вАЬthough your sins are like red, they will become white like snow; though they are red like scarlet, they will be like wool.вАЭ The point is not that the sins will be covered up, though still retained. The metaphorical language must be allowed some flexibility and should not be pressed into a rigid literalistic mold. The peopleвАЩs sins will be removed and replaced by ethical purity. The sins that are now as obvious as the color red will be washed away and the ones who are sinful will be transformed.
n2300147: tn Heb вАЬlistenвАЭ; KJV вАЬobedientвАЭ; NASB вАЬIf you consent and obey.вАЭ
n2300148: sn The wordplay in the Hebrew draws attention to the options. The people can obey, in which case they will вАЬeatвАЭ v. 19 (вАП„™÷є÷Љ„Р„Ы÷µ„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО [to Њkhelu], Qal active participle of вАП„Р÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО) GodвАЩs blessing, or they can disobey, in which case they will be devoured (Heb вАЬeaten,вАЭ вАП„™÷∞÷Љ„Р÷ї„Ы÷∞÷Љ„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО, [te Њukkelu], Qal passive/Pual of вАП„Р÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО) by GodвАЩs judgment.
n2300149: tn Heb вАЬfor the mouth of the Lord has spoken.вАЭ The introductory вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) may be asseverative (as reflected in the translation) or causal/explanatory, explaining why the option chosen by the people will become reality (it is guaranteed by the divine word).
n2300150: tn Heb вАЬHow she has become a prostitute, the faithful city!вАЭ The exclamatory вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Ы÷Є„ФвАО ( Њekhah, вАЬhow!вАЭ) is used several times as the beginning of a lament (see Lam 1:1; 2:1; 4:1-2). Unlike a number of other OT passages that link references to IsraelвАЩs harlotry to idolatry, Isaiah here makes the connection with social and moral violations.
n2300152: tn Or вАЬassassins.вАЭ This refers to the oppressive rich and/or their henchmen. R. Ortlund (Whoredom, 78) posits that it serves as a synecdoche for all varieties of criminals, the worst being mentioned to imply all lesser ones. Since Isaiah often addressed his strongest rebuke to the rulers and leaders of Israel, he may have in mind the officials who bore the responsibility to uphold justice and righteousness.
n2300153: tn The pronoun is feminine singular; personified Jerusalem (see v. 21) is addressed.
n2300154: tn Or вАЬdross.вАЭ The word refers to the scum or impurites floating on the top of melted metal.
n2300155: sn The metaphors of silver becoming impure and beer being watered down picture the moral and ethical degeneration that had occurred in Jerusalem.
n2300156: tn Or вАЬstubbornвАЭ; CEV вАЬhave rejected me.вАЭ
n2300157: tn Heb вАЬand companions ofвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); CEV вАЬfriends of crooks.вАЭ
n2300159: sn Isaiah may have chosen the word for gifts (вАП„©÷Ј„Б„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, shalmonim; a hapax legomenon here), as a sarcastic pun on what these rulers should have been doing. Instead of attending to peace and wholeness (вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО, shalom), they sought after payoffs (вАП„©÷Ј„Б„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО).
n2300161: sn The rich oppressors referred to in Isaiah and the other eighth-century prophets were not rich capitalists in the modern sense of the word. They were members of the royal military and judicial bureaucracies in Israel and Judah. As these bureaucracies grew, they acquired more and more land and gradually commandeered the economy and legal system. At various administrative levels bribery and graft become commonplace. The common people outside the urban administrative centers were vulnerable to exploitation in such a system, especially those, like widows and orphans, who had lost their family provider through death. Through confiscatory taxation, conscription, excessive interest rates, and other oppressive governmental measures and policies, they were gradually disenfranchised and lost their landed property, and with it, their rights as citizens. The socio-economic equilibrium envisioned in the law of Moses was radically disturbed.
n2300162: tn Heb вАЬthe master, the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ].вАЭ On the title вАЬthe Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies,вАЭ see the note at v. 9.
n2300163: tn Or вАЬthe Mighty One of Israel.вАЭ
n2300164: tn Heb вАЬconsole myselfвАЭ (i.e., by getting revenge); NRSV вАЬpour out my wrath on.вАЭ
n2300165: sn The Lord here identifies with the oppressed and comes as their defender and vindicator.
n2300166: tn Heb вАЬturn my hand against you.вАЭ The second person pronouns in vv. 25-26 are feminine singular. Personified Jerusalem is addressed. The idiom вАЬturn the hand againstвАЭ has the nuance of вАЬstrike with the hand, attack,вАЭ in Ps 81:15 HT (81:14 ET); Ezek 38:12; Amos 1:8; Zech 13:7. In Jer 6:9 it is used of gleaning grapes.
n2300167: tn Heb вАЬI will purify your dross as [with] flux.вАЭ вАЬFluxвАЭ refers here to minerals added to the metals in a furnace to prevent oxides from forming. For this interpretation of II вАП„С÷є÷Љ„®вАО (bor), see HALOT 153 s.v. II вАП„С÷є÷Љ„®вАО and 750 s.v. вАП„°÷і„Щ„ТвАО.
n2300168: sn The metaphor comes from metallurgy; slag is the substance left over after the metallic ore has been refined.
n2300169: tn Heb вАЬI will restore your judges as in the beginning, and your counselors as in the beginning.вАЭ In this context, where social injustice and legal corruption are denounced (see v. 23), the вАЬjudgesвАЭ are probably government officials responsible for making legal decisions, while the вАЬadvisersвАЭ are probably officials who helped the king establish policies. Both offices are also mentioned in 3:2.
n2300170: sn The third person reference to the Lord in v. 28 indicates that the prophet is again (see vv. 21-24a) speaking. Since v. 27 is connected to v. 28 by a conjunction, it is likely that the prophetвАЩs words begin with v. 27.
n2300171: tn Heb вАЬZion will be ransomed with justice.вАЭ Both cola in this verse end with similar terms: justice and righteousness (each preceded by the preposition вАП„С÷∞÷ЉвАО [be]). At issue is whether these virtues describe the means or result of the deliverance and whether they delineate GodвАЩs justice/righteousness or that of the covenant people. If the righteousness of Israelite returnees is in view, the point seems to be that the reestablishment of Zion as a center of justice (GodвАЩs people living in conformity with GodвАЩs demand for equity and justice) will deliver the city from its past humiliation and restore it to a place of prominence (see 2:2-4; cf. E. Kissane, Isaiah, 1:19). Most scholars conclude that вАЬrighteousness and вАЬjusticeвАЭ refers to God alone (J. Ridderbos, Isaiah [BSC], 50; J. Watts, Isaiah [WBC], 1:25; E. J. Young, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:89; cf. NLT, TEV) or serves as a double reference to both divine and human justice and righteousness (J. A. Motyer, The Prophecy of Isaiah, 51; J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:10; H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:72). If it refers to both sides of the coin, these terms highlight the objective divine work of redemption and the subjective human response of penitence (Motyer, 51).
n2300172: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬher repentant ones/returnees with righteousness.вАЭ The form вАП„©÷Є„Б„С÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (shaveha, вАЬher repentant onesвАЭ), as pointed in MT, is a masculine plural Qal participle from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬreturnвАЭ). Used substantivally, it refers to the вАЬreturning (i.e., repentant) ones.вАЭ It is possible that the parallel line (with its allusion to being freed by a ransom payment) suggests that the form be repointed to вАП„©÷і„Б„С÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (shivyah, вАЬher captivityвАЭ), a reading that has support from the LXX. Some slightly emend the form to read вАП„Х÷∞„©÷Є„Б„С÷Є„ФвАО (veshavah, вАЬand will returnвАЭ). According to this view, the verb from the first line applies to the second line as well with the following translation as a result: вАЬshe will be released when fairness is restored.вАЭ Regardless, it makes best sense in the context to regard this as a reference to repentant Israelites returning to the land of promise. This understanding provides a better contrast with the rebels and sinners in 1:28.
n2300173: tn Heb вАЬand [there will be] a shattering of rebels and sinners together.вАЭ
n2300174: tc The Hebrew text (and the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa) has the third person here, though a few Hebrew mss (and Targums) read the second person, which is certainly more consistent with the following context. The third person form is the more difficult reading and probably original. This disagreement in person has caused some to emend the first verb (third plural) to a second plural form (followed by most English translations). The BHS textual apparatus suggests that the second plural form be read even though there is only sparse textual evidence. LXX, Syriac, and the Vulgate change all the second person verbs in 1:29-31 to third person verbs. It is likely that the change to a second person form represents an attempt at syntactical harmonization (J. de Waard, Isaiah, 10). The abrupt change from third person to second person may have been intentional for rhetorical impact (GKC 462 ¬І144.p). The rapid change from exclamation (they did!) to reproach (you desired!) might be regarded as a rhetorical figure focusing attention on the addressees and their conditions (de Waard, 10; E. K√ґnig, Stilistik, Rhetorik, Poetik, 239). This use of the third person could also be understood as an impersonal third person: вАЬone will be ashamedвАЭ (de Waard, 10). In v. 29 the prophet continues his description of the sinners (v. 28), but then suddenly makes a transition to direct address (switching from third to second person) in the middle of his sentence.
n2300175: tn The second person pronouns in vv. 29-30 are masculine plural, indicating that the rebellious sinners (v. 28) are addressed.
n2300176: tn Or вАЬgardensвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NAB вАЬgroves.вАЭ
n2300177: tn Or вАЬa gardenвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n230022: tn Heb вАЬthe word which Isaiah son of Amoz saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem.вАЭ
n230023:
tn The verse begins with a verb that functions as a вАЬdiscourse particleвАЭ and is not translated. In numerous places throughout the OT, the вАЬto beвАЭ verb with a prefixed conjunction (вАП„Х÷∞„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО [vehayah] and вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ф÷і„ЩвАО [vayehi]) occurs in this fashion to introduce a circumstantial clause and does not require translation.
sn вАЬIn future daysвАЭ refers generally to the future, but here and in Micah 4:1 it may also refer to the final period of history (see the note at Gen 49:1).
n230025: tn Heb вАЬas the chief of the mountains, and will be lifted up above the hills.вАЭ The image of Mount Zion being elevated above other mountains and hills pictures the prominence it will attain in the future.
n230026: tn The prefixed verb form with simple vav (вАП„ХвАО) introduces a purpose/result clause after the preceding prefixed verb form (probably to be taken as a cohortative; see IBHS 650 ¬І39.2.2a).
n230027: tn Heb вАЬhis ways.вАЭ In this context GodвАЩs вАЬwaysвАЭ are the standards of moral conduct he decrees that people should live by.
n230028: tn The cohortative with vav (вАП„ХвАО) after the prefixed verb form indicates the ultimate purpose/goal of their action.
n2300210: tn Heb вАЬfor out of Zion will go instruction.вАЭ
n2300211: sn Instead of referring to the large plow as a whole, the plowshare is simply the metal tip which actually breaks the earth and cuts the furrow.
n2300212: sn This implement was used to prune the vines, i.e., to cut off extra leaves and young shoots (H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:93; M. Klingbeil, NIDOTTE 1:1117-18). It was a short knife with a curved hook at the end sharpened on the inside like a sickle. Breaking weapons and fashioning agricultural implements indicates a transition from fear and stress to peace and security.
n2300213: tn Heb вАЬhouse,вАЭ referring to the family line or descendants (likewise in v. 6).
n2300214: tn Heb вАЬletвАЩs walk in the light of the Lord.вАЭ In this context, which speaks of the LordвАЩs instruction and commands, the вАЬlight of the LordвАЭ refers to his moral standards by which he seeks to guide his people. One could paraphrase, вАЬletвАЩs obey the LordвАЩs commands.вАЭ
n2300215: tn The words вАЬO LordвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. Isaiah addresses the Lord in prayer.
n2300216: tc Heb вАЬthey are full from the east.вАЭ Various scholars retain the BHS reading and suggest that the prophet makes a general statement concerning IsraelвАЩs reliance on foreign customs (J. Watts, Isaiah [WBC], 1:32; J. de Waard, Isaiah, 12-13). Nevertheless, it appears that a word is missing. Based on the parallelism (note вАЬomen readersвАЭ in 2:6c), many suggest that вАП„І÷є„°÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (qosemim, вАЬdivinersвАЭ) or вАП„Ю÷і„І÷∞„°÷Є„ЭвАО (miqsam, вАЬdivinationвАЭ) has been accidentally omitted. Homoioteleuton could account for the omission of an original вАП„І÷є„°÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (note how this word and the following вАП„Ю÷і„І÷ґ÷Љ„У÷ґ„ЭвАО [miqqedem, вАЬfrom the eastвАЭ] both end in mem); an original вАП„Ю÷і„І÷∞„°÷Є„ЭвАО could have fallen out by homoioarcton (note how this word and the following вАП„Ю÷і„І÷ґ÷Љ„У÷ґ„ЭвАО both begin with mem).
n2300217: tn Heb вАЬand omen readers like the Philistines.вАЭ Through this line and the preceding, the prophet contends that Israel has heavily borrowed the pagan practices of the east and west (in violation of Lev 19:26; Deut 18:9-14).
n2300218: tn Heb вАЬand with the children of foreigners they [?].вАЭ The precise meaning of the final word is uncertain. Some take this verb (I вАП„©÷Є„В„§÷Ј„ІвАО, safaq) to mean вАЬslap,вАЭ supply the object вАЬhands,вАЭ and translate, вАЬthey slap [hands] with foreignersвАЭ; HALOT 1349 s.v. I вАП„©„В„§„ІвАО. This could be a reference to foreign alliances. This translation has two disadvantages: It requires the conjectural insertion of вАЬhandsвАЭ and the use of this verb with its object prefixed with a вАП„С÷∞÷ЉвАО (bet) preposition with this meaning does not occur elsewhere. The other uses of this verb refer to clapping at someone, an indication of hostility. The translation above assumes the verb is derived from II вАП„©„В„§„ІвАО (вАЬto suffice,вАЭ attested in the Qal in 1 Kgs 20:10; HALOT 1349 s.v. II вАП„©„В„§„ІвАО). In this case the point is that a sufficient number of foreigners (in this case, too many!) live in the land. The disadvantage of this option is that the preposition prefixed to вАЬthe children of foreignersвАЭ does not occur with this verb elsewhere. The chosen translation is preferred since it continues the idea of abundant foreign influence and does not require a conjectural insertion or emendation.
n2300220: sn JudahвАЩs royal bureaucracy had accumulated great wealth and military might, in violation of Deut 17:16-17.
n2300222: tn Heb вАЬmen bow down, men are low.вАЭ Since the verbs вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ч÷Є„ЧвАО (shakhakh) and вАП„©÷Є„Б„§÷Ј„ЬвАО (shafal) are used later in this discourse to describe how God will humiliate proud men (see vv. 11, 17), some understand v. 9a as a prediction of judgment, вАЬmen will be brought down, men will be humiliated.вАЭ However, these prefixed verbal forms with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive appear to carry on the description that precedes and are better taken with the accusation. They draw attention to the fact that human beings actually bow down and worship before the lifeless products of their own hands.
n2300223: tn Heb вАЬdonвАЩt lift them up.вАЭ The idiom вАЬlift upвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„©÷Є„В„РвАО with вАП„Ь÷∞вАО, nasa Њ with preposition lamed) can mean вАЬspare, forgiveвАЭ (see Gen 18:24, 26). Here the idiom plays on the preceding verbs. The idolaters are bowed low as they worship their false gods; the prophet asks God not to вАЬlift them up.вАЭ
n2300224: tn Heb вАЬfrom the dread of the Lord,вАЭ that is, from the dread that he produces in the objects of his judgment.вАЭ The words вАЬget awayвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2300225: tn Heb вАЬand the eyes of the pride of men will be brought low, and the arrogance of men will be brought down.вАЭ The repetition of the verbs вАП„©÷Є„Б„§÷Ј„ЬвАО (shafal) and вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ч÷Є„ЧвАО (shakhakh) from v. 9 draws attention to the appropriate nature of the judgment. Those proud men who вАЬbow lowвАЭ before idols will be forced to вАЬbow lowвАЭ before God when he judges their sin.
n2300227: tn Heb вАЬindeed [or вАЬforвАЭ] the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] has a day.вАЭ
n2300229: sn The cedars of Lebanon and oaks of Bashan were well-known for their size and prominence. They make apt symbols here for powerful men who think of themselves as prominent and secure.
n2300230: sn The high mountains and hills symbolize the apparent security of proud men, as do the high tower and fortified wall of v. 15.
n2300231: tn Heb вАЬthe ships of Tarshish.вАЭ This probably refers to large ships either made in or capable of traveling to the distant western port of Tarshish.
n2300232: tn Heb вАЬdesirableвАЭ; NAB, NIV вАЬstatelyвАЭ; NRSV вАЬbeautiful.вАЭ
n2300233:
tn On the meaning of this word, which appears only here in the Hebrew Bible, see H. R. Cohen, Biblical Hapax Legomena (SBLDS), 41-42.
sn The ships mentioned in this verse were the best of their class, and therefore an apt metaphor for the proud men being denounced in this speech.
n2300234: tn Heb вАЬand the pride of men will be brought down, and the arrogance of men will be brought low.вАЭ As in v. 11, the repetition of the verbs вАП„©÷Є„Б„§÷Ј„ЬвАО (shafal) and вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ч÷Є„ЧвАО (shakhakh) from v. 9 draws attention to the appropriate nature of the judgment. Those proud men who вАЬbow lowвАЭ before idols will be forced to вАЬbow lowвАЭ before God when he judges their sin.
n2300235: tn Or вАЬelevatedвАЭ; NCV вАЬpraisedвАЭ; CEV вАЬhonored.вАЭ
n2300236:
tc The verb вАЬpass awayвАЭ is singular in the Hebrew text, despite the plural subject (вАЬworthless idolsвАЭ) that precedes. The verb should be emended to a plural; the final vav (вАП„ХвАО) has been accidentally omitted by haplography (note the vav at the beginning of the immediately following form).
tn Heb вАЬwill completely pass awayвАЭ; ASV вАЬshall utterly pass away.вАЭ
n2300237: tn The identity of the grammatical subject is unclear. The вАЬidolsвАЭ could be the subject; they will вАЬgoвАЭ into the caves and holes when the idolaters throw them there in their haste to escape GodвАЩs judgment (see vv. 20-21). The picture of the idols, which represent the foreign deities worshiped by the people, fleeing from the Lord would be highly polemical and fit the overall mood of the chapter. However it seems more likely that the idolaters themselves are the subject, for v. 10 uses similar language in sarcastically urging them to run from judgment.
n2300238: tn Heb вАЬdustвАЭ; ASV вАЬinto the holes of the earth.вАЭ
n2300239: tn Heb вАЬfrom the dread of the Lord,вАЭ that is, from the dread that he produces in the objects of his judgment.вАЭ The words вАЬtrying to escapeвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2300240: tn Or вАЬland.вАЭ It is not certain if these verses are describing the judgment of Judah (see vv. 6-9) or a more universal judgment on all proud men.
n2300243: tn Heb вАЬto the shrews and to the bats.вАЭ On the meaning of вАП„Ч÷≤„§÷Ј„®÷∞„§÷Є÷Љ„®÷Є„ФвАО (khafarparah, вАЬshrewвАЭ), see HALOT 341 s.v. вАП„Ч÷≤„§÷Ј„®÷∞„§÷Є÷Љ„®÷Є„ФвАО. The BHS text as it stands (вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ч÷∞„§÷є÷Љ„®вАО вАП„§÷µ÷Љ„®„Х÷є„™вАО, perot lakhpor), makes no sense. Based on TheodotionвАЩs transliteration and a similar reading in the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa, most scholars suggest that the MT mistakenly divided a noun (a hapax legomenon) that should be translated вАЬmoles,вАЭ вАЬshrews,вАЭ or вАЬrodents.вАЭ
n2300244: sn The precise point of vv. 20-21 is not entirely clear. Are they taking the idols into their hiding places with them because they are so attached to their man-made images? Or are they discarding the idols along the way as they retreat into the darkest places they can find? In either case it is obvious that the gods are incapable of helping them.
n2300245: tn Heb вАЬfrom the dread of the Lord,вАЭ that is, from the dread that he produces in the objects of his judgment.вАЭ The words вАЬtrying to escapeвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2300246: tn Or вАЬland.вАЭ It is not certain if these verses are describing the judgment of Judah (see vv. 6-9) or a more universal judgment on all proud men. Almost all English versions translate вАЬearth,вАЭ taking this to refer to universal judgment.
n230031: tn Heb вАЬthe master, the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, the вАЬLord of hostsвАЭ].вАЭ On the title вАЬthe Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies,вАЭ see the note at 1:9.
n230033: tn Heb вАЬsupport and support.вАЭ The masculine and feminine forms of the noun are placed side-by-side to emphasize completeness. See GKC 394 ¬І122.v.
n230034: tn Heb вАЬall the support of food, and all the support of water.вАЭ
n230035: tn Heb вАЬelderвАЭ (so ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); NCV вАЬolder leaders.вАЭ
n230036: tn Heb вАЬthe ones lifted up with respect to the face.вАЭ For another example of the Hebrew idiom, see 2 Kgs 5:1.
n230037: tn Heb вАЬand the wise with respect to magic.вАЭ On the meaning of вАП„Ч÷≤„®÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО (kharashim, вАЬmagicвАЭ), see HALOT 358 s.v. III вАП„Ч„®„©„БвАО. Some understand here a homonym, meaning вАЬcraftsmen.вАЭ In this case, one could translate, вАЬskilled craftsmenвАЭ (cf. NIV, NASB).
n230038: tn The words вАЬthe Lord saysвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The prophet speaks in vv. 1-3 (note the third person reference to the Lord in v. 1), but here the Lord himself announces that he will intervene in judgment. It is unclear where the LordвАЩs words end and the prophetвАЩs pick up again. The prophet is apparently speaking again by v. 8, where the Lord is referred to in the third person. Since vv. 4-7 comprise a thematic unity, the quotation probably extends through v. 7.
n230039: tn вАП„™÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„Ь„Х÷Љ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ta њalulim) is often understood as an abstract plural meaning вАЬwantonness, crueltyвАЭ (cf. NLT). In this case the chief characteristic of these leaders is substituted for the leaders themselves. However, several translations make the parallelism tighter by emending the form to вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њolelim, вАЬchildrenвАЭ; cf. ESV, NASB, NCV, NIV, NKJV, NRSV). This emendation is unnecessary for at least two reasons. The word in the MT highlights the cruelty or malice of the вАЬleadersвАЭ who are left behind in the wake of GodвАЩs judgment. The immediate context makes clear the fact that they are mere youths. The coming judgment will sweep away the leaders, leaving a vacuum which will be filled by incompetent, inexperienced youths.
n2300310: tn Heb вАЬman against man, and a man against his neighbor.вАЭ
n2300311: tn Heb вАЬand those lightly esteemed those who are respected.вАЭ The verb вАП„®÷Є„Ф÷Ј„СвАО (rahav) does double duty in the parallelism.
n2300312: tn Heb вАЬ[in] the house of his fatherвАЭ (so ASV); NIV вАЬat his fatherвАЩs home.вАЭ
n2300313: tn The words вАЬand sayвАЭ are supplied for stylistic reasons.
n2300314:
tn Heb вАЬyour handвАЭ; NASB вАЬunder your charge.вАЭ
sn The manвАЩs motives are selfish. He tells his brother to assume leadership because he thinks he has some wealth to give away.
n2300317: tn Heb вАЬwrapper [of wounds]вАЭ; KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬhealer.вАЭ
n2300318: tn Heb вАЬfor their tongue and their deeds [are] to the Lord.вАЭ
n2300319: tn Heb вАЬto rebel [against] the eyes of his majesty.вАЭ The word вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„С„Х÷є„УвАО (kavod) frequently refers to the LordвАЩs royal splendor that is an outward manifestation of his authority as king.
n2300320: sn This refers to their proud, arrogant demeanor.
n2300321: tn Heb вАЬanswers against themвАЭ; NRSV вАЬbears witness against them.вАЭ
n2300322: tn Heb вАЬtheir sin, like Sodom, they declare, they do not conceal [it].вАЭ
n2300324: tn Or вАЬthe righteousвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, TEV); NLT вАЬthose who are godly.вАЭ
n2300326: tn Heb вАЬfor the fruit of their deeds they will eat.вАЭ
n2300327: tn Heb вАЬfor the work of his hands will be done to him.вАЭ
n2300328: sn This may refer to the prophet or to the Lord.
n2300329: tc The Hebrew text appears to read literally, вАЬMy people, his oppressors, he deals severely, and women rule over them.вАЭ The correct text and precise meaning of the verse are debated. The translation above assumes (1) an emendation of вАП„†÷є„Т÷∞„©÷Є„В„Щ„ХвАО (nogesayv, вАЬhis oppressorsвАЭ) to вАП„†÷є„Т÷∞„©÷і„В„Щ„ЭвАО (nogeshim, вАЬoppressorsвАЭ) by moving the mem (вАП„ЭвАО) on the following form to the end of the word and dropping the vav (вАП„ХвАО) as virtually dittographic; (2) an emendation of вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО (me њolel, a singular participle that does not agree with the preceding plural subject) to вАП„Ґ÷є„Ь÷∞„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО ( њolelu), a third plural Poel perfect from вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њalal, вАЬdeal severelyвАЭ; note that the following form begins with a vav [вАП„ХвАО]; the text may be haplographic or misdivided); and (3) an emendation (with support from the LXX) of вАП„†÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО (nashim, вАЬwomenвАЭ) to вАП„†÷є„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО (noshim, вАЬcreditorsвАЭ; a participle from вАП„†÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„РвАО, nasha Њ). Another option is to emend вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО to вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њolelim, вАЬchildrenвАЭ) and read, вАЬMy peopleвАЩs oppressors are children; women rule over them.вАЭ In this case the point is the same as in v. 4; the leadership void left by the judgment will be filled by those incompetent to lead the communityвАФchildren and women.
n2300330: tn Heb вАЬand the way of your paths they confuse.вАЭ The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (bala њ, вАЬconfuseвАЭ; HALOT 135 s.v. I вАП„С„Ь„ҐвАО) is a homonym of the more common вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (вАЬswallowвАЭ; see HALOT 134 s.v. вАП„С„Ь„ҐвАО).
n2300331: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њammim, вАЬnationsвАЭ) but the context makes it clear that the Lord is judging his covenant people. As indicated by the LXX the text should read вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( њammo, вАЬhis peopleвАЭ). The final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) on the form in the Hebrew is either dittographic or enclitic. When the mem was added or read as a plural ending, the vav (вАП„ХвАО) was then misread as a yod (вАП„ЩвАО).
n2300332: tn The words вАЬhe saysвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2300333: tn The pronominal element is masculine plural; the leaders are addressed.
n2300334: tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ba њar, вАЬgraze, ruinвАЭ; HALOT 146 s.v. II вАП„С„Ґ„®вАО) is a homonym of the more common вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ba њar, вАЬburnвАЭ; see HALOT 145 s.v. I вАП„С„Ґ„®вАО).
n2300335: sn The vineyard is a metaphor for the nation here. See 5:1-7.
n2300336: tn Heb вАЬthe plunder of the poor [is] in your housesвАЭ (so NASB).
n2300337: sn The rhetorical question expresses the LordвАЩs outrage at what the leaders have done to the poor. He finds it almost unbelievable that they would have the audacity to treat his people in this manner.
n2300338:
tn Heb Traditionally, the вАЬLord of hosts.вАЭ On the title вАЬthe Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies,вАЭ see the note at 1:9.
sn The use of this title, which also appears in v. 1, forms an inclusio around vv. 1-15. The speech begins and ends with a reference to вАЬthe Sovereign Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies.вАЭ
n2300340: tn Heb вАЬwith an outstretched neck.вАЭ They proudly hold their heads high so that others can see the jewelry around their necks.
n2300342: tn Heb вАЬand with their feet they jingle.вАЭ
n2300343: tn In the Hebrew text vv. 16-17 are one long sentence, вАЬBecause the daughters of Zion are proud and walkвА¶, the Lord will afflictвА¶.вАЭ In v. 17 the Lord refers to himself in the third person.
n2300344: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in v. 18 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2300346: tn Or вАЬa scabвАЭ (KJV, ASV); NIV, NCV, CEV вАЬsores.вАЭ
n2300347: tn The precise meaning of this line is unclear because of the presence of the rare word вАП„§÷є÷Љ„™вАО (pot). Since the verb in the line means вАЬlay bare, make naked,вАЭ some take вАП„§÷є÷Љ„™вАО as a reference to the genitals (cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV, CEV). (In 1 Kgs 7:50 a noun вАП„§÷є÷Љ„™вАО appears, with the apparent meaning вАЬsocket.вАЭ) J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:139, n. 2), basing his argument on alleged Akkadian evidence and the parallelism of the verse, takes вАП„§÷є÷Љ„™вАО as вАЬforehead.вАЭ
n2300348: sn The translation assumes that the direct quotation ends with v. 17. The introductory formula вАЬin that dayвАЭ and the shift from a poetic to prosaic style indicate that a new speech unit begins in v. 18.
n2300350: tn Or вАЬthe beauty of [their] ankle jewelry.вАЭ
n2300351: tn Heb вАЬhouses of breath.вАЭ HALOT 124 s.v. вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„Щ÷і„™вАО defines them as вАЬscent-bottlesвАЭ; cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬperfume boxes.вАЭ
n2300352:
tn The precise meaning of many of the words in this list is uncertain.
sn The rhetorical purpose for such a lengthy list is to impress on the audience the guilt of these women with their proud, materialistic attitude, whose husbands and fathers have profited at the expense of the poor.
n2300353: tn Heb вАЬand it will be in place of spices there will be a stench.вАЭ The nouns for вАЬspicesвАЭ and вАЬstenchвАЭ are right next to each other in the MT for emphatic contrast. The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300354: tn The pronoun is feminine singular, suggesting personified Zion, as representative of its women, is the addressee. The reference to вАЬher gatesвАЩ in v. 26 makes this identification almost certain.
n2300355: tn Heb вАЬyour strength in battle.вАЭ The verb in the first clause provides the verbal idea for the second clause.
n2300356: tn Heb вАЬshe will be empty, on the ground she will sit.вАЭ Jerusalem is personified as a destitute woman who sits mourning the empty city.
n230041:
tn Or вАЬin that dayвАЭ (ASV).
sn The seven-to-one ratio emphasizes the great disparity that will exist in the population due to the death of so many men in battle.
n230042: tn Heb вАЬeatвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV); CEV вАЬbuy.вАЭ
n230043:
tn Heb вАЬwearвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV); NCV вАЬmake.вАЭ
sn In Jewish understanding a husband should provide food and cloth to his wife. These women are so desperate as to be willing to exempt the man from some of his traditional, fundamental duties as a husband.
n230044: tn Heb вАЬonly let your name be called over us.вАЭ
n230045: sn This refers to the humiliation of being unmarried and childless. The womenвАЩs words reflect the cultural standards of ancient Israel, where a womanвАЩs primary duties were to be a wife and mother.
n230047: tn Heb вАЬand the vegetation of the Lord will become beauty and honor.вАЭ Many English versions understand the phrase вАП„¶÷ґ„Ю÷Ј„Ч „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (tsemakh yehvah) as a messianic reference and render it, вАЬthe Branch of the LordвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT, and others). Though вАП„¶÷ґ„Ю÷Ј„ЧвАО (tsemakh) is used by later prophets of a royal descendant (Jer 23:5; 33:15; Zech 3:8; 6:12), those passages contain clear contextual indicators that a human ruler is in view and that the word is being used in a metaphorical way of offspring. However, in Isa 4:2 there are no such contextual indicators. To the contrary, in the parallel structure of the verse вАП„¶÷ґ„Ю÷Ј„Ч „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО corresponds to вАЬproduce of the land,вАЭ a phrase that refers elsewhere exclusively to literal agricultural produce (see Num 13:20, 26; Deut 1:25). In the majority of its uses вАП„¶÷ґ„Ю÷Ј„ЧвАО refers to literal crops or vegetation (in Ps 65:10 the Lord is the source of this vegetation). A reference to the Lord restoring crops would make excellent sense in Isa 4 and the prophets frequently included this theme in their visions of the future age (see Isa 30:23-24; 32:20; Jer 31:12; Ezek 34:26-29; and Amos 9:13-14).
n230048: tn Heb вАЬand the fruit of the land will become pride and beauty for the remnant of Israel.вАЭ
n230049: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300411: tn Or вАЬset apart,вАЭ cf. CEV вАЬspecial.вАЭ
n2300412: tn Heb вАЬall who are written down for life in Jerusalem.вАЭ A city register is envisioned; everyone whose name appears on the roll will be spared. This group comprises the remnant of the city referred to earlier in the verse.
n2300413: tn Heb вАЬwhenвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB); CEV вАЬafterвАЭ; NRSV вАЬonce.вАЭ
n2300414: tn The word refers elsewhere to vomit (Isa 28:8) and fecal material (Isa 36:12). Many English versions render this somewhat euphemistically as вАЬfilthвАЭ (e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV). Ironically in GodвАЩs sight the beautiful jewelry described earlier is nothing but vomit and feces, for it symbolizes the moral decay of the cityвАЩs residents (cf. NLT вАЬmoral filthвАЭ).
n2300416: tn Heb вАЬby a spirit of judgment and by a spirit of burning.вАЭ The precise meaning of the second half of the verse is uncertain. вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh) can be understood as вАЬwind,вАЭ in which case the passage pictures the Lord using a destructive wind as an instrument of judgment. However, this would create a mixed metaphor, for the first half of the verse uses the imagery of washing and rinsing to depict judgment. Perhaps the image would be that of a windstorm accompanied by heavy rain. вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО can also mean вАЬspirit,вАЭ in which case the verse may be referring to the LordвАЩs Spirit or, more likely, to a disposition that the Lord brings to the task of judgment. It is also uncertain if вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ba њar) here means вАЬburningвАЭ or вАЬsweeping away, devastating.вАЭ
n2300417: tn Heb вАЬover all the place, Mount Zion.вАЭ Cf. NLT вАЬJerusalemвАЭ; CEV вАЬthe whole city.вАЭ
n2300418:
tn Heb вАЬa cloud by day, and smoke, and brightness of fire, a flame by night.вАЭ Though the accents in the Hebrew text suggest otherwise, it might be preferable to take вАЬsmokeвАЭ with what follows, since one would expect smoke to accompany fire.
sn The imagery of the cloud by day and fire by night recalls the days of Moses, when a cloud and fire were tangible reminders that the Lord was guiding and protecting his people (Exod 13:21-22; 14:19, 24). In the future age envisioned in Isa 4, the LordвАЩs protective presence will be a reality.
n2300419: tn Heb вАЬindeed (or вАЬforвАЭ) over all the glory, a canopy.вАЭ This may allude to Exod 40:34-35, where a cloud overshadows the meeting tent as it is filled with GodвАЩs glory.
n2300420: tn Heb вАЬa shelter it will be for shade by day from heat, and for a place of refuge and for a hiding place from cloudburst and rain.вАЭ Since both of the last nouns of this verse can mean rain, they can either refer to the rain storm and the rain as distinct items or together refer to a heavy downpour. Regardless, they do not represent unrelated phenomena.
n230051: tn It is uncertain who is speaking here. Possibly the prophet, taking the role of best man, composes a love song for his friend on the occasion of his wedding. If so, вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷і„Щ„УвАО (yadid) should be translated вАЬmy friend.вАЭ The present translation assumes that Israel is singing to the Lord. The word вАП„У„Х÷є„УвАО (dod, вАЬloverвАЭ) used in the second line is frequently used by the woman in the Song of Solomon to describe her lover.
n230052: sn Israel, viewing herself as the LordвАЩs lover, refers to herself as his vineyard. The metaphor has sexual connotations, for it pictures her capacity to satisfy his appetite and to produce children. See Song 8:12.
n230053: tn Heb вАЬon a horn, a son of oil.вАЭ Apparently вАП„І÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЯвАО (qeren, вАЬhornвАЭ) here refers to the horn-shaped peak of a hill (BDB 902 s.v.) or to a mountain spur, i.e., a ridge that extends laterally from a mountain (HALOT 1145 s.v. вАП„І÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЯвАО; H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:180). The expression вАЬson of oilвАЭ pictures this hill as one capable of producing olive trees. IsaiahвАЩs choice of вАП„І÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЯвАО, a rare word for hill, may have been driven by paronomastic concerns, i.e., because вАП„І÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЯвАО sounds like вАП„Ы÷ґ÷Љ„®÷ґ„ЭвАО (kerem, вАЬvineyardвАЭ).
n230054: tn Or, вАЬdug it upвАЭ (so NIV); KJV вАЬfenced it.вАЩ See HALOT 810 s.v. вАП„Ґ„Ц„ІвАО.
n230055:
tn Heb вАЬwild grapes,вАЭ i.e., sour ones (also in v. 4).
sn At this point the love song turns sour as the Lord himself breaks in and completes the story (see vv. 3-6). In the final line of v. 2 the love song presented to the Lord becomes a judgment speech by the Lord.
n230057: tn Heb вАЬmen,вАЭ but in a generic sense.
n230058: tn Heb вАЬand it will become [a place for] grazing.вАЭ вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ba њar, вАЬgrazingвАЭ) is a homonym of the more often used verb вАЬto burn.вАЭ
n230059: tn Heb вАЬand it will become a trampled placeвАЭ (NASB вАЬtrampled groundвАЭ).
n2300510: tn Heb вАЬit will not be pruned or hoedвАЭ (so NASB); ASV and NRSV both similar.
n2300512: tn Heb вАЬthe house of IsraelвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2300513: tn Heb вАЬmen,вАЭ but in a generic sense.
n2300514: tn Heb вАЬbut, look, disobedience.вАЭ The precise meaning of вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„В„§÷Є÷Љ„ЧвАО (mispakh), which occurs only here in the OT, is uncertain. Some have suggested a meaning вАЬbloodshed.вАЭ The term is obviously chosen for its wordplay value; it sounds very much like вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„§÷Є÷Љ„ШвАО (mishpat, вАЬjusticeвАЭ). The sound play draws attention to the point being made; the people have not met the LordвАЩs expectations.
n2300515: tn Heb вАЬbut, look, a cry for help.вАЭ The verb (вАЬhe waitedвАЭ) does double duty in the parallelism. вАП„¶÷∞„Ґ÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsea њqah) refers to the cries for help made by the oppressed. It sounds very much like вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsedaqah, вАЬfairnessвАЭ). The sound play draws attention to the point being made; the people have not met the LordвАЩs expectations.
n2300516: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] those who make a house touch a house.вАЭ
n2300517:
tn Heb вАЬ[who] bring a field near a field.вАЭ
sn This verse does not condemn real estate endeavors per se, but refers to the way in which the rich bureaucrats of Judah accumulated property by exploiting the poor, in violation of the covenantal principle that the land belonged to God and that every family was to have its own portion of land. See the note at 1:23.
n2300518: tn Heb вАЬuntil the end of the placeвАЭ; NASB вАЬuntil there is no more room.вАЭ
n2300519: tn Heb вАЬand you are made to dwell alone in the midst of the land.вАЭ
n2300520: tn Heb вАЬin my ears, the Lord of armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts].вАЭ
n2300521: tn Heb вАЬgreat and good [houses], without a resident.вАЭ
n2300522: tn Heb вАЬa ten-yoke vineyard.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„¶÷ґ„Ю÷ґ„УвАО (tsemed, вАЬyokeвАЭ) is here a unit of square measure. Apparently a ten-yoke vineyard covered the same amount of land it would take ten teams of oxen to plow in a certain period of time. The exact size is unknown.
n2300523: tn Heb вАЬone bath.вАЭ A bath was a liquid measure. Estimates of its modern equivalent range from approximately six to twelve gallons.
n2300524: tn Heb вАЬa homer.вАЭ A homer was a dry measure, the exact size of which is debated. Cf. NCV вАЬten bushelsвАЭ; CEV вАЬfive bushels.вАЭ
n2300525: tn Heb вАЬan ephah.вАЭ An ephah was a dry measure; there were ten ephahs in a homer. So this verse envisions major crop failure, where only one-tenth of the anticipated harvest is realized.
n2300526: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] those who arise early in the morning, [who] chase beer.вАЭ
n2300527:
tn Heb вАЬ[who] delay until dark, [until] wine enflames them.вАЭ
sn This verse does not condemn drinking per se, but refers to the carousing lifestyle of the rich bureaucrats, made possible by wealth taken from the poor. Their carousing is not the fundamental problem, but a disgusting symptom of the real diseaseвАФtheir social injustice.
n2300528: tn Two types of stringed instruments are specifically mentioned in the Hebrew text, the вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„†÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (kinnor, вАЬzitherвАЭ) and вАП„†÷ґ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (nevel, вАЬharpвАЭ).
n2300529: tn Heb вАЬthe work of the Lord they do not look at, and the work of his hands they do not see.вАЭ GodвАЩs вАЬworkвАЭ can sometimes be his creative deeds, but in this context it is the judgment that he is planning to bring upon his people (cf. vv. 19, 26; 10:12; 28:21).
n2300530: sn It is not certain if the prophet or the Lord is speaking at this point.
n2300531: tn The suffixed (perfect) form of the verb is used; in this way the coming event is described for rhetorical effect as occurring or as already completed.
n2300532: tn The third masculine singular suffix refers back to вАЬmy people.вАЭ
n2300533: tn Heb вАЬTheir glory will be men of hunger.вАЭ вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„С„Х÷є„УвАО (kavod, вАЬgloryвАЭ) is in opposition to вАП„Ф÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„ЯвАО (hamon, вАЬmassesвАЭ) and refers here to the rich and prominent members of the nation. Some prefer to repoint вАП„Ю÷∞„™÷µ„ЩвАО (metey, вАЬmen ofвАЭ) as вАП„Ю÷µ„™÷µ„ЩвАО (metey, вАЬdead ones ofвАЭ).
n2300534: tn The third masculine singular suffix refers back to вАЬmy people.вАЭ
n2300535: tn Heb вАЬand their masses will be parched [by] thirst.вАЭ
n2300536: tn Heb вАЬSheolвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, NRSV); the underworld, the land of the dead, according to the OT world view. Cf. NAB вАЬthe nether worldвАЭ; TEV, CEV вАЬthe world of the deadвАЭ; NLT вАЬthe grave.вАЭ
n2300537:
tn Heb вАЬso Sheol will make wide its throat, and open its mouth without limit.вАЭ
sn Death is portrayed in both the OT (Prov 1:12; Hab 2:5) and Canaanite myth as voraciously swallowing up its prey. In the myths Death is portrayed as having вАЬa lip to the earth, a lip to the heavensвА¶and a tongue to the stars.вАЭ (G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 69, text 5 ii 2-3.) Death describes his own appetite as follows: вАЬBut my appetite is the appetite of lions in the wasteвА¶.If it is in very truth my desire to consume вАШclayвАЩ [a reference to his human victims], then in truth by the handfuls I must eat it, whether my seven portions [indicating fullness and completeness] are already in the bowl or whether Nahar [the god of the river responsible for ferrying victims from the land of the living to the land of the dead] has to mix the cup.вАЭ (Driver, 68-69, text 5 i 14-22).
n2300538: tn Heb вАЬand her splendor and her masses will go down, and her tumult and the one who exults in her.вАЭ The antecedent of the four feminine singular pronominal suffixes used in v. 14b is unclear. The likely referent is personified Zion/Jerusalem (see 3:25-26; 4:4-5).
n2300539: tn Heb вАЬmen are brought down, men are brought low, the eyes of pride are brought low.вАЭ
n2300540: tn Or вАЬelevatedвАЭ; TEV вАЬthe Lord Almighty shows his greatness.вАЭ
n2300541: tn Heb вАЬby judgment/justice.вАЭ When God justly punishes the evildoers denounced in the preceding verses, he will be recognized as a mighty warrior.
n2300542:
tn Heb вАЬThe holy God will be set apart by fairness.вАЭ In this context GodвАЩs holiness is his sovereign royal authority, which implies a commitment to justice (see the note on the phrase вАЬthe sovereign king of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4). When God judges evildoers as they deserve, his sovereignty will be acknowledged.
sn The appearance of вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„§÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat, вАЬjusticeвАЭ) and вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsedaqah, вАЬfairnessвАЭ) here is rhetorically significant, when one recalls v. 7. There God denounces his people for failing to produce a society where вАЬjusticeвАЭ and вАЬfairnessвАЭ are valued and maintained. God will judge his people for their failure, taking вАЬjusticeвАЭ and вАЬfairnessвАЭ into his own hands.
n2300543: tn Or вАЬyoung ramsвАЭ; NIV, NCV вАЬsheepвАЭ; NLT вАЬflocks.вАЭ
n2300544:
tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand ruins, fatlings, resident foreigners, will eat.вАЭ This part of the verse has occasioned various suggestions of emendation. The parallelism is tighter if the second line refers to animals grazing. The translation, вАЬamid the ruins the fatlings and young sheep graze,вАЭ assumes an emendation of вАЬresident foreignersвАЭ (вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, garim) to вАЬyoung goats/sheepвАЭ (вАП„Т÷∞÷Љ„У÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО, gedaim)вАФconfusion of dalet and resh is quite commonвАФand understands вАЬfatlingsвАЭ and вАЬyoung sheepвАЭ as a compound subject or as in apposition as the subject of the verb. However, no emendations are necessary if the above translation is correct. The meaning of вАП„Ю÷µ„Ч÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mekhim) has a significant impact on oneвАЩs textual decision and translation. The noun can refer to a sacrificial (вАЬfatвАЭ) animal as it does in its only other occurrence (Ps 66:15). However, it could signify the rich of the earth (cf. Ps 22:29 [MT 30]: вАЬthe fat ones of the earthвАЭ), using a different word for вАЬfatnessвАЭ (Ps 22:29: вАП„У÷Є÷Љ„©÷ґ„Б„ЯвАО, dashen). If so, it serves a figurative reference to the rich. Consequently, the above translation coheres with the first half of the verse. Just as the sheep are out of place grazing in these places (вАЬas in their pastureвАЭ), the sojourners would not have expected to have the chance to eat in these locations. Both animals and itinerant foreigners would eat in places not normal for them.
sn The image completes the picture begun in v. 14 and adds to the irony. When judgment comes, Sheol will eat up the sinners who frequent the feasts; then the banqueting halls will lie in ruins and only sheep will eat there.
n2300546: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬWoe to those who pull evil with the ropes of emptiness, and, as [with] ropes of a cart, sin.вАЭ Though several textual details are unclear, the basic idea is apparent. The sinners are so attached to their sinful ways (compared here to a heavy load) that they strain to drag them along behind them. If вАП„©÷Є„Б„Х÷∞„РвАО (shave Њ, вАЬemptinessвАЭ) is retained, it makes a further comment on their lifestyle, denouncing it as one that is devoid of what is right and destined to lead to nothing but destruction. Because вАЬemptinessвАЭ does not form a very tight parallel with вАЬcartвАЭ in the next line, some emend вАП„©÷Є„Б„Х÷∞„РвАО to вАП„©÷ґ„В„ФвАО (se, вАЬsheepвАЭ) and вАП„Ґ÷≤„Т÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њagalah, вАЬcartвАЭ) to вАП„Ґ÷µ„Т÷ґ„ЬвАО ( њegel, вАЬcalfвАЭ): вАЬThose who pull evil along with a sheep halter are as good as dead, who pull sin with a calf ropeвАЭ (following the lead of the LXX and improving the internal parallelism of the verse). In this case, the verse pictures the sinners pulling sin along behind them as one pulls an animal with a halter. For a discussion of this view, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:163, n. 1. Nevertheless, this emendation is unnecessary. The above translation emphasizes the folly of the Israelites who hold on to their sin (and its punishment) even while they hope for divine intervention.
n2300547: tn Heb вАЬlet his work hurry, let it hasten.вАЭ The pronoun вАЬhisвАЭ refers to God, as the parallel line makes clear. The reference to his вАЬworkвАЭ alludes back to v. 12, which refers to his вАЬworkвАЭ of judgment. With these words the people challenged the prophetвАЩs warning of approaching judgment. They were in essence saying that they saw no evidence that God was about to work in such a way.
n2300548: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2300549: tn Heb вАЬdraw nearвАЭ (so NASB); NRSV вАЬhasten to fulfillment.вАЭ
n2300550: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] those who call.вАЭ See the note at v. 8.
n2300551: sn In this verse the prophet denounces the perversion of moral standards. Darkness and bitterness are metaphors for evil; light and sweetness symbolize uprightness.
n2300552: tn Heb вАЬthe wise in their own eyes.вАЭ
n2300553:
tn Heb вАЬ[who] before their faces are understanding ones.вАЭ
sn Verses 18-21 contain three вАЬwoe-sayingsвАЭ that are purely accusatory and have no formal announcement of judgment attached (as in the вАЬwoe-sayingsвАЭ recorded in vv. 8-17). While this lack of symmetry is odd, it has a clear rhetorical purpose. Having established a pattern in vv. 8-17, the prophet deviates from it in vv. 18-21 to grab his audienceвАЩs attention. By placing the вАЬwoesвАЭ in rapid succession and heaping up the accusatory elements, he highlights the peopleвАЩs guilt and introduces an element of tension and anticipation. One is reasonably certain that judgment will come, and when it does, it will be devastating. This anticipated devastation is described in frightening detail after the sixth and final woe (see vv. 22-30).
n2300554: tn The language used here is quite sarcastic and paves the way for the shocking description of the enemy army in vv. 25-30. The rich leaders of Judah are nothing but вАЬparty animalsвАЭ who are totally incapable of withstanding real warriors.
n2300555:
tn Heb вАЬand the just cause of the innocent ones they turn aside from him.вАЭ
sn In vv. 22-23 the prophet returns to themes with which he opened his speech. The accusatory elements of vv. 8, 11-12, 18-23 are arranged in a chiastic manner: (A) social injustice (8), (B) carousing (11-12a), (C) spiritual insensitivity (12b) // (C') spiritual insensitivity (18-21), (B') carousing (22), (A') social injustice (23).
n2300556: tn Heb вАЬa tongue of fireвАЭ (so NASB), referring to a tongue-shaped flame.
n2300557: sn They are compared to a flowering plant that withers quickly in a hot, arid climate.
n2300559: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2300560: tn Heb вАЬthe anger of the Lord rages.вАЭ
n2300561: tn Or вАЬextendsвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬhe hath stretched forth.вАЭ
n2300562: tn Or вАЬgarbageвАЭ (NCV, CEV, NLT); NAB, NASB, NIV вАЬrefuse.вАЭ
n2300563: tn Heb вАЬin all this his anger is not turned, and still his hand is outstretched.вАЭ
n2300564: tc The Hebrew text has literally, вАЬfor nations from a distance.вАЭ The following verses use singular forms to describe this nation, so the final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) on вАП„Ь÷∞„Т÷Љ„Х÷є„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (legoyim) may be enclitic or dittographic. In the latter case one could read вАП„Ь÷∞„Т„Х÷є„Щ „Ю÷µ„®÷Є„Ч„Х÷є„ІвАО (legoy merakhoq, вАЬfor a nation from a distanceвАЭ; see Deut 28:49; Joel 3:8). Another possibility is to emend the text from вАП„Ь÷Ј„Т÷Љ„Х÷є„Щ÷і„Э „Ю÷µ„®÷Є„Ч„Х÷є„ІвАО (laggoyim merakhoq) to вАП„Ь÷∞„Т„Х÷є„Щ „Ю÷і„Ю÷ґ÷Љ„®÷∞„Ч÷Є„ІвАО (legoy mimmerkhaq, вАЬfor a nation from a distant placeвАЭ), a phrase which occurs in Jer 5:15. In this case an error of misdivision has occurred in MT, the mem of the prefixed preposition being accidentally taken as a plural ending on the preceding word.
n2300565: tn Heb вАЬhe.вАЭ Singular forms are used throughout vv. 26-30 to describe this nation, but for stylistic reasons the translation uses the plural for these collective singulars.
n2300566: tn Heb вАЬand the belt on his waist is not opened, and the thong of his sandals is not torn in two.вАЭ
n2300567: tn Heb вАЬbentвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV); NIV вАЬare strung.вАЭ
n2300569: sn They are like a windstorm in their swift movement and in the way they kick up dust.
n2300571: tn Heb вАЬover itвАЭ; the referent (the prey) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2300572: tn Heb вАЬlike the growling of the sea.вАЭ
n2300573:
tn Heb вАЬand one will gaze toward the land, and look, darkness of distress, and light will grow dark by its [the landвАЩs?] clouds.вАЭ
sn The motif of light turning to darkness is ironic when compared to v. 20. There the sinners turn light (= moral/ethical good) to darkness (= moral/ethical evil). Now ironically the Lord will turn light (= the sinnersвАЩ sphere of existence and life) into darkness (= the judgment and death).
n230062: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in vv. 8, 11 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n230063: tn Hebrew вАП„©÷Є„В„®÷Є„£вАО (saraf, вАЬseraphвАЭ) literally means вАЬburning one,вАЭ perhaps suggesting that these creatures had a fiery appearance (cf. TEV, CEV вАЬflaming creaturesвАЭ; NCV вАЬheavenly creatures of fireвАЭ). Elsewhere in the OT the word вАЬseraphвАЭ refers to poisonous snakes (Num 21:6; Deut 8:15; Isa 14:29; 30:6). Perhaps they were called вАЬburning onesвАЭ because of their appearance or the effect of their venomous bites, which would cause a victim to burn up with fever. It is possible that the seraphs seen by Isaiah were at least partially serpentine in appearance. Though it might seem strange for a snake-like creature to have wings, two of the texts where вАЬseraphsвАЭ are snakes describe them as вАЬflyingвАЭ (Isa 14:29; 30:6), perhaps referring to their darting movements. See the note at 14:29.
n230064: sn Some understand вАЬfeetвАЭ here as a euphemistic reference to the genitals.
n230065:
tn Some have seen a reference to the Trinity in the seraphsвАЩ threefold declaration, вАЬholy, holy, holy.вАЭ This proposal has no linguistic or contextual basis and should be dismissed as allegorical. Hebrew sometimes uses repetition for emphasis. (See IBHS 233-34 ¬І12.5a; and GKC 431-32 ¬І133.k.) By repeating the word вАЬholy,вАЭ the seraphs emphasize the degree of the LordвАЩs holiness. For another example of threefold repetition for emphasis, see Ezek 21:27 (Heb. v. 32). (Perhaps Jer 22:29 provides another example.)
sn Or вАЬThe Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies has absolute sovereign authority!вАЭ The basic sense of the word вАЬholyвАЭ is вАЬset apart from that which is commonplace, special, unique.вАЭ In this context the LordвАЩs holiness is first and foremost his transcendent sovereignty as the ruler of the world. He is вАЬset apartвАЭ from the world over which he rules. Note the emphasis on the elevated position of his throne in v. 1 and his designation as вАЬthe kingвАЭ in v. 5. At the same time his holiness encompasses his moral authority, which derives from his royal position. As king he has the right to dictate to his subjects how they are to live; indeed his very own character sets the standard for proper behavior. He is вАЬset apartвАЭ from his subjects in a moral sense as well. He sets the standard; they fall short of it. Note that in v. 5 Isaiah laments that he is morally unworthy to be in the kingвАЩs presence.
n230066: tn Perhaps in this context, the title has a less militaristic connotation and pictures the Lord as the ruler of the heavenly assembly. See the note at 1:9.
n230067: tn On the phrase вАП„Р÷Ј„Ю÷Љ„Х÷є„™ „Ф÷Ј„°÷і÷Љ„§÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њammot hassippim, вАЬpivots of the framesвАЭ) see HALOT 763 s.v. вАП„°÷Ј„£вАО.
n230068: tn Isaiah uses the suffixed (perfect) form of the verb for rhetorical purposes. In this way his destruction is described as occurring or as already completed. Rather than understanding the verb as derived from вАП„У÷Є÷Љ„Ю÷Ј„ФвАО (damah, вАЬbe destroyedвАЭ), some take it from a proposed homonymic root вАП„У„Ю„ФвАО, which would mean вАЬbe silent.вАЭ In this case, one might translate, вАЬI must be silent.вАЭ
n230069: tn Heb вАЬa man unclean of lips am I.вАЭ Isaiah is not qualified to praise the king. His lips (the instruments of praise) are вАЬuncleanвАЭ because he has been contaminated by sin.
n2300610: tn Heb вАЬand among a nation unclean of lips I live.вАЭ
n2300611: tn Perhaps in this context, the title has a less militaristic connotation and pictures the Lord as the ruler of the heavenly assembly. See the note at 1:9.
n2300612: tn Or вАЬritually cleansed,вАЭ or вАЬatoned forвАЭ (NIV).
n2300613: tn Heb вАЬfor us.вАЭ The plural pronoun refers to the Lord, the seraphs, and the rest of the heavenly assembly.
n2300614: sn Do we take this commission at face value? Does the Lord really want to prevent his people from understanding, repenting, and being healed? Verse 9, which ostensibly records the content of IsaiahвАЩs message, is clearly ironic. As far as we know, Isaiah did not literally proclaim these exact words. The Hebrew imperatival forms are employed rhetorically and anticipate the response Isaiah will receive. When all is said and done, Isaiah might as well preface and conclude every message with these ironic words, which, though imperatival in form, might be paraphrased as follows: вАЬYou continually hear, but donвАЩt understand; you continually see, but donвАЩt perceive.вАЭ Isaiah might as well command them to be spiritually insensitive, because, as the preceding and following chapters make clear, the people are bent on that anyway. (This ironic command is comparable to saying to a particularly recalcitrant individual, вАЬGo ahead, be stubborn!вАЭ) Verse 10b is also clearly sarcastic. On the surface it seems to indicate IsaiahвАЩs hardening ministry will prevent genuine repentance. But, as the surrounding chapters clearly reveal, the people were hardly ready or willing to repent. Therefore, IsaiahвАЩs preaching was not needed to prevent repentance! Verse 10b reflects the peopleвАЩs attitude and might be paraphrased accordingly: вАЬOtherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their mind, repent, and be restored, and they certainly wouldnвАЩt want that, would they?вАЭ Of course, this sarcastic statement may also reveal that the Lord himself is now bent on judgment, not reconciliation. Just as PharaohвАЩs rejection of YahwehвАЩs ultimatum ignited judgment and foreclosed, at least temporarily, any opportunity for repentance, so the Lord may have come to the point where he has decreed to bring judgment before opening the door for repentance once more. The sarcastic statement in verse 10b would be an emphatic way of making this clear. (Perhaps we could expand our paraphrase: вАЬOtherwise they mightвА¶repent, and be restored, and they certainly wouldnвАЩt want that, would they? Besides, itвАЩs too late for that!вАЭ) Within this sarcastic framework, verse 10a must also be seen as ironic. As in verse 9 the imperatival forms should be taken as rhetorical and as anticipating the peopleвАЩs response. One might paraphrase: вАЬYour preaching will desensitize the minds of these people, make their hearing dull, and blind their eyes.вАЭ From the outset the Lord might as well command Isaiah to harden the people, because his preaching will end up having that effect. Despite the use of irony, we should still view this as a genuine, albeit indirect, act of divine hardening. After all, God did not have to send Isaiah. By sending him, he drives the sinful people further from him, for IsaiahвАЩs preaching, which focuses on the LordвАЩs covenantal demands and impending judgment upon covenantal rebellion, forces the people to confront their sin and then continues to desensitize them as they respond negatively to the message. As in the case of Pharaoh, YahwehвАЩs hardening is not arbitrarily imposed on a righteous or even morally neutral object. Rather his hardening is an element of his righteous judgment on recalcitrant sinners. Ironically, IsraelвАЩs rejection of prophetic preaching in turn expedites disciplinary punishment, and brings the battered people to a point where they might be ready for reconciliation. The prophesied judgment (cf. 6:11-13) was fulfilled by 701 b.c. when the Assyrians devastated the land (a situation presupposed by Isa 1:2-20; see especially vv. 4-9). At that time the divine hardening had run its course and Isaiah is able to issue an ultimatum (1:19-20), one which Hezekiah apparently took to heart, resulting in the sparing of Jerusalem (see Isa 36-39 and cf. Jer 26:18-19 with Mic 3:12).This interpretation, which holds in balance both IsraelвАЩs moral responsibility and the LordвАЩs sovereign work among his people, is consistent with other pertinent texts both within and outside the Book of Isaiah. Isa 3:9 declares that the people of Judah вАЬhave brought disaster upon themselves,вАЭ but Isa 29:9-10 indicates that the Lord was involved to some degree in desensitizing the people. Zech 7:11-12 looks back to the pre-exilic era (cf. v. 7) and observes that the earlier generations stubbornly hardened their hearts, but Ps 81:11-12, recalling this same period, states that the Lord вАЬgave them over to their stubborn hearts.вАЭ
n2300615: tn Heb вАЬand great is the abandonment in the midst of the land.вАЭ
n2300616: tn Or вАЬbe burnedвАЭ (NRSV); NIV вАЬlaid waste.вАЭ
n2300617: tn By the time of Mishnaic Hebrew the terms вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„Р÷µ„Ь÷Є„Ф „Х÷∞„Ы÷Є„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷є„ЯвАО (ka Њelah veka Њallon) meant вАЬlike a terebinth or like an oak.вАЭ They may have originally been generic terms for large trees, вАЬlike a massive tree or like a big tree.вАЭ See HALOT 52, s.v. I вАП„Р÷µ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО and 54, s.v. I вАП„Р÷µ„Ь„Х÷є„ЯвАО. These two trees were often part of cultic worship and this significance is prioritized in the translation.
n2300618: tc The translation accepts the emendation in BHS, reading вАП„Р÷≤„©÷µ„Б„®÷Є„ФвАО ( Њasherah) rather than вАП„Р÷≤„©÷ґ„Б„®вАО ( Њasher, вАЬwhichвАЭ). The term вАП„Р÷≤„©÷µ„Б„®÷Є„ФвАО may refer to the goddess Asherah or a sacred pole which presumably represented the goddess at worship sites. The translation also treats the Asherah as the third in a series of items, as if вАП„Х÷∞„Ы÷Ј„Р÷≤„©÷µ„Б„®÷Є„ФвАО (veka Њasherah, вАЬand like an AsherahвАЭ). But it may just as well be modifying the previous noun so that the whole phrase reads вАЬlike a terebinth and like the oak of an Asherah.вАЭ See J. D. W. Watts, Isaiah 1-33 (WBC), 101, 103.
n2300619: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷ґ÷Љ„С÷ґ„™вАО (matsevet) occurs only 4 times, twice in 2 Sam 18:18 and twice here. Most translations render it as вАЬstumpвАЭ (NASB, NIV, NRSV, ESV). HALOT identifies it as a memorial stone in 2 Sam 18:18 and says for Isa 6:13 that вАЬthe earlier translation вАШroot-stockвАЩ is questionableвАЭ (HALOT 621 s.v.).
n2300620: tc The MT reads вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„ЭвАО (bam, вАЬin themвАЭ) while the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads вАП„С„Ю„ФвАО (bamah, вАЬhigh placeвАЭ). The syntax of вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„ЭвАО is difficult in context and only translated by the KJV, вАЬas an oak, whose substance is in them, when they cast their leaves: so the holy seed shall be the substance thereof.вАЭ The KJVвАЩs reference to casting leaves assumes other emendations, or misinterprets or guesses at another meaning for the rare term вАП„©÷Ј„Б„Ь÷ґ÷Љ„Ы÷ґ„™вАО (shalleket, вАЬfelledвАЭ). The other major translations omit rendering вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„ЭвАО into English. The LXX has omitted this among several words lost to haplography.
n2300621: tc The MT reads вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„©÷Ј„Б„Ь÷ґ÷Љ„Ы÷ґ„™вАО (beshalleket, вАЬin fellingвАЭ) as part of вАЬlike a terebinth or like an oak, which in felling a pillar in them holy seed her pillar.вАЭ The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ю„©„Б„Ь„Ы„™вАО which appears to be a Hophal feminine participle from вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь÷Ј„ЪвАО (shalakh) meaning вАЬ[being] thrown down.вАЭ Though the text is difficult, the references to sacred trees and a sacred pillar suggest that the destruction of a high place is in view, an apt metaphor for the judgment of idolatrous Judah.
n2300622: tn Heb вАЬa holy offspring [is] its sacred pillar.вАЭ The text is difficult, leaving its meaning and its application unclear. If вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷ґ÷Љ„С÷ґ„™вАО (matsevet) is taken as вАЬstump,вАЭ one can see in this statement a brief glimpse of hope. The tree (the nation) is chopped down, but the stump (a righteous remnant) remains from which God can restore the nation. However, if вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷ґ÷Љ„С÷ґ„™вАО is taken as вАЬsacred pillarвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷ґ÷Љ„С÷Є„ФвАО, matsevah; see the previous note), it is much more difficult to take the final statement in a positive sense. In this case вАЬholy offspringвАЭ alludes to GodвАЩs ideal for his covenant people, the offspring of the patriarchs. Ironically that вАЬholyвАЭ nation is more like a вАЬsacred pillarвАЭ and it will be thrown down like a sacred pillar from a high place and its land destroyed like the sacred trees located at such shrines. Understood in this way, the ironic statement is entirely negative in tone, just like the rest of the preceding announcement of judgment. It also reminds the people of their failure; they did not oppose pagan religion, instead they embraced it. Now they will be destroyed in the same way they should have destroyed paganism. Another approach (see J. D. W. Watts, Isaiah 1-33, [WBC], 101, 109) is to take v. 12 and the beginning of 13 as the prophet asking a question, essentially вАЬwill it be destroyed?вАЭ Then the Lord answers beginning with the analogy, вАЬLike one of the large sacred trees.вАЭ If вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷ґ÷Љ„С÷Є„ФвАО is to be taken as a memorial, then the holy seed would serve as a reminder of their failure. But the question and answer would address a relevant question anticipated for the audience.
n230071: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n230073: tn Or perhaps, вАЬbut they were unable to attack it.вАЭ This statement sounds like a summary of the whole campaign. The following context explains why they were unable to defeat the southern kingdom. The parallel passage (2 Kgs 16:5; cf. Num 22:11; 1 Sam 17:9 for a similar construction) affirms that Syria and Israel besieged Ahaz. Consequently, the statement that вАЬthey were not able to battle against themвАЭ must refer to the inability to conquer Ahaz.
n230074: tn Heb вАЬhouse.вАЭ In this context the вАЬhouse of DavidвАЭ includes King Ahaz, his family, and the royal court. See also Jer 21:12; Zech 12:7-8, 10, 12, for a similar use of the phrase.
n230075: tn Heb вАЬrests upon.вАЭ Most understand the verb as вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh, вАЬrestвАЭ), but HALOT 685 s.v. II вАП„†„Ч„ФвАО proposes that this is a hapax legomenon which means вАЬstand by.вАЭ
n230076: tn Heb вАЬand his heart shook and the heart of his people shook, like the shaking of the trees of the forest before the wind.вАЭ The singular pronoun вАЬhisвАЭ is collective, referring to the Davidic house/family. вАП„Ь÷µ„С÷Є„СвАО (levav, вАЬheartвАЭ) here refers to the seat of the emotions.
