n230077: tn The name means вАЬa remnant will return.вАЭ Perhaps in this context, where the Lord is trying to encourage Ahaz, the name suggests that only a few of the enemy invaders will return home; the rest will be defeated.
n230078: tn Heb вАЬthe field of the washerвАЭ; traditionally вАЬthe fullerвАЩs fieldвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV); NIV вАЬthe WashermanвАЩs Field.вАЭ
n230079: tn Heb вАЬguard yourself and be quiet,вАЭ but the two verbs should be coordinated.
n2300710: tn Heb вАЬand let not your heart be weakвАЭ; ASV вАЬneither let thy heart be faint.вАЭ
n2300711: sn The derogatory metaphor indicates that the power of Rezin and Pekah is ready to die out.
n2300712: tn This sentence opens with the conjunction вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„Я „Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ya њan ki, вАЬbecauseвАЭ). Consequently some take vv. 5-6 with what precedes, as another reason why Ahaz might be tempted to fear (see v. 4). However, it is more likely that vv. 5-6 give the basis for the LordвАЩs announcement in vv. 7-9. The conjunction вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„Я „Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО here introduces the basis for judgment (as in 3:16; 8:6; 29:13), which is then followed by the formal announcement of judgment.
n2300713: tn Heb вАЬand let us break it open for ourselvesвАЭ; NASB вАЬmake for ourselves a breach in its wallsвАЭ; NLT вАЬfight our way into.вАЭ
n2300714:
tn Heb вАЬand we will make the son of Tabeel king in its midst.вАЭ
sn The precise identity of this would-be puppet king is unknown. He may have been a Syrian official or the ruler of one of the small neighboring states. See Y. Aharoni, Land of the Bible, 370.
n2300715:
tn Heb вАЬEphraim will be too shattered to be a nationвАЭ; NIV вАЬto be a people.вАЭ
sn This statement is problematic for several reasons. It seems to intrude stylistically, interrupting the symmetry of the immediately preceding and following lines. Furthermore, such a long-range prophecy lacks punch in the midst of the immediate crisis. After all, even if Israel were destroyed sometime within the next 65 years, a lot could still happen during that time, including the conquest of Judah and the demise of the Davidic family. Finally, the significance of the time frame is uncertain. Israel became an Assyrian province within the next 15 years and ceased to exist as a nation. For these reasons many regard the statement as a later insertion, but why a later editor would include the reference to вАЬ65 yearsвАЭ remains a mystery. Some try to relate the prophecy to the events alluded to in Ezra 4:2, 10, which refers to how the Assyrian kings Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal settled foreigners in former Israelite territory, perhaps around 670 b.c. However, even if the statement is referring to these events, it lacks rhetorical punch in its immediate context and has the earmarks of a later commentary that has been merged with the text in the process of transmission.
n2300716: tn Heb вАЬif you do not believe, you will not endure.вАЭ The verb forms are second plural; the Lord here addresses the entire Davidic family and court. (Verse 4 was addressed to the king.) There is a wordplay in the Hebrew text, designed to draw attention to the alternatives set before the king (cf. 1:20). вАЬBelieveвАЭ (вАП„™÷Ј„Р÷≥„Ю÷і„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО, ta Њaminu) is a Hiphil form of the verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЯвАО ( Њaman); вАЬendureвАЭ (вАП„™÷µ„Р÷Є„Ю÷µ„†„Х÷ЉвАО, te Њamenu) is a Niphal form of this same verb.
n2300717: tn Heb вАЬMake it as deep as Sheol or make it high upwards.вАЭ These words suggest that Ahaz can feel free to go beyond the bounds of ordinary human experience.
n2300718: tn Ahaz uses the verb вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ФвАО (nasah, вАЬtestвАЭ) in its negative sense of вАЬchallenge, provoke.вАЭ However, this is false piety, a smokescreen designed to cover up his lack of faith in the Lord.
n2300719: tn Heb вАЬand he said.вАЭ The subject is unexpressed, but the reference to вАЬmy GodвАЭ at the end of the verse indicates the prophet is speaking.
n2300720: tn The verb is second plural in form, because the prophet addresses the whole family of David. He continues to use the plural in v. 14 (with one exception; see the notes on that verse), but then switches back to the second singular (addressing Ahaz specifically) in vv. 16-17.
n2300721: tn Heb вАЬhouse.вАЭ See the note at v. 2.
n2300722: sn The address to the вАЬhouse of DavidвАЭ is designed to remind Ahaz and his royal court of the protection promised to them through the Davidic covenant. The kingвАЩs refusal to claim GodвАЩs promise magnifies his lack of faith.
n2300723: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Р„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њot, вАЬsignвАЭ) can refer to a miraculous event (see v. 11), but it does not carry this sense inherently. Elsewhere in Isaiah the word usually refers to a natural occurrence or an object/person vested with special significance (see 8:18; 19:20; 20:3; 37:30; 55:13; 66:19). Only in 38:7-8, 22 does it refer to a miraculous deed that involves suspending or overriding natural laws. The sign outlined in vv. 14-17 involves GodвАЩs providential control over events and their timing, but not necessarily miraculous intervention.
n2300724: tn Heb вАЬthe young woman.вАЭ The Hebrew article has been rendered as a demonstrative pronoun (вАЬthisвАЭ) in the translation to bring out its force. In addition, the syntactical sequence of вАП„Ф÷і„†÷µ÷Љ„ФвАО (hinneh) followed by the article followed by a noun always refers to something definite and present to the speaker and audience. It is very likely that Isaiah pointed to a woman who was present at the scene of the prophetвАЩs interview with Ahaz. Isaiah had met him where the people wash clothes (7:3) and likely there were many women present at the scene. IsaiahвАЩs address to the вАЬhouse of DavidвАЭ and his use of second plural forms directly suggest other people were present, and his use of the second feminine singular verb form (вАЬyou will nameвАЭ) later in the verse is best explained if addressed to a woman who is present.
n2300725: tn Traditionally, вАЬvirgin.вАЭ Because this verse from Isaiah is quoted in Matt 1:23 in connection with JesusвАЩ birth, the Isaiah passage has been regarded since the earliest Christian times as a prophecy of ChristвАЩs virgin birth. Much debate has taken place over the best way to translate this Hebrew term, although ultimately oneвАЩs view of the doctrine of the virgin birth of Christ is unaffected. Though the Hebrew word used here (вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО,  њalmah) can sometimes refer to a woman who is a virgin (Gen 24:43), it does not carry this meaning inherently. The word is simply the feminine form of the corresponding masculine noun вАП„Ґ÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„ЭвАО ( њelem, вАЬyoung manвАЭ; cf. 1 Sam 17:56; 20:22). The Aramaic and Ugaritic cognate terms are both used of women who are not virgins. The word seems to pertain to age, not sexual experience, and would normally be translated вАЬyoung woman.вАЭ The LXX translator(s) who later translated the Book of Isaiah into Greek sometime between the second and first century b.c., however, rendered the Hebrew term by the more specific Greek word ѕАќ±ѕБќЄќ≠ќљќњѕВ (parthenos), which does mean вАЬvirginвАЭ in a technical sense. This is the Greek term that also appears in the citation of Isa 7:14 in Matt 1:23. Therefore, regardless of the meaning of the term in the OT context, in the NT MatthewвАЩs usage of the Greek term ѕАќ±ѕБќЄќ≠ќљќњѕВ clearly indicates that from his perspective a virgin birth has taken place.
n2300726: tn Elsewhere the adjective вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (harah), when used predicatively, refers to a past pregnancy (from the narratorвАЩs perspective, 1 Sam 4:19), to a present condition (Gen 16:11; 38:24; 2 Sam 11:5), and to a conception that is about to occur in the near future (Judg 13:5, 7). (There is some uncertainty about the interpretation of Judg 13:5, 7, however. See the notes to those verses.) In Isa 7:14 one could translate, вАЬthe young woman is pregnant.вАЭ In this case the woman is probably a member of the royal family. Another option, the one followed in the present translation, takes the adjective in an imminent future sense, вАЬthe young woman is about to conceive.вАЭ In this case the woman could be a member of the royal family, or, more likely, the prophetess with whom Isaiah has sexual relations shortly after this (see 8:3).
n2300727: tn Heb вАЬand you will call his name.вАЭ The words вАЬyoung womanвАЭ are supplied in the translation to clarify the identity of the addressee. The verb is normally taken as an archaic third feminine singular form here, and translated, вАЬshe will call.вАЭ However the form (вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„Р„™вАО, qara Њt) is more naturally understood as second feminine singular, in which case the words would be addressed to the young woman mentioned just before this. In the three other occurrences of the third feminine singular perfect of I вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО (qara Њ, вАЬto callвАЭ), the form used is вАП„І÷Є„®÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (qar Њah; see Gen 29:35; 30:6; 1 Chr 4:9). A third feminine singular perfect вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„Р„™вАО does appear in Deut 31:29 and Jer 44:23, but the verb here is the homonym II вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО (вАЬto meet, encounterвАЭ). The form вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„Р„™вАО (from I вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО, вАЬto callвАЭ) appears in three other passages (Gen 16:11; Isa 60:18; Jer 3:4 [Qere]) and in each case is second feminine singular.
n2300728: sn The name Immanuel means вАЬGod [is] with us.вАЭ
n2300729: tn Or, perhaps вАЬcream,вАЭ frequently, вАЬcurdsвАЭ (NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); KJV, ASV вАЬbutterвАЭ; CEV вАЬyogurt.вАЭ
n2300730: tn Heb вАЬfor his knowing.вАЭ Traditionally the preposition has been translated in a temporal sense, вАЬwhen he knows.вАЭ However, though the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) can sometimes have a temporal force, it never carries such a nuance in any of the 40 other passages where it is used with the infinitive construct of вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ). Most often the construction indicates purpose/result. This sense is preferable here. The following context indicates that sour milk and honey will epitomize the devastation that GodвАЩs judgment will bring upon the land. Cultivated crops will be gone, and the people will be forced to live off the milk produced by their goats and the honey they find in the thickets. As the child is forced to eat a steady diet of this sour milk and honey, he will be reminded of the consequences of sin and motivated to make correct moral decisions in order to avoid further outbreaks of divine discipline.
n2300731: tn Heb вАЬfor, because.вАЭ The particle introduces the entire following context (vv. 16-25), which explains why Immanuel will be an appropriate name for the child, why he will eat sour milk and honey, and why experiencing such a diet will contribute to his moral development.
n2300732: sn Since вАЬtwo kingsвАЭ are referred to later in the verse, the вАЬlandвАЭ must here refer to Syria-Israel.
n2300733: tn Heb вАЬthe land will be abandoned, which you fear because of its two kings.вАЭ After the verb вАП„І„Х÷Љ„•вАО (quts, вАЬloathe, dreadвАЭ) the phrase вАП„Ю÷і„§÷∞÷Љ„†÷µ„ЩвАО (mippeney, вАЬfrom beforeвАЭ) introduces the cause of loathing/dread (see Gen 27:46; Exod 1:12; Num 22:3).
n2300734: tn Heb вАЬdaysвАЭ (so KJV, NAB); NASB, NRSV вАЬsuch days.вАЭ
n2300735: sn Initially the prophecy appears to be a message of salvation. Immanuel seems to have a positive ring to it, sour milk and honey elsewhere symbolize prosperity and blessing (see Deut 32:13-14; Job 20:17), verse 16 announces the defeat of JudahвАЩs enemies, and verse 17a could be taken as predicting a return to the glorious days of David and Solomon. However, the message turns sour in verses 17b-25. God will be with his people in judgment, as well as salvation. The curds and honey will be signs of deprivation, not prosperity, the relief announced in verse 16 will be short-lived, and the new era will be characterized by unprecedented humiliation, not a return to glory. Because of AhazвАЩs refusal to trust the Lord, potential blessing would be transformed into a curse, just as Isaiah turns an apparent prophecy of salvation into a message of judgment. Because the words вАЬthe king of AssyriaвАЭ are rather awkwardly tacked on to the end of the sentence, some regard them as a later addition. However, the very awkwardness facilitates the prophetвАЩs rhetorical strategy here, as he suddenly turns what sounds like a positive message into a judgment speech. Actually, вАЬthe king of Assyria,вАЭ stands in apposition to the earlier object вАЬdays,вАЭ and specifies who the main character of these coming вАЬdaysвАЭ will be.
n2300736: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300737: sn Swarming flies are irritating; bees are irritating and especially dangerous because of the pain they inflict with their sting (see Deut 1:44; Ps 118:12). The metaphors are well chosen, for the Assyrians (symbolized by the bees) were much more powerful and dangerous than the Egyptians (symbolized by the flies). Nevertheless both would put pressure on Judah, for Egypt wanted Judah as a buffer state against Assyrian aggression, while Assyrian wanted it as a base for operations against Egypt. Following the reference to sour milk and honey, the metaphor is especially apt, for flies are attracted to dairy products and bees can be found in the vicinity of honey.
n2300738: tn Heb вАЬand shall restвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NASB, NIV, NRSV вАЬand settle.вАЭ
n2300739: tn The meaning of this word (вАП„†÷Ј„Ф÷≤„Ь÷є„ЬвАО, nahalol) is uncertain; some understand this as referring to another type of thorn bush. For bibliography, see HALOT 676 s.v. I *вАП„†÷Ј„Ф÷≤„Ь÷є„ЬвАО.
n2300740: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so ASV, NASB); KJV вАЬIn the same day.вАЭ
n2300741: tn Heb вАЬthe riverвАЭ (so KJV); NASB вАЬthe Euphrates.вАЭ The name of the river has been supplied in the present translation for clarity.
n2300742: tn Heb вАЬthe hair of the feet.вАЭ The translation assumes that the word вАЬfeetвАЭ is used here as a euphemism for the genitals. See BDB 920 s.v. вАП„®÷ґ„Т÷ґ„ЬвАО.
n2300743: tn Heb вАЬin that day.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300744: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated, see note on 2:2.
n2300745: tn Heb вАЬin that day.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300746: tn Heb вАЬwill becomeвАЭ (so NASB); NAB вАЬshall be turned to.вАЭ
n2300747: tn Heb вАЬwith arrows and a bow.вАЭ The more common English idiom is вАЬbow[s] and arrow[s].вАЭ
n2300748: tn Heb вАЬgoвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV); TEV вАЬgo hunting.вАЭ
n2300750: tn Heb вАЬand all the hills which were hoed with a hoe, you will not go there [for] fear of the thorns and briers.вАЭ
n2300751:
tn Heb вАЬand it will become a pasture for cattle and a trampling place for sheep.вАЭ
sn At this point one is able to summarize the content of the вАЬsignвАЭ (vv. 14-15) as follows: A young woman known to be present when Isaiah delivered this message to Ahaz (perhaps a member of the royal family or the prophetess mentioned in 8:3) would soon give birth to a boy whom the mother would name Immanuel, вАЬGod is with us.вАЭ Eventually Immanuel would be forced to eat sour milk and honey, which would enable him to make correct moral decisions. How would this situation come about and how would it constitute a sign? Before this situation developed, the Israelites and Syrians would be defeated. But then the Lord would usher in a period of time unlike any since the division of the kingdom almost 200 years before. The Assyrians would overrun the land, destroy the crops, and force the people to subsist on goatsвАЩ milk and honey. At that time, as the people saw Immanuel eating his sour milk and honey, the Davidic family would be forced to acknowledge that God was indeed with them. He was present with them in the Syrian-Israelite crisis, fully capable of rescuing them, but he was also present with them in judgment, disciplining them for their lack of trust. The moral of the story is quite clear: Failure to appropriate GodвАЩs promises by faith can turn potential blessing into disciplinary judgment.
n230081: sn Probably made of metal, wood, or leather. See HALOT 193 s.v. вАП„Т÷і÷Љ„Ь÷Є÷Љ„Щ„Х÷є„ЯвАО.
n230083: tn Heb вАЬwith the stylus of a man.вАЭ The significance of the qualifying genitive вАЬa manвАЭ is uncertain. For various interpretations see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:219, n. 1.
n230084: tn Heb вАЬquickly, [the] plunder; it hurries, [the] loot.вАЭ The first word (вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ф÷µ„®вАО, maher) is either a Piel imperative (вАЬhurry [to]вАЭ) or infinitive (вАЬhurrying,вАЭ or вАЬquicklyвАЭ). The third word (вАП„Ч÷Є„©„БвАО, khash) is either a third masculine singular perfect or a masculine singular participle, in either case from the root вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„©„БвАО (khush, вАЬhurryвАЭ). Perhaps it is best to translate, вАЬOne hastens to the plunder, one hurries to the loot.вАЭ In this case вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ф÷µ„®вАО is understood as an infinitive functioning as a verb, the subject of вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„©„БвАО is taken as indefinite, and the two nouns are understood as adverbial accusatives. As we discover in v. 3, this is the name of the son to be born to Isaiah through the prophetess.
n230085: tn The form in the text is a cohortative with prefixed vav (вАП„ХвАО), suggesting that the Lord is announcing what he will do. Some prefer to change the verb to an imperative, вАЬand summon as witnesses,вАЭ a reading that finds support from the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa. Another option is to point the prefixed conjunction as a vav consecutive and translate, вАЬSo I summoned as witnesses.вАЭ In this case Isaiah is recalling his response to the LordвАЩs commission. In any case, the reference to witnesses suggests that the name and the child who bears it will function as signs.
n230086: tn The expression вАП„І÷Є„®÷Ј„С „Р÷ґ„ЬвАО (qarav  Њel) means вАЬdraw near toвАЭ or вАЬapproach,вАЭ but is also used as a euphemism for the intended purpose of sexual relations.
n230088: sn The childвАЩs name foreshadows what will happen to JudahвАЩs enemies; when their defeat takes place, the child will be a reminder that God predicted the event and brought it to pass. As such the child will be a reminder of GodвАЩs protective presence with his people.
n230089: tn The Hebrew text begins with вАЬbecause.вАЭ In the Hebrew text vv. 6-7 are one long sentence, with v. 6 giving the reason for judgment and v. 7 formally announcing it.
n2300810: sn The phrase вАЬwaters of ShiloahвАЭ probably refers to a stream that originated at the Gihon Spring and supplied the city of Jerusalem with water. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:225. In this context these waters stand in contrast to the flood waters of Assyria and symbolize GodвАЩs presence and blessings.
n2300811: tn The precise meaning of v. 6 has been debated. The translation above assumes that вАЬthese peopleвАЭ are the residents of Judah and that вАП„Ю÷Є„©„В„Х÷є„©„ВвАО (masos) is alternate form of вАП„Ю÷Є„°„Х÷є„°вАО (masos, вАЬdespair, meltвАЭ; see HALOT 606 s.v. вАП„Ю„°„°вАО). In this case vv. 7-8 in their entirety announce GodвАЩs disciplinary judgment on Judah. However, вАЬthese peopleвАЭ could refer to the Israelites and perhaps also the Syrians (cf v. 4). In this case вАП„Ю÷Є„©„В„Х÷є„©„ВвАО probably means вАЬjoy.вАЭ One could translate, вАЬand rejoice over Rezin and the son of Remaliah.вАЭ In this case v. 7a announces the judgment of Israel, with vv. 7b-8 then shifting the focus to the judgment of Judah.
n2300812: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2300813: tn Heb вАЬthe mighty and abundant waters of the river.вАЭ The referent of вАЬthe riverвАЭ here, the Euphrates River, has been specified in the translation for clarity. As the immediately following words indicate, these waters symbolize the Assyrian king and his armies, which will, as it were, inundate the land.
n2300814: tn Heb вАЬit will go up over all its stream beds and go over all its banks.вАЭ
n2300815: tn Heb вАЬand the spreading out of his wings [will be over] the fullness of the breadth of your land.вАЭ The metaphor changes here from raging flood to predatory bird.
n2300816: sn The appearance of the name Immanuel (вАЬGod is with usвАЭ) is ironic at this point, for God is present with his people in judgment. Immanuel is addressed here as if he has already been born and will see the judgment occur. This makes excellent sense if his birth has just been recorded. There are several reasons for considering Immanuel and Maher Shalal Hash Baz one and the same. 8:3 is a birth account which could easily be understood as recording the fulfillment of the birth prophecy of 7:14. The presence of a formal record/witnesses (8:1-2) suggests a sign function for the child (cf. 7:14). As in 7:14-16, the removal of JudahвАЩs enemies would take place before the child reached a specified age (cf. 8:4). Both 7:17-25 and 8:7-8 speak of an Assyrian invasion of Judah which would follow the defeat of Israel/Syria. The major objection to this view is the fact that different names appear, but such a phenomenon is not without parallel in the OT (cf. Gen 35:18). The name Immanuel may emphasize the basic fact of GodвАЩs presence, while the name Maher focuses on the specific nature of GodвАЩs involvement. In 7:14 the mother is viewed as naming the child, while in 8:3 Isaiah is instructed to give the childвАЩs name, but one might again point to Gen 35:18 for a precedent. The sign of the childвАЩs age appears to be different in 8:4 than in 7:15-16, but 7:15-16 pertains to the judgment on Judah, as well as the defeat of Israel/Syria (cf. vv. 17-25), while 8:4 deals only with the downfall of Israel/Syria. Some argue that the suffixed form вАЬyour landвАЭ in 8:8 points to a royal referent (a child of Ahaz or the Messiah), but usage elsewhere shows that the phrase does not need to be so restricted. While the suffix can refer to the king of a land (cf. Num 20:17; 21:22; Deut 2:27; Judg 11:17, 19; 2 Sam 24:13; 1 Kgs 11:22; Isa 14:20), it can also refer to one who is a native of a particular land (cf. Gen 12:1; 32:9; Jonah 1:8). (See also the use of вАЬhis landвАЭ in Isa 13:14 [where the suffix refers to a native of a land] and 37:7 [where it refers to a king].)
n2300817: tn The verb вАП„®÷є„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (ro њu) is a Qal imperative, masculine plural from вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њa њ, вАЬbreakвАЭ). Elsewhere both transitive (Job 34:24; Ps 2:9; Jer 15:12) and intransitive (Prov 25:19; Jer 11:16) senses are attested for the Qal of this verb. Because no object appears here, the form is likely intransitive: вАЬbe broken.вАЭ In this case the imperative is rhetorical (like вАЬbe shatteredвАЭ later in the verse) and equivalent to a prediction, вАЬyou will be broken.вАЭ On the rhetorical use of the imperative in general, see IBHS 572 ¬І34.4c; GKC 324 ¬І110.c.
n2300818: tn The imperatival form (Heb вАЬbe shatteredвАЭ) is rhetorical and expresses the speakerвАЩs firm conviction of the outcome of the nationsвАЩ attack. See the note on вАЬbe broken.вАЭ
n2300819: tn The initial imperative (вАЬget ready for battleвАЭ) acknowledges the reality of the nationsвАЩ hostility; the concluding imperative (Heb вАЬbe shatteredвАЭ) is rhetorical and expresses the speakersвАЩ firm conviction of the outcome of the nationsвАЩ attack. (See the note on вАЬbe broken.вАЭ) One could paraphrase, вАЬOkay, go ahead and prepare for battle since thatвАЩs what you want to do, but your actions will backfire and youвАЩll be shattered.вАЭ This rhetorical use of the imperatives is comparable to saying to a child who is bent on climbing a high tree, вАЬOkay, go ahead, climb the tree and break your arm!вАЭ What this really means is: вАЬOkay, go ahead and climb the tree since thatвАЩs what you really want to do, but your actions will backfire and youвАЩll break your arm.вАЭ The repetition of the statement in the final two lines of the verse gives the challenge the flavor of a taunt (ancient Israelite вАЬtrash talking,вАЭ as it were).
n2300820: tn Heb вАЬspeak a word, but it will not stand.вАЭ
n2300821: sn In these vv. 9-10 the tone shifts abruptly from judgment to hope. Hostile nations like Assyria may attack GodвАЩs people, but eventually they will be destroyed, for God is with his people, sometimes to punish, but ultimately to vindicate. In addition to being a reminder of GodвАЩs presence in the immediate crisis faced by Ahaz and Judah, Immanuel (whose name is echoed in this concluding statement) was a guarantee of the nationвАЩs future greatness in fulfillment of GodвАЩs covenantal promises. Eventually God would deliver his people from the hostile nations (vv. 9-10) through another child, an ideal Davidic ruler who would embody GodвАЩs presence in a special way (see 9:6-7). Jesus the Messiah is the fulfillment of the Davidic ideal prophesied by Isaiah, the one whom Immanuel foreshadowed. Through the miracle of the incarnation he is literally вАЬGod with us.вАЭ Matthew realized this and applied IsaiahвАЩs ancient prophecy of ImmanuelвАЩs birth to Jesus (Matt 1:22-23). The first Immanuel was a reminder to the people of GodвАЩs presence and a guarantee of a greater child to come who would manifest GodвАЩs presence in an even greater way. The second Immanuel is вАЬGod with usвАЭ in a heightened and infinitely superior sense. He вАЬfulfillsвАЭ IsaiahвАЩs Immanuel prophecy by bringing the typology intended by God to realization and by filling out or completing the pattern designed by God. Of course, in the ultimate fulfillment of the type, the incarnate ImmanuelвАЩs mother must be a virgin, so Matthew uses a Greek term (ѕАќ±ѕБќЄќ≠ќљќњѕВ, parthenos), which carries that technical meaning (in contrast to the Hebrew word вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО [ њalmah], which has the more general meaning вАЬyoung womanвАЭ). Matthew draws similar analogies between NT and OT events in 2:15, 18. The linking of these passages by analogy is termed вАЬfulfillment.вАЭ In 2:15 God calls Jesus, his perfect Son, out of Egypt, just as he did his son Israel in the days of Moses, an historical event referred to in Hos 11:1. In so doing he makes it clear that Jesus is the ideal Israel prophesied by Isaiah (see Isa 49:3), sent to restore wayward Israel (see Isa 49:5, cf. Matt 1:21). In 2:18 HerodвАЩs slaughter of the infants is another illustration of the oppressive treatment of GodвАЩs people by foreign tyrants. HerodвАЩs actions are analogous to those of the Assyrians, who deported the Israelites, causing the personified land to lament as inconsolably as a mother robbed of her little ones (Jer 31:15).
n2300822:
tc The MT reads вАП„ЫвАО (kaf, вАЬaccording toвАЭ), but many manuscripts read the more grammatical вАП„СвАО (bet, вАЬwithвАЭ).
tn Heb вАЬwith strength of hand/power.вАЭ
n2300823: tc Heb вАЬhe warned me against (or вАЬfromвАЭ) walking in the way of these people, saying.вАЭ Some want to change the pointing of the suffix and thereby emend the Qal imperfect вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷і„°÷∞÷Љ„®÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (veyissereni, вАЬhe was warning meвАЭ) to the more common Piel perfect вАП„Щ÷і„°÷∞÷Љ„®÷Ј„†÷і„ЩвАО (yisserani, вАЬhe warned meвАЭ). Others follow the lead of the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa and read вАП„Щ÷∞„°÷і„Щ„®÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (yesireni, вАЬhe was turning me aside,вАЭ a Hiphil imperfect from вАП„°„Х÷Љ„®вАО, sur). None of these are expected syntax. When a perfect verb is followed by a vav plus imperfect (an uncommon construction), the latter represents a contrasting shift to the future (Ps 91:14; Mal 1:4) or a modal clause, such as a purpose (Isa 41:26; Job 23:3; 41:3; Song 6:1). Otherwise the vav plus imperfect is proposed to be a preterite, often with support from the versions (Isa 41:5; 42:6). While a simple vav plus perfect might be considered, it is more likely that the vav should be repointed and the form read as a preterite (and most likely as Piel since the only two Qal finite forms are both text critical issues).
n2300824:
tn Heb вАЬDo not say, вАШConspiracy,вАЩ regarding everything about which these people say, вАШConspiracy.вАЩвАЭ The verb translated вАЬdo not sayвАЭ is second masculine plural, indicating that this exhortation is directed to Isaiah and other followers of the Lord (see v. 16).
sn The background of this command is uncertain. Perhaps the вАЬconspiracyвАЭ in view is the alliance between Israel and Syria. Some of the people may even have thought that individuals in Judah were plotting with Israel and Syria to overthrow the king.
n2300825: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ], him you must set apart.вАЭ The word order is emphatic, with the object being placed first.
n2300826: tn Heb вАЬhe is your [object of] fear; he is your [object of] terror.вАЭ The roots вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷µ„РвАО (yare Њ) and вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО ( њarats) are repeated from v. 12b.
n2300827: tn Because the metaphor of protection (вАЬsanctuaryвАЭ) does not fit the negative mood that follows in vv. 14b-15, some contend that вАП„Ю÷і„І÷∞„У÷Љ÷Є„©„БвАО (miqdash, вАЬsanctuaryвАЭ) probably needs to be emended to an original вАП„Ю„Х÷є„І÷µ„©„БвАО (moqesh, вАЬsnareвАЭ), a word that appears in the next line (cf. NAB and H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:355-56). If the MT reading is retained (as in the above translation), the fact that Yahweh is a sanctuary wraps up the point of v. 13 and stands in contrast to GodвАЩs treatment of those who rebel against him (the rest of v. 14).
n2300828: sn The two вАЬhousesвАЭ of Israel (= the patriarch Jacob) are the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah.
n2300829: tn These words are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. вАП„Х÷∞„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (vehayah, вАЬand he will beвАЭ) does double duty in the parallel structure of the verse.
n2300831: tn Heb вАЬover themвАЭ (so NASB); NCV вАЬover this rock.вАЭ
n2300832: tn Heb вАЬtie up [the] testimony.вАЭ The вАЬtestimonyвАЭ probably refers to the prophetic messages God has given him. When the prophecies are fulfilled, he will be able to produce this official, written record to confirm the authenticity of his ministry and to prove to the people that God is sovereign over events.
n2300833: tn Heb вАЬseal [the] instruction among my followers.вАЭ The вАЬinstructionвАЭ probably refers to the prophetвАЩs exhortations and warnings. When the people are judged for the sins, the prophet can produce these earlier messages and essentially say, вАЬI told you so.вАЭ In this way he can authenticate his ministry and impress upon the people the reality of GodвАЩs authority over them.
n2300834: tn Heb вАЬwho hides his face from the house of Jacob.вАЭ
n2300835: sn This refers to Shear Jashub (7:3) and Maher Shalal Hash Baz (8:1, 3).
n2300836: tn Or вАЬsigns and portentsвАЭ (NAB, NRSV). The names of all three individuals has symbolic value. IsaiahвАЩs name (which meant вАЬthe Lord deliversвАЭ) was a reminder that the Lord was the nationвАЩs only source of protection; Shear JashubвАЩs name was meant, at least originally, to encourage Ahaz (see the note at 7:3), and Maher Shalal Hash BazвАЩs name was a guarantee that God would defeat Israel and Syria (see the note at 8:4). The word вАП„Ю„Х÷є„§÷ґ„™вАО (mofet, вАЬportentвАЭ) can often refer to some miraculous event, but in 20:3 it is used, along with its synonym вАП„Р„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њot, вАЬsignвАЭ) of IsaiahвАЩs walking around half-naked as an object lesson of what would soon happen to the Egyptians.
n2300837: tn It is uncertain if the prophet or the Lord is speaking in vv. 19-22. If the latter, then vv. 19-22 resume the speech recorded in vv. 12-15, after the prophetвАЩs response in vv. 16-18.
n2300838: tn Heb вАЬinquire of the ritual pits and of the magicians who chirp and mutter.вАЭ The Hebrew word вАП„Р„Х÷є„СвАО ( Њov, вАЬritual pitвАЭ) refers to a pit used by a magician to conjure up underworld spirits. In 1 Sam 28:7 the witch of Endor is called a вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷≤„Ь÷Ј„™ „Р„Х÷є„СвАО (ba њalat  Њov, вАЬowner of a ritual pitвАЭ). See H. Hoffner, вАЬSecond Millennium Antecedents to the Hebrew  Њ√ФбЄЖ,вАЭ JBL 86 (1967): 385-401.
n2300839: tn Heb вАЬShould a nation not inquire of its gods on behalf of the living, (by inquiring) of the dead?вАЭ These words appear to be a continuation of the quotation begun in the first part of the verse. вАП„Р÷±„Ь÷є„Ф÷Є„Щ„ХвАО ( Њelohayv) may be translated вАЬits godsвАЭ or вАЬits God.вАЭ Some take the second half of the verse as the prophetвАЩs (or the LordвАЩs) rebuke of the people who advise seeking oracles at the ritual pits, but in this case the words вАЬthe dead on behalf of the livingвАЭ are difficult to explain.
n2300840: tn Heb вАЬto [the] instruction and to [the] testimony.вАЭ The words вАЬthen you must recallвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text vv. 19-20a are one long sentence, reading literally, вАЬWhen they say to youвА¶, to the instruction and to the testimony.вАЭ On the identity of the вАЬinstructionвАЭ and вАЬtestimonyвАЭ see the notes at v. 16.
n2300841: tn Heb вАЬIf they do not speak according to this word, [it is] because it has no light of dawn.вАЭ The literal translation suggests that вАЬthis wordвАЭ refers to the instruction/testimony. However, it is likely that вАП„Р÷і„Э „Ь÷є„РвАО ( Њim-lo Њ) is asseverative here, as in 5:9. In this case вАЬthis wordвАЭ refers to the quotation recorded in v. 19. For a discussion of the problem see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 230, n. 9. The singular pronoun in the second half of the verse is collective, referring back to the nation (see v. 19b).
n2300842: tn Heb вАЬhe will pass through it.вАЭ The subject of the collective singular verb is the nation. (See the preceding note.) The immediately preceding context supplies no antecedent for вАЬitвАЭ (a third feminine singular suffix in the Hebrew text); the suffix may refer to the land, which would be a reasonable referent with a verb of motion. Note also that вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets, вАЬlandвАЭ) does appear at the beginning of the next verse.
n2300843: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300846: tn The precise meaning of вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷Љ„£вАО (me њuf) is uncertain; the word occurs only here. See BDB 734 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„Ґ„Х÷Љ„£вАО.
n2300847: tn Heb вАЬand darkness, pushed.вАЭ The word вАП„Ю÷∞„†÷ї„У÷Љ÷Є„ЧвАО (menudakh) appears to be a Pual participle from вАП„†„У„ЧвАО (вАЬpushвАЭ), but the Piel is unattested for this verb and the Pual occurs only here.
n230091: sn In the Hebrew text (BHS) the chapter division comes one verse later than in the English Bible; 9:1 (8:23 HT). Thus 9:2-21 in the English Bible = 9:1-20 in the Hebrew text. Beginning with 10:1 the verse numbers in the English Bible and the Hebrew Bible are again the same.
n230092: tn The Hebrew text reads, вАЬIndeed there is no gloom for the one to whom there was anxiety for her.вАЭ The feminine singular pronominal suffix вАЬherвАЭ must refer to the land (cf. vv. 22a, 23b). So one could translate, вАЬIndeed there will be no gloom for the land which was anxious.вАЭ In this case the statement introduces the positive message to follow. Some assume an emendation of вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ, вАЬnoвАЭ) to вАП„Ь„Х÷євАО (lo, вАЬto himвАЭ) and of вАП„Ь÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (lah, вАЬto herвАЭ) to вАП„Ь„Х÷євАО (lo, вАЬto himвАЭ), yielding this literal reading: вАЬindeed there is gloom for him, for the one to whom there was anxiety for him.вАЭ In this case the statement concludes the preceding description of judgment.
n230093: tn The Lord must be understood as the subject of the two verbs in this verse.
n230094: sn The statement probably alludes to the Assyrian conquest of Israel in ca. 734-733 b.c., when Tiglath-Pileser III annexed much of IsraelвАЩs territory and reduced Samaria to a puppet state.
n230095: tn Heb вАЬJust as in earlier times he humiliatedвА¶, [in] the latter times he has brought honor.вАЭ The main verbs in vv. 1b-4 are Hebrew perfects. The prophet takes his rhetorical stance in the future age of restoration and describes future events as if they have already occurred. To capture the dramatic effect of the original text, the translation uses the English present or present perfect.
n230096: sn These three geographical designations may refer to provinces established by the Assyrians in 734-733 b.c. The вАЬway of the seaвАЭ is the province of Dor, along the Mediterranean coast, the вАЬregion beyond the JordanвАЭ is the province of Gilead in Transjordan, and вАЬGalilee of the nationsвАЭ (a title that alludes to how the territory had been overrun by foreigners) is the province of Megiddo located west of the Sea of Galilee. See Y. Aharoni, Land of the Bible, 374.
n230097: sn The darkness symbolizes judgment and its effects (see 8:22); the light represents deliverance and its effects, brought about by the emergence of a conquering Davidic king (see vv. 3-6).
n230098: tn Traditionally вАП„¶÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (tsalmavet) has been interpreted as a compound noun, meaning вАЬshadow of deathвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NIV), but usage indicates that the word, though it sometimes refers to death, means вАЬdarkness.вАЭ The term should probably be repointed as an abstract noun вАП„¶÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„™вАО (tsalmut). See the note at Ps 23:4.
n230099: sn The Lord is addressed directly in vv. 3-4.
n2300910: tc The Hebrew consonantal text reads вАЬYou multiply the nation, you do not make great the joy.вАЭ The particle вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ, вАЬnotвАЭ) is obviously incorrect; the marginal reading has вАП„Ь„Х÷євАО (lo, вАЬto himвАЭ). In this case, one should translate, вАЬYou multiply the nation, you increase his (i.e., their) joy.вАЭ However, the parallelism is tighter if one emends вАП„Ф÷Ј„Т÷Љ„Х÷є„Щ „Ь„Х÷євАО (haggoy lo, вАЬthe nation, to himвАЭ) to вАП„Ф÷Ј„Т÷і÷Љ„Щ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (haggilah, вАЬthe joy,вАЭ a noun attested in Isa 65:18), which corresponds to вАП„Ф÷Ј„©÷і÷Љ„В„Ю÷∞„Ч÷Є„ФвАО (hasimkhah, вАЬthe joyвАЭ) later in the verse (H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:386). As attractive as this reading is, it has no textual evidence supporting it. The MT reading (accepting the marginal reading вАЬto himвАЭ for the negative particle вАЬnotвАЭ) affirms that Yahweh caused the nation to grow in population and increased their joy.
n2300911: tn Heb вАЬas they are happy.вАЭ The word вАЬwarriorsвАЭ is supplied in the translation to clarify the word picture. This last simile comes close to reality, for vv. 4-5 indicate that the people have won a great military victory over their oppressors.
n2300912: tn Heb вАЬfor the yoke of his burden, and the staff of his shoulder, the scepter of the oppressor against him.вАЭ The singular pronouns are collective, referring to the people. The oppressed nation is compared to an ox weighed down by a heavy yoke and an animal that is prodded and beaten.
n2300913: sn This alludes to GideonвАЩs victory over Midian (Judg 7-8), when the Lord delivered Israel from an oppressive foreign invader.
n2300914: tn Heb вАЬIndeed every boot marching with shaking.вАЭ On the meaning of вАП„°÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЯвАО (se Њon, вАЬbootвАЭ) and the related denominative verb, both of which occur only here, see HALOT 738 s.v. вАП„°÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЯвАО.
n2300915: tn The Hebrew perfect (translated вАЬhas been bornвАЭ and вАЬhas been givenвАЭ) is used here as the prophet takes a rhetorical stance in the future. See the note at 9:1.
n2300916: tn Heb вАЬdominion has come to be on his shoulder.вАЭ In most occurrences, a burden is placed on the shoulder, but authority is also placed on the shoulder in Isa 22:22.
n2300917: tn Or вАЬand dominion was on his shoulders, and he called his name.вАЭ The prefixed verbs with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive are used with the same rhetorical sense as the perfects in v. 6a. See the preceding note. There is great debate over the syntactical structure of the verse. No subject is indicated for the verb вАЬhe called.вАЭ If all the titles that follow are ones given to the king, then the subject of the verb must be indefinite, вАЬone calls.вАЭ However, some have suggested that one to three of the titles that follow refer to God, not the king. For example, the traditional punctuation of the Hebrew text suggests the translation, вАЬand the Wonderful Adviser, the Mighty God called his name, вАШEverlasting Father, Prince of Peace.вАЩвАЭ
n2300918: tn Or вАЬExtraordinary Strategist,вАЭ вАЬa wonder of a counselor,вАЭ or вАЬone who plans a miraculous thingвАЭ (HALOT 928 s.v. вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„Ь÷ґ„РвАО). Some have seen two titles here (вАЬWonderfulвАЭ and вАЬCounselor,вАЭ cf. KJV, ASV). However, the pattern of the following three titles (each contains two elements) and the use of the roots вАП„§÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„РвАО (pala Њ) and вАП„Щ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„•вАО (ya њats) together in Isa 25:1 (cf. вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„Щ „Ґ÷Є„©÷і„В„Щ„™÷Є „§÷ґ÷Љ„Ь÷ґ„Р „Ґ÷µ„¶„Х÷є„™ „Ю÷µ„®÷Є„Ч„Х÷є„І „Р÷±„Ю„Х÷Љ„†÷Є„Ф „Р÷є„Ю÷ґ„ЯвАО) and 28:29 (cf. вАП„Ф÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„Р „Ґ÷µ„¶÷Є„ФвАО) suggest otherwise. The term вАП„Щ„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„•вАО (yo њets) could be taken as appositional (genitive or otherwise) of species (вАЬa wonder, i.e., a wonder as a counselor,вАЭ cf. NAB вАЬWonder-CounselorвАЭ) or as a substantival participle for which вАП„§÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„РвАО provides the direct object (вАЬone who counsels wondersвАЭ). вАП„Щ„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„•вАО is used as a royal title elsewhere (cf. Mic 4:9). Here it probably refers to the kingвАЩs ability to devise military strategy, as suggested by the context (cf. vv. 3-4 and the following title вАП„Р÷µ„Ь „Т÷і÷Љ„С÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО,  Њel gibbor). In Isa 11:2 (also a description of this king) вАП„Ґ÷µ„¶÷Є„ФвАО ( њetsah) is linked with вАП„Т÷∞÷Љ„С„Х÷Љ„®÷Є„ФвАО (gevurah, the latter being typically used of military might, cf. BDB 150 s.v.). Note also вАП„Ґ÷µ„¶÷Є„Ф „Х÷Љ„Т÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„®÷Є„Ф „Ь÷Ј„Ю÷і÷Љ„Ь÷∞„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО in Isa 36:5. вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„Ь÷ґ„РвАО (pele Њ) is typically used of God (cf. however Lam 1:9). Does this suggest the deity of the messianic ruler? The NT certainly teaches he is God, but did Isaiah necessarily have this in mind over 700 years before his birth? Since Isa 11:2 points out that this king will receive the spirit of the Lord, which will enable him to counsel, it is possible to argue that the kingвАЩs counsel is вАЬextraordinaryвАЭ because it finds its source in the divine spirit.
n2300919: tn вАП„Т÷і÷Љ„С÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (gibbor) is probably an attributive adjective (вАЬmighty GodвАЭ), though one might translate вАЬGod is a warriorвАЭ or вАЬGod is mighty.вАЭ Scholars have interpreted this title in two ways. A number of them have argued that the title portrays the king as GodвАЩs representative on the battlefield, whom God empowers in a supernatural way (see J. H. Hayes and S. A. Irvine, Isaiah, 181-82). They contend that this sense seems more likely in the original context of the prophecy. They would suggest that having read the NT, we might in retrospect interpret this title as indicating the coming kingвАЩs deity, but it is unlikely that Isaiah or his audience would have understood the title in such a bold way. Ps 45:6 addresses the Davidic king as вАЬGodвАЭ because he ruled and fought as GodвАЩs representative on earth. Ancient Near Eastern art and literature picture gods training kings for battle, bestowing special weapons, and intervening in battle. According to Egyptian propaganda, the Hittites described Rameses II as follows: вАЬNo man is he who is among us, It is Seth great-of-strength, Baal in person; Not deeds of man are these his doings, They are of one who is uniqueвАЭ (See Miriam Lichtheim, Ancient Egyptian Literature, 2:67). According to proponents of this view, Isa 9:6 probably envisions a similar kind of response when friends and foes alike look at the Davidic king in full battle regalia. When the kingвАЩs enemies oppose him on the battlefield, they are, as it were, fighting against God himself. The other option is to regard this title as a reference to God, confronting IsaiahвАЩs readers with the divinity of this promised вАЬchild.вАЭ The use of this same title that clearly refers to God in a later passage (Isa 10:21) supports this interpretation. Other passages depict Yahweh as the great God and great warrior (Deut 10:17; Jer. 32:18). Although this connection of a child who is born with deity is unparalleled in any earlier biblical texts, IsaiahвАЩs use of this title to make this connection represents IsaiahвАЩs attempt (at GodвАЩs behest) to advance Israel in their understanding of the ideal Davidic king for whom they long.
n2300920: tn This title must not be taken in an anachronistic Trinitarian sense. (To do so would be theologically problematic, for the вАЬSonвАЭ is the messianic king and is distinct in his person from God the вАЬFather.вАЭ) Rather, in its original context the title pictures the king as the protector of his people. For a similar use of вАЬfatherвАЭ see Isa 22:21 and Job 29:16. This figurative, idiomatic use of вАЬfatherвАЭ is not limited to the Bible. In a Phoenician inscription (ca. 850-800 b.c.) the ruler Kilamuwa declares: вАЬTo some I was a father, to others I was a mother.вАЭ In another inscription (ca. 800 b.c.) the ruler Azitawadda boasts that the god Baal made him вАЬa father and a motherвАЭ to his people. (See ANET 499-500.) The use of вАЬeverlastingвАЭ might suggest the deity of the king (as the one who has total control over eternity), but Isaiah and his audience may have understood the term as royal hyperbole emphasizing the kingвАЩs long reign or enduring dynasty (for examples of such hyperbolic language used of the Davidic king, see 1 Kgs 1:31; Pss 21:4-6; 61:6-7; 72:5, 17). The New Testament indicates that the hyperbolic language (as in the case of the title вАЬMighty GodвАЭ) is literally realized in the ultimate fulfillment of the prophecy, for Jesus will rule eternally.
n2300921: tn This title pictures the king as one who establishes a safe socio-economic environment for his people. It hardly depicts him as a meek individual, for he establishes peace through military strength (as the preceding context and the first two royal titles indicate). His people experience safety and prosperity because their invincible king destroys their enemies. See Pss 72 and 144 for parallels to these themes.
n2300922: tc The MT has вАП„Ь÷∞„Э÷Ј„®÷∞„С÷µ÷Љ„ФвАО (lemarbeh, вАЬto the abundance ofвАЭ), where the first two letters вАП„Ь„ЭвАО were incorrectly duplicated from the end of the previous word (вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО, shalom) ending v. 6. Notice that the mem is in the form for ending words, i.e., вАП„ЭвАО not the expected вАП„ЮвАО. A few Hebrew mss, the LXX, Targum, and Vulgate reflect a text with вАП„®„С„ФвАО, вАЬgreat is the dominion.вАЭ
n2300923: tn Heb вАЬand to peace there will be no endвАЭ (KJV and ASV both similar). On the political and socio-economic sense of вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom) in this context, see the note at v. 6 on вАЬPrince of Peace.вАЭ
n2300924: tn Heb вАЬover the throne of David, and over his kingdom.вАЭ The referent of the pronoun вАЬhisвАЭ (i.e., David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2300925: tn The pronoun вАЬitвАЭ (both times in this line) refers back to вАЬkingdom;вАЭ the noun and pronoun are both feminine.
n2300926: tn Heb вАЬwith/by justice and fairnessвАЭ; ASV вАЬwith justice and with righteousness.вАЭ
n2300927: sn In this context the LordвАЩs вАЬzealвАЭ refers to his intense devotion to and love for his people which prompts him to vindicate them and to fulfill his promises to David and the nation.
n2300928: sn The following speech (9:8-10:4) assumes that God has already sent judgment (see v. 9), but it also announces that further judgment is around the corner (10:1-4). The speech seems to describe a series of past judgments on the northern kingdom which is ready to intensify further in the devastation announced in 10:1-4. It may have been written prior to the Assyrian conquest of the northern kingdom in 734-733 b.c., or sometime between that invasion and the downfall of Samaria in 722 b.c. The structure of the speech displays four panels, each of which ends with the refrain, вАЬThrough all this, his anger did not subside; his hand remained outstretchedвАЭ (9:12b; 17b; 21b; 10:4b): Panel I: (A) Description of past judgment (9:8); (B) Description of the peopleвАЩs attitude toward past judgment (9:9-10); (C) Description of past judgment (9:11-12a); (D) Refrain (9:12b); Panel II: (A) Description of the peopleвАЩs attitude toward past judgment (9:13); (B) Description of past judgment (9:14-17a); (C) Refrain (9:17b); Panel III: (A) Description of past judgment (9:18-21a); (B) Refrain (9:21b); Panel IV: (A) Woe oracle announcing future judgment (10:1-4a); (B) Refrain (10:4b).
n2300929: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in v. 17 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2300930: tn Heb вАЬsent a wordвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NRSV); NASB вАЬsends a message.вАЭ
n2300931: tn The present translation assumes that this verse refers to judgment that had already fallen. Both verbs (perfects) are taken as indicating simple past; the vav (вАП„ХвАО) on the second verb is understood as a simple vav conjunctive. Another option is to understand the verse as describing a future judgment (see 10:1-4). In this case the first verb is a perfect of certitude; the vav on the second verb is a vav consecutive.
n2300932: tn The translation assumes that vv. 9-10 describe the peopleвАЩs response to a past judgment (v. 8). The perfect is understood as indicating simple past and the vav (вАП„ХвАО) is taken as conjunctive. Another option is to take the vav on the perfect as consecutive and translate, вАЬall the people will know.вАЭ
n2300933: tn Heb вАЬand the people, all of them, knew; Ephraim and the residents of Samaria.вАЭ
n2300934: tn Heb вАЬwith pride and arrogance of heart, saying.вАЭ
n2300935: sn Though judgment (see v. 8) had taken away the prosperity they did have (symbolized by the bricks and sycamore fig trees), they arrogantly expected the future to bring even greater prosperity (symbolized by the chiseled stone and cedars).
n2300936: tn The translation assumes that the prefixed verb with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive continues the narrative of past judgment.
n2300937: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬadversaries of Rezin against him [i.e., them].вАЭ The next verse describes how the Syrians (over whom Rezin ruled, see 7:1, 8) and the Philistines encroached on IsraelвАЩs territory. Since the Syrians and Israelites were allies by 735 b.c. (see 7:1), the hostilities described probably occurred earlier, while Israel was still pro-Assyrian. In this case one might understand the phrase вАП„¶÷Є„®÷µ„Щ „®÷∞„¶÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (tsare retsin, вАЬadversaries of RezinвАЭ) as meaning вАЬadversaries sent from Rezin.вАЭ However, another option, the one chosen in the translation above, is to emend the phrase to вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (tsarayv, вАЬhis [i.e., their] adversariesвАЭ). This creates tighter parallelism with the next line (note вАЬhis [i.e., their] enemiesвАЭ). The phrase in the Hebrew text may be explained as virtually dittographic.
n2300938: tn The prefixed verbal form is understood as a preterite, used, as is often the case in poetry, without vav consecutive. Note that prefixed forms with vav consecutive both precede (вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷∞„©÷Ј„В„Т÷µ÷Љ„СвАО, vayesaggev, вАЬhe provokedвАЭ) and follow in v. 12 (вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷є÷Љ„Р„Ы÷∞„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО, vayyo Њkhelu, вАЬand they devouredвАЭ) this verb.
n2300939: tn Heb вАЬand they devoured Israel with all the mouthвАЭ; NIV вАЬwith open mouthвАЭ; TLB вАЬWith bared fangs.вАЭ
n2300940: tn Heb вАЬin all this his anger is not turned, and still his hand is outstretched.вАЭ One could translate in the past tense here (and in 9:17b and 21b), but the appearance of the refrain in 10:4b, where it follows a woe oracle prophesying a future judgment, suggests it is a dramatic portrait of the judge which did not change throughout this period of past judgment and will remain unchanged in the future. The English present tense is chosen to best reflect this dramatic mood. (See also 5:25b, where the refrain appears following a dramatic description of coming judgment.)
n2300941: tn This verse describes the peopleвАЩs response to the judgment described in vv. 11-12. The perfects are understood as indicating simple past.
n2300942: sn The metaphor in this line is that of a reed being cut down.
n2300943: tn Heb вАЬthe elder and the one lifted up with respect to the face.вАЭ For another example of the Hebrew idiom, see 2 Kgs 5:1.
n2300944: tn Heb вАЬand the ones being led were swallowed up.вАЭ Instead of taking вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷ї„Ь÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mebulla њim) from вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (bala њ, вАЬto swallowвАЭ), HALOT 134 s.v. вАП„С„Ь„ҐвАО proposes a rare homonymic root вАП„С„Ь„ҐвАО (вАЬconfuseвАЭ) here.
n2300945: tn The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ь„Р „Щ„Ч„Ю„Х„ЬвАО (вАЬhe did not spareвАЭ) which is an obvious attempt to tighten the parallelism (note вАЬhe took no pityвАЭ in the next line). Instead of taking вАП„©÷Є„В„Ю÷Ј„ЧвАО (samakh) in one of its well attested senses (вАЬrejoice over, be pleased withвАЭ), some propose, with support from Arabic, a rare homonymic root meaning вАЬbe merciful.вАЭ
n2300946: tn The translation understands the prefixed verbs вАП„Щ÷і„©÷∞„В„Ю÷Ј„ЧвАО (yismakh) and вАП„Щ÷∞„®÷Ј„Ч÷µ„ЭвАО (yerakhem) as preterites without vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive. (See v. 11 and the note on вАЬhe stirred up.вАЭ)
n2300947: tn Or вАЬdefiledвАЭ; cf. ASV вАЬprofaneвАЭ; NAB вАЬprofanedвАЭ; NIV вАЬungodly.вАЭ
n2300948: tn вАП„Ю÷µ„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (mera њ) is a Hiphil participle from вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њa њ, вАЬbe evilвАЭ). The intransitive Hiphil has an exhibitive force here, indicating that they exhibited outwardly the evidence of an inward condition by committing evil deeds.
n2300949: tn Or вАЬfoolishnessвАЭ (NASB), here in a moral-ethical sense.
n2300950:
tn Heb вАЬin all this his anger is not turned, and still his hand is outstretched.вАЭ
sn See the note at 9:12.
n2300951: tn Or вАЬIndeedвАЭ (cf. NIV вАЬSurelyвАЭ). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2300952: sn Evil was uncontrollable and destructive, and so can be compared to a forest fire.
n2300953: tn Heb вАЬand they swirled [with] the rising of the smokeвАЭ (cf. NRSV).
n2300954: tn The precise meaning of the verb вАП„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷Ј„ЭвАО ( њetam), which occurs only here, is uncertain, though the context strongly suggests that it means вАЬburn, scorch.вАЭ
n2300955: sn The uncontrollable fire of the peopleвАЩs wickedness (v. 18) is intensified by the fire of the LordвАЩs judgment (v. 19). God allows (or causes) their wickedness to become self-destructive as civil strife and civil war break out in the land.
n2300956: tn Heb вАЬmen were not showing compassion to their brothers.вАЭ The idiom вАЬmen to their brothersвАЭ is idiomatic for reciprocity. The prefixed verbal form is either a preterite without vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive or an imperfect used in a customary sense, describing continual or repeated behavior in past time.
n2300957: tn Or вАЬcut.вАЭ The verb вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„Ц÷Ј„®вАО (gazar) means вАЬto cut.вАЭ If it is understood here, then one might paraphrase, вАЬThey slice off meat on the right.вАЭ However, HALOT 187 s.v. I вАП„Т„Ц„®вАО, proposes here a rare homonym meaning вАЬto devour.вАЭ
n2300958: tn The prefixed verbal form is either a preterite without vav consecutive or an imperfect used in a customary sense, describing continual or repeated behavior in past time.
n2300959: tn Some suggest that вАП„Ц÷∞„®÷є„Ґ„Х÷євАО (zero њo, вАЬhis armвАЭ) be repointed вАП„Ц÷Ј„®÷∞„Ґ„Х÷євАО (zar њo, вАЬhis offspringвАЭ). In either case, the metaphor is that of a desperately hungry man who resorts to an almost unthinkable act to satisfy his appetite. He eats everything he can find to his right, but still being unsatisfied, then turns to his left and eats everything he can find there. Still being desperate for food, he then resorts to eating his own flesh (or offspring, as this phrase is metaphorically understood by some English versions, e.g., NIV, NCV, TEV, NLT). The reality behind the metaphor is the political turmoil of the period, as the next verse explains. There was civil strife within the northern kingdom; even the descendants of Joseph were at each otherвАЩs throats. Then the northern kingdom turned on their southern brother, Judah.
n2300960: tn The words вАЬfought againstвАЭ are supplied in the translation both here and later in this verse for stylistic reasons.
n2300961:
tn Heb вАЬin all this his anger is not turned, and still his hand is outstretchedвАЭ (KJV and ASV both similar); NIV вАЬhis hand is still upraised.вАЭ
sn See the note at 9:12.
n230101: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] those who decree evil decrees.вАЭ
n230102: tn Heb вАЬ[to] the writers who write out harm.вАЭ The participle and verb are in the Piel, suggesting repetitive action.
n230103: tn Or вАЬrobвАЭ (ASV, NASB, NCV, NRSV); KJV вАЬtake away the right from the poor.вАЭ
n230104:
tn Heb вАЬso that widows are their plunder, and they can loot orphans.вАЭ
sn On the socio-economic background of vv. 1-2, see the note at 1:23.
n230105: tn Heb вАЬthe day of visitationвАЭ (so KJV, ASV), that is, the day when God arrives to execute justice on the oppressors.
n230106: tn Heb вАЬexcept one kneels in the place of the prisoner, and in the place of the slain [who] fall.вАЭ On the force of вАП„С÷і÷Љ„Ь÷∞„™÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (bilti, вАЬexceptвАЭ) and its logical connection to what precedes, see BDB 116 s.v. вАП„С÷µ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО. On the force of вАП„™÷Ј÷Љ„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (takhat, вАЬin the place ofвАЭ) here, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:258, n. 6.
n230107:
tn Heb вАЬin all this his anger was not turned, and still his hand was outstretchedвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬhis hand is stretched out still.вАЭ
sn See the note at 9:12.
n230108: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] Assyria, the club of my anger.вАЭ
n230109: tn Heb вАЬa cudgel is he, in their hand is my anger.вАЭ It seems likely that the final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) on вАП„С÷∞„Щ÷Є„У÷Є„ЭвАО (beyadam) is not a pronominal suffix (вАЬin their handвАЭ), but an enclitic mem. If so, one can translate literally, вАЬa cudgel is he in the hand of my anger.вАЭ
n2301010: sn Throughout this section singular forms are used to refer to Assyria; perhaps the king of Assyria is in view (see v. 12).
n2301011: tn Or вАЬdefiledвАЭ; cf. ASV вАЬprofaneвАЭ; NAB вАЬimpiousвАЭ; NCV вАЬseparated from God.вАЭ
n2301012: tn Heb вАЬand against the people of my anger I ordered him.вАЭ
n2301013: tn Heb вАЬto make it [i.e., the people] a trampled place.вАЭ
n2301014: tn Heb вАЬbut he, not so does he intend, and his heart, not so does it think.вАЭ
n2301015: tn Heb вАЬfor to destroy [is] in his heart, and to cut off nations, not a few.вАЭ
n2301017: sn CalnehвА¶CarchemishвА¶HamathвА¶ArpadвА¶SamariaвА¶Damascus. The city states listed here were conquered by the Assyrians between 740-717 b.c. The point of the rhetorical questions is that no one can stand before AssyriaвАЩs might. On the geographical, rather than chronological arrangement of the cities, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:264, n. 4.
n2301018: tn Heb вАЬJust as my hand found the kingdoms of the idol[s].вАЭ The comparison is expanded in v. 11a (note вАЬasвАЭ) and completed in v. 11b (note вАЬsoвАЭ).
n2301020:
tn The statement is constructed as a rhetorical question in the Hebrew text: вАЬIs it not [true that] just as I have done to Samaria and its idols, so I will do to Jerusalem and its idols?вАЩвАЭ
sn This statement indicates that the prophecy dates sometime between 722-701 b.c.
n2301021: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301022: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in vv. 16, 23, 24, 33 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2301023: tn Heb вАЬhis work on/against.вАЭ Cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV вАЬonвАЭ; NIV вАЬagainst.вАЭ
n2301024: tn Heb вАЬIвАЭ; The Lord is speaking here, as in vv. 5-6a.
n2301025: tn Heb вАЬI will visit [judgment] on the fruit of the greatness of the heart of the king of Assyria, and on the glory of the height of his eyes.вАЭ The proud Assyrian king is likened to a large, beautiful fruit tree.
n2301026: tn Heb вАЬremoved the borders of nationsвАЭ; cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV вАЬboundaries.вАЭ
n2301027: tc The consonantal text (Kethib) has вАП„Ы÷∞÷Љ„Р÷Ј„С÷і÷Љ„Щ„®вАО (ke Њabbir, вАЬlike a strong oneвАЭ); the marginal reading (Qere) is вАП„Ы÷Ј÷Љ„С÷і„Щ„®вАО (kavir, вАЬmighty oneвАЭ).
n2301028: tn Heb вАЬand I brought down, like a strong one, ones sitting [or вАЬlivingвАЭ].вАЭ The participle вАП„Щ„Х÷є„©÷∞„Б„С÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (yoshevim, вАЬones sittingвАЭ) could refer to the inhabitants of the nations, but the translation assumes that it refers to those who sit on thrones, i.e., rulers. See BDB 442 s.v. вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„СвАО and HALOT 444 s.v. вАП„Щ„©„Б„СвАО.
n2301029: sn The AssyriansвАЩ conquests were relatively unopposed, like robbing a birdвАЩs nest of its eggs when the mother bird is absent.
n2301030: tn Heb вАЬthe one who pushes it back and forthвАЭ; KJV вАЬhim that shaketh itвАЭ; ASV вАЬhim that wieldeth it.вАЭ
n2301031: sn The irrational arrogance of the Assyrians (v. 15) will prompt the judgment about to be described.
n2301032: tn Heb вАЬwill send leanness against his healthy onesвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬwill send a wasting disease.вАЭ
n2301033: tc Heb вАЬand in the place of his glory burning will burn, like the burning of fire.вАЭ The highly repetitive text (вАП„Щ÷µ„І÷Ј„У „Щ÷∞„І÷є„У „Ы÷і÷Љ„Щ„І„Х÷є„У „Р÷µ„©„БвАО, yeqad yeqod kiqodвАЩesh) may be dittographic; if the second consonantal sequence вАП„Щ„І„УвАО is omitted, the text would read вАЬand in the place of his glory, it will burn like the burning of fire.вАЭ
n2301034: tn In this context the вАЬLight of IsraelвАЭ is a divine title (note the parallel title вАЬHoly OneвАЭ). The title points to GodвАЩs royal splendor, which overshadows and, when transformed into fire, destroys the вАЬmajestic gloryвАЭ of the king of Assyria (v. 16b).
n2301035: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2301036: tn Heb вАЬhis.вАЭ In vv. 17-19 the Assyrian king and his empire are compared to a great forest and orchard that are destroyed by fire (symbolic of the Lord).
n2301037: tn Heb вАЬfrom breath to flesh it will destroy.вАЭ The expression вАЬfrom breath to fleshвАЭ refers to the two basic components of a person, the immaterial (lifeвАЩs breath) and the material (flesh). Here the phrase is used idiomatically to indicate totality.
n2301038: tn The precise meaning of this line is uncertain. вАП„Ю÷Є„°÷Ј„°вАО (masas), which is used elsewhere of substances dissolving or melting, may here mean вАЬwaste awayвАЭ or вАЬdespair.вАЭ вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„°вАО (nasas), which appears only here, may mean вАЬbe sickвАЭ or вАЬstagger, despair.вАЭ See BDB 651 s.v. I вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„°вАО and HALOT 703 s.v. I вАП„†„°„°вАО. One might translate the line literally, вАЬlike the wasting away of one who is sickвАЭ (cf. NRSV вАЬas when an invalid wastes awayвАЭ).
n2301039: tn Heb вАЬand the rest of the trees of his forest will be counted, and a child will record them.вАЭ
n2301040: tn Or вАЬin that day.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301042: tn Heb вАЬon one who strikes him down.вАЭ This individual is the king (вАЬforeign leaderвАЭ) of the oppressing nation (which NLT specifies as вАЬthe AssyriansвАЭ).
n2301043: tn Or вАЬsincerelyвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NAB, NRSV вАЬin truth.вАЭ
n2301044: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2301045: tn The referent of вАП„Р÷µ„Ь „Т÷і÷Љ„С÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО ( Њel gibbor, вАЬmighty GodвАЭ) is uncertain. The title appears only here and in 9:6, where it is one of the royal titles of the coming ideal Davidic king. (Similar titles appear in Deut 10:17 and Neh 9:32 [вАЬthe great, mighty, and awesome GodвАЭ] and in Jer 32:18 [вАЬthe great and mighty GodвАЭ]. Both titles refer to God.) Though Hos 3:5 pictures Israel someday seeking вАЬDavid their king,вАЭ and provides some support for a messianic interpretation of Isa 10:21, the Davidic king is not mentioned in the immediate context of Isa 10:21 (see Isa 11, however). The preceding verse mentions Israel relying on the Lord, so it is likely that the title refers to God here.
n2301047: sn The twofold appearance of the statement вАЬa remnant will come backвАЭ (вАП„©÷∞„Б„Р÷Є„® „Щ÷Є„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО, she Њar yashuv) in vv. 21-22 echoes and probably plays off the name of IsaiahвАЩs son Shear Jashub (see 7:3). In its original context the name was meant to encourage Ahaz (see the note at 7:3), but here it has taken on new dimensions. In light of AhazвАЩs failure and the judgment it brings down on the land, the name Shear Jashub now foreshadows the destiny of the nation. According to vv. 21-22, there is good news and bad news. The good news is that a remnant of GodвАЩs people will return; the bad news is that only a remnant will be preserved and come back. Like the name Immanuel, this name foreshadows both judgment (see the notes at 7:25 and 8:8) and ultimate restoration (see the note at 8:10).
n2301048: tn Or вАЬpredeterminedвАЭ; cf. ASV, NASB вАЬis determinedвАЭ; TEV вАЬis in store.вАЭ
n2301049: tn вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsedaqah) often means вАЬrighteousness,вАЭ but here it refers to GodвАЩs just judgment.
n2301051: tn Heb вАЬIndeed (or perhaps вАЬforвАЭ) destruction and what is decreed the Sovereign Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies is about to accomplish in the middle of all the land.вАЭ The phrase вАП„Ы÷Є„Ь÷Є„Р „Х÷∞„†÷ґ„Ч÷±„®÷Є„¶÷Є„ФвАО (khala Њ venekheratsah, вАЬdestruction and what is decreedвАЭ) is a hendiadys; the two terms express one idea, with the second qualifying the first.
n2301052: tn Heb вАЬtherefore.вАЭ The message that follows is one of encouragement, for it focuses on the eventual destruction of the Assyrians. Consequently вАЬthereforeвАЭ relates back to vv. 5-21, not to vv. 22-23, which must be viewed as a brief parenthesis in an otherwise positive speech.
n2301053: tn Heb вАЬin the way [or вАЬmannerвАЭ] of Egypt.вАЭ
n2301054: tc The Hebrew text has simply вАЬfury,вАЭ but the pronominal element can be assumed on the basis of what immediately follows (see вАЬmy angerвАЭ in the clause). It is possible that the suffixed yod (вАП„ЩвАО) has been accidentally dropped by virtual haplography. Note that a vav (вАП„ХвАО) is prefixed to the form that immediately follows; yod and vav are very similar in later script phases.
n2301055: tn Heb вАЬhimвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB); the singular refers to the leader or king who stands for the entire nation. This is specified by NCV, CEV as вАЬthe Assyrians.вАЭ
n2301056: sn According to Judg 7:25, the Ephraimites executed the Midianite general Oreb at a rock which was subsequently named after the executed enemy.
n2301057: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand his staff [will be] against the sea, and he will lift it in the way [or вАЬmannerвАЭ] of Egypt.вАЭ One could emend the text of вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь „Ф÷Ј„Щ÷Є÷Љ„ЭвАО ( њal hayyam, вАЬagainst the seaвАЭ) to вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷µ„Щ„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО ( њalehem, вАЬagainst themвАЭ). The proposed shift from the third singular pronoun (note вАЬbeat himвАЭ earlier in the verse) to the plural is not problematic, for the singular is collective. Note that a third plural pronoun is used at the end of v. 25 (вАЬtheir destructionвАЭ). If the text is retained, as the translation does, вАЬthe seaвАЭ symbolizes AssyriaвАЩs hostility, the metaphor being introduced because of the reference to Egypt. The final phrase, вАЬin the way/manner of Egypt,вАЭ probably refers to the way in which God used the staff of Moses to bring judgment down on Egypt.
n2301058: tn Or вАЬin that dayвАЭ (KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301059: tn Heb вАЬhe [i.e., the Lord] will remove his [i.e, AssyriaвАЩs] burden from upon your shoulder.вАЭ
n2301060: tc The meaning of this line is uncertain. The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand the yoke will be destroyed (or perhaps, вАЬpulled downвАЭ) because of fatness.вАЭ Perhaps this is a bizarre picture of an ox growing so fat that it breaks the yoke around its neck or can no longer fit into its yoke. Fatness would symbolize the LordвАЩs restored blessings; the removal of the yoke would symbolize the cessation of Assyrian oppression. Because of the difficulty of the metaphor, many prefer to emend the text at this point. Some emend вАП„Х÷∞„Ч÷ї„С÷Ј÷Љ„ЬвАО (vekhubbal, вАЬand it will be destroyed,вАЭ a perfect with prefixed vav), to вАП„Щ÷і„Ч÷∞„С÷є÷Љ„ЬвАО (yikhbol, вАЬ[it] will be destroyed,вАЭ an imperfect), and take the verb with what precedes, вАЬand their yoke will be destroyed from your neck.вАЭ Proponents of this view (cf. NAB, NRSV) then emend вАП„Ґ÷є„ЬвАО ( њol, вАЬyokeвАЭ) to вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њalah, вАЬhe came upвАЭ) and understand this verb as introducing the following description of the Assyrian invasion (vv. 28-32). вАП„Ю÷і„§÷∞÷Љ„†÷µ„Щ „©÷Є„Б„Ю÷ґ„ЯвАО (mippeney shamen, вАЬbecause of fatnessвАЭ) is then emended to read вАЬfrom before RimmonвАЭ (NAB, NRSV), вАЬfrom before Samaria,вАЭ or вАЬfrom before Jeshimon.вАЭ Although this line may present difficulties, it appears best to regard the line as a graphic depiction of GodвАЩs abundant blessings on his servant nation.
n2301061: sn Verses 28-31 display a staccato style; the statements are short and disconnected (no conjunctions appear in the Hebrew text). The translation to follow strives for a choppy style that reflects the mood of the speech.
n2301062:
tn Heb вАЬhe,вАЭ that is, the Assyrians (as the preceding context suggests). Cf. NCV вАЬThe army of Assyria.вАЭ
sn Verses 28-32 describe an invasion of Judah from the north. There is no scholarly consensus on when this particular invasion took place, if at all. J. H. Hayes and S. A. Irvine (Isaiah, 209-10) suggest the text describes the Israelite-Syrian invasion of Judah (ca. 735 b.c.), but this proposal disregards the preceding context, which prophesies the destruction of Assyria. Some suggest that this invasion occurred in conjunction with SargonвАЩs western campaign of 713-711 b.c., but there is no historical evidence of such an invasion at that time. Many others identify the invasion as SennacheribвАЩs in 701 b.c., but historical records indicate Sennacherib approached Jerusalem from the southwest. J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:274-75) prefers to see the description as rhetorical and as not corresponding to any particular historical event, but Hayes and Irvine argue that the precise geographical details militate against such a proposal. Perhaps it is best to label the account as rhetorical-prophetic. The prophecy of the invasion was not necessarily intended to be a literal itinerary of the AssyriansвАЩ movements; rather its primary purpose was to create a foreboding mood. Geographical references contribute to this purpose, but they merely reflect how one would expect an Assyrian invasion to proceed, not necessarily how the actual invasion would progress. Despite its rhetorical nature, the prophecy does point to the invasion of 701 b.c., as the announcement of the invadersвАЩ downfall in vv. 33-34 makes clear; it was essentially fulfilled at that time. For further discussion of the problem, see R. E. Clements, Isaiah (NCBC), 117-19. On the geographical details of the account, see Y. Aharoni, Land of the Bible, 393.
n2301064: tc The Hebrew text reads вАЬPoor [is] Anathoth.вАЭ The parallelism is tighter if вАП„Ґ÷≤„†÷і„Щ÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО ( њaniyyah, вАЬpoorвАЭ) is emended to вАП„Ґ÷≤„†÷і„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО ( њaniha, вАЬanswer herвАЭ). Note how the preceding two lines have an imperative followed by a proper name.
n2301065: tc The consonantal text (Kethib) has вАЬa mountain of a house (вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™вАО, bet), Zion,вАЭ but the marginal reading (Qere) correctly reads вАЬthe mountain of the daughter (вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„™вАО, bat) of Zion.вАЭ On the phrase вАЬDaughter Zion,вАЭ see the note on the same phrase in 1:8.
n2301066:
tc The Hebrew text reads вАЬwith terrifying power,вАЭ or вАЬwith a crash.вАЭ вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„¶÷Є„ФвАО (ma њaratsah, вАЬterrifying powerвАЭ or вАЬcrashвАЭ) occurs only here. Several have suggested an emendation to вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Є„УвАО (ma њatsad, вАЬaxвАЭ) parallel to вАЬaxвАЭ in v. 34; see HALOT 615 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Є„УвАО and H. Wildberger, Isaiah, 1:448.
sn As in vv. 12 (see the note there) and 18, the Assyrians are compared to a tree/forest in vv. 33-34.
n2301067: tn Heb вАЬthe exalted of the height.вАЭ This could refer to the highest branches (cf. TEV) or the tallest trees (cf. NIV, NRSV).
n2301068: tn The Hebrew text has, вАЬand Lebanon, by/as [?] a mighty one, will fall.вАЭ The translation above takes the preposition вАП„С÷∞÷ЉвАО (bet) prefixed to вАЬmighty oneвАЭ as indicating identity, вАЬLebanon, as a mighty one, will fall.вАЭ In this case вАЬmighty oneвАЭ describes Lebanon. (In Ezek 17:23 and Zech 11:2 the adjective is used of LebanonвАЩs cedars.) Another option is to take the preposition as indicating agency and interpret вАЬmighty oneвАЭ as a divine title (see Isa 33:21). One could then translate, вАЬand Lebanon will fall by [the agency of] the Mighty One.вАЭ
n230111: sn The text mentions DavidвАЩs father Jesse, instead of the great king himself. Perhaps this is done for rhetorical reasons to suggest that a new David, not just another disappointing Davidic descendant, will arise. Other prophets call the coming ideal Davidic king вАЬDavidвАЭ or picture him as the second coming of David, as it were. See Jer 30:9; Ezek 34:23-24; 37:24-25; Hos 3:5; Mic 5:2 (as well as the note there).
n230112: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Щ÷і„§÷∞„®÷ґ„ФвАО (yifreh, вАЬwill bear fruit,вАЭ from вАП„§÷Є÷Љ„®÷Є„ФвАО, parah), but the ancient versions, as well as the parallelism suggest that вАП„Щ÷і„§÷∞„®÷Ј„ЧвАО (yifrakh, вАЬwill sproutвАЭ, from вАП„§÷Є÷Љ„®÷Ј„ЧвАО, parakh) is the better reading here. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:276, n. 2.
n230113: sn Like David (1 Sam 16:13), this king will be energized by the LordвАЩs Spirit.
n230114: tn Heb вАЬa spirit of wisdom and understanding.вАЭ The synonyms are joined here to emphasize the degree of wisdom he will possess. His wisdom will enable him to make just legal decisions (v. 3). A very similar phrase occurs in Eph 1:17.
n230115: tn Heb вАЬa spirit of counsel [or вАЬstrategyвАЭ] and strength.вАЭ The construction is a hendiadys; the point is that he will have the strength/ability to execute the plans/strategies he devises. This ability will enable him to suppress oppressors and implement just policies (v. 4).
n230116: tn Heb вАЬa spirit of knowledge and fear of the Lord.вАЭ вАЬKnowledgeвАЭ is used here in its covenantal sense and refers to a recognition of GodвАЩs authority and a willingness to submit to it. See Jer 22:16. вАЬFearвАЭ here refers to a healthy respect for GodвАЩs authority which produces obedience. Taken together the two terms emphasize the single quality of loyalty to the Lord. This loyalty guarantees that he will make just legal decisions and implement just policies (vv. 4-5).
n230117: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand his smelling is in the fear of the Lord.вАЭ In Amos 5:21 the Hiphil of вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh, вАЬsmellвАЭ) carries the nuance of вАЬsmell with delight, get pleasure from.вАЭ There the Lord declares that he does not вАЬsmell with delightвАЭ (i.e., get pleasure from) IsraelвАЩs religious assemblies, which probably stand by metonymy for the incense offered during these festivals. In Isa 11:3 there is no sacrificial context to suggest such a use, but it is possible that вАЬthe fear of the LordвАЭ is likened to incense. This coming king will get the same kind of delight from obeying (fearing) the Lord, as a deity does in the incense offered by worshipers. Some regard such an explanation as strained in this context, and prefer to omit this line from the text as a virtual dittograph of the preceding statement.
n230118: tn Heb вАЬby what appears to his eyesвАЭ; KJV вАЬafter the sight of his eyesвАЭ; NIV вАЬby what he sees with his eyes.вАЭ
n230119: tn Heb вАЬby what is heard by his earsвАЭ; NRSV вАЬby what his ears hear.вАЭ
n2301110: tn Heb вАЬwith justiceвАЭ (so NAB) or вАЬwith righteousnessвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2301111: tn Heb вАЬmake decisions with rectitudeвАЭ; cf. ASV, NRSV вАЬand decide with equity.вАЭ
n2301112: tn Or вАЬlandвАЭ (NAB, NCV, CEV). It is uncertain if the passage is picturing universal dominion or focusing on the kingвАЩs rule over his covenant people. The reference to GodвАЩs вАЬholy mountainвАЭ in v. 9 and the description of renewed Israelite conquests in v. 14 suggest the latter, though v. 10 seems to refer to a universal kingdom (see 2:2-4).
n2301113: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand he will strike the earth with the scepter of his mouth.вАЭ Some have suggested that in this context вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets, вАЬearthвАЭ) as an object of judgment seems too broad in scope. The parallelism is tighter if one emends the word to вАП„•вАО(вАП„ЩвАО)вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷івАО ( њarits, вАЬpotentate, tyrantвАЭ). The phrase вАЬscepter of his mouthвАЭ refers to the royal (note вАЬscepterвАЭ) decrees that he proclaims with his mouth. Because these decrees will have authority and power (see v. 2) behind them, they can be described as вАЬstrikingвАЭ the tyrants down. Nevertheless, the MT reading may not need emending. Isaiah refers to the entire вАЬearthвАЭ as the object of GodвАЩs judgment in several places without specifying the wicked as the object of the judgment (Isa 24:17-21; 26:9, 21; 28:22; cf. 13:11).
n2301114: tn Heb вАЬand by the breath of his lips he will kill the wicked.вАЭ The вАЬbreath of his lipsвАЭ refers to his speech, specifically in this context his official decrees that the wicked oppressors be eliminated from his realm. See the preceding note.
n2301115: tn Heb вАЬJustice will be the belt [or вАЬundergarmentвАЭ] on his waist, integrity the belt [or вАЬundergarmentвАЭ] on his hips.вАЭ The point of the metaphor is uncertain. If a belt worn outside the robe is in view, then the point might be that justice/integrity will be readily visible or that these qualities will give support to his rule. If an undergarment is in view, then the idea might be that these characteristics support his rule or that they are basic to everything else.
n2301116: tn The verb вАП„Т÷Љ„Х÷Љ„®вАО (gur) normally refers to living as a dependent, resident foreigner in another society.
n2301117: tc The Hebrew text reads, вАЬand an ox, and a young lion, and a fatling together.вАЭ Since the preceding lines refer to two animals and include a verb, many emend вАП„Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„®÷і„Щ„РвАО (umeri Њ, вАЬand the fatlingвАЭ) to an otherwise unattested verb вАП„Щ÷і„Ю÷∞„®÷∞„Р„Х÷ЉвАО (yimre Њu, вАЬthey will grazeвАЭ); cf. NAB, TEV, CEV. One of the Qumran copies of Isaiah confirms this suggestion (1QIsaa). The present translation assumes this change.
n2301118: tn Heb вАЬand a cow and a bear will grazeвАФtogetherвАФthey will lie down, their young.вАЭ This is a case of pivot pattern; вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„У÷Љ÷Є„ХвАО (yakhdav, вАЬtogetherвАЭ) goes with both the preceding and following statements.
n2301119: tn Heb вАЬone sucking,вАЭ i.e., still being nursed by his mother.
n2301120: tn Or perhaps, вАЬcobraвАЭ (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NCV); KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬasp.вАЭ
n2301121: tc The Hebrew text has the otherwise unattested вАП„Ю÷∞„Р„Х÷Љ„®÷Ј„™вАО (me Њurat, вАЬplace of lightвАЭ), i.e., opening of a hole. Some prefer to emend to вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„™вАО (me њarat, вАЬcave, denвАЭ).
n2301122: tn Heb вАЬone who is weanedвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV).
