n2301123: sn The transformation of the animal kingdom depicted here typifies what will occur in human society under the just rule of the ideal king (see vv. 3-5). The categories вАЬpredator-preyвАЭ (i.e., oppressor-oppressed) will no longer exist.
n2301124: tn Heb вАЬin all my holy mountain.вАЭ In the most basic sense the LordвАЩs вАЬholy mountainвАЭ is the mountain from which he rules over his kingdom (see Ezek 28:14, 16). More specifically it probably refers to Mount Zion/Jerusalem or to the entire land of Israel (see Pss 2:6; 15:1; 43:3; Isa 56:7; 57:13; Ezek 20:40; Ob 16; Zeph 3:11). If the LordвАЩs universal kingdom is in view in this context (see the note on вАЬearthвАЭ at v. 4), then the phrase would probably be metonymic here, standing for GodвАЩs worldwide dominion (see the next line).
n2301125: tn Heb вАЬfor the earth will be full of knowledge of the Lord, as the waters cover the sea.вАЭ The translation assumes that a universal kingdom is depicted here, but вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets) could be translated вАЬlandвАЭ (see the note at v. 4). вАЬKnowledge of the LordвАЭ refers here to a recognition of the LordвАЩs sovereignty which results in a willingness to submit to his authority. See the note at v. 2.
n2301126: tn Or вАЬin that dayвАЭ (KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301128: tn Heb вАЬa root from Jesse, which stands for a signal flag of the nations, of him nations will inquireвАЭ [or вАЬseekвАЭ].
n2301129: tn Or вАЬin that dayвАЭ (KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301130: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2301131: tc The Hebrew text reads, вАЬthe Lord will again, a second time, his hand.вАЭ The auxiliary verb вАП„Щ„Х÷є„°÷і„Щ„£вАО (yosif), which literally means вАЬadd,вАЭ needs a main verb to complete it. Consequently many emend вАП„©÷µ„Б„†÷і„Щ„™вАО (shenit, вАЬa second timeвАЭ) to an infinitive. Some propose the form вАП„©÷Ј„Б„†÷є÷Љ„™вАО (shannot, a Piel infinitive construct from вАП„©÷Є„Б„†÷Є„ФвАО, shanah) and relate it semantically to an Arabic cognate meaning вАЬto be high.вАЭ If the Hebrew text is retained a verb must be supplied. вАЬSecond timeвАЭ would allude back to the events of the Exodus (see vv. 15-16).
n2301132: tn Or вАЬacquireвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV вАЬrecover.вАЭ
n2301133: tn Heb вАЬthe remnant of his people who remain.вАЭ
n2301134: sn Perhaps a reference to Upper (i.e., southern) Egypt (so NIV, NLT; NCV вАЬSouth EgyptвАЭ).
n2301137: tn Or perhaps, вАЬthe islands of the sea.вАЭ
n2301138: tn Or вАЬthe banished of Israel,вАЭ i.e., the exiles.
n2301139: tn Heb вАЬturn asideвАЭ; KJV, NASB, NRSV вАЬdepart.вАЭ
n2301140: tn Heb вАЬhostile ones of Judah.вАЭ Elsewhere when the substantival participle of вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Є„®вАО (tsarar) takes a pronominal suffix or appears in a construct relationship, the following genitive is objective. (For a list of texts see BDB 865 s.v. III вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО) In this case the phrase вАЬhostile ones of JudahвАЭ means вАЬthose who are hostile toward Judah,вАЭ i.e., JudahвАЩs enemies. However, the parallel couplet that follows suggests that JudahвАЩs hostility toward Ephraim is in view. In this case вАЬhostile ones of JudahвАЭ means вАЬhostile ones from Judah.вАЭ The translation above assumes the latter, giving the immediate context priority over general usage.
n2301141: tn Heb вАЬfly.вАЭ Ephraim/Judah are compared to a bird of prey.
n2301142: tn Heb вАЬon the shoulder of Philistia toward the sea.вАЭ This refers to the slopes of the hill country west of Judah. See HALOT 506 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„™÷µ„£вАО.
n2301143: tn Heb вАЬEdom and Moab [will be the place of] the outstretching of their hand,вАЭ i.e., included in their area of jurisdiction (see HALOT 648 s.v. вАП„ЧвАО(вАП„Х÷євАО)вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ь÷євАО).
n2301144: tn The verb is usually understood as вАЬput under the ban, destroy,вАЭ or emended to вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Є„СвАО (kharav, вАЬdry upвАЭ). However, HALOT 354 s.v. II вАП„Ч„®„ЭвАО proposes a homonymic root meaning вАЬdivide.вАЭ
n2301147: tn Heb вАЬthe riverвАЭ; capitalized in some English versions (e.g., ASV, NASB, NRSV) as a reference to the Euphrates River.
n2301148: tn Heb вАЬwith the [?] of his windвАЭ [or вАЬbreathвАЭ]. The Hebrew term вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Щ÷Є„ЭвАО ( њayam) occurs only here. Some attempt to relate the word to an Arabic root and translate, вАЬscorching [or вАЬhotвАЭ] wind.вАЭ This interpretation fits especially well if one reads вАЬdry upвАЭ in the previous line. Others prefer to emend the form to вАП„Ґ÷є„¶÷ґ„ЭвАО ( њotsem, вАЬstrongвАЭ). See HALOT 817 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Ј„ЭвАО.
n2301149: tn Heb вАЬseven streams.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„†÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО (nakhal, вАЬstreamвАЭ) refers to a wadi, or seasonal stream, which runs during the rainy season, but is otherwise dry. The context (see v. 15b) here favors the translation, вАЬdried-up streams.вАЭ The number seven suggests totality and completeness. Here it indicates that GodвАЩs provision for escape will be thorough and more than capable of accommodating the returning exiles.
n2301150: tn Heb вАЬand there will be a highway for the remnant of his people who remain, from Assyria.вАЭ
n230123: tn The words вАЬin himвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n230124: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬfor my strength and protection [is] the Lord, the Lord (Heb вАЬYah, Yahweh).вАЭ The word вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah) is probably dittographic or explanatory here (note that the short form of the name [вАП„Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО, yah] precedes, and that the graphically similar вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ф÷і„ЩвАО [vayehi] follows). Exod 15:2, the passage from which the words of v. 2b are taken, has only вАП„Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО. The word вАП„Ц÷і„Ю÷∞„®÷Є„™вАО (zimrat) is traditionally understood as meaning вАЬsong,вАЭ in which case one might translate, вАЬfor the Lord gives me strength and joyвАЭ (i.e., a reason to sing); note that in v. 5 the verb вАП„Ц÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (zamar, вАЬsingвАЭ) appears. Many recent commentators, however, have argued that the noun is here instead a homonym, meaning вАЬprotectionвАЭ or вАЬstrength.вАЭ See HALOT 274 s.v. III *вАП„Ц„Ю„®вАО.
n230125: tn Or вАЬsalvationвАЭ (so many English versions, e.g., KJV, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB вАЬmy savior.вАЭ
n230126:
tn Or вАЬsalvationвАЭ (so many English versions, e.g., KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); CEV вАЬvictory.вАЭ
sn Water is here a metaphor for renewed life; the springs symbolize the restoration of GodвАЩs favor.
n230128: tn Heb вАЬcall in his name,вАЭ i.e., вАЬinvoke his name.вАЭ
n230129: tn Heb вАЬbring to remembrance that his name is exalted.вАЭ The LordвАЩs вАЬnameвАЭ stands here for his character and reputation.
n2301210: tc The translation follows the marginal reading (Qere), which is a Hophal participle from вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ), understood here in a gerundive sense.
n2301211: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2301212: tn Or вАЬis greatвАЭ (TEV). However, the context emphasizes his mighty acts of deliverance (cf. NCV), not some general or vague character quality.
n230131: sn Isa 13-23 contains a series of judgment oracles against various nations. It is likely that Israel, not the nations mentioned, actually heard these oracles. The oracles probably had a twofold purpose. For those leaders who insisted on getting embroiled in international politics, these oracles were a reminder that Judah need not fear foreign nations or seek international alliances for security reasons. For the righteous remnant within the nation, these oracles were a reminder that IsraelвАЩs God was indeed the sovereign ruler of the earth, worthy of his peopleвАЩs trust.
n230132: tn The term вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„В÷Љ÷Є„РвАО (massa Њ, вАЬpronouncement, a lifting up [of the voice]вАЭ) is a technical term introducing a message from the Lord (cf. Nah 1:1; Hab 1:1; Zech 9:1; Mal 1:1). Derived from the root вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ, вАЬto liftвАЭ), it is probably connected to the phrase вАЬto raise oneвАЩs voiceвАЭ (HALOT 639 s.v. II вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„В÷Љ÷Є„РвАО) and is usually translated as вАЬoracleвАЭ or вАЬutterance.вАЭ Because the root can also mean вАЬto carry (a burden)вАЭ it has also been suggested that its nuance is of a burdensome message (KJV). Here it is the message which the prophet saw, suggesting that it is the report of a prophetic vision. In Nahum 1:1, the oracle is called вАЬthe book of vision.вАЭ
n230133: sn The Lord is speaking here (see v. 3).
n230134: tn Heb вАЬmy consecrated ones,вАЭ i.e., those who have been set apart by God for the special task of carrying out his judgment.
n230135: tn Heb вАЬmy warriors with respect to my anger.вАЭ
n230136: tn Heb вАЬthe boasting ones of my prideвАЭ; cf. ASV, NASB, NRSV вАЬmy proudly exulting ones.вАЭ
n230137: sn In vv. 4-10 the prophet appears to be speaking, since the Lord is referred to in the third person. However, since the Lord refers to himself in the third person later in this chapter (see v. 13), it is possible that he speaks throughout the chapter.
n230138: tn Heb вАЬa sound, a roar, [is] on the mountains, like many people.вАЭ
n2301310: tn Heb вАЬfrom the end of the sky.вАЭ
n2301311: tn Or вАЬangerвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV вАЬthe weapons of his indignation.вАЭ
n2301312: tn Or perhaps, вАЬlandвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NLT). Even though the heading and subsequent context (see v. 17) indicate BabylonвАЩs judgment is in view, the chapter has a cosmic flavor suggesting that the coming judgment is universal in scope. Perhaps BabylonвАЩs downfall occurs in conjunction with a wider judgment, or the cosmic style is poetic hyperbole used to emphasize the magnitude and importance of the coming event.
n2301313: tn Heb вАЬthe day of the LordвАЭ (so KJV, NAB).
n2301314:
tn Heb вАЬlike destruction from the Sovereign One it comes.вАЭ The comparative preposition (вАП„ЫвАО, kaf) has here the rhetorical nuance, вАЬin every way like.вАЭ The point is that the destruction unleashed will have all the earmarks of divine judgment. One could paraphrase, вАЬit comes as only destructive divine judgment can.вАЭ On this use of the preposition in general, see GKC 376 ¬І118.x.
sn The divine name used here is вАП„©÷Ј„Б„У÷Љ÷Ј„ЩвАО (shaddai, вАЬShaddaiвАЭ). Shaddai (or El Shaddai) is the sovereign king/judge of the world who grants life/blesses and kills/judges. In Genesis he blesses the patriarchs with fertility and promises numerous descendants. Outside Genesis he both blesses/protects and takes away life/happiness. The patriarchs knew God primarily as El Shaddai (Exod 6:3). While the origin and meaning of this name is uncertain (see discussion below), its significance is clear. The name is used in contexts where God appears as the source of fertility and life. In Gen 17:1-8 he appears to Abram, introduces himself as El Shaddai, and announces his intention to make the patriarch fruitful. In the role of El Shaddai God repeats these words (now elevated to the status of a decree) to Jacob (35:11). Earlier Isaac had pronounced a blessing upon Jacob in which he asked El Shaddai to make Jacob fruitful (28:3). Jacob later prays that his sons will be treated with mercy when they return to Egypt with Benjamin (43:14). The fertility theme is not as apparent here, though one must remember that Jacob viewed Benjamin as the sole remaining son of the favored and once-barren Rachel (cf. 29:31; 30:22-24; 35:16-18). It is quite natural that he would appeal to El Shaddai to preserve BenjaminвАЩs life, for it was El ShaddaiвАЩs miraculous power which made it possible for Rachel to give him sons in the first place. In 48:3 Jacob, prior to blessing JosephвАЩs sons, tells him how El Shaddai appeared to him at Bethel (cf. chapter 28) and promised to make him fruitful. When blessing Joseph on his deathbed Jacob refers to Shaddai (we should probably read вАЬEl Shaddai,вАЭ along with a few Hebrew mss, Smr, LXX, and Syriac) as the one who provides abundant blessings, including вАЬblessings of the breast and wombвАЭ (49:25). (The direct association of the name with вАП„©÷Є„Б„У÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО [shadayim, вАЬbreastsвАЭ] suggests the name might mean вАЬthe one of the breastвАЭ [i.e., the one who gives fertility], but the juxtaposition is probably better explained as wordplay. Note the wordplay involving the name and the root вАП„©÷Є„Б„У÷Ј„УвАО [shadad, вАЬdestroyвАЭ] here in Isa 13:6 and in Joel 1:15.) Outside Genesis the name Shaddai (minus El, вАЬGodвАЭ) is normally used when God is viewed as the sovereign king who blesses/protects or curses/brings judgment. The name appears in the introduction to two of BalaamвАЩs oracles (Num 24:4, 16) of blessing upon Israel. Naomi employs the name when accusing the Lord of treating her bitterly by taking the lives of her husband and sons (Ruth 1:20-21). In Ps 68:14; Isa 13:6; and Joel 1:15 Shaddai judges his enemies through warfare, while Ps 91:1 depicts him as the protector of his people. (In Ezek 1:24 and 10:5 the sound of the cherubimвАЩs wings is compared to ShaddaiвАЩs powerful voice. The reference may be to the mighty divine warriorвАЩs battle cry which accompanies his angry judgment.) Last but not least, the name occurs 31 times in the Book of Job. Job and his вАЬfriendsвАЭ assume that Shaddai is the sovereign king of the world (11:7; 37:23a) who is the source of life (33:4b) and is responsible for maintaining justice (8:3; 34:10-12; 37:23b). He provides abundant blessings, including children (22:17-18; 29:4-6), but can also discipline, punish, and destroy (5:17; 6:4; 21:20; 23:16). It is not surprising to see the name so often in this book, where the theme of GodвАЩs justice is primary and even called into question (24:1; 27:2). The most likely proposal is that the name means вАЬGod, the one of the mountainвАЭ (an Akkadian cognate means вАЬmountain,вАЭ to which Heb. вАП„©÷Ј„Б„УвАО [shad, вАЬbreastвАЭ] is probably related). For a discussion of proposed derivations see T. N. D. Mettinger, In Search of God, 70-71. The name may originally depict God as the sovereign judge who, in Canaanite style, rules from a sacred mountain. Isa 14:13 and Ezek 28:14, 16 associate such a mountain with God, while Ps 48:2 refers to Zion as вАЬZaphon,вАЭ the Canaanite Olympus from which the high god El ruled. (In Isa 14 the Canaanite god El may be in view. Note that Isaiah pictures pagan kings as taunting the king of Babylon, suggesting that pagan mythology may provide the background for the language and imagery.)
n2301315: tn Heb вАЬdropвАЭ; KJV вАЬbe faintвАЭ; ASV вАЬbe feebleвАЭ; NAB вАЬfall helpless.вАЭ
n2301317: tn Heb вАЬtheir faces are faces of flames.вАЭ Their faces are flushed with fear and embarrassment.
n2301319: tn Heb вАЬ[with] cruelty, and fury, and rage of anger.вАЭ Three synonyms for вАЬangerвАЭ are piled up at the end of the line to emphasize the extraordinary degree of divine anger that will be exhibited in this judgment.
n2301321: tn Or вАЬlandвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NLT).
n2301322: tn Heb вАЬdo not flash forth their light.вАЭ
n2301323: tn Heb вАЬdoes not shed forth its light.вАЭ
n2301324: sn The Lord is definitely speaking (again?) at this point. See the note at v. 4.
n2301325: tn Or вАЬI will bring disaster on the world.вАЭ Hebrew вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (ra њah) could refer to the judgment (i.e., disaster, calamity) or to the evil that prompts it. The structure of the parallel line favors the latter interpretation.
n2301326: tn Or perhaps, вАЬthe violentвАЭ; cf. NASB, NIV вАЬthe ruthless.вАЭ
n2301327: tn The verb is supplied in the translation from the first line. The verb in the first line (вАЬI will make scarceвАЭ) does double duty in the parallel structure of the verse.
n2301328: tn Or вАЬthe sky.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2301329: tn Heb вАЬfrom its placeвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NCV).
n2301330: tn Heb вАЬand in the day of the raging of his anger.вАЭ
n2301331: tn Or вАЬlike a gazelle being chased.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301332: tn Heb вАЬhis peopleвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV) or вАЬhis nationвАЭ (cf. TEV вАЬtheir own countriesвАЭ).
n2301333: tn Heb вАЬcarried off,вАЭ i.e., grabbed from the fleeing crowd. See HALOT 764 s.v. вАП„°„§„ФвАО.
n2301334: tn Heb вАЬwill fallвАЭ (so KJV, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬwill be run through with a sword.вАЭ
n2301336: sn They cannot be bought off, for they have a lust for bloodshed.
n2301337: tn Heb вАЬand bows cut to bits young men.вАЭ вАЬBowsвАЭ stands by metonymy for arrows.
n2301339: tn Heb вАЬtheir eye does not.вАЭ Here вАЬeyeвАЭ is a metonymy for the whole person.
n2301341:
tn Heb вАЬthe beauty of the pride of the Chaldeans.вАЭ
sn The Chaldeans were a group of tribes who lived in southern Mesopotamia. They established the so-called neo-Babylonian empire in the late seventh century b.c. Their most famous king, Nebuchadnezzar, conquered Judah in 605 b.c. and destroyed Jerusalem in 586 b.c.
n2301342: tn Heb вАЬand BabylonвА¶will be like the overthrow by God of Sodom and Gomorrah.вАЭ On вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ф÷∞„§÷µ÷Љ„Ы÷Ј„™вАО (mahpekhat, вАЬoverthrowвАЭ) see the note on the word вАЬdestructionвАЭ in 1:7.
n2301343: tn Heb вАЬshe will not be inhabited forever, and she will not be dwelt in to generation and generation (i.e., forever).вАЭ The Lord declares that Babylon, personified as a woman, will not be inhabited. In other words, her people will be destroyed, and the Chaldean empire will come to a permanent end.
n2301344: tn Or вАЬArabвАЭ (NAB, NASB, NIV); cf. CEV, NLT вАЬnomads.вАЭ
n2301345: tn вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ф÷µ„ЬвАО (yahel) is probably an alternate form or a scribal error of вАП„Щ÷ґ„Р÷±„Ф÷Ј„ЬвАО (ye Њehal). See GKC 186 ¬І68.k.
n2301346: tn The words вАЬtheir flocksвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The Hebrew text does not supply the object here, but see Jer 33:12.
n2301347: tn The word вАЬruinedвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2301348: tn The precise referent of this word in uncertain. See HALOT 29 s.v. *вАП„Р÷є„Ч÷ЈвАО. Various English versions translate as вАЬowlsвАЭ (e.g., NAB, NASB), вАЬwild dogsвАЭ (NCV); вАЬjackalsвАЭ (NIV); вАЬhowling creaturesвАЭ (NRSV, NLT).
n2301350: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬwild dogs will yip among his widows, and jackals in the palaces of pleasure.вАЭ The verb вАЬyipвАЭ is supplied in the second line; it does double duty in the parallel structure. вАЬHis widowsвАЭ makes little sense in this context; many emend the form (вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю„†„Х÷є„™÷Є„Щ„ХвАО,  Њalmnotayv) to the graphically similar вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„Ю÷∞„†„Х÷є„™÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО ( Њarmenoteha, вАЬher fortressesвАЭ), a reading that is assumed in the present translation. The use of вАЬwidowsвАЭ may represent an intentional wordplay on вАЬfortresses,вАЭ indicating that the fortresses are like dejected widows (J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:308, n. 1).
n2301351: tn Heb вАЬnear to come is her time.вАЭ
n2301352: sn When was the prophecy of BabylonвАЩs fall fulfilled? Some argue that the prophecy was fulfilled in 689 b.c. when the Assyrians under Sennacherib sacked and desecrated the city (this event is alluded to in 23:13). This may have been an initial phase in the fulfillment of the prophecy, but the reference to the involvement of the Medes (v. 17) and the suggestion that BabylonвАЩs demise will bring about the restoration of Israel (14:1-2) indicate that the fall of Babylon to the Medes and Persians in 538 b.c. is the primary focus of the prophecy. (After all, the Lord did reveal to Isaiah that the Chaldeans [not the Assyrians] would someday conquer Jerusalem and take the people into exile [see 39:5-7].) However, the vivid picture of destruction in vv. 15-22 raises a problem. The Medes and Persians did not destroy the city; in fact CyrusвАЩ takeover of Babylon, though preceded by a military campaign, was relatively peaceful and even welcomed by some Babylonian religious officials. How then does one explain the prophecyвАЩs description of the cityвАЩs violent fall? As noted above, the events of 689 b.c. and 538 b.c. may have been merged in the prophecy. However, it is more likely that the language is stylized and exaggerated for rhetorical effect. See Isa 34:11-15; Jer 50:39-40 (describing BabylonвАЩs fall in 538 b.c.); 51:36-37 (describing BabylonвАЩs fall in 538 b.c.); Zeph 2:13-15; the extra-biblical Sefire treaty curses; and AshurbanipalвАЩs description of the destruction of Elam in his royal annals. In other words, the events of 538 b.c. essentially, though not necessarily literally, fulfill the prophecy.
n230141: tn The sentence begins with вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki), which is understood as asseverative (вАЬcertainlyвАЭ) in the translation. Another option is to translate, вАЬFor the Lord will have compassion.вАЭ In this case one of the reasons for BabylonвАЩs coming demise (13:22b) is the LordвАЩs desire to restore his people.
n230142: tn The words вАЬas his special peopleвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n230145: tn Heb вАЬand the house of Israel will take possession of them [i.e., the nations], on the land of the Lord, as male servants and female servants.вАЭ
n230146: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n230147: tn Heb вАЬyou will lift up this taunt over the king of Babylon, saying.вАЭ
n230148: tc The word in the Hebrew text (вАП„Ю÷Ј„У÷∞„Ф÷µ„С÷Є„ФвАО, madhevah) is unattested elsewhere and of uncertain meaning. Many (following the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa) assume a confusion of dalet and resh (вАП„УвАО and вАП„®вАО) and emend the form to вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Ф÷µ„С÷Є„ФвАО (marhevah, вАЬonslaughtвАЭ). See HALOT 548 s.v. II *вАП„Ю÷і„У÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО and HALOT 633 s.v. *вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Ф÷µ„С÷Є„ФвАО.
n230149: tn Or perhaps, вАЬheвАЭ (cf. KJV; NCV вАЬthe king of BabylonвАЭ). The present translation understands the referent of the pronoun (вАЬitвАЭ) to be the вАЬclub/scepterвАЭ of the preceding line.
n2301410: tn Heb вАЬit was striking down nations in fury [with] a blow without ceasing.вАЭ The participle (вАЬstriking downвАЭ) suggests repeated or continuous action in past time.
n2301411: tn Heb вАЬit was ruling in anger nations [with] oppression without restraint.вАЭ The participle (вАЬrulingвАЭ) suggests repeated or continuous action in past time.
n2301413: tn The word вАЬsingingвАЭ is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. Note that the personified trees speak in the second half of the verse.
n2301414: tn Heb вАЬlay downвАЭ (in death); cf. NAB вАЬlaid to rest.вАЭ
n2301415: tn Heb вАЬthe [wood]cutter does not come up against us.вАЭ
n2301416: sn Sheol is the proper name of the subterranean world which was regarded as the land of the dead.
n2301417: tn Heb вАЬarousing.вАЭ The form is probably a Polel infinitive absolute, rather than a third masculine singular perfect, for Sheol is grammatically feminine (note вАЬstirred upвАЭ). See GKC 466 ¬І145.t.
n2301418: tn Heb вАЬall the rams of the earth.вАЭ The animal epithet is used metaphorically here for leaders. See HALOT 903 s.v. *вАП„Ґ÷Ј„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„УвАО.
n2301419: tn Heb вАЬlifting from their thrones all the kings of the nations.вАЭ вАП„Ф÷µ„І÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (heqim, a Hiphil perfect third masculine singular) should be emended to an infinitive absolute (вАП„Ф÷Є„І÷µ„Щ„ЭвАО, haqem). See the note on вАЬrousesвАЭ earlier in the verse.
n2301420: tn Or вАЬprideвАЭ (NCV, CEV); KJV, NIV, NRSV вАЬpomp.вАЭ
n2301422: tn Heb вАЬunder you maggots are spread out, and worms are your cover.вАЭ
n2301423:
tn The Hebrew text has вАП„Ф÷µ„Щ„Ь÷µ„Ь „С÷ґ÷Љ„Я÷Њ„©÷Є„Б„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (helel ben shakhar, вАЬHelel son of ShacharвАЭ), which is probably a name for the morning star (Venus) or the crescent moon. See HALOT 245 s.v. вАП„Ф÷µ„Щ„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО.
sn What is the background for the imagery in vv. 12-15? This whole section (vv. 4b-21) is directed to the king of Babylon, who is clearly depicted as a human ruler. Other kings of the earth address him in vv. 9ff., he is called вАЬthe manвАЭ in v. 16, and, according to vv. 19-20, he possesses a physical body. Nevertheless the language of vv. 12-15 has led some to see a dual referent in the taunt song. These verses, which appear to be spoken by other pagan kings to a pagan king (cf. vv. 9-11), contain several titles and motifs that resemble those of Canaanite mythology, including references to Helel son of Shachar, the stars of El, the mountain of assembly, the recesses of Zaphon, and the divine title Most High. Apparently these verses allude to a mythological story about a minor god (Helel son of Shachar) who tried to take over Zaphon, the mountain of the gods. His attempted coup failed, and he was hurled down to the underworld. The king of Babylon is taunted for having similar unrealized delusions of grandeur. Some Christians have seen an allusion to the fall of Satan here, but this seems contextually unwarranted (see J. Martin, вАЬIsaiah,вАЭ BKCOT, 1061).
n2301424: tn Some understand the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„©„БвАО (khalash) to mean вАЬweaken,вАЭ but HALOT 324 s.v. II вАП„Ч„Ь„©„БвАО proposes a homonym here meaning вАЬdefeat.вАЭ
n2301425: sn In this line the taunting kings hint at the literal identity of the king, after likening him to the god Helel and a tree. The verb вАП„Т÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (gada њ, вАЬcut downвАЭ) is used of chopping down trees in 9:10 and 10:33.
n2301426: tn Heb вАЬyou, you said in your heart.вАЭ
n2301427: sn In Canaanite mythology the stars of El were astral deities under the authority of the high god El.
n2301428: sn Zaphon, the Canaanite version of Olympus, was the вАЬmountain of assemblyвАЭ where the gods met.
n2301429: tn Heb вАЬthe high places.вАЭ This word often refers to the high places where pagan worship was conducted, but here it probably refers to the вАЬbacksвАЭ or tops of the clouds. See HALOT 136 s.v. вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО.
n2301430: sn Normally in the OT the title вАЬMost HighвАЭ belongs to the God of Israel, but in this context, where the mythological overtones are so strong, it probably refers to the Canaanite high god El.
n2301431: tn The prefixed verb form is taken as a preterite. Note the use of perfects in v. 12 to describe the kingвАЩs downfall.
n2301432: tn The Hebrew term вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„®вАО (bor, вАЬcisternвАЭ) is sometimes used metaphorically to refer to the place of the dead or the entrance to the underworld.
n2301433: tn The word вАЬthinkingвАЭ is supplied in the translation in order to make it clear that the next line records their thoughts as they gaze at him.
n2301434: tc The pronominal suffix is masculine, even though its antecedent appears to be the grammatically feminine noun вАЬworld.вАЭ Some have suggested that the form вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Є„Щ„ХвАО ( њarayv, plural noun with third masculine singular suffix) should be emended to вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО ( њareha, plural noun with third feminine singular suffix). This emendation may be unnecessary in light of other examples of lack of agreement between a suffix and its antecedent noun.
n2301435: tn Heb вАЬand his prisoners did not let loose to [their] homes.вАЭ This really means, вАЬhe did not let loose his prisoners and send them back to their homes.вАЭ On the elliptical style, see GKC 366 ¬І117.o.
n2301436: sn It is unclear where the quotation of the kings, begun in v. 10b, ends. However, the reference to the вАЬkings of the nationsвАЭ in v. 18 (see also v. 9) seems to indicate that the quotation has ended at this point and that IsraelвАЩs direct taunt (cf. vv. 4b-10a) has resumed. In fact, the references to the вАЬkings of the nationsвАЭ may form a stylistic inclusio or frame around the quotation.
n2301437: tc The phrase вАЬall of themвАЭ does not appear in the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa.
n2301438: sn This refers to the typically extravagant burial of kings.
n2301439: tn Heb вАЬhouseвАЭ (so KJV, ASV), but in this context a tomb is in view. Note the verb вАЬlie downвАЭ in the preceding line and the reference to a вАЬgraveвАЭ in the next line.
n2301440: tn Heb вАЬlike a shoot that is abhorred.вАЭ The simile seems a bit odd; apparently it refers to a small shoot that is trimmed from a plant and tossed away. Some prefer to emend вАП„†÷µ„¶÷ґ„®вАО (netser, вАЬshootвАЭ); some propose вАП„†÷µ„§÷ґ„ЬвАО (nefel, вАЬmiscarriageвАЭ). In this case one might paraphrase: вАЬlike a horrible-looking fetus that is delivered when a woman miscarries.вАЭ
n2301443: tn вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (bor) literally means вАЬcisternвАЭ; cisterns were constructed from stones. On the metaphorical use of вАЬcisternвАЭ for the underworld, see the note at v. 15.
n2301444: tn Heb вАЬlike a trampled corpse.вАЭ Some take this line with what follows.
n2301445: tn Heb вАЬyou will not be united with them in burialвАЭ (so NASB).
n2301446: tn Or вАЬthe place of slaughter for.вАЭ
n2301447: tn Heb вАЬfor the sin of their fathers.вАЭ
n2301448: sn J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:320, n. 10) suggests that the garrison cities of the mighty empire are in view here.
n2301449: tn Heb вАЬI will cut off from Babylon name and remnantвАЭ (ASV, NAB, and NRSV all similar).
n2301451: tn Heb вАЬI will make her into a possession of wild animals.вАЭ It is uncertain what type of animal вАП„І÷і„§÷є÷Љ„УвАО (qippod) refers to. Some suggest a rodent (cf. NASB, NRSV вАЬhedgehogвАЭ), others, an owl (cf, NAB, NIV, TEV).
n2301452: tn Heb вАЬI will sweep her away with the broom of destruction.вАЭ
n2301453: sn Having announced the downfall of the Chaldean empire, the Lord appends to this prophecy a solemn reminder that the Assyrians, the major Mesopotamian power of IsaiahвАЩs day, would be annihilated, foreshadowing what would subsequently happen to Babylon and the other hostile nations.
n2301455: tn Heb вАЬhim.вАЭ This is a collective singular referring to the nation, or a reference to the king of Assyria, who by metonymy stands for the entire nation.
n2301456: tn Heb вАЬand his [i.e., AssyriaвАЩs] yoke will be removed from them [the people?], and his [AssyriaвАЩs] burden from his [the nationвАЩs?] shoulder will be removed.вАЭ There are no antecedents in this oracle for the suffixes in the phrases вАЬfrom themвАЭ and вАЬfrom his shoulder.вАЭ Since the LordвАЩs land and hills are referred to in the preceding line and the statement seems to echo 10:27, it is likely that GodвАЩs people are the referents of the suffixes; the translation uses вАЬmy peopleвАЭ to indicate this.
n2301457: tn Heb вАЬand this is the hand that is outstretched over all the nations.вАЭ
n2301459: tn Heb вАЬHis hand is outstretched, and who will turn it back?вАЭ
n2301461: sn Perhaps 715 b.c., but the precise date is uncertain.
n2301463: sn The identity of this вАЬclubвАЭ (also referred to as a вАЬserpentвАЭ in the next line) is uncertain. It may refer to an Assyrian king, or to Ahaz. For discussion see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:331-32. The viper/adder referred to in the second half of the verse is his successor.
n2301464: tn Heb вАЬflying burning one.вАЭ The designation вАЬburning oneвАЭ may allude to the serpentвАЩs appearance or the effect of its poisonous bite. (See the note at 6:2.) The qualifier вАЬflyingвАЭ probably refers to the serpentвАЩs quick, darting movements, though one might propose a homonym here, meaning вАЬbiting.вАЭ (See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:332, n. 18.) Some might think in terms of a mythological flying, fire-breathing dragon (cf. NAB вАЬa flying saraphвАЭ; CEV вАЬa flying fiery dragonвАЭ), but this proposal does not make good sense in 30:6, where the phrase вАЬflying burning oneвАЭ appears again in a list of desert animals.
n2301465: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬthe firstborn of the poor will graze.вАЭ вАЬFirstbornвАЭ may be used here in an idiomatic sense to indicate the very poorest of the poor. See BDB 114 s.v. вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ы„Х÷є„®вАО. The translation above assumes an emendation of вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ы„Х÷є„®÷µ„ЩвАО (bekhore, вАЬfirstborn ofвАЭ) to вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ы÷Є„®÷Ј„ЩвАО (bekharay, вАЬin my pasturesвАЭ).
n2301467: tn Or вАЬdespairвАЭ (see HALOT 555 s.v. вАП„Ю„Х„ТвАО). The form вАП„†÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„ТвАО (namog) should be taken here as an infinitive absolute functioning as an imperative. See GKC 199-200 ¬І72.v.
n2301468: tn Heb вАЬand there is no one going alone in his appointed places.вАЭ The meaning of this line is uncertain. вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„У÷µ„УвАО (boded) appears to be a participle from вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„У÷Ј„УвАО (badad, вАЬbe separateвАЭ; see BDB 94 s.v. вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„У÷Ј„УвАО). вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷Є„УвАО (mo њad) may mean вАЬassemblyвАЭ or, by extension, вАЬmultitudeвАЭ (see HALOT 558 s.v. *вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷Є„УвАО), but the referent of the third masculine pronominal suffix attached to the noun is unclear. It probably refers to the вАЬnationвАЭ mentioned in the next line.
n2301469: sn The question forces the Philistines to consider the dilemma they will faceвАФsurrender and oppression, or battle and death.
n230153: tn Heb вАЬeven Dibon [to] the high places to weep.вАЭ The verb вАЬwent upвАЭ does double duty in the parallel structure.
n230154: tn Heb вАЬover [or вАЬforвАЭ] Nebo and over [or вАЬforвАЭ] Medeba.вАЭ
n230155: sn Shaving the head and beard were outward signs of mourning and grief.
n230156: tn The words вАЬthe people ofвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n230157: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬFor this reason the soldiers of Moab shout; his inner being quivers for him.вАЭ To achieve tighter parallelism, some emend the first line, changing вАП„Ч÷≤„Ь÷ї„¶÷µ„ЩвАО (khalutse, вАЬsoldiersвАЭ) to вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ь÷∞„¶÷µ„ЩвАО (khaltse, вАЬloinsвАЭ) and вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (yari њu, вАЬthey shout,вАЭ from вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ґ÷ЈвАО, rua њ) to вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷∞„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (yor њu, вАЬthey quiverвАЭ), a verb from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (yara њ), which also appears in the next line. One can then translate v. 4b as вАЬFor this reason the insides of the Moabites quiver; their whole body shakesвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV).
n230158: tn Heb вАЬfor Moab.вАЭ For rhetorical purposes the speaker (the Lord?; see v. 9) plays the role of a mourner.
n230159: tn The vocalization of the Hebrew text suggests вАЬthe bars of her gates,вАЭ but the form should be repointed to yield, вАЬher fugitives.вАЭ See HALOT 156-57 s.v. вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„®÷і„Ч÷ЈвАО, and BDB 138 s.v. вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„®÷і„Щ„Ф÷ЈвАО.
n2301510: tn The words вАЬare stretched outвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2301511: tn Heb вАЬFor the ascent of Luhith, with weeping they go up it; for [on] the road to Horonaim an outcry over shattering they raise up.вАЭ
n2301512: tn Heb вАЬare waste placesвАЭ; cf. NRSV вАЬare a desolation.вАЭ
n2301513: tn Heb вАЬto Eglaim [is] her wailing, and [to] Beer Elim [is] her wailing.вАЭ
n2301514: tc The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads вАЬDibonвАЭ instead of вАЬDimonвАЭ in this verse.
n2301515: tn Heb вАЬIndeed, I will place on Dimon added things.вАЭ Apparently the Lord is speaking.
n2301516: tn The words вАЬwill attackвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n230161: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬSend [a plural imperatival form is used] a ram [to] the ruler of the land.вАЭ The term вАП„Ы÷Ј÷Љ„®вАО (kar, вАЬramвАЭ) should be emended to the plural вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (karim). The singular form in the text is probably the result of haplography; note that the next word begins with a mem (вАП„ЮвАО).
n230162: tn The Hebrew text has вАЬtoward [across?] the desert.вАЭ
n230163: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n230164: tn Heb вАЬlike a bird fleeing, thrust away [from] a nest, the daughters of Moab are [at] the fords of Arnon.вАЭ
n230165: sn It is unclear who is being addressed in this verse. Perhaps the prophet, playing the role of a panic-stricken Moabite refugee, requests the leaders of Judah (the imperatives are plural) to take pity on the fugitives.
n230166: tn Heb вАЬMake your shade like night in the midst of noonday.вАЭ вАЬShadeвАЭ here symbolizes shelter, while the heat of noonday represents the intense suffering of the Moabites. By comparing the desired shade to night, the speaker visualizes a huge, dark shadow cast by a large tree that would provide relief from the sunвАЩs heat.
n230168: tn That is, вАЬlive as resident foreigners.вАЭ
n230169: tn Heb вАЬBe a hiding place for them.вАЭ
n2301610: tn The present translation understands вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) as asseverative, but one could take it as explanatory (вАЬfor,вАЭ KJV, NASB) or temporal (вАЬwhen,вАЭ NAB, NRSV). In the latter case, v. 4b would be logically connected to v. 5.
n2301611: tn A perfect verbal form is used here and in the next two lines for rhetorical effect; the demise of the oppressor(s) is described as if it had already occurred.
n2301612: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬthey will be finished, the one who tramples, from the earth.вАЭ The plural verb form вАП„™÷Ј÷Љ„Ю÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО, (tammu, вАЬdisappearвАЭ) could be emended to agree with the singular subject вАП„®÷є„Ю÷µ„°вАО (romes, вАЬthe one who tramplesвАЭ) or the participle can be emended to a plural (вАП„®÷є„Ю÷µ„°÷і„ЭвАО, romesim) to agree with the verb. The translation assumes the latter. Haplography of mem (вАП„ЭвАО) seems likely; note that the word after вАП„®÷є„Ю÷µ„°вАО begins with a mem.
n2301613: tn Heb вАЬand a throne will be established in faithfulness, and he will sit on it in reliability, in the tent of David.вАЭ
n2301614: tn Heb вАЬone who judges and seeks justice, and one experienced in fairness.вАЭ Many understand вАП„Ю÷∞„Ф÷і„®вАО (mehir) to mean вАЬquick, promptвАЭ (see BDB 555 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„Ф÷і„Щ„®вАО), but HALOT 552 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„Ф÷і„Щ„®вАО offers the meaning вАЬskillful, experienced,вАЭ and translates the phrase in v. 5 вАЬzealous for what is right.вАЭ
n2301615: tn вАП„Ґ÷ґ„С÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО ( њevrah) often means вАЬanger, fury,вАЭ but here it appears to refer to boastful outbursts or excessive claims. See HALOT 782 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷ґ„С÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО.
n2301618: tn The Hebrew text has, вАЬfor the raisin cakes of Kir Hareseth you [masculine plural] moan, surely destroyed.вАЭ The вАЬraisin cakesвАЭ could have cultic significance (see Hos 3:1), but the next verse focuses on agricultural disaster, so here the raisin cakes are mentioned as an example of the fine foods that are no longer available (see 2 Sam 6:19; Song 2:5) because the vines have been destroyed by the invader (see v. 8). Some prefer to take вАП„Р÷≤„©÷і„Б„Щ„©÷µ„Б„ЩвАО ( Њashishe, вАЬraisin cakes ofвАЭ) as вАЬmen ofвАЭ (see HALOT 95 s.v. *вАП„Р÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„©„БвАО; cf. NIV). The verb form вАП„™÷ґ„Ф÷∞„Т÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (tehgu, вАЬyou moanвАЭ) is probably the result of dittography (note that the preceding word ends in tav [вАП„™вАО]) and should be emended to вАП„Ф„Т„ХвАО (a perfect, third plural form), вАЬthey moan.вАЭ
n2301619: tn Heb вАЬSo I weep with the weeping of Jazer.вАЭ Once more the speaker (the Lord?вАФsee v. 10b) plays the role of a mourner (see 15:5).
n2301620: tc The form вАП„Р÷≤„®÷Ј„Щ÷Є÷Љ„Х÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њarayyavekh) should be emended to вАП„Р÷≤„®÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Є„Щ÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њaravvayekh; the vav [вАП„ХвАО] and yod [вАП„ЩвАО] have been accidentally transposed) from вАП„®÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ravah, вАЬbe saturatedвАЭ).
n2301621: tn Heb вАЬfor over your fruit and over your harvest shouting has fallen.вАЭ The translation assumes that the shouting is that of the conqueror (Jer 51:14). Another possibility is that the shouting is that of the harvesters (see v. 10b, as well as Jer 25:30), in which case one might translate, вАЬfor the joyful shouting over the fruit and crops has fallen silent.вАЭ
n2301622: tn Heb вАЬwine in the vats the treader does not tread.вАЭ
n2301623: sn The Lord appears to be the speaker here. See 15:9.
n2301624: tn Heb вАЬso my intestines sigh for Moab like a harp.вАЭ The word вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷Ј„ЩвАО (me њay, вАЬintestinesвАЭ) is used here of the seat of the emotions. English idiom requires the word вАЬheart.вАЭ The point of the comparison to a harp is not entirely clear. Perhaps his sighs of mourning resemble a harp in sound, or his constant sighing is like the repetitive strumming of a harp.
n2301625: tn The verb is supplied in the translation; вАЬsighsвАЭ in the preceding line does double duty in the parallel structure.
n2301626: tn Heb вАЬKir HeresвАЭ (so ASV, NRSV, TEV, CEV), a variant name for вАЬKir HaresethвАЭ (see v. 7).
n2301627: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2301628: tn Heb вАЬwhen he appears, when he grows tired, Moab on the high places, and enters his temple to pray, he will not prevail.вАЭ It is possible that вАЬwhen he grows tiredвАЭ is an explanatory gloss for the preceding вАЬwhen he appears.вАЭ
n2301629: tn Heb вАЬin three years, like the years of a hired worker.вАЭ The three years must be reckoned exactly, just as a hired worker would carefully keep track of the time he had agreed to work for an employer in exchange for a predetermined wage.
n2301630: tn Heb вАЬand the splendor of Moab will be disgraced with all the great multitude, and a small little remnant will not be strong.вАЭ
n230172: tn Three cities are known by this name in the OT: (1) an Aroer located near the Arnon, (2) an Aroer in Ammon, and (3) an Aroer of Judah. (See BDB 792-93 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷є„Ґ÷µ„®вАО, and HALOT 883 s.v. II вАП„Ґ÷≤„®„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„®вАО.) There is no mention of an Aroer in Syrian territory. For this reason some want to emend the text here to вАП„Ґ÷≤„Ц÷ї„С„Х÷є„™ „Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Щ„Ф÷Є „Ґ÷≤„У÷µ„Щ „Ґ÷Ј„УвАО ( њazuvot  њarayha  њade  њad, вАЬher cities are permanently abandonedвАЭ). However, Aroer near the Arnon was taken by Israel and later conquered by the Syrians. (See Josh 12:2; 13:9, 16; Judg 11:26; 2 Kgs 10:33). This oracle pertains to Israel as well as Syria (note v. 3), so it is possible that this is a reference to Israelite and/or Syrian losses in Transjordan.
n230173: tn Heb вАЬand they lie down, and there is no one scaring [them].вАЭ
n230174: tn Heb вАЬand kingship from DamascusвАЭ; cf. NASB вАЬAnd sovereignty from Damascus.вАЭ
n230175: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n230177: tn Heb вАЬand the fatness of his flesh will be made lean.вАЭ
n230178: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, NIV); KJV вАЬAt that day.вАЭ
n230179: tn Heb вАЬman will gaze toward his maker.вАЭ
n2301711: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2301713: tn Heb вАЬand that which his fingers made he will not see, the Asherah poles and the incense altars.вАЭ
n2301715: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬlike the abandonment of the wooded height and the top one.вАЭ The following relative clause appears to allude back to the Israelite conquest of the land, so it seems preferable to emend вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ч÷є„®÷ґ„©„Б „Х÷∞„Ф÷Є„Р÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„®вАО (hakhoresh veha Њamir, вАЬthe wooded height and the top oneвАЭ) to вАП„Ч÷є„®÷µ„©„Б÷µ„Щ „Ф÷Є„Р÷±„Ю÷є„®÷і„ЩвАО (khoreshe ha Њemori, вАЬ[like the abandonment] of the wooded heights of the AmoritesвАЭ).
n2301716: tn Heb вАЬyou have forgottenвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV).
n2301717: tn Heb вАЬand the rocky cliff of your strength you do not remember.вАЭ
n2301718: tn Heb вАЬa vine, a strange one.вАЭ The substantival adjective вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО (zar) functions here as an appositional genitive. It could refer to a cultic plant of some type, associated with a pagan rite. But it is more likely that it refers to an exotic, or imported, type of vine, one that is foreign (i.e., вАЬstrangeвАЭ) to Israel.
n2301719: tn Heb вАЬin the day of your planting you [?].вАЭ The precise meaning of the verb вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„©„В÷Ј„Т÷∞„©„В÷µ„Т÷і„ЩвАО (tesagsegi) is unclear. It is sometimes derived from вАП„©„В„Х÷Љ„ТвАО/вАП„°„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (sug, вАЬto fence inвАЭ; see BDB 691 s.v. II вАП„°„Х÷Љ„ТвАО). In this case one could translate вАЬyou build a protective fence.вАЭ However, the parallelism is tighter if one derives the form from вАП„©„В÷Є„Т÷Є„РвАО/вАП„©„В÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (saga Њ/sagah, вАЬto growвАЭ); see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:351, n. 4. For this verb, see BDB 960 s.v. вАП„©„В÷Є„Т÷Є„РвАО.
n2301720: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬa heap of harvest.вАЭ However, better sense is achieved if вАП„†÷µ„УвАО (ned, вАЬheapвАЭ) is emended to a verb. Options include вАП„†÷Ј„УвАО (nad, Qal perfect third masculine singular from вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО [nadad, вАЬflee, departвАЭ]), вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (Qal perfect third masculine singular from вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО), вАП„†÷є„У÷µ„УвАО (noded, Qal active participle from вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО), and вАП„†÷Є„УвАО (nad, Qal perfect third masculine singular, or participle masculine singular, from вАП„†„Х÷Љ„УвАО [nud, вАЬwander, flutterвАЭ]). See BDB 626 s.v. вАП„†„Х÷Љ„УвАО and HALOT 672 s.v. I вАП„†„У„УвАО. One could translate literally: вАЬ[the harvest] departs,вАЭ or вАЬ[the harvest] flies away.вАЭ
n2301721: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] the massing of the many nations.вАЭ
n2301722: tn Heb вАЬlike the loud noise of the seas, they make a loud noise.вАЭ
n2301724: tn Heb вАЬlike the uproar of mighty waters they are in an uproar.вАЭ
n2301725: tn Heb вАЬthe peoples are in an uproar like the uproar of mighty waters.вАЭ
n2301726: tn Or вАЬrebukes.вАЭ The verb and related noun are used in theophanies of GodвАЩs battle cry, which terrifies his enemies. See, for example, Pss 18:15; 76:7; 106:9; Isa 50:2; Nah 1:4, and A. Caquot, TDOT 3:49-53.
n2301727: tn Or perhaps вАЬtumbleweedвАЭ (NAB, NIV, CEV); KJV вАЬlike a rolling thing.вАЭ
n2301728: tn Heb вАЬat the time of evening, look, sudden terror.вАЭ
n2301730: tn Heb вАЬthis is the portion of those who plunder us, and the lot of those who loot us.вАЭ
n230181: sn The significance of the qualifying phrase вАЬbuzzing wingsвАЭ is uncertain. Some suggest that the designation points to Cush as a land with many insects. Another possibility is that it refers to the swiftness with which this landвАЩs messengers travel (v. 2a); they move over the sea as swiftly as an insect flies through the air. For a discussion of the options, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:359-60.
n230182: tn The precise meaning of the qualifying terms is uncertain. вАП„Ю÷∞„Ю÷ї„©„Б÷Љ÷Є„Ъ÷∞вАО (memushakh) appears to be a Pual participle from the verb вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (mashakh, вАЬto draw, extendвАЭ). Lexicographers theorize that it here refers to people who вАЬstretch out,вАЭ as it were, or are tall. See BDB 604 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, and HALOT 645-46 s.v. вАП„Ю„©„Б„Ъ÷∞вАО. вАП„Ю„Х÷є„®÷Є„ШвАО (morat) is taken as a Pual participle from вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„ШвАО (marat), which can mean вАЬto pull out [hair],вАЭ in the Qal, вАЬbecome baldвАЭ in the Niphal, and вАЬbe wiped cleanвАЭ in the Pual. Lexicographers theorize that the word here refers to people with bare, or smooth, skin. See BDB 598-99 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„ШвАО, and HALOT 634-35 s.v. вАП„Ю„®„ШвАО. These proposed meanings, which are based on etymological speculation, must be regarded as tentative.
n230183: tn Heb вАЬfrom it and onwards.вАЭ HALOT 245 s.v. вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО suggests the translation вАЬfar and wide.вАЭ
n230184: tn Once more the precise meaning of the qualifying terms is uncertain. The expression вАП„І÷Ј„Х „І÷Є„ХвАО (qav qav) is sometimes related to a proposed Arabic cognate and taken to mean вАЬstrengthвАЭ (see BDB 876 II вАП„І÷Ј„ХвАО). Others, on the basis of Isa 28:10, 13, understand the form as gibberish (literally, вАЬkav, kavвАЭ) and take it to be a reference to this nationвАЩs strange, unknown language. The form вАП„Ю÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„ФвАО (mevusah) appears to be derived from вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„°вАО (bus, вАЬto trampleвАЭ), so lexicographers suggest the meaning вАЬtramplingвАЭ or вАЬsubjugation,вАЭ i.e., a nation that subdues others. See BDB 101 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„°вАО and HALOT 541 s.v. вАП„Ю÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„ФвАО. These proposals, which are based on etymological speculation, must be regarded as tentative.
n230185: tn The precise meaning of the verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Є„РвАО (baza Њ), which occurs only in this oracle (see also v. 7) in the OT, is uncertain. BDB 102 s.v. suggests вАЬdivideвАЭ on the basis of alleged Aramaic and Arabic cognates; HALOT 117 s.v., citing an alleged Arabic cognate, suggests вАЬwash away.вАЭ
n230186: tn Or вАЬbe quiet, inactiveвАЭ; NIV вАЬwill remain quiet.вАЭ
n230187: tn Heb вАЬlike the glowing heat because of light.вАЭ The precise meaning of the line is uncertain.
n230188: tn Heb вАЬa cloud of dew,вАЭ or вАЬa cloud of light rain.вАЭ
n230189: tc Some medieval Hebrew mss, with support from the LXX, Syriac Peshitta, and Latin Vulgate, read вАЬthe day.вАЭ
n2301810: sn It is unclear how the comparisons in v. 4b relate to the preceding statement. How is waiting and watching similar to heat or a cloud? For a discussion of interpretive options, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:362.
n2301811: tn Heb вАЬand the unripe, ripening fruit is maturing.вАЭ
n2301812: tn On the meaning of вАП„Ц÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ц÷Ј„ЬвАО (zalzal, вАЬshoot [of the vine] without fruit budsвАЭ) see HALOT 272 s.v. *вАП„Ц÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ц÷Ј„ЬвАО.
n2301813: tn Heb вАЬthe tendrils he will remove, he will cut off.вАЭ
n2301814: tn Heb вАЬthey will be left togetherвАЭ (so NASB).
n2301815: tn Heb вАЬthe beasts of the earthвАЭ (so KJV, NASB).
n2301816: tn On the interpretive difficulties of this verse, see the notes at v. 2, where the same terminology is used.
n2301817: tn The words вАЬthe tributeвАЭ are repeated here in the translation for clarity.
n2301818: tn Heb вАЬto the place of the name of the Lord who commands armies [traditionally, the Lord of hosts], Mount Zion.вАЭ
n230192: tn Heb вАЬand the heart of Egypt melts within it.вАЭ
n230193: tn Heb I will provoke Egypt against EgyptвАЭ (NAB similar).
n230194: tn Heb вАЬand they will fight, a man against his brother, and a man against his neighbor, city against city, kingdom against kingdom.вАЭ Civil strife will extend all the way from the domestic level to the provincial arena.
n230195: tn Heb вАЬand the spirit of Egypt will be laid waste in its midst.вАЭ
n230196: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (bala њ, вАЬconfuseвАЭ) is a homonym of the more common вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (bala њ, вАЬswallowвАЭ); see HALOT 135 s.v. I вАП„С„Ь„ҐвАО.
n230197: tn Heb вАЬthey will inquire of the idols and of the spirits of the dead and of the ritual pits and of the magicians.вАЭ Hebrew вАП„Р„Х÷є„СвАО ( Њov, вАЬritual pitвАЭ) refers to a pit used by a magician to conjure up underworld spirits. See the note on вАЬincantationsвАЭ in 8:19.
n230198: tn Heb вАЬwill dry up and be dry.вАЭ Two synonyms are joined for emphasis.
n230199: tn Heb вАЬriversвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NAB, CEV вАЬstreamsвАЭ; TEV вАЬchannels.вАЭ
n2301910: tn The verb form appears as a Hiphil in the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa; the form in MT may be a so-called вАЬmixed form,вАЭ reflecting the Hebrew Hiphil stem and the functionally corresponding Aramaic Aphel stem. See HALOT 276 s.v. I вАП„Ц„†„ЧвАО.
n2301911: tn Heb вАЬthe plants by the river, by the mouth of the river.вАЭ
n2301912: tn Heb вАЬwill dry up, [being] scattered, and it will vanish.вАЭ
n2301913: tn Or perhaps, вАЬwill disappearвАЭ; cf. TEV вАЬwill be useless.вАЭ
n2301914: tn BDB 301 s.v. вАП„Ч„Х÷є„®÷і„ЩвАО suggests the meaning вАЬwhite stuffвАЭ for вАП„Ч„Х÷є„®÷і„ЩвАО (khori); the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ч÷Є„Х÷µ„®„Х÷ЉвАО (khaveru), probably a Qal perfect, third plural form of вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„®вАО, (khur, вАЬbe white, paleвАЭ). See HALOT 299 s.v. I вАП„Ч„Х„®вАО. The latter reading is assumed in the translation above.
n2301915: tn Some interpret вАП„©„Б÷Є„™÷є„™÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (shatoteha) as вАЬher foundations,вАЭ i.e., leaders, nobles. See BDB 1011 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„™вАО. Others, on the basis of alleged cognates in Akkadian and Coptic, repoint the form вАП„©„Б÷∞„™÷і„Щ„™÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (shetiteha) and translate вАЬher weavers.вАЭ See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:370.
n2301916: tn Heb вАЬcrushed.вАЭ Emotional distress is the focus of the context (see vv. 8-9, 10b).
n2301917: tn Heb вАЬsad of soulвАЭ; cf. NIV, NLT вАЬsick at heart.вАЭ
n2301918: tn Or вАЬcertainly the officials of Zoan are fools.вАЭ вАП„Р÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (вАЩakh) can carry the sense, вАЬonly, nothing but,вАЭ or вАЬcertainly, surely.вАЭ
n2301919: tn Heb вАЬA son of wise men am I, a son of ancient kings.вАЭ The term вАП„С÷Љ÷ґ„ЯвАО (ben, вАЬson ofвАЭ) could refer to literal descent, but many understand the word, at least in the first line, in its idiomatic sense of вАЬmember [of a guild].вАЭ See HALOT 138 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷ґ„ЯвАО and J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:371. If this is the case, then one can take the word in a figurative sense in the second line as well, the вАЬson of ancient kingsвАЭ being one devoted to their memory as preserved in their literature.
n2301920: tn Heb вАЬWhere are they? Where are your wise men?вАЭ The juxtaposition of the interrogative pronouns is emphatic. See HALOT 38 s.v. вАП„Р÷ґ„ЩвАО.
n2301921: tn Heb вАЬNophвАЭ (so KJV); most recent English versions substitute the more familiar вАЬMemphis.вАЭ
n2301922: tn Heb вАЬthe cornerstone.вАЭ The singular form should be emended to a plural.
n2301923: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord has mixed into her midst a spirit of blindness.вАЭ
n2301924: tn Heb вАЬlike the going astray of a drunkard in his vomit.вАЭ
n2301925: tn Heb вАЬAnd there will not be for Egypt a deed, which head and tail, shoot and stalk, can do.вАЭ In 9:14-15 the phrase вАЬhead or tailвАЭ refers to leaders and prophets, respectively. This interpretation makes good sense in this context, where both leaders and advisers (probably including prophets and diviners) are mentioned (vv. 11-14). Here, as in 9:14, вАЬshoots or stalkвАЭ picture a reed, which symbolizes the leadership of the nation in its entirety.
n2301926: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV); likewise at the beginning of vv. 18 and 19.
n2301927: tn Heb вАЬEgypt,вАЭ which stands by metonymy for the countryвАЩs inhabitants.
n2301928: sn As the rest of the verse indicates, the point of the simile is that the Egyptians will be relatively weak physically and will wilt in fear before the LordвАЩs onslaught.
n2301929: tn Heb вАЬand he will tremble and be afraid because of the brandishing of the hand of the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ], which he brandishes against him.вАЭ Since according to the imagery here the LordвАЩs вАЬhandвАЭ is raised as a weapon against the Egyptians, the term вАЬfistвАЭ has been used in the translation.
n2301930: tn Heb вАЬand the land of Judah will become [a source of] shame to Egypt. Everyone to whom one mentions it [i.e., the land of Judah] will fear because of the plan of the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] which he is planning against him.вАЭ
n2301931: sn The significance of the number вАЬfiveвАЭ in this context is uncertain. For a discussion of various proposals, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:376-77.
n2301932: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Ґ÷і„Щ„® „Ф÷Ј„Ф÷ґ„®÷ґ„°вАО ( њir haheres, вАЬCity of DestructionвАЭ; cf. NASB, NIV) but this does not fit the positive emphasis of vv. 18-22. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa and some medieval Hebrew mss read вАП„Ґ÷і„Щ„® „Ф÷Є„Ч÷ґ„®÷ґ„°вАО ( њir hakheres, вАЬCity of the Sun,вАЭ i.e., Heliopolis). This reading also finds support from SymmachusвАЩ Greek version, the Targum, and the Vulgate. See HALOT 257 s.v. вАП„Ч÷ґ„®÷ґ„°вАО and HALOT 355 s.v. II вАП„Ч÷ґ„®÷ґ„°вАО.
n2301933: tn This word is sometimes used of a sacred pillar associated with pagan worship, but here it is associated with the worship of the Lord.
n2301934: tn The masculine noun вАП„Ю÷і„Ц÷∞„С÷Љ÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (mizbeakh, вАЬaltarвАЭ) in v. 19 is probably the subject of the masculine singular verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah) rather than the feminine noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷Љ÷µ„С÷Є„ФвАО (matsevah, вАЬsacred pillarвАЭ), also in v. 19.
n2301935: tn Heb вАЬa sign and a witness to the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] in the land of Egypt.вАЭ
n2301936: tn вАП„®÷Є„СвАО (rav) is a substantival participle (from вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО, riv) meaning вАЬone who strives, contends.вАЭ
n2301937: tn Heb вАЬEgypt.вАЭ For stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy, the present translation uses the pronoun (вАЬtheyвАЭ) here.
n2301939: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV); likewise at the beginning of vv. 23 and 24.
n2301940: tn Heb вАЬhe will be entreated.вАЭ The Niphal has a tolerative sense here, вАЬhe will allow himself to be entreated.вАЭ
n2301941: tn The text could be translated, вАЬand Egypt will serve AssyriaвАЭ (cf. NAB), but subjugation of one nation to the other does not seem to be a theme in vv. 23-25. Rather the nations are viewed as equals before the Lord (v. 25). Therefore it is better to take вАП„Р÷ґ„™вАО ( Њet) in v. 23b as a preposition, вАЬtogether with,вАЭ rather than the accusative sign. The names of the two countries are understood to refer by metonymy to their respective inhabitants.
n2301944: tn Heb вАЬwhich the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] will bless [it], saying.вАЭ The third masculine singular suffix on the form вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„®÷≤„Ы„Х÷євАО (berakho) should probably be emended to a third feminine singular suffix вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„®÷≤„Ы÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (berakhah), for its antecedent would appear to be the feminine noun вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets, вАЬearthвАЭ) at the end of v. 24.
n230201:
tn Heb вАЬIn the year the commanding general came to Ashdod, when Sargon king of Assyria sent him, and he fought against Ashdod and captured it.вАЭ
sn This probably refers to the Assyrian campaign against Philistia in 712 or 711 b.c.
n230203: tn The word used here (вАП„Ґ÷Є„®„Х÷є„ЭвАО,  њarom) sometimes means вАЬnaked,вАЭ but here it appears to mean simply вАЬlightly dressed,вАЭ i.e., stripped to oneвАЩs undergarments. See HALOT 883 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷Є„®„Х÷є„ЭвАО. The term also occurs in vv. 3, 4.
n230204: tn Heb вАЬlightly dressed and barefoot, and bare with respect to the buttocks, the nakedness of Egypt.вАЭ
n230205: tn Heb вАЬand they will be afraid and embarrassed because of Cush their hope and Egypt their beauty.вАЭ
n230207: sn This probably refers to the coastal region of Philistia (cf. TEV).
n230212: sn The phrase is quite cryptic, at least to the modern reader. Verse 9 seems to indicate that this message pertains to Babylon. Southern Mesopotamia was known as the Sealand in ancient times, because of its proximity to the Persian Gulf. Perhaps the reference to Babylon as a вАЬwildernessвАЭ foreshadows the destruction that would overtake the city, making it like an uninhabited wilderness.
n230214: tn Heb вАЬa severe revelation has been related to me.вАЭ
n230215: sn This is often interpreted to mean вАЬall the groaningвАЭ that Babylon has caused others.
n230216: tn Heb вАЬmy waist is filled with shaking [or вАЬanguishвАЭ].вАЭ
n230217: tn Or perhaps, вАЬbent over [in pain]вАЭ; cf. NRSV вАЬI am bowed down.вАЭ
n2302110: tn The precise meaning of the verb in this line is debated. Some prefer to derive the form from the homonymic вАП„¶÷Є„§÷є„ФвАО (tsafoh, вАЬkeep watchвАЭ) and translate вАЬpost a guardвАЭ (cf. KJV вАЬwatch in the watchtowerвАЭ; ASV вАЬset the watchвАЭ).
n2302111: tn The verbal forms in the first three lines are infinitives absolute, which are functioning here as finite verbs. It is uncertain if the forms should have an imperatival or indicative/descriptive force here.
n2302112: sn Smearing the shields with oil would make them more flexible and effective in battle. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:394.
n2302113: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in vv. 8, 16 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2302115: tn The Hebrew text has, вАЬthe lion,вАЭ but this makes little sense here. вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„Щ÷µ„ФвАО ( Њaryeh, вАЬlionвАЭ) probably needs to be emended to an original вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷є„Р÷ґ„ФвАО (haro Њeh, вАЬthe one who seesвАЭ), i.e., the guard mentioned previously in v. 6. The Dead Sea Scrolls (1Q Isaa) and the Syriac support an original вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷є„Р÷ґ„ФвАО (haro Њeh, вАЬthe one who seesвАЭ).
n2302116: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay). Some translations take this to refer to the Lord (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV), while others take it to refer to the guardвАЩs human master (вАЬmy lordвАЭ; cf. NIV, NLT).
n2302117: tn Or вАЬ[with] teams of horses,вАЭ or perhaps, вАЬwith a pair of horsemen.вАЭ
n2302118: tn Heb вАЬand he answered and saidвАЭ (so KJV, ASV).
n2302119: tn Heb вАЬMy trampled one, and the son of the threshing floor.вАЭ
n2302120: tn The noun вАП„У÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (dumah) means вАЬsilence,вАЭ but here it is a proper name, probably referring to a site in northern Arabia or to the nation of Edom. See BDB 189 s.v. II вАП„У÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО. If Dumah was an area in northern Arabia, it would be of interest to the Edomites because of its strategic position on trade routes which they used. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:398.
n2302121: sn Seir is another name for Edom. See BDB 973 s.v. вАП„©„В÷µ„Ґ÷і„Щ„®вАО.
n2302122: sn The вАЬnightвАЭ probably here symbolizes distress and difficult times. See BDB 539 s.v. вАП„Ь÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО.
n2302123: sn Dumah will experience some relief, but it will be short-lived as night returns.
n2302124: sn The point of the watchmanвАЩs final instructions (вАЬif you want to ask, ask; come againвАЭ) is unclear. Perhaps they are included to add realism to the dramatic portrayal. The watchman sends the questioner away with the words, вАЬFeel free to come back and ask again.вАЭ
n2302125: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2302126: tn Heb вАЬin still a year, like the years of a hired worker.вАЭ See the note at 16:14.
n2302127: tn Heb вАЬand the remnant of the number of the bow, the mighty men of the sons of Kedar, will be few.вАЭ
n2302128: tn Or вАЬforвАЭ (KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV).
n230222: sn The following message pertains to Jerusalem. The significance of referring to the city as the Valley of Vision is uncertain. Perhaps the Hinnom Valley is in view, but why it is associated with a prophetic revelatory вАЬvisionвАЭ is not entirely clear. Maybe the Hinnom Valley is called this because the destruction that will take place there is the focal point of this prophetic message (see v. 5).
n230224: tn Heb вАЬthe boisterous town.вАЭ The phrase is parallel to вАЬthe noisy cityвАЭ in the preceding line.
n230225: sn Apparently they died from starvation during the siege that preceded the final conquest of the city. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:409.
n230226: tn Verse 3 reads literally, вАЬAll your leaders ran away; apart from a bow they were captured; all your found ones were captured together; to a distant place they fled.вАЭ J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:403, n. 3) suggests that the lines of the verse are arranged chiastically; lines 1 and 4 go together, while lines 2 and 3 are parallel. To translate the lines in the order they appear in the Hebrew text is misleading to the English reader, who is likely unfamiliar with, or at least insensitive to, chiastic parallelism. Consequently, the main translation arranges the lines as follows: line 1 (Hebrew) = line 1 (in translation); line 2 (Hebrew) = line 4 (in translation); line 3 (Hebrew) = line 3 (in translation); line 4 (Hebrew) = line 2 (in translation).
n230227: tn Heb вАЬall your found ones.вАЭ To achieve tighter parallelism (see вАЬyour leadersвАЭ) some prefer to emend the form to вАП„Р÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„¶÷Ј„Щ÷і„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њammitsayikh, вАЬyour strong onesвАЭ) or to вАП„†÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷Є„¶÷Ј„Щ÷і„Ъ÷∞вАО (ne Њematsayikh, вАЬyour strengthened onesвАЭ).
n230228: tn Heb вАЬapart from [i.e., without] a bow they were capturedвАЭ; cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬwithout the use of a bow.вАЭ
n230229: tn Heb вАЬlook away from meвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NRSV).
n2302211: tn Heb вАЬthe daughter of my people.вАЭ вАЬDaughterвАЭ is here used metaphorically to express the speakerвАЩs emotional attachment to his people, as well as their vulnerability and weakness.
n2302212: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬSovereignвАЭ here and in vv. 12, 14, 15 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2302213: tn Heb вАЬFor [there is] a day of panic, and trampling, and confusion for the master, the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ].вАЭ
n2302214: tn The traditional accentuation of the Hebrew text suggests that this phrase goes with what precedes.
n2302215: tn The precise meaning of this statement is unclear. Some take вАП„І÷і„®вАО (qir) as вАЬwallвАЭ and interpret the verb to mean вАЬtear down.вАЭ However, tighter parallelism (note the reference to crying for help in the next line) is achieved if one takes both the verb and noun from a root, attested in Ugaritic and Arabic, meaning вАЬmake a sound.вАЭ See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:404, n. 5.
n2302216: sn Perhaps вАЬthe hillвАЭ refers to the temple mount.
n2302217: tn Heb вАЬ[with] the chariots of men, horsemen.вАЭ
n2302218: sn A distant region in the direction of Mesopotamia; see Amos 1:5; 9:7.
n2302220: sn The Elamites and men of Kir may here symbolize a fierce army from a distant land. If this oracle anticipates a Babylonian conquest of the city (see 39:5-7), then the Elamites and men of Kir are perhaps viewed here as mercenaries in the Babylonian army. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:410.
n2302221: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302222: tn Heb вАЬtaking a stand, take their stand.вАЭ The infinitive absolute emphasizes the following finite verb. The translation attempts to bring out this emphasis with the adverb вАЬconfidently.вАЭ
n2302223: tn Heb вАЬhe,вАЭ i.e., the enemy invader. NASB, by its capitalization of the pronoun, takes this to refer to the Lord.
n2302225: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV); likewise at the beginning of v. 12.
n2302226: sn Perhaps this refers to a royal armory, or to SolomonвАЩs вАЬHouse of the Forest of Lebanon,вАЭ where weapons may have been kept (see 1 Kgs 10:16-17).
n2302227: tn Heb вАЬthe breaks of the City of David, you saw that they were many.вАЭ
n2302229: tn Heb вАЬyou demolished the houses to fortify the wall.вАЭ
n2302230: tn Heb вАЬlook atвАЭ; NAB, NRSV вАЬdid not look to.вАЭ
n2302231: tn The antecedent of the third feminine singular suffix here and in the next line is unclear. The closest feminine noun is вАЬpoolвАЭ in the first half of the verse. Perhaps this вАЬold poolвАЭ symbolizes the entire city, which had prospered because of GodвАЩs provision and protection through the years.
n2302233: tn Heb вАЬfor baldness and the wearing of sackcloth.вАЭ See the note at 15:2.
n2302235: tn The prophet here quotes what the fatalistic people are saying. The introductory вАЬyou sayвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarification; the concluding verb вАЬwe dieвАЭ makes it clear the people are speaking. The six verbs translated as imperatives are actually infinitives absolute, functioning here as finite verbs.
n2302236: tn Heb вАЬit was revealed in my ears [by?] the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ].вАЭ
n2302237: tn Heb вАЬCertainly this sin will not be atoned for until you die.вАЭ This does not imply that their death will bring atonement; rather it emphasizes that their sin is unpardonable. The statement has the form of an oath.
n2302238: tn Heb вАЬwho is over the houseвАЭ (so ASV); NASB вАЬwho is in charge of the royal household.вАЭ
n2302239: tn The words вАЬand tell himвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2302240: tn Heb вАЬWhat to you here? And who to you here?вАЭ The point of the second question is not entirely clear. The interpretation reflected in the translation is based on the following context, which suggests that Shebna has no right to think of himself so highly and arrange such an extravagant burial place for himself.
n2302242: tn Heb вАЬwill throw you with a throwing.вАЭ
n2302243: tn Heb вАЬO manвАЭ (so NASB); NAB вАЬmortal manвАЭ; NRSV вАЬmy fellow.вАЭ
n2302244: tn Heb вАЬand the one who wraps you [will] wrap.вАЭ
n2302245: tn Heb вАЬand he will tightly [or вАЬsurelyвАЭ] wind you [with] winding like a ball, to a land broad of hands [i.e., вАЬsidesвАЭ].вАЭ
n2302246: tn Heb вАЬand there the chariots of your splendor.вАЭ
n2302247: sn Apparently the reference to chariots alludes to ShebnaвАЩs excessive pride, which in turn brings disgrace to the royal family.
n2302248: tn Heb вАЬI will push you away from.вАЭ
n2302249: tn Heb вАЬhe will throw you down.вАЭ The shift from the first to third person is peculiar and abrupt, but certainly not unprecedented in Hebrew poetry. See GKC 462 ¬І144.p. The third person may be indefinite (вАЬone will throw you downвАЭ), in which case the passive translation is justified.
n2302250: tn Or вАЬin that day.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302251: tn Heb вАЬand your dominion I will place in his hand.вАЭ
n2302252: tn Heb вАЬa father to.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„Р÷Є„СвАО ( Њav, вАЬfatherвАЭ) is here used metaphorically of one who protects and supports those under his care and authority, like a father does his family. For another example of this metaphorical use of the word, see Job 29:16.
n2302254: sn This may refer to a literal insignia worn by the chief administrator. Even so, it would still symbolize the administratorвАЩs authority to grant or exclude access to the king. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:422.
n2302255: sn The metaphor depicts how secure his position will be.
n2302256: tn Heb вАЬand he will become a glorious throne for the house of his father.вАЭ
n2302257: tn Heb вАЬand all the glory of the house of his father they will hang on him.вАЭ The Lord returns to the peg metaphor of v. 23a. EliakimвАЩs secure position of honor will bring benefits and jobs to many others in the family.
n2302258: tn The precise meaning and derivation of this word are uncertain. Cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬthe issueвАЭ; CEV вАЬrelatives.вАЭ
n2302259: tn Heb вАЬall the small vessels, from the vessels that are bowls to all the vessels that are jars.вАЭ The picture is that of a single peg holding the weight of all kinds of containers hung from it.
n2302261: sn EliakimвАЩs authority, though seemingly secure, will eventually be removed, and with it his familyвАЩs prominence.
n230232: tn Heb вАЬships of Tarshish.вАЭ This probably refers to large ships either made in or capable of traveling to the distant, western port of Tarshish.
n230233: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬfor it is destroyed, from a house, from entering.вАЭ The translation assumes that the mem (вАП„ЮвАО) on вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷і„™вАО (bayit) was originally an enclitic mem suffixed to the preceding verb. This assumption allows one to take вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷і„™вАО as the subject of the preceding verb. It is used in a metaphorical sense for the port city of Tyre. The preposition min (вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО) prefixed to вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ) indicates negative consequence: вАЬso that no one can enter.вАЭ See BDB 583 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО 7.b.
n230234: tn Heb вАЬthe Kittim,вАЭ a designation for the people of Cyprus. See HALOT 504-05 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„™÷Љ÷і„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО.
n230237: tc The Hebrew text (23:2b-3a) reads literally, вАЬmerchant of Sidon, the one who crosses the sea, they filled you, and on the deep waters.вАЭ Instead of вАП„Ю÷і„Ь÷∞„Р„Х÷Љ„Ъ÷∞вАО (mil Њukh, вАЬthey filled youвАЭ) the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads вАП„Ю„Ь„Р„Ы„Щ„ЪвАО (вАЬyour messengersвАЭ). The translation assumes an emendation of вАП„Ю÷і„Ь÷∞„Р„Х÷Љ„Ъ÷∞вАО to вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Р÷Є„Ы÷Є„ХвАО (mal Њakhav, вАЬhis messengersвАЭ), taking the vav (вАП„ХвАО) on вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (uvemayim) as improperly placed; instead it should be the final letter of the preceding word.
n230238: tn Heb вАЬseed of Shihor.вАЭ вАЬShihorвАЭ probably refers to the east branch of the Nile. See Jer 2:18 and BDB 1009 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ч„Х÷є„®вАО.
n2302311: tn Heb вАЬmerchandiseвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬa mart of nationsвАЭ; NLT вАЬthe merchandise mart of the world.вАЭ
n2302312: tn J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:430-31) sees here a reference to Yam, the Canaanite god of the sea. He interprets the phrase вАП„Ю÷Є„Ґ„Х÷є„Ц „Ф÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (ma њoz hayyam, вАЬfortress of the seaвАЭ) as a title of Yam, translating вАЬMighty One of the Sea.вАЭ A more traditional view is that the phrase refers to Sidon.
n2302313:
tn Or вАЬvirginsвАЭ (KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB).
sn The sea is personified here as a lamenting childless woman. The foreboding language anticipates the following announcement of TyreвАЩs demise, viewed here as a child of the sea, as it were.
n2302314: tn Heb вАЬthey will be in pain at the report of Tyre.вАЭ
n2302315: tn Heb вАЬIs this to you, boisterous one?вАЭ The pronoun вАЬyouвАЭ is masculine plural, like the imperatives in v. 6, so it is likely addressed to the Egyptians and residents of the coast. вАЬBoisterous oneвАЭ is a feminine singular form, probably referring to the personified city of Tyre.
n2302316: tn Heb вАЬin the days of antiquity [is] her beginning.вАЭ
n2302317: tn The precise meaning of вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„Ш÷і„Щ„®÷Є„ФвАО (hamma њatirah) is uncertain. The form is a Hiphil participle from вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ш÷Ј„®вАО ( њatar), a denominative verb derived from вАП„Ґ÷≤„Ш÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО ( њatarah, вАЬcrown, wreathвАЭ). The participle may mean вАЬone who wears a crownвАЭ or вАЬone who distributes crowns.вАЭ In either case, TyreвАЩs prominence in the international political arena is in view.
n2302318: tn Heb вАЬthe honoredвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV); NIV вАЬrenowned.вАЭ
n2302319: tn Heb вАЬthe pride of all the beauty.вАЭ
n2302320: tc This meaning of this verse is unclear. The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬCross over your land, like the Nile, daughter of Tarshish, there is no more waistband.вАЭ The translation assumes an emendation of вАП„Ю÷µ„Ц÷Ј„ЧвАО (mezakh, вАЬwaistbandвАЭ) to вАП„Ю÷Є„Ч÷є„ЦвАО (makhoz, вАЬharbor, marketplaceвАЭ; see Ps 107:30). The term вАП„Ґ÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО ( њavar, вАЬcross overвАЭ) is probably used here of traveling over the water (as in v. 6). The command is addressed to personified Tarshish, who here represents her merchants. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ґ„С„У„ЩвАО (вАЬwork, cultivateвАЭ) instead of вАП„Ґ÷і„С÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО ( њivri, вАЬcross overвАЭ). In this case one might translate вАЬCultivate your land, like they do the Nile regionвАЭ (cf. NIV, CEV). The point would be that the people of Tarshish should turn to agriculture because they will no longer be able to get what they need through the marketplace in Tyre.
n2302321: tn Heb вАЬhis hand he stretched out over the sea.вАЭ
n2302322: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord.вАЭ For stylistic reasons the pronoun (вАЬheвАЭ) has been used in the translation here.
n2302323: tn Heb вАЬconcerning Canaan, to destroy her fortresses.вАЭ NIV, NLT translate вАЬCanaanвАЭ as вАЬPhoeniciaвАЭ here.
n2302324: tn Or вАЬviolated, raped,вАЭ the point being that Daughter Sidon has lost her virginity in the most brutal manner possible.
n2302325: tn Heb вАЬ[to the] Kittim, get up, cross over; even there there will be no rest for you.вАЭ On вАЬKittimвАЭ see the note on вАЬCyprusвАЭ at v. 1.
n2302327: tn For the meaning of this word, see HALOT 118 s.v. *вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ч„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО.
n2302328: tn Or вАЬlaid bare.вАЭ For the meaning of this word, see HALOT 889 s.v. вАП„Ґ„®„®вАО.
n2302329: sn This verse probably refers to the Assyrian destruction of Babylon.
n2302330: tn Heb вАЬships of Tarshish.вАЭ See the note at v. 1.
n2302331: tn Or вАЬin that dayвАЭ (KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302332: sn The number seventy is probably used in a stereotypical, nonliteral sense here to indicate a long period of time that satisfies completely the demands of GodвАЩs judgment.
n2302333: tn Heb вАЬlike the days of a king.вАЭ
n2302334: tn Heb вАЬAt the end of seventy years it will be for Tyre like the song of the prostitute.вАЭ
n2302336: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302337: tn Heb вАЬvisit [with favor]вАЭ (cf. KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV); NIV вАЬwill deal with.вАЭ
n2302338: tn Heb вАЬand she will return to her [prostituteвАЩs] wages and engage in prostitution with all the kingdoms of the earth on the face of the earth.вАЭ
n2302339:
tn Heb вАЬfor eating to fullness and for beautiful covering[s].вАЭ
sn The point of this verse, which in its blatant nationalism comes precariously close to comparing the Lord to one who controls or manages a prostitute, is that Tyre will become a subject of Israel and her God. TyreвАЩs commercial profits will be used to enrich the LordвАЩs people.
n230241: tn Heb вАЬand it will be like the people, like the priest.вАЭ
n230242: tn Heb вАЬlike the servant, like his master.вАЭ
n230243: tn Heb вАЬlike the female servant, like her mistress.вАЭ
n230244: tn Heb вАЬlike the buyer, like the seller.вАЭ
n230245: tn Heb вАЬlike the lender, like the borrower.вАЭ
n230246: tn Heb вАЬlike the creditor, just as the one to whom he lends.вАЭ
n230247: tn Heb вАЬfor the Lord has spoken this word.вАЭ
n230248: tn Some prefer to read вАЬlandвАЭ here, but the word pair вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО/вАП„™÷Љ÷µ„С÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њerets/tevel [see the corresponding term in the parallel line]) elsewhere clearly designates the earth/world (see 1 Sam 2:8; 1 Chr 16:30; Job 37; 12; Pss 19:4; 24:1; 33:8; 89:11; 90:2; 96:13; 98:9; Prov 8:26, 31; Isa 14:16-17; 34:1; Jer 10:12; 51:15; Lam 4:12). According to L. Stadelmann, вАП„™„С„ЬвАО designates вАЬthe habitable part of the worldвАЭ (The Hebrew Conception of the World [AnBib], 130).
n230249: tn Or вАЬmournsвАЭ (BDB 5 s.v. вАП„Р÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО). HALOT 6-7 lists the homonyms I вАП„Р„С„ЬвАО (вАЬmournвАЭ) and II вАП„Р„С„ЬвАО (вАЬdry upвАЭ). They propose the second here on the basis of parallelism.
n2302410: tn Heb вАЬthe height of the people of the earth.вАЭ The translation assumes an emendation of the singular form вАП„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷є„ЭвАО (merom, вАЬheight ofвАЭ) to the plural construct вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷є„Ю÷µ„ЩвАО (merome, вАЬhigh ones ofвАЭ; note the plural verb at the beginning of the line), and understands the latter as referring to the prominent people of human society.
n2302411: tn Heb вАЬbeneathвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬunderвАЭ; NAB вАЬbecause of.вАЭ
n2302412: sn Isa 26:21 suggests that the earthвАЩs inhabitants defiled the earth by shedding the blood of their fellow human beings. See also Num 35:33-34, which assumes that bloodshed defiles a land.
n2302414:
tn Or вАЬeverlasting covenantвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB вАЬthe ancient covenantвАЭ; CEV вАЬtheir agreement that was to last forever.вАЭ
sn For a lengthy discussion of the identity of this covenant/treaty, see R. Chisholm, вАЬThe вАШEverlasting CovenantвАЩ and the вАШCity of ChaosвАЩ: Intentional Ambiguity and Irony in Isaiah 24, вАЭ CTR 6 (1993): 237-53. In this context, where judgment comes upon both the pagan nations and GodвАЩs covenant community, the phrase вАЬpermanent treatyвАЭ is intentionally ambiguous. For the nations this treaty is the Noahic mandate of Gen 9:1-7 with its specific stipulations and central regulation (Gen 9:7). By shedding blood, the warlike nations violated this treaty, which promotes population growth and prohibits murder. For Israel, which was also guilty of bloodshed (see Isa 1:15, 21; 4:4), this вАЬpermanent treatyвАЭ would refer more specifically to the Mosaic Law and its regulations prohibiting murder (Exod 20:13; Num 35:6-34), which are an extension of the Noahic mandate.
n2302415: sn Ancient Near Eastern treaties often had вАЬcurses,вАЭ or threatened judgments, attached to them. (See Deut 28 for a biblical example of such curses.) The party or parties taking an oath of allegiance acknowledged that disobedience would activate these curses, which typically threatened loss of agricultural fertility as depicted in the following verses.
