n2302416: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ЭвАО ( Њasham, вАЬbe guiltyвАЭ) is here used metonymically to mean вАЬpay, suffer for oneвАЩs guiltвАЭ (see HALOT 95 s.v. вАП„Р„©„Б„ЭвАО).
n2302417: tn BDB 359 s.v. вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО derives the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„®„Х÷ЉвАО (kharu) from вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (kharar, вАЬburnвАЭ), but HALOT 351 s.v. II вАП„Ч„®„ФвАО understands a hapax legomenon вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (kharah, вАЬto diminish in number,вАЭ a homonym of вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО) here, relating it to an alleged Arabic cognate meaning вАЬto decrease.вАЭ The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ч„Х„®„ХвАО, perhaps understanding the root as вАП„Ч÷Є„Х÷Ј„®вАО (khavar, вАЬgrow paleвАЭ; see Isa 29:22 and HALOT 299 s.v. I вАП„Ч„Х„®вАО).
n2302418: tn Heb вАЬand mankind is left small [in number].вАЭ
n2302419: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬall the joyful in heart,вАЭ but the context specifies the context as parties and drinking bouts.
n2302420: tn Heb вАЬthe joyвАЭ (again later in this verse).
n2302421: tn Heb вАЬwith a song they do not drink wine.вАЭ
n2302422: tn Heb вАЬthe city of chaosвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV). Isaiah uses the term вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (tohu) rather frequently of things (like idols) that are empty and worthless (see BDB 1062 s.v.), so the word might characterize the city as rebellious or morally worthless. However, in this context, which focuses on the effects of divine judgment, it probably refers to the ruined or worthless condition in which the city is left (note the use of the word in Isa 34:11). For a discussion of the identity of this city, see R. Chisholm, вАЬThe вАШEverlasting CovenantвАЩ and the вАШCity of ChaosвАЩ: Intentional Ambiguity and Irony in Isaiah 24, вАЭ CTR 6 (1993): 237-53. In the context of universal judgment depicted in Isa 24, this city represents all the nations and cities of the world which, like Babylon of old and the powers/cities mentioned in chapters 13-23, rebel against GodвАЩs authority. Behind the stereotypical language one can detect various specific manifestations of this symbolic and paradigmatic city, including Babylon, Moab, and Jerusalem, all of which are alluded or referred to in chapters 24-27.
n2302423: tn Heb вАЬevery house is closed up from entering.вАЭ
n2302424: tn Heb вАЬ[there is] an outcry over the wine in the streets.вАЭ
n2302425: tn Heb вАЬall joy turns to evening,вАЭ the darkness of evening symbolizing distress and sorrow.
n2302426: tn Heb вАЬthe joy of the earth disappears.вАЭ
n2302427: tn Heb вАЬand there is left in the city desolation.вАЭ
n2302428: tn Heb вАЬand [into] rubble the gate is crushed.вАЭ
n2302429: tn Heb вАЬin the midst ofвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB).
n2302430: sn The judgment will severely reduce the earthвАЩs population. See v. 6.
n2302431: sn The remnant of the nations (see v. 13) may be the unspecified subject. If so, then those who have survived the judgment begin to praise God.
n2302433: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬin the lights,вАЭ interpreted by some to mean вАЬin the region of light,вАЭ referring to the east. Some scholars have suggested the emendation of вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷ї„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ba Њurim) to вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Р÷і„Щ÷Љ÷µ„Щ „Ф÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (be Њiyye hayyam, вАЬalong the seacoastsвАЭ), a phrase that is repeated in the next line. In this case, the two lines form synonymous parallelism. If one retains the MT reading (as above), вАЬin the eastвАЭ and вАЬalong the seacoastsвАЭ depict the two ends of the earth to refer to all the earth (as a merism).
n2302434: tn The word вАЬextolвАЭ is supplied in the translation; the verb in the first line does double duty in the parallelism.
n2302435: tn Heb вАЬname,вАЭ which here stands for GodвАЩs reputation achieved by his mighty deeds.
n2302436: sn The identity of the subject is unclear. Apparently in vv. 15-16a an unidentified group responds to the praise they hear in the west by exhorting others to participate.
n2302437: tn Heb вАЬBeauty belongs to the just one.вАЭ These words may summarize the main theme of the songs mentioned in the preceding line.
n2302438: sn The prophet seems to contradict what he hears the group saying. Their words are premature because more destruction is coming.
n2302439:
tn Heb вАЬand [with] deception deceivers deceive.вАЭ
tn Verse 16b is a classic example of Hebrew wordplay. In the first line (вАЬIвАЩm wasting awayвА¶.вАЭ) four consecutive words end with hireq yod (вАП ÷і„ЩвАО); in the second line all forms are derived from the root вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Ј„УвАО (bagad). The repetition of sound draws attention to the prophetвАЩs lament.
n2302440: tn Heb вАЬ[are] upon you, O inhabitant of the earth.вАЭ The first line of v. 17 provides another classic example of Hebrew wordplay. The names of the three instruments of judgment (вАП„§÷Є„ЧвАО,вАП„§÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„™ вАО,вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО [pakhad, fakhat, fakh]) all begin with the letters вАП„§вАО and вАП„ЧвАО (pe and khet) and the first two end in dental consonants (вАП„УвАО and вАП„™вАО, dalet and tav). Once again the repetition of sound draws attention to the statement and contributes to the theme of the inescapability of judgment. As their similar-sounding names suggest, terror, pit, and snare are allies in destroying the objects of divine wrath.
n2302441: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302442: tn Heb вАЬfrom the heightвАЭ; KJV вАЬfrom on high.вАЭ
n2302443: sn The language reflects the account of the Noahic Flood (see Gen 7:11).
n2302444: tn Once more repetition is used to draw attention to a statement. In the Hebrew text each line ends with вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets, вАЬearthвАЭ). Each line also uses a Hitpolel verb form from a geminate root preceded by an emphatic infinitive absolute.
n2302445: tn Heb вАЬstaggering, staggers.вАЭ The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute before the finite verb for emphasis and sound play.
n2302446: tn The words вАЬin a windstormвАЭ are supplied in the translation to clarify the metaphor.
n2302447: tn Or вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV). The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302449: tn Heb вАЬthe host of the height in the height.вАЭ The вАЬhost of the height/heavenвАЭ refers to the heavenly luminaries (stars and planets; see, among others, Deut 4:19; 17:3; 2 Kgs 17:16; 21:3, 5; 23:4-5; 2 Chr 33:3, 5) that populate the divine/heavenly assembly in mythological and prescientific Israelite thought (see Job 38:7; Isa 14:13).
n2302450: tn Heb вАЬthey will be gathered [in] a gathering [as] a prisoner in a cistern.вАЭ It is tempting to eliminate вАП„Р÷≤„°÷µ„§÷Є„ФвАО ( Њasefah, вАЬa gatheringвАЭ) as dittographic or as a gloss, but sound repetition is one of the main characteristics of the style of this section of the chapter.
n2302451: tn Heb вАЬand after a multitude of days.вАЭ
n2302452: tn Heb вАЬvisitedвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). This verse can mean to visit for good or for evil. The translation assumes the latter, based on v. 21a. However, BDB 823 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО B.Niph.2 suggests the meaning вАЬvisit graciouslyвАЭ here, in which case one might translate вАЬthey will be released.вАЭ
n2302456: tn Or вАЬtake his throne,вАЭ вАЬbecome king.вАЭ
n230251: sn The prophet speaks here as one who has observed the coming judgment of the proud.
n230253: tn Heb вАЬplans from long ago [in] faithfulness, trustworthiness.вАЭ The feminine noun вАП„Р÷±„Ю„Х÷Љ„†÷Є„ФвАО ( Њemunah, вАЬfaithfulnessвАЭ) and masculine noun вАП„Р÷є„Ю÷ґ„ЯвАО ( Њomen, вАЬtrustworthinessвАЭ), both of which are derived from the root вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman), are juxtaposed to emphasize the basic idea conveyed by the synonyms. Here they describe the absolute reliability of the divine plans.
n230255: tn The Hebrew text has вАЬyou have made from the city.вАЭ The prefixed mem (вАП„ЮвАО) on вАП„Ґ÷і„Щ„®вАО ( њir, вАЬcityвАЭ) was probably originally an enclitic mem suffixed to the preceding verb. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:456, n. 3.
n230256: tc Some with support from the LXX emend вАП„Ц÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (zarim, вАЬforeignersвАЭ) to вАП„Ц÷µ„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (zedim, вАЬthe insolentвАЭ).
n230257: tn The Hebrew text has a singular form, but it should be emended to a plural or eliminated altogether. The noun may have been accidentally copied from the preceding verse.
n230258: tn Or perhaps, вАЬthe violentвАЭ; NIV, NRSV вАЬthe ruthless.вАЭ
n230259: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬlike a rainstorm of a wall,вАЭ which might be interpreted to mean, вАЬlike a rainstorm battering against a wall.вАЭ The translation assumes an emendation of вАП„І÷і„Щ„®вАО (qir, вАЬwallвАЭ) to вАП„І÷є„®вАО (qor, вАЬcold, winterвАЭ; cf. Gen 8:22). See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:457, n. 6, for discussion.
n2302512: tn Heb вАЬ[like] heat in the shadow of a cloud.вАЭ
n2302513: tn The translation assumes that the verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷ґ„ФвАО (ya њaneh) is a Hiphil imperfect from вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО ( њanah, вАЬbe afflicted, humiliatedвАЭ). In this context with вАЬsongвАЭ as object it means to вАЬquietвАЭ (see HALOT 853-54 s.v. II вАП„Ґ„†„ФвАО). Some prefer to emend the form to the second person singular, so that it will agree with the second person verb earlier in the verse. BDB 776 s.v. III вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО Qal.1 understands the form as Qal, with вАЬsongвАЭ as subject, in which case one might translate вАЬthe song of tyrants will be silent.вАЭ An emendation of the form to a Niphal (вАП„Щ÷µ„Ґ÷Є„†÷ґ„ФвАО, ye њaneh) would yield the same translation.
n2302514: sn That is, Mount Zion (see 24:23); cf. TEV; NLT вАЬIn Jerusalem.вАЭ
n2302515: tn Heb вАЬAnd the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] will make for all the nations on this mountain a banquet of meats, a banquet of wine dregs, meats filled with marrow, dregs that are filtered.вАЭ
n2302516: tn The Hebrew text reads, вАЬthe face of the shroud, the shroud over all the nations.вАЭ Some emend the second вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷є„ШвАО (hallot) to a passive participle вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ШвАО (hallut, вАЬthat is wrappedвАЭ).
n2302517: sn The point of the imagery is unclear. Perhaps the shroud/covering referred to was associated with death in some way (see v. 8).
n2302518: sn The image of the Lord вАЬswallowingвАЭ death would be especially powerful, for death was viewed in Canaanite mythology and culture as a hungry enemy that swallows its victims. See the note at 5:14.
n2302519: tn Heb вАЬhas spokenвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2302520: tn Heb вАЬand one will say in that day.вАЭ
n2302523: tn Heb вАЬfor the hand of the Lord will rest on this mountainвАЭ; TEV вАЬwill protect Mount ZionвАЭ; NCV вАЬwill protect (rest on NLT) Jerusalem.вАЭ
n2302524: tn Heb вАЬunder him,вАЭ i.e., вАЬin his place.вАЭ
n2302525: tc The marginal reading (Qere) is вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷євАО (bemo, вАЬinвАЭ). The consonantal text (Kethib) has вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„ЩвАО (bemi, вАЬin the water ofвАЭ).
n2302526: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (Moab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2302527: tn The antecedent of the third masculine singular pronominal suffix is probably the masculine noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„™÷∞„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (matben, вАЬheap of strawвАЭ) in v. 10 rather than the feminine noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„У÷∞„Ю÷µ„†÷Є„ФвАО (madmenah, вАЬmanure pileвАЭ), also in v. 10.
n2302528: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2302529: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (Moab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2302530: tn The Hebrew text has, вАЬhe will bring down his pride along with the [?] of his hands.вАЭ The meaning of вАП„Р÷Є„®÷∞„С÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њarbot), which occurs only here in the OT, is unknown. Some (see BDB 70 s.v. вАП„Р÷Є„®÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО) translate вАЬartifice, cleverness,вАЭ relating the form to the verbal root вАП„Р÷Є„®÷Є„СвАО ( Њarav, вАЬto lie in wait, ambushвАЭ), but this requires some convoluted semantic reasoning. HALOT 83 s.v. *вАП„Р÷Є„®÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО suggests the meaning вАЬ[nimble] movements.вАЭ The translation above, which attempts to relate the form to the preceding context, is purely speculative.
n2302532: tn Heb вАЬa fortification, the high point of your walls.вАЭ
n2302533: tn Heb вАЬhe will bring [it] down, he will make [it] touch the ground, even to the dust.вАЭ
n230262: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n230263: tn Heb вАЬdeliverance he makes walls and a rampart.вАЭ
n230264: tn Heb вАЬ[one of] firm purpose you will keep [in] peace, peace, for in you he possesses trust.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„Щ÷µ„¶÷ґ„®вАО (yetser) refers to what one devises in the mind; вАП„°÷Є„Ю„Х÷Љ„Ъ÷∞вАО (samukh) probably functions here like an attributive adjective and carries the nuance вАЬfirm.вАЭ So the phrase literally means, вАЬa firm purpose,вАЭ but as the object of the verb вАЬkeep, guard,вАЭ it must stand by metonymy for the one(s) who possess a firm purpose. In this context the вАЬrighteous nationвАЭ (v. 2) is probably in view and the вАЬfirm purposeвАЭ refers to their unwavering faith in GodвАЩs vindication (see 25:9). In this context вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom, вАЬpeaceвАЭ), which is repeated for emphasis, likely refers to national security, not emotional or psychological composure (see vv. 1-2). The passive participle вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ш„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (batuakh) expresses a state that results from the subjectвАЩs action.
n230265: tn Or вАЬforevermore.вАЭ For other uses of the phrase вАП„Ґ÷≤„У÷µ„Щ÷Њ„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО ( њade  њad) see Isa 65:18 and Pss 83:17; 92:7.
n230266: tc The Hebrew text has вАЬfor in Yah, the Lord, an everlasting rock.вАЭ Some have suggested that the phrase вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (beyah, вАЬin YahвАЭ) is the result of dittography. A scribe seeing вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ki yehvah) in his original text would somehow have confused the letters and accidentally inserted вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО between the words (bet and kaf [вАП„СвАО and вАП„ЫвАО] can be confused in later script phases). A number of English versions retain both divine names for emphasis (ESV, NIV, NKJV, NRSV, NLT). One of the Qumran texts (1QIsaa) confirms the MT reading as well.
n230268: tn The translation assumes that вАП„Щ÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь÷ґ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (yashpilennah) goes with the preceding words вАЬan elevated town,вАЭ and that вАП„Щ÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (yashpilah) belongs with the following words, вАЬto the ground.вАЭ See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:469, n. 7.
n230269: sn The literary structure of chap. 26 is not entirely clear. The chapter begins with an eschatological song of praise and ends with a lament and prophetic response (vv. 16-21). It is not certain where the song of praise ends or how vv. 7-15 fit into the structure. Verses 10-11a seem to lament the presence of evil and v. 11b anticipates the arrival of judgment, so it is possible that vv. 7-15 are a prelude to the lament and announcement that conclude the chapter.
n2302610:
tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬupright, the path of the righteous you make level.вАЭ There are three possible ways to translate this line. Some take вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО (yashar) as a divine title: вАЬO Upright OneвАЭ (cf. NASB, NIV, NKJV, NRSV, NLT). Others regard вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО as the result of dittography (вАП„Ю÷µ„Щ„©„Б÷Є„®÷і„Щ„Э „Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„® „Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„Т÷Љ„ЬвАО, mesharim yashar ma њgal) and do not include it in the translation. Another possibility is to keep вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО and render the line as вАЬthe path of the righteous that you prepare is straight.вАЭ
sn The metaphor of a level/smooth road/path may refer to their morally upright manner of life (see v. 8a), but verse 7b, which attributes the smooth path to the Lord, suggests that the LordвАЩs vindication and blessing may be the reality behind the metaphor here.
n2302611: tn The Hebrew text has, вАЬyes, the way of your judgments.вАЭ The translation assumes that вАЬwayвАЭ is related to the verb вАЬwe waitвАЭ as an adverbial accusative (вАЬin the way of your judgments we waitвАЭ). вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„Ш÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (mishpatekha, вАЬyour judgmentsвАЭ) could refer to the LordвАЩs commandments, in which case one might translate, вАЬas we obey your commands.вАЭ However, in verse 9 the same form refers to divine acts of judgment on evildoers.
n2302612: tn Heb вАЬyour name and your remembrance [are] the desire of [our?] being.вАЭ
n2302613: tn Heb вАЬwith my soul I.вАЭ This is a figure for the speaker himself (вАЬIвАЭ).
n2302614: tn Or вАЬlong for, desire.вАЭ The speaker acknowledges that he is eager to see God come in judgment (see vv. 8, 9b).
n2302615: tn The translation understands вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО (tsedeq) in the sense of вАЬjustice,вАЭ but it is possible that it carries the nuance вАЬrighteousness,вАЭ in which case one might translate, вАЬthose who live in the world learn to live in a righteous mannerвАЭ (cf. NCV).
n2302616: tn As in verse 9b, the translation understands вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО (tsedeq) in the sense of вАЬjustice,вАЭ but it is possible that it carries the nuance вАЬrighteousness,вАЭ in which case one might translate, вАЬthey do not learn to live in a righteous manner.вАЭ
n2302617: tn Heb вАЬin a land of uprightness they act unjustlyвАЭ; NRSV вАЬthey deal perversely.вАЭ
n2302618: tn Heb вАЬO Lord, your hand is lifted up.вАЭ
n2302619: tn Heb вАЬThey will see and be ashamed of zeal of people.вАЭ Some take the prefixed verbs as jussives and translate the statement as a prayer, вАЬLet them see and be put to shame.вАЭ The meaning of the phrase вАП„І÷і„†÷∞„Р÷Ј„™ „Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО (qin Њat  њam, вАЬzeal of peopleвАЭ) is unclear. The translation assumes that this refers to GodвАЩs angry judgment upon people. Another option is to understand the phrase as referring to GodвАЩs zealous, protective love of his covenant people. In this case one might translate, вАЬby your zealous devotion to your people.вАЭ
n2302620: tn Heb вАЬyes, fire, your enemies, will consume them.вАЭ Many understand the prefixed verb form to be jussive and translate, вАЬlet [fire] consumeвАЭ (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV). The mem suffixed to the verb may be enclitic; if a pronominal suffix, it refers back to вАЬyour enemies.вАЭ
n2302621: tn Heb вАЬO Lord, you establish peace for us.вАЭ
n2302622: tc Some suggest emending вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„Э „Ы÷Љ÷Є„ЬвАО (gam kol, вАЬeven allвАЭ) to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Т÷∞„Ю÷ї„ЬвАО (kigmul, вАЬaccording to the deed[s] ofвАЭ) One might then translate вАЬfor according to what our deeds deserve, you have acted on our behalf.вАЭ Nevertheless, accepting the MT as it stands, the prophet affirms that Yahweh deserved all the credit for anything Israel had accomplished.
n2302623: sn In light of what is said in verse 14b, the вАЬdeadвАЭ here may be the вАЬmastersвАЭ mentioned in verse 13.
n2302624: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ь÷Є„Ы÷µ„ЯвАО (lakhen) normally indicates a cause-effect relationship between what precedes and follows and is translated, вАЬtherefore.вАЭ Here, however, it infers the cause from the effect and brings out what is implicit in the previous statement. See BDB 487 s.v.
n2302625: tn Heb вАЬvisited [for harm]вАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV); NAB, NRSV вАЬyou have punished.вАЭ
n2302626: tn Heb вАЬyou have added to the nation.вАЭ The last line of the verse suggests that geographical expansion is in view. вАЬThe nationвАЭ is Judah.
n2302628: tn The meaning of this verse is unclear. It appears to read literally, вАЬO Lord, in distress they visit you, they pour out [?] an incantation, your discipline to them.вАЭ вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) may here carry the sense of вАЬseek with interestвАЭ (cf. Ezek 23:21 and BDB 823 s.v.) or вАЬseek in vainвАЭ (cf. Isa 34:16), but it is peculiar for the Lord to be the object of this verb. вАП„¶÷Є„І„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (tsaqun) may be a Qal perfect third plural form from вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„ІвАО (tsuq, вАЬpour out, meltвАЭ), though the verb is not used of pouring out words in its two other occurrences. Because of the appearance of вАП„¶÷Ј„®вАО (tsar, вАЬdistressвАЭ) in the preceding line, it is tempting to emend the form to a noun and derive it from вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„ІвАО (вАЬbe in distressвАЭ) The term вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„©„БвАО (lakhash) elsewhere refers to an incantation (Isa 3:3; Jer 8:17; Eccl 10:11) or amulet (Isa 3:20). Perhaps here it refers to ritualistic prayers or to magical incantations used to ward off evil.
n2302629: tn On the use of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷євАО (kemo, вАЬlike, asвАЭ) here, see BDB 455 s.v. IsraelвАЩs distress and suffering, likened here to the pains of childbirth, seemed to be for no purpose. A woman in labor endures pain with the hope that a child will be born; in IsraelвАЩs case no such positive outcome was apparent. The nation was like a woman who strains to bring forth a child but cannot push the baby through to daylight. All her effort produces nothing.
n2302630: tn Heb вАЬand the inhabitants of the world do not fall.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Є„§÷Ј„ЬвАО (nafal) apparently means here, вАЬbe born,вАЭ though the Qal form of the verb is not used with this nuance anywhere else in the OT. (The Hiphil appears to be used in the sense of вАЬgive birthвАЭ in v. 19, however.) The implication of verse 18b seems to be that Israel hoped its suffering would somehow end in deliverance and an increase in population. The phrase вАЬinhabitants of the worldвАЭ seems to refer to the human race in general, but the next verse, which focuses on IsraelвАЩs dead, suggests the referent may be more limited.
n2302631: sn At this point the Lord (or prophet) gives the people an encouraging oracle.
n2302632: tn Heb вАЬdustвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2302633: tn Heb вАЬfor the dew of lights [is] your dew.вАЭ The pronominal suffix on вАЬdewвАЭ is masculine singular, like the suffixes on вАЬyour deadвАЭ and вАЬyour corpsesвАЭ in the first half of the verse. The statement, then, is addressed to collective Israel, the speaker in verse 18. The plural form вАП„Р„Х÷є„®÷є„™вАО ( Њorot) is probably a plural of respect or magnitude, meaning вАЬbright lightвАЭ (i.e., morningвАЩs light). Dew is a symbol of fertility and life. Here IsraelвАЩs вАЬdew,вАЭ as it were, will soak the dust of the ground and cause the corpses of the dead to spring up to new life, like plants sprouting up from well-watered soil.
n2302634: sn It is not certain whether the resurrection envisioned here is intended to be literal or figurative. A comparison with 25:8 and Dan 12:2 suggests a literal interpretation, but Ezek 37:1-14 uses resurrection as a metaphor for deliverance from exile and the restoration of the nation (see Isa 27:12-13).
n2302636: tn Heb вАЬout of his placeвАЭ (so KJV, ASV).
n2302637: sn This implies that rampant bloodshed is one of the reasons for divine judgment. See the note at 24:5.
n230272: tn Heb вАЬhard, severeвАЭ; cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬcruelвАЭ; KJV вАЬsoreвАЭ; NLT вАЬterrible.вАЭ
n230273: tn Heb вАЬfleeingвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV). Some translate вАЬslipperyвАЭ or вАЬslithering.вАЭ See the same Hebrew phrase in Job 26:13.
n230274:
tn The description of Leviathan should be compared with the following excerpts from Ugaritic mythological texts: (1) вАЬWas not the dragon (Ugaritic tnn, cognate with Hebrew вАП„™÷Ј„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО [tannin, translated вАЬsea monsterвАЭ here]) vanquished and captured? I did destroy the wriggling (Ugaritic  њqltn, cognate to Hebrew вАП„Ґ÷≤„І÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷Є„™„Х÷є„ЯвАО [ њaqallaton, translated вАЬsquirmingвАЭ here]) serpent, the tyrant with seven heads (cf. Ps 74:14).вАЭ (See CTA 3 iii 38-39.) (2) вАЬfor all that you smote Leviathan the slippery (Ugaritic brh, cognate to Hebrew вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷і„Ч÷ЈвАО [bariakh, translated вАЬfast-movingвАЭ here]) serpent, [and] made an end of the wriggling serpent, the tyrant with seven headsвАЭ (See CTA 5 i 1-3.)
sn In the Ugaritic mythological texts Leviathan is a sea creature that symbolizes the destructive water of the sea and in turn the forces of chaos that threaten the established order. Isaiah here applies imagery from Canaanite mythology to YahwehвАЩs eschatological victory over his enemies. Elsewhere in the OT, the battle with the sea motif is applied to YahwehвАЩs victories over the forces of chaos at creation and in history (cf. Pss 74:13-14; 77:16-20; 89:9-10; Isa 51:9-10). YahwehвАЩs subjugation of the chaos waters is related to His kingship (cf. Pss 29:3, 10; 93:3-4). Apocalyptic literature employs the imagery as well. The beasts of Dan 7 emerge from the sea, while Rev 13 speaks of a seven-headed beast coming from the sea.
n230276: tn Heb вАЬvineyard of delight,вАЭ or вАЬvineyard of beauty.вАЭ Many medieval mss read вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Э „Ч÷ґ„Ю÷ґ„®вАО (kerem khemer, вАЬvineyard of wineвАЭ), i.e., вАЬa productive vineyard.вАЭ
n230277: tn Heb вАЬher.вАЭ Apparently вАЬvineyardвАЭ is the antecedent, though normally this noun is understood as masculine (see Lev 25:3, however).
n230279: tn Heb вАЬlest [someone] visit [harm] upon it, night and day I guard it.вАЭ
n2302710: tn Heb вАЬit.вАЭ The feminine singular suffix apparently refers back to the expression вАЬthorns and briers,вАЭ understood in a collective sense. For other examples of a cohortative expressing resolve after a hypothetical statement introduced by вАП„Ю÷і„ЩвАО with вАП„†÷Є„™÷Є„ЯвАО (mi with natan), see Judg 9:29; Jer 9:1-2; Ps 55:6.
n2302711: tn Heb вАЬit.вАЭ The feminine singular suffix apparently refers back to the expression вАЬthorns and briers,вАЭ understood in a collective sense.
n2302712: tn Heb вАЬor let him take hold of my refuge.вАЭ The subject of the third masculine singular verb form is uncertain. Apparently the symbolic вАЬthorns and briersвАЭ are in view, though in v. 4b a feminine singular pronoun was used to refer to them.
n2302713: tc The Hebrew text has, вАЬhe makes peace with me; peace he makes with me.вАЭ Some contend that two alternative readings are preserved here and one should be deleted. The first has the object вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom, вАЬpeaceвАЭ) preceding the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„В÷Є„ФвАО ( њasah, вАЬmakeвАЭ); the second reverses the order. Another option is to retain both statements, although repetitive, to emphasize the need to make peace with Yahweh.
n2302714: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬthe coming ones, let Jacob take root.вАЭ вАП„Ф÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (habba Њim, вАЬthe coming onesвАЭ) should probably be emended to вАП„Щ÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„Э „С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (yamim ba Њim, вАЬdays [are] comingвАЭ) or вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„Э „Ф÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (biyamim habba Њim, вАЬin the coming daysвАЭ).
n2302715: tn Heb вАЬfruitвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2302716: sn This apparently refers to a future population explosion. See 26:18.
n2302717: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬLike the striking down of the one striking him down does he strike him down?вАЭ The meaning of the text is unclear, but this may be a rhetorical question, suggesting that Israel has not experienced divine judgment to the same degree as her oppressors. In this case вАЬthe one strikingвА¶downвАЭ refers to IsraelвАЩs oppressors, while the pronoun вАЬhimвАЭ refers to Israel. The subject of the final verb (вАЬdoes he strikeвА¶downвАЭ) would then be God, while the pronoun вАЬhimвАЭ would again refer to Israel.
n2302718: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬOr like the killing of his killed ones is he killed?вАЭ If one accepts the interpretation of the parallel line outlined in the previous note, then this line too would contain a rhetorical question suggesting that Israel has not experienced destruction to the same degree as its enemies. In this case вАЬhis killed onesвАЭ refer to the ones who struck Israel down, and Israel would be the subject of the final verb (вАЬis he killedвАЭ).
n2302719: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬin [?], in sending her away, you oppose her.вАЭ The meaning of this line is uncertain. The form вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„°÷Ј„Р„°÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (besasse Њah) is taken as an infinitive from вАП„°÷Ј„Р„°÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (sasse Њah) with a prepositional prefix and a third feminine singular suffix. (The MT does not have a mappiq in the final he [вАП„ФвАО], however). According to HALOT 738 s.v. вАП„°÷Ј„Р„°÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО the verb is a Palpel form from an otherwise unattested root cognate with an Arabic verb meaning вАЬto gather beasts with a call.вАЭ Perhaps it means вАЬto call, summonвАЭ here, but this is a very tentative proposal. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ч÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (beshalekhah, вАЬin sending her awayвАЭ) appears to be a Piel infinitive with a prepositional prefix and a third feminine singular suffix. Since the Piel of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Є„ЧвАО (shalakh) can sometimes mean вАЬdivorceвАЭ (HALOT 1514-15 s.v.) and the following verb вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv, вАЬopposeвАЭ) can be used in legal contexts, it is possible that divorce proceedings are alluded to here. This may explain why Israel is referred to as feminine in this verse, in contrast to the masculine forms used in vv. 6-7 and 9.
n2302720: tn The Hebrew text has no object expressed, but one can understand a third feminine singular pronominal object and place a mappiq in the final he (вАП„ФвАО) of the form to indicate the suffix.
n2302721: sn The вАЬeast windвАЭ here symbolizes violent divine judgment.
n2302722: tn Or вАЬbe atoned forвАЭ (NIV); cf. NRSV вАЬbe expiated.вАЭ
n2302723: tn Heb вАЬand this [is] all the fruit of removing his sin.вАЭ The meaning of the statement is not entirely clear, though вАЬremoving his sinвАЭ certainly parallels вАЬJacobвАЩs sin will be removedвАЭ in the preceding line. If original, вАЬall the fruitвАЭ may refer to the result of the decision to remove sin, but the phrase may be a textual variation of an original вАП„Ь÷∞„Ы÷Ј„§÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (lekhapper, вАЬto atone forвАЭ), which in turn might be a gloss on вАП„Ф÷Є„°÷і„®вАО (hasir, вАЬremovingвАЭ).
n2302724: tn Heb вАЬwhen he makes the stones of an altar.вАЭ The singular вАЬaltarвАЭ is collective here; pagan altars are in view, as the last line of the verse indicates. See also 17:8.
n2302725: sn As interpreted and translated above, this verse says that Israel must totally repudiate its pagan religious practices in order to experience GodвАЩs forgiveness and restoration. Another option is to understand вАЬin this wayвАЭ and вАЬthisвАЭ in v. 9a as referring back to the judgment described in v. 8. In this case вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„§÷Ј„®вАО (kafar, вАЬatone forвАЭ) is used in a sarcastic sense; JacobвАЩs sin is вАЬatoned forвАЭ and removed through severe judgment. Following this line of interpretation, one might paraphrase the verse as follows: вАЬSo in this way (through judgment) JacobвАЩs sin will be вАЬatoned for,вАЭ and this is the way his sin will be removed, when he (i.e., God) makes all the altar stones like crushed limestoneвА¶.вАЭ This interpretation is more consistent with the tone of judgment in vv. 8 and 10-11.
n2302726: sn The identity of this city is uncertain. The context suggests that an Israelite city, perhaps Samaria or Jerusalem, is in view. For discussions of interpretive options see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:496-97, and Paul L. Redditt, вАЬOnce Again, the City in Isaiah 24-27, вАЭ HAR 10 (1986), 332.
n2302727: tn The singular form in the text is probably collective.
n2302728: tn Heb вАЬand destroy her branches.вАЭ The city is the antecedent of the third feminine singular pronominal suffix. Apparently the city is here compared to a tree. See also v. 11.
n2302730: tn Heb вАЬwomen come [and] light it.вАЭ The city is likened to a dead tree with dried-up branches that is only good for firewood.
n2302731: tn Heb вАЬfor not a people of understanding [is] he.вАЭ
n2302732: tn Heb вАЬand it will be in that day.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302733: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord will beat out.вАЭ The verb is used of beating seeds or grain to separate the husk from the kernel (see Judg 6:11; Ruth 2:17; Isa 28:27), and of beating the olives off the olive tree (Deut 24:20). The latter metaphor may be in view here, where a tree metaphor has been employed in the preceding verses. See also 17:6.
n2302734: tn Heb вАЬthe river,вАЭ a frequent designation in the OT for the Euphrates. For clarity most modern English versions substitute the name вАЬEuphratesвАЭ for вАЬthe riverвАЭ here.
n2302735: sn The Israelites will be freed from exile (likened to beating the olives off the tree) and then gathered (likened to collecting the olives).
n2302736: tn Heb вАЬand it will be in that day.вАЭ The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2302737: tn Traditionally, вАЬgreatвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NLT); CEV вАЬloud.вАЭ
n230281: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] the crown [or вАЬwreathвАЭ] of the splendor [or вАЬprideвАЭ] of the drunkards of Ephraim.вАЭ The вАЬcrownвАЭ is Samaria, the capital city of the northern kingdom (Ephraim). Priests and prophets are included among these drunkards in v. 7.
n230282: tn Heb вАЬthe beauty of his splendor.вАЭ In the translation the masculine pronoun (вАЬhisвАЭ) has been replaced by вАЬitsвАЭ because the referent (the вАЬcrownвАЭ) is the city of Samaria.
n230284: tn Heb вАЬones overcome with wine.вАЭ The words вАЬthe crown ofвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The syntactical relationship of the final phrase to what precedes is uncertain. вАП„Ф÷≤„Ь„Х÷Љ„Ю÷µ„Щ „Щ÷Є„Щ÷і„ЯвАО (halume yayin, вАЬones overcome with wineвАЭ) seems to correspond to вАП„©„Б÷і„Ы÷Љ÷є„®÷µ„Щ „Р÷ґ„§÷∞„®÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shikkore  Њefrayim, вАЬdrunkards of EphraimвАЭ) in line 1. The translation assumes that the phrase вАЬthe splendid crownвАЭ is to be understood in the final line as well.
n230285: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in vv. 16, 22 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n230286: tn Heb вАЬLook, a strong and powerful [one] belongs to the Lord.вАЭ
n230287: tn Heb вАЬlike a rainstorm of hail, a wind of destruction.вАЭ
n230288: tn Heb вАЬlike a rainstorm of mighty, overflowing waters.вАЭ
n230289: tn The words вАЬthat crownвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The object of the verb is unexpressed in the Hebrew text.
n2302811: tn Heb вАЬwhich the one seeing sees, while still it is in his hand he swallows it.вАЭ
n2302813: tn Heb вАЬand [he will become] a spirit of justice for the one who sits [i.e., presides] over judgment, // and strength [for] the ones who turn back battle at the city gate.вАЭ The Lord will provide internal stability and national security.
n2302814: tn Heb вАЬthese.вАЭ The demonstrative pronoun anticipates вАЬpriests and prophetsвАЭ two lines later.
n2302815: tn According to HALOT 135 s.v. III вАП„С„Ь„ҐвАО, the verb form is derived from вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (bala њ, вАЬconfuseвАЭ), not the more common вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (вАЬswallowвАЭ). See earlier notes at 3:12 and 9:16.
n2302818: tn Heb вАЬfilth, without a place.вАЭ The Hebrew phrase вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ „Ю÷Є„І„Х÷є„ЭвАО (beli maqom, вАЬwithout a place,вАЭ see HALOT 133 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО) probably means there is no (clean) space on the table, since it is covered with filth. The translation follows the line division of the MT. Some translations (NASB, ESV, NRSV) move вАЬfilthвАЭ to the previous line as вАЬfilthy vomit,вАЭ but the Hebrew lines are no longer balanced.
n2302819: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2302820: tn Heb вАЬWho is he teaching knowledge? For whom is he explaining a message?вАЭ The translation assumes that the Lord is the subject of the verbs вАЬteachingвАЭ and вАЬexplaining,вАЭ and that the prophet is asking the questions. See v. 12. According to some vv. 9-10 record the peopleвАЩs sarcastic response to the LordвАЩs message through Isaiah.
n2302821: tn Heb вАЬfrom the breasts.вАЭ The words вАЬtheir motherвАЩsвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The translation assumes that this is the prophetвАЩs answer to the questions asked in the first half of the verse. The Lord is trying to instruct people who are вАЬinfantsвАЭ morally and ethically.
n2302822: tn The meaning of this verse has been debated. The final line says вАЬa little there, a little there,вАЭ while the preceding lines have a series of redundancies (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ „¶÷Ј„Х „Ь÷Є„¶÷Є„Х „¶÷Ј„Х „Ь÷Є„¶÷Є„Х „І÷Ј„Х „Ь÷Є„І÷Є„Х „І÷Ј„Х „Ь÷Є„І÷Є„ХвАО, ki tsav latsav, tsav latsav, qav laqav, qav laqav). The present translation assumes that the repetitive syllables are gibberish that resembles baby talk (cf v. 9b) and mimics what the people will hear when foreign invaders conquer the land (v. 11). In this case вАП„Ц÷∞„Ґ÷µ„Щ„®вАО (ze њer, вАЬa littleвАЭ) refers to the short syllabic structure of the babbling (cf. CEV, REB and see HALOT 1010 s.v. вАП„¶÷Ј„ХвАО). Some take вАП„¶÷Ј„ХвАО (tsav) as a derivative of вАП„¶÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (tsavah, вАЬcommandвАЭ) and translate the first part of the statement as вАЬcommand after command, command after command.вАЭ Proponents of this position (followed by many English versions) also take вАП„І÷Ј„ХвАО (qav) as a noun meaning вАЬmeasuring lineвАЭ (see v. 17), understood here in the abstract sense of вАЬstandardвАЭ or вАЬrule.вАЭ
n2302823: sn This verse alludes to the coming Assyrian invasion, when the people will hear a foreign language that sounds like gibberish to them. The Lord is the subject of the verb вАЬwill speak,вАЭ as v. 12 makes clear. He once spoke in meaningful terms, but in the coming judgment he will speak to them, as it were, through the mouth of foreign oppressors. The apparent gibberish they hear will be an outward reminder that God has decreed their defeat.
n2302825: sn This message encapsulates the LordвАЩs invitation to his people to find security in his protection and blessing.
n2302826: tn Heb вАЬAnd the message of the Lord will be to them, вАШtsav latsav,вАЩ etc.вАЭ See the note at v. 10. In this case the вАЬLordвАЩs messageвАЭ is not the foreignerвАЩs strange sounding words (as in v. 10), but the LordвАЩs repeated appeals to them (like the one quoted in v. 12). As time goes on, the LordвАЩs appeals through the prophets will have no impact on the people; they will regard prophetic preaching as gibberish.
n2302827: tn Heb вАЬas a result they will go and stumble backward.вАЭ Perhaps an infant falling as it attempts to learn to walk is the background image here (cf. v. 9b). The Hebrew term вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (lema њan) could be taken as indicating purpose (вАЬin order thatвАЭ), rather than simple result. In this case the peopleвАЩs insensitivity to the message is caused by the Lord as a means of expediting their downfall.
n2302828: sn When divine warnings and appeals become gibberish to the spiritually insensitive, they have no guidance and are doomed to destruction.
n2302830: sn Sheol is the underworld, land of the dead, according to the OT world view.
n2302831: tn Elsewhere the noun вАП„Ч÷є„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО (khozeh) refers to a prophet who sees visions. In v. 18 the related term вАП„Ч÷Є„Ц„Х÷Љ„™вАО (khazut, вАЬvisionвАЭ) is used. The parallelism in both verses (note вАЬtreatyвАЭ) seems to demand a meaning вАЬagreementвАЭ for both nouns. Perhaps вАП„Ч÷є„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО and вАП„Ч„Ц„Х÷Љ„™вАО are used in a metonymic sense in vv. 15 and 18. Another option is to propose a homonymic root. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:514, and HALOT 301 s.v. II вАП„Ч÷є„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО.
n2302832: tn Heb вАЬthe overwhelming scourge, when it passes byвАЭ (NRSV similar).
n2302833: sn вАЬLieвАЭ and вАЬdeceitful wordвАЭ would not be the terms used by the people. They would likely use the words вАЬpromiseвАЭ and вАЬreliable word,вАЭ but the prophet substitutes вАЬlieвАЭ and вАЬdeceitful wordвАЭ to emphasize that this treaty with death will really prove to be disappointing.
n2302834: tc The Hebrew text has a third person verb form, which does not agree with the first person suffix that precedes. The form should be emended to вАП„Щ÷є„°÷µ„УвАО (yosed), a Qal active participle used in a present progressive or imminent future sense.
n2302835: tn Traditionally вАЬtested,вАЭ but the implication is that it has passed the test and stands approved.
n2302836: sn The reality behind the metaphor is not entirely clear from the context. The stone appears to represent someone or something that gives Zion stability. Perhaps the ideal Davidic ruler is in view (see 32:1). Another option is that the image of beginning a building project by laying a precious cornerstone suggests that God is about to transform Zion through judgment and begin a new covenant community that will experience his protection (see 4:3-6; 31:5; 33:20-24; 35:10).
n2302837: tn Heb вАЬwill not hurry,вАЭ i.e., act in panic.
n2302839: tn On the meaning of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„§÷Ј„®вАО (kafar) in this context, see HALOT 494 s.v. I вАП„Ы„§„®вАО and J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:515, n. 9.
n2302840: tn Normally the noun вАП„Ч÷Є„Ц„Х÷Љ„™вАО (khazut) means вАЬvision.вАЭ See the note at v. 15.
n2302843: tn Heb вАЬyou will become a trampling place for it.вАЭ
n2302845: tn The words вАЬit will come throughвАЭ are supplied in the translation. The verb вАЬwill sweep byвАЭ does double duty in the parallel structure.
n2302846: sn The bed and blanket probably symbolize their false sense of security. A bed that is too short and a blanket that is too narrow may promise rest and protection from the cold, but in the end they are useless and disappointing. In the same way, their supposed treaty with death will prove useless and disappointing.
n2302847: sn This probably alludes to DavidвАЩs victory over the Philistines at Baal Perazim. See 2 Sam 5:20.
n2302848: sn This probably alludes to the LordвАЩs victory over the Canaanites at Gibeon, during the days of Joshua. See Josh 10:10-11.
n2302849: sn GodвАЩs judgment of his own people is called вАЬhis peculiar workвАЭ and вАЬhis strange task,вАЭ because he must deal with them the way he treated their enemies in the past.
n2302850: tn Or вАЬthe whole earthвАЭ (KJV, ASV, NAB, NCV).
n2302852: tn Heb вАЬto my wordвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬhear my speech.вАЭ
n2302853: tn Heb вАЬAll the day does the plowman plow in order to plant?вАЭ The phrase вАЬall the dayвАЭ here has the sense of вАЬcontinually, always.вАЭ See BDB 400 s.v. вАП„Щ„Х÷є„ЭвАО.
n2302854: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬplace wheat [?], and barley [?], and grain in its territory.вАЭ The term вАП„©„В„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (sorah) is sometimes translated вАЬ[in] its place,вАЭ but the word is unattested in the MT elsewhere. It is probably due to dittography of the immediately following вАП„©„В÷∞„Ґ÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (seo њrah, вАЬbarleyвАЭ). The meaning of вАП„†÷і„°÷∞„Ю÷Є„ЯвАО (nisman) is also uncertain. It may be due to dittography of the immediately following вАП„Ы÷ї„°÷Љ÷ґ„Ю÷ґ„™вАО (kussemet, вАЬgrainвАЭ).
n2302855: tn Heb вАЬhe teaches him the proper way; his God instructs him.вАЭ
n2302857: sn Both of these seeds are too small to use the ordinary threshing techniques.
n2302858: sn A flail was a hand-held threshing tool that had one stick as its handle and another swinging stick attached to its top. The swinging stick was used to beat the grain off of the stalks, which were laying on the ground.
n2302859: sn Verses 23-29 emphasize that God possesses great wisdom and has established a natural order. Evidence of this can be seen in the way farmers utilize divinely imparted wisdom to grow and harvest crops. GodвАЩs dealings with his people will exhibit this same kind of wisdom and order. Judgment will be accomplished according to a divinely ordered timetable and, while severe enough, will not be excessive. Judgment must come, just as planting inevitably follows plowing. God will, as it were, thresh his people, but he will not crush them to the point where they will be of no use to him.
n230291: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] Ariel.вАЭ The meaning of the name вАЬArielвАЭ is uncertain. The name may mean вАЬaltar hearthвАЭ (see v. 2) or, if compound, вАЬlion of God.вАЭ The name is used here as a title for Mount Zion/Jerusalem (see vv. 7-8).
n230292: tn Heb вАЬthe town where David camped.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО (khanah, вАЬcampвАЭ) probably has the nuance вАЬlay siege toвАЭ here. See v. 3. Another option is to take the verb in the sense of вАЬlived, settled.вАЭ
n230293: tn Heb вАЬAdd year to year; let your festivals occur in cycles.вАЭ This is probably a sarcastic exhortation to the people to keep up their religious rituals, which will not prevent the coming judgment. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:527.
n230294: tn The term вАП„Р÷≤„®÷і„Щ„Р÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њari Њel, вАЬArielвАЭ) is the word translated вАЬaltar hearthвАЭ here. The point of the simile is not entirely clear. Perhaps the image likens JerusalemвАЩs coming crisis to a sacrificial fire.
n230295: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Ы÷Ј„У÷Љ„Х÷Љ„®вАО (khaddur, вАЬlike a circleвАЭ), i.e., вАЬlike an encircling wall.вАЭ Some emend this phrase to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„У÷Є„Х÷і„УвАО (kedavid, вАЬlike DavidвАЭ), which is supported by the LXX (see v. 1). However, the rendering in the LXX could have arisen from a confusion of the dalet (вАП„УвАО) and resh (вАП„®вАО).
n230296: tn The meaning of вАП„Ю÷ї„¶÷Љ÷Є„СвАО (mutsav) is not certain. Because of the parallelism (note вАЬsiege worksвАЭ), some translate вАЬtowers.вАЭ The noun is derived from вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„СвАО (natsav, вАЬtake oneвАЩs standвАЭ) and may refer to the troops stationed outside the city to prevent entrance or departure.
n230298: tn Heb вАЬand from the dust your word will be low.вАЭ
n230299: tn Heb вАЬand your voice will be like a ritual pit from the earth.вАЭ The Hebrew вАП„Р„Х÷є„СвАО ( Њov, вАЬritual pitвАЭ) refers to a pit used by a magician to conjure up underworld spirits. See the note on вАЬincantationsвАЭ in 8:19. Here the word is used metonymically for the voice that emerges from such a pit.
n2302910: tn Heb вАЬand from the dust your word will chirp.вАЭ The words вАЬas if muttering an incantationвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. See the parallelism and 8:19.
n2302911: tn Or вАЬviolent menвАЭ; cf. NASB вАЬthe ruthless ones.вАЭ
n2302912: tn Heb вАЬfrom the Lord of HeavenвАЩs Armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] there will be visitation.вАЭ The third feminine singular passive verb form вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷µ„УвАО (tippaqed, вАЬshe/it will be visitedвАЭ) is used here in an impersonal sense. See GKC 459 ¬І144.b.
n2302913: tn Or вАЬthat he [or вАЬhis appetiteвАЭ] is unsatisfied.вАЭ
n2302914: tn Or вАЬthat he is faint and that he [or вАЬhis appetiteвАЭ] longs [for water].вАЭ
n2302915: tn The form вАП„Ф÷і„™÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ф÷∞„Ю÷∞„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (hitmahmehu) is a Hitpalpel imperative from вАП„Ю÷Є„Ф÷Ј„Ф÷ЉвАО (mahah, вАЬhesitateвАЭ). If it is retained, one might translate вАЬhalt and be amazed.вАЭ The translation assumes an emendation to вАП„Ф÷і„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷∞„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (hittammehu), a Hitpael imperative from вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ФвАО (tamah, вАЬbe amazedвАЭ). In this case, the text, like Hab 1:5, combines the Hitpael and Qal imperatival forms of вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ФвАО (tamah). A literal translation might be вАЬShock yourselves and be shocked!вАЭ The repetition of sound draws attention to the statement. The imperatives here have the force of an emphatic assertion. On this use of the imperative in Hebrew, see GKC 324 ¬І110.c and IBHS 572 ¬І34.4c.
n2302916: tn Heb вАЬBlind yourselves and be blind!вАЭ The Hitpalpel and Qal imperatival forms of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (sha њa њ, вАЬbe blindвАЭ) are combined to draw attention to the statement. The imperatives have the force of an emphatic assertion.
n2302917: tc Some prefer to emend the last two verbs from their perfect form to an imperative (e.g., NAB, NCV, NRSV), since the people are addressed in the immediately preceding and following contexts.
n2302918: tn Heb вАЬa disposition [or вАЬspiritвАЭ] of deep sleep.вАЭ Through this mixed metaphor (sleep is likened to a liquid which one pours and in turn symbolizes spiritual dullness) the prophet emphasizes that God himself has given the people over to their spiritual insensitivity as a form of judgment.
n2302921: tn Heb вАЬand if the scroll is handed to one who does not know a scroll.вАЭ
n2302922: tn Heb вАЬI do not know a scroll.вАЭ
n2302923: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2302924: tn Heb вАЬBecause these people draw near to me with their mouth.вАЭ
n2302925: tn Heb вАЬand with their lips they honor me.вАЭ
n2302926: tn Heb вАЬbut their heart is far from me.вАЭ The heart is viewed here as the seat of the will, from which genuine loyalty derives.
n2302927: tn Heb вАЬtheir fear of me is a commandment of men that has been taught.вАЭ
n2302928: tn Heb вАЬTherefore I will again do something amazing with these people, an amazing deed, an amazing thing.вАЭ This probably refers to the amazing transformation predicted in vv. 17-24, which will follow the purifying judgment implied in vv. 15-16.
n2302929: tn Heb вАЬthe wisdom of their wise ones will perish, the discernment of their discerning ones will keep hidden.вАЭ
n2302930: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] those who deeply hide counsel from the Lord.вАЭ This probably alludes to political alliances made without seeking the LordвАЩs guidance. See 30:1-2 and 31:1.
n2302931: tn Heb вАЬand their works are in darkness, and they say.вАЭ
n2302932: tn The rhetorical questions suggest the answer: вАЬNo one.вАЭ They are confident that their deeds are hidden from others, including God.
n2302933: tn Heb вАЬyour overturning.вАЭ The predicate is suppressed in this exclamation. The idea is, вАЬO your perversity! How great it is!вАЭ See GKC 470 ¬І147.c. The people вАЬoverturnвАЭ all logic by thinking their authority supersedes GodвАЩs.
n2302934: tn The expected answer to this rhetorical question is: вАЬOf course not.вАЭ On the interrogative use of вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim), see BDB 50 s.v.
n2302935: tn Heb вАЬthat the thing made should say.вАЭ
n2302936: tn The Hebrew text phrases this as a rhetorical question, вАЬIs it not yet a little, a short [time]?вАЭ
n2302937: sn The meaning of this verse is debated, but it seems to depict a reversal in fortunes. The mighty forest of Lebanon (symbolic of the proud and powerful; see 2:13; 10:34) will be changed into a common orchard, while the common orchard (symbolic of the oppressed and lowly) will grow into a great forest. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:538.
n2302939:
tn Heb вАЬand out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see.вАЭ
sn Perhaps this depicts the spiritual transformation of the once spiritually insensitive nation (see vv. 10-12, cf. also 6:9-10).
n2302941: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2302942: tn Heb вАЬand all the watchers of wrong will be cut off.вАЭ
n2302943: tn Heb вАЬthe ones who make a man a sinner with a word.вАЭ The Hiphil of вАП„Ч÷Є„Ш÷Є„РвАО (khata Њ) here has a delocutive sense: вАЬdeclare a man sinful/guilty.вАЭ
n2302944: sn Legal disputes were resolved at the city gate, where the town elders met. See Amos 5:10.
n2302945: tn Heb вАЬand deprive by emptiness the innocent.вАЭ
n2302946: tn Heb вАЬSo this is what the Lord has said to the house of Jacob, the one who ransomed Abraham.вАЭ The relative pronoun must refer back to вАЬthe Lord.вАЭ It is uncertain to what event in AbrahamвАЩs experience this refers. Perhaps the name вАЬAbrahamвАЭ stands here by metonymy for his descendants through Jacob. If so, the Exodus is in view.
n2302947: tn Heb вАЬand his face will no longer be pale.вАЭ
n2302948: tn Heb вАЬfor when he sees his children, the work of my hands in his midst.вАЭ
n2302949: tn Or вАЬtreat as holyвАЭ (also in the following line); NASB, NRSV вАЬwill sanctify.вАЭ
n2302950: sn Holy One of Jacob is similar to the phrase вАЬHoly One of IsraelвАЭ common throughout Isaiah; see the sn at Isa 1:4.
n2302951: tn Or вАЬfear,вАЭ in the sense of вАЬstand in awe of.вАЭ
n2302952: tn Heb вАЬand the ones who stray in spirit will know understanding.вАЭ
n2302953: tn Heb вАЬwill learn instructionвАЭ; cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT вАЬwill accept instruction.вАЭ
n230303: tn Heb вАЬmaking a plan, but not from me.вАЭ
n230304: tn Heb вАЬand pouring out a libation, but not [from] my spirit.вАЭ This translation assumes that the verb вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (nasakh) means вАЬpour out,вАЭ and that the cognate noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„°÷Љ÷µ„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (massekhah) means вАЬlibation.вАЭ In this case вАЬpouring out a libationвАЭ alludes to a ceremony that formally ratifies an alliance. Another option is to understand the verb вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО as a homonym meaning вАЬweave,вАЭ and the cognate noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„°÷Љ÷µ„Ы÷Є„ФвАО as a homonym meaning вАЬcovering.вАЭ In this case forming an alliance is likened to weaving a garment.
n230306: tn Heb вАЬthose who go to descend to Egypt, but [of] my mouth they do not inquire.вАЭ
n230307: tn Heb вАЬto seek protection in the protection of Pharaoh, and to seek refuge in the shade of Egypt.вАЭ
n230308: sn This probably refers to JudahвАЩs officials and messengers.
n230309: sn Zoan was located in the Egyptian delta in the north; Hanes was located somewhere in southern region of lower Egypt, south of Memphis; the exact location is debated.
n2303010: tn The present translation follows the marginal (Qere) reading of the Hebrew text; the consonantal text (Kethib) has вАЬmade to stink, decay.вАЭ
n2303012: tc Heb вАЬ[a land of] a lioness and a lion, from them.вАЭ Some emend вАП„Ю÷µ„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО (mehem, вАЬfrom themвАЭ) to вАП„Ю÷µ„Ф÷µ„ЭвАО (mehem), an otherwise unattested Hiphil participle from вАП„Ф÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (hamam, вАЬmove noisilyвАЭ). Perhaps it would be better to take the initial mem (вАП„ЮвАО) as enclitic and emend the form to вАП„Ф÷є„Ю÷ґ„ФвАО (homeh), a Qal active participle from вАП„Ф÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (hamah, вАЬto make a noiseвАЭ); cf. J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:542, n. 9.
n2303013: tn Heb вАЬflying burning ones.вАЭ See the note at 14:29.
n2303014: sn This verse describes messengers from Judah transporting wealth to Egypt in order to buy PharaohвАЩs protection through a treaty.
n2303015: tn Heb вАЬAs for Egypt, with vanity and emptiness they help.вАЭ
n2303016: tn Heb вАЬRahabвАЭ (вАП„®÷Ј„Ф÷Ј„СвАО, rahav), which also appears as a name for Egypt in Ps 87:4. The epithet is also used in the OT for a mythical sea monster symbolic of chaos. See the note at 51:9. A number of English versions use the name вАЬRahabвАЭ (e.g., ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV) while others attempt some sort of translation (cf. CEV вАЬa helpless monsterвАЭ; TEV, NLT вАЬthe Harmless DragonвАЭ).
n2303017: tn The MT reads вАЬRahab, they, sitting.вАЭ The translation above assumes an emendation of вАП„Ф÷µ„Э „©„Б÷Є„С÷ґ„™вАО (hem shavet) to вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„™вАО (hammashbat), a Hophal participle with prefixed definite article, meaning вАЬthe one who is made to cease,вАЭ i.e., вАЬdestroyed,вАЭ or вАЬsilenced.вАЭ See HALOT 444-45 s.v. вАП„Щ„©„Б„СвАО.
n2303018: tn The referent of the third feminine singular pronominal suffix is uncertain. Perhaps it refers to the preceding message, which accuses the people of rejecting the LordвАЩs help in favor of an alliance with Egypt.
n2303019: tn Heb вАЬwith them.вАЭ On the use of the preposition here, see BDB 86 s.v. II вАП„Р÷µ„™вАО.
n2303020: sn Recording the message will enable the prophet to use it in the future as evidence that God warned his people of impending judgment and clearly spelled out the nationвАЩs guilt. An official record of the message will also serve as proof of the prophetвАЩs authority as GodвАЩs spokesman.
n2303021: tn Or perhaps, вАЬinstructionвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV); NCV, TEV вАЬteachings.вАЭ
n2303022: tn Heb вАЬwhoвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV). A new sentence was started here in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2303023: tn Heb вАЬDo not see for us right things.вАЭ
n2303024: tn Heb вАЬTell us smooth things, see deceptive things.вАЭ
n2303025: sn The imagery refers to the way or path of truth, as revealed by God to the prophet.
n2303026: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2303027: tn The sentence actually begins with the word вАЬbecause.вАЭ In the Hebrew text vv. 12-13 are one long sentence.
n2303028: tn Heb вАЬand you trust in oppression and cunning.вАЭ
n2303029: tn Heb вАЬand you lean on itвАЭ; NAB вАЬand depend on it.вАЭ
n2303030: tn The verse reads literally, вАЬSo this sin will become for you like a breach ready to fall, bulging on a high wall, the breaking of which comes suddenly, in a flash.вАЭ Their sin produces guilt and will result in judgment. Like a wall that collapses their fall will be swift and sudden.
n2303031: tn Heb вАЬIts shattering is like the shattering of a jug of [i.e., вАЬmade byвАЭ] potters, [so] shattered one cannot save [any of it].вАЭ
n2303032: tn The words вАЬlarge enoughвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2303033: tn Heb вАЬto remove fire from the place of kindling.вАЭ
n2303034: tn On the meaning of вАП„Т÷ґ„С÷ґ„РвАО (geveh, вАЬcisternвАЭ) see HALOT 170 s.v.
n2303035: tn Heb вАЬin returning and in quietness you will be delivered.вАЭ Many English versions render the last phrase вАЬshall be savedвАЭ or something similar (e.g., NAB, NASB, NRSV).
n2303036: tn Heb вАЬin quietness and in trust is your strengthвАЭ (NASB and NRSV both similar).
n2303037: tn Heb вАЬOne thousand from before [or вАЬbecause ofвАЭ] one battle cry.вАЭ вАП„Т÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (ge њarah) is often defined as вАЬthreat,вАЭ but in war contexts it likely refers to a shout or battle cry. See Ps 76:6.
n2303038: tn Heb вАЬfrom before [or вАЬbecause ofвАЭ] the battle cry of five you will flee.
n2303039: tn Heb вАЬuntil you are leftвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV).
n2303040: tn Heb вАЬTherefore the Lord waits to show you mercy, and therefore he is exalted to have compassion on you.вАЭ The logical connection between this verse and what precedes is problematic. The point seems to be that JudahвАЩs impending doom does not bring God joy. Rather the prospect of their suffering stirs within him a willingness to show mercy and compassion, if they are willing to seek him on his terms.
n2303041: tn Heb вАЬBlessed are all who wait for him.вАЭ
n2303043: tn Heb вАЬFor people in Zion will live; in Jerusalem, you will weep no more.вАЭ The phrase вАЬin JerusalemвАЭ could be taken with what precedes. Some prefer to emend вАП„Щ÷µ„©„Б÷µ„СвАО (yeshev, вАЬwill live,вАЭ a Qal imperfect) to вАП„Щ÷є„©„Б÷µ„СвАО (yoshev, a Qal active participle) and translate вАЬFor [you] people in Zion, who live in Jerusalem, you will weep no more.вАЭ
n2303044: tn Heb вАЬhe will indeed show you mercy at the sound of your crying out; when he hears, he will answer you.вАЭ
n2303045: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2303046: tn Heb вАЬand the Master will give to you breadвАФdistress, and waterвАФoppression.вАЭ
n2303047: tn Heb вАЬbut your teachers will no longer be hidden; your eyes will be seeing your teachers.вАЭ The translation assumes that the form вАП„Ю„Х÷є„®÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (morekha) is a plural participle, referring to spiritual leaders such as prophets and priests. Another possibility is that the form is actually singular (see GKC 273-74 ¬І93.ss) or a plural of respect, referring to God as the master teacher. See HALOT 560-61 s.v. III вАП„Ю„Х÷є„®÷ґ„ФвАО. For discussion of the views, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:560.
n2303048: tn Heb вАЬyour earsвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2303049: tn The word вАЬcorrectвАЩ is supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2303050: tn Heb вАЬthe platings of your silver idols.вАЭ
n2303051: tn Heb вАЬthe covering of your gold image.вАЭ
n2303052: tn Heb вАЬand he will give rain for your seed that you plant in the ground, and food [will be] the produce of the ground, and it will be rich and abundant.вАЭ
n2303054: tn Heb вАЬthe oxen and the donkeys that work the ground.вАЭ
n2303055: sn Crops will be so abundant that even the work animals will eat well.
n2303057: sn Light here symbolizes restoration of divine blessing and prosperity. The number вАЬsevenвАЭ is used symbolically to indicate intensity. The exact meaning of the phrase вАЬthe light of seven daysвАЭ is uncertain; it probably means вАЬseven times brighterвАЭ (see the parallel line).
n2303058:
tn Heb вАЬthe fracture of his peopleвАЭ (so NASB).
sn The Lord is here compared to a physician setting a broken bone in a bandage or cast.
n2303059: tn Heb вАЬthe injury of his wound.вАЭ The joining of synonyms emphasizes the severity of the wound. Another option is to translate, вАЬthe wound of his blow.вАЭ In this case the pronominal suffix might refer to the Lord, not the people, yielding the translation, вАЬthe wound which he inflicted.вАЭ
n2303060: sn The вАЬnameвАЭ of the Lord sometimes stands by metonymy for the Lord himself, see Exod 23:21; Lev 24:11; Pss 54:1 (54:3 HT); 124:8. In Isa 30:27 the point is that he reveals that aspect of his character which his name suggestsвАФhe comes as Yahweh (вАЬhe is presentвАЭ), the ever present helper of his people who annihilates their enemies and delivers them. The name вАЬYahwehвАЭ originated in a context where God assured a fearful Moses that he would be with him as he confronted Pharaoh and delivered Israel from slavery in Egypt. See Exod 3.
n2303061: tn Heb вАЬhis anger burns, and heaviness of elevation.вАЭ The meaning of the phrase вАЬheaviness of elevationвАЭ is unclear, for вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„В÷Љ÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (massa Њah, вАЬelevationвАЭ) occurs only here. Some understand the term as referring to a cloud (elevated above the earthвАЩs surface), in which case one might translate, вАЬand in heavy cloudsвАЭ (cf. NAB вАЬwith lowering cloudsвАЭ). Others relate the noun to вАП„Ю÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (masa Њ, вАЬburdenвАЭ) and interpret it as a reference to judgment. In this case one might translate, вАЬand with severe judgment.вАЭ The present translation assumes that the noun refers to his glory and that вАЬheavinessвАЭ emphasizes its degree.
n2303062: tn Heb вАЬhis lips are full of anger, and his tongue is like consuming fire.вАЭ The LordвАЩs lips and tongue are used metonymically for his word (or perhaps his battle cry; see v. 31).
n2303063: tn Heb вАЬhis breath is like a flooding river.вАЭ This might picture the Lord breathing heavily as he runs down his enemy, but in light of the preceding verse, which mentions his lips and tongue, вАЬbreathвАЭ probably stands metonymically for the word or battle cry that he expels from his mouth as he shouts. In Isa 34:16 and Ps 33:6 the LordвАЩs вАЬbreathвАЭ is associated with his command.
n2303064: tn Heb вАЬshaking nations in a sieve of worthlessness.вАЭ It is not certain exactly how вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„РвАО (shave Њ, вАЬemptiness, worthlessnessвАЭ) modifies вАЬsieve.вАЭ A sieve is used to separate grain from chaff and isolate what is worthless so that it might be discarded. Perhaps the nations are likened to such chaff; GodвАЩs judgment will sift them out for destruction.
n2303065: tn Heb вАЬand a bit that leads astray [is] in the jaws of the peoples.вАЭ Here the nations are likened to a horse that can be controlled by a bit placed in its mouth. In this case the Lord uses his sovereign control over the вАЬhorseвАЭ to lead it to its demise.
n2303066: tn Heb вАЬ[you will have] joy of heart, like the one going with a flute to enter the mountain of the Lord to the Rock of Israel.вАЭ The image here is not a foundational rock, but a rocky cliff where people could hide for protection (for example, the fortress of Masada).
n2303067: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord will cause the splendor of his voice to be heard.вАЭ
n2303068: tn Heb вАЬand reveal the lowering of his arm.вАЭ
n2303069: tn Heb вАЬand a flame of consuming fire.вАЭ
n2303070: tn Heb вАЬIndeed by the voice of the Lord Assyria will be shattered.вАЭ
n2303071: tc The Hebrew text has вАЬevery blow from a founded [i.e., вАЬappointedвАЭ?] cudgel.вАЭ The translation above, with support from a few medieval Hebrew mss, assumes an emendation of вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (musadah, вАЬfoundedвАЭ) to вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®÷є„ФвАО (musaroh, вАЬhis disciplineвАЭ).
n2303072: tn Heb вАЬwhich the Lord lays on him.вАЭ
n2303074: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand with battles of brandishing [weapons?] he will fight against him.вАЭ Some prefer to emend вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷∞„Ю÷і„Ь÷∞„Ч÷≤„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (uvemilkhamot, вАЬand with battles ofвАЭ) to вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷і„Ю÷∞„Ч÷є„Ь„Х÷є„™вАО (uvimkholot, вАЬand with dancingвАЭ). Note the immediately preceding references to musical instruments.
n2303076: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬfor arranged from before [or вАЬyesterdayвАЭ] is [?].вАЭ The meaning of вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„§÷∞„™÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (tofteh), which occurs only here, is unknown. The translation above (as with most English versions) assumes an emendation to вАП„™÷Љ÷є„§÷ґ„™вАО (tofet, вАЬTophethвАЭ; cf. NASB, NIV, NLT) and places the final he (вАП„ФвАО) on the beginning of the next word as an interrogative particle. Topheth was a place near Jerusalem used as a burial ground (see Jer 7:32; 19:11).
n2303077: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬAlso it is made ready for the king, one makes it deep and wide.вАЭ If one takes the final he (вАП„ФвАО) on вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„§÷∞„™÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (tofteh) and prefixes it to вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (gam) as an interrogative particle (see the preceding note), one can translate, вАЬIs it also made ready for the king?вАЭ In this case the question is rhetorical and expects an emphatic affirmative answer, вАЬOf course it is!вАЭ
n2303078:
tn Heb вАЬits pile of wood, fire and wood, one makes abundant.вАЭ
sn Apparently this alludes to some type of funeral rite.
n230311: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] those who go down to Egypt for help.вАЭ
n230312: tn Heb вАЬand trust in chariots for they are many.вАЭ
n230313: tn Heb вАЬand in horsemen for they are very strong [or вАЬnumerousвАЭ].вАЭ
n230314: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n230315: sn This statement appears to have a sarcastic tone. The royal advisers who are advocating an alliance with Egypt think they are wise, but the Lord possesses wisdom as well and will thwart their efforts.
n230316: tn Heb вАЬand he does not turn aside [i.e., вАЬretractвАЭ] his wordsвАЭ; NIV вАЬdoes not take back his words.вАЭ
n230317: tn Heb вАЬand he will arise against the house of the wicked.вАЭ
n230319: tn Heb вАЬand against the help of the doers of sin.вАЭ
n2303110: tn Heb вАЬwill extendвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NASB, NCV вАЬstretch out.вАЭ
n2303111: tn Heb вАЬtogether all of them will come to an end.вАЭ
n2303112: tn Heb вАЬAs a lion growls, a young lion over its prey.вАЭ In the Hebrew text the opening comparison is completed later in the verse (вАЬso the Lord will come downвА¶вАЭ), after a parenthesis describing how fearless the lion is. The present translation divides the verse into three sentences for English stylistic reasons.
n2303113: tn Heb вАЬThough there is summoned against it fullness of shepherds, by their voice it is not terrified, and to their noise it does not respond.вАЭ
n2303114: tn Some prefer to translate the phrase вАП„Ь÷і„¶÷∞„С÷Љ÷є„Р „Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (litsbo Њ  њal) as вАЬfight against,вАЭ but the following context pictures the Lord defending, not attacking, Zion.
n2303115: tn Heb вАЬjust as birds fly.вАЭ The words вАЬover a nestвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2303117: tn The only other occurrence of this verb is in Exod 12:13, 23, 27, where the Lord вАЬpasses overвАЭ (i.e., вАЬsparesвАЭ) the Israelite households as he comes to judge their Egyptian oppressors. The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„°÷Ј„ЧвАО (pesakh, вАЬPassoverвАЭ) is derived from the verb. The use of the verb in Isa 31:5 is probably an intentional echo of the Exodus event. As in the days of Moses the Lord will spare his people as he comes to judge their enemies.
n2303118: tn Heb вАЬReturn to the one [against] whom the sons of Israel made deep rebellion.вАЭ The syntax is awkward here. A preposition is omitted by ellipsis after the verb (see GKC 446 ¬І138.f, n. 2), and there is a shift from direct address (note the second plural imperative вАЬreturnвАЭ) to the third person (note вАЬthey made deepвАЭ). For other examples of abrupt shifts in person in poetic style, see GKC 462 ¬І144.p.
n2303120: tn Heb вАЬrejectвАЭ (so NIV); NRSV, TEV, CEV, NLT вАЬthrow away.вАЭ
n2303121: tn Heb вАЬtheir idols of silver and their idols of gold which your hands made for yourselves [in] sin.вАЭ вАП„Ч÷Є„Ш÷Є„РвАО (khata Њ, вАЬsinвАЭ) is understood as an adverbial accusative of manner. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:573, n. 4.
n2303122: tn Heb вАЬAssyria will fall by a sword, not of a man.вАЭ
n2303123: tn Heb вАЬand a sword not of humankind will devour him.вАЭ
n2303124: tn Heb вАЬhe will flee for himself from before a sword.вАЭ
n2303125: tn Heb вАЬrocky cliffвАЭ (cf. ASV, NASB вАЬrockвАЭ), viewed metaphorically as a place of defense and security.
n2303126: tn Heb вАЬHis rocky cliff, because of fear, will pass away [i.e., вАЬperishвАЭ].вАЭ
n2303127: tn Heb вАЬand they will be afraid of the flag, his officers.вАЭ
n2303128: sn The вАЬfireвАЭ and вАЬfirepotвАЭ here symbolize divine judgment, which is heating up like a fire in Jerusalem, waiting to be used against the Assyrians when they attack the city.
n230323: tn Heb вАЬa man,вАЭ but вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish) probably refers here to вАЬeachвАЭ of the officials mentioned in the previous verse.
n230325: tn The Hebrew text as vocalized reads literally вАЬwill not gaze,вАЭ but this is contradictory to the context. The verb form should be revocalized as вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷є„Ґ÷ґ„Щ„†÷Є„ФвАО (tesho њenah) from вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (sh њa њa, вАЬbe blindedвАЭ); see Isa 6:10; 29:9.
n230327: tn Heb вАЬthe heart of rashness will understand knowledgeвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬThe flighty will become wise and capable.вАЭ
n230328: tn Or вАЬfoolishness,вАЭ in a moral-ethical sense. See 9:17.
n230329: tn Heb вАЬand his heart commits sinвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬhis heart will work iniquityвАЭ; NASB вАЬinclines toward wickedness.вАЭ
n2303210: tn Heb вАЬin order to do [or вАЬso that he doesвАЭ] what is godless [or вАЬdefiledвАЭ].вАЭ
n2303211: tn Heb вАЬso that he leaves empty the appetite [or вАЬdesireвАЭ] of the hungry.вАЭ
n2303212: tn Heb вАЬand the drink of the thirsty he causes to fail.вАЭ
n2303213: tn Heb вАЬas for a deceiver, his implements [or вАЬweaponsвАЭ] are evil.вАЭ
n2303214: tn Or вАЬhe plans evil thingsвАЭ; NIV вАЬhe makes up evil schemes.вАЭ
n2303215: tn Heb вАЬto ruin the poor with words of falsehood, even when the needy speak what is just.вАЭ
n2303216: tn Heb вАЬand he upon honorable things stands.вАЭ
n2303217: tn Or вАЬself-assuredвАЭ; NASB, NRSV вАЬwho are at ease.вАЭ
n2303221: tn The imperatival forms in v. 11 are problematic. The first (вАП„Ч÷і„®÷∞„У„Х÷ЉвАО, khiredu, вАЬtrembleвАЭ) is masculine plural in form, though spoken to a feminine plural addressee (вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„†÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО, sha Њanannot, вАЬcomplacent onesвАЭ). The four imperatival forms that follow (вАП„®÷∞„Т÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО, regazah, вАЬshake with fearвАЭ; вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО, peshotah, вАЬstrip off your clothesвАЭ; вАП„Ґ÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО,  њorah, вАЬexpose yourselvesвАЭ; and вАП„Ч÷≤„Т„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО, khagorah, вАЬput onвАЭ) all appear to be lengthened (so-called вАЬemphaticвАЭ) masculine singular forms, even though they too appear to be spoken to a feminine plural addressee. GKC 131-32 ¬І48.i suggests emending вАП„Ч÷і„®÷∞„У„Х÷ЉвАО (khiredu) to вАП„Ч÷≤„®÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (kharadah) and understanding all five imperatives as feminine plural вАЬAramaizedвАЭ forms.
n2303222: tc The Hebrew text has вАЬover mourning breasts.вАЭ The reference to вАЬbreastsвАЭ would make sense in light of v. 11, which refers to the practice of women baring their breasts as a sign of sorrow (see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:585). However, one expects the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal) to introduce the source or reason for mourning (see vv. 12b-13a) and the participle вАП„°÷є„§÷∞„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sofedim, вАЬmourningвАЭ) seems odd modifying вАЬbreasts.вАЭ The translation above assumes a twofold emendation: (1) вАП„©„Б÷Є„У÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shadayim, вАЬbreastsвАЭ) is emended to [вАП„ЭвАО]вАП„©„В÷Є„У÷Ј„ЩвАО (saday[m], вАЬfield,вАЭ a term that also appears in Isa 56:9). The final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) would be enclitic in this case, not a plural indicator. (The Hebrew noun вАП„©„В÷Є„У÷ґ„ФвАО (sadeh, вАЬfieldвАЭ) forms its plural with an вАП„Х÷є„™вАО- [-ot] ending). (2) The plural participle вАП„°÷є„§÷∞„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО is emended to вАП„°÷∞„§÷є„У÷Є„ФвАО (sefodah), a lengthened imperatival form, meaning вАЬmourn.вАЭ For an overview of various suggestions that have been made for this difficult line, see Oswalt, 586, n. 12).
n2303223: tn вАЬMournвАЭ is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text vv. 12-13 are one long sentence.
n2303224: tn Heb вАЬindeed, over all the houses of joy.вАЭ It is not certain if this refers to individual homes or to places where parties and celebrations were held.
n2303225: sn This same phrase is used in 22:2.
n2303227: tn Hebrew вАП„Ґ÷є„§÷ґ„ЬвАО ( њofel), probably refers here to a specific area within the city of Jerusalem. See HALOT 861 s.v. II вАП„Ґ÷є„§÷ґ„ЬвАО.
n2303228: tn The Hebrew text has вАП„С÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„У „Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®„Х÷є„™вАО (ve њad me њarot). The force of вАП„С÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО, which usually means вАЬbehind, through, round about,вАЭ or вАЬfor the benefit of,вАЭ is uncertain here. HALOT 616 s.v. *вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО takes вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®„Х÷є„™вАО (me њarot) as a homonym of вАЬcaveвАЭ and defines it here as вАЬcleared field.вАЭ Despite these lexical problems, the general point of the statement seems clearвАФthe city will be uninhabited.
n2303229: tn Heb вАЬthe joy of wild donkeys, a pasture for flocks.вАЭ
n2303230: tn The words вАЬThe desolation will continueвАЭ refer to the previous context and have been added for smoothness.
n2303231: tn Heb вАЬuntil a spirit is emptied out on us from on high.вАЭ The words вАЬthis desolation will continueвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification and stylistic purposes. The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО ( њarah), used here in the Niphal, normally means вАЬlay bare, expose.вАЭ The term вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh, вАЬspiritвАЭ) is often understood here as a reference to the divine spirit (cf. 44:3 and NASB, NIV, CEV, NLT), but it appears here without an article (cf. NRSV вАЬa spiritвАЭ), pronominal suffix, or a genitive (such as вАЬof the LordвАЭ). The translation assumes that it carries an impersonal nuance вАЬvivacity, vigorвАЭ in this context.
n2303232: tn The term вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ю÷ґ„ЬвАО (karmel) may refer to fertile land, an orchard, a garden of vines, a plantation of trees, or fresh grain. The dictionaries may subdivide these meaning under homonyms (see BDB 502, NIDOTTE 718, HALOT 499, The Concise DCH, 183). The picture of transformation is clearer when it is understood that a вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„®вАО (midbar, вАЬwildernessвАЭ) is an area that does not receive enough rainfall to support trees.
n2303233: sn The same statement appears in 29:17b, where, in conjunction with the preceding line, it appears to picture a reversal. Here it seems to depict supernatural growth. The desert will blossom into an orchard, and the trees of the orchard will multiply and grow tall, becoming a forest.
n2303234: sn This new era of divine blessing will also include a moral/ethical transformation, as justice and fairness fill the land and replace the social injustice so prevalent in IsaiahвАЩs time.
n2303235: tn Heb вАЬand the product of fairness will be peace.вАЭ
n2303236: tn Heb вАЬand the work of fairness [will be] calmness and security forever.вАЭ
n2303237: tn Or вАЬin safe resting placesвАЭ; NAB, NRSV вАЬquiet resting places.вАЭ
n2303238: tn Heb вАЬand [?] when the forest descends.вАЭ The form вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО (uvarad) is often understood as an otherwise unattested denominative verb meaning вАЬto hailвАЭ (HALOT 154 s.v. I вАП„С„®„УвАО). In this case one might translate, вАЬand it hails when the forest is destroyedвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NIV). Perhaps the text alludes to a powerful wind and hail storm that knocks down limbs and trees. Some prefer to emend the form to вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО (veyarad), вАЬand it descends,вАЭ which provides better, though not perfect, symmetry with the parallel line (cf. NAB). Perhaps вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО should be dismissed as dittographic. In this case the statement (вАЬwhen the forest descendsвАЭ) lacks a finite verb and seems incomplete, but perhaps it is subordinate to v. 20.
n2303239: tn Heb вАЬand in humiliation the city is laid low.вАЭ
n2303241:
tn Heb вАЬwho set free the foot of the ox and donkeyвАЭ; NIV вАЬletting your cattle and donkeys range free.вАЭ
sn This verse seems to anticipate a time when fertile land is available to cultivate and crops are so abundant that the farm animals can be allowed to graze freely.
n230331:
tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] the destroyer.вАЭ
sn In this context вАЬthe destroyerвАЭ appears to refer collectively to the hostile nations (vv. 3-4). Assyria would probably have been primary in the minds of the prophet and his audience.
n230332: tn Heb вАЬand the deceitful oneвАЭ; NAB, NIV вАЬO traitorвАЭ; NRSV вАЬyou treacherous one.вАЭ In the parallel structure вАП„Ф„Х÷є„ЩвАО (hoy, вАЬwoe [to]вАЭ) does double duty.
n230333: tc The form in the Hebrew text appears to derive from an otherwise unattested verb вАП„†÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (nalah). The translation follows the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa in reading вАП„Ы„Ы„Ь„™„ЪвАО, a Piel infinitival form from the verbal root вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah), meaning вАЬfinish.вАЭ
n230334: tn Heb вАЬBe their arm each morning.вАЭ вАЬArmвАЭ is a symbol for strength. The mem suffixed to the noun has been traditionally understood as a third person suffix, but this is contrary to the context, where the people speak of themselves in the first person. The mem (вАП„ЮвАО) is probably enclitic with ellipsis of the pronoun, which can be supplied from the context. See J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:589, n. 1.
n230335: tn Heb вАЬ[Be] also our deliverance in the time of distress.вАЭ
n230336: tn Heb вАЬat the sound of tumult the nations run away.вАЭ
n230337: tn Heb вАЬbecause of your exaltation the nations scatter.вАЭ
n230338: tn The pronoun is plural; the statement is addressed to the nations who have stockpiled plunder from their conquests of others.
n230339: tn Heb вАЬand your plunder is gathered, the gathering of the locust.вАЭ
n2303310: tn Heb вАЬlike a swarm of locusts swarming on it.вАЭ
n2303311: tn Or вАЬelevatedвАЭ; NCV, NLT вАЬis very great.вАЭ
n2303313: tn Heb вАЬon highвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); CEV вАЬin the heavens.вАЭ
n2303314: tn Heb вАЬand he is the stability of your times.вАЭ
n2303315: tn Heb вАЬa rich store of deliverance, wisdom, and knowledge.вАЭ
n2303316: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord, it is his treasure.вАЭ
n2303317: tn The meaning of the Hebrew word is unknown. Proposals include вАЬheroesвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV вАЬvaliant onesвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬbrave menвАЭ); вАЬpriests,вАЭ вАЬresidents [of Jerusalem].вАЭ The present translation assumes that the term is synonymous with вАЬmessengers of peace,вАЭ with which it corresponds in the parallel structure of the verse.
n2303318: tn Heb вАЬmessengers of peace,вАЭ apparently those responsible for negotiating the agreements that have been broken (see v. 8).
n2303319: tn Or вАЬdesolateвАЭ (NAB, NASB); NIV, NRSV, NLT вАЬdeserted.вАЭ
n2303320: tn Heb вАЬthe one passing by on the road ceases.вАЭ
n2303321: tn Heb вАЬone breaks a treatyвАЭ; NAB вАЬCovenants are broken.вАЭ
n2303322: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬhe despises cities.вАЭ The term вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њarim, вАЬcitiesвАЭ) probably needs to be emended to an original вАП„Ґ÷µ„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њedim, вАЬ[legal] witnessesвАЭ), a reading that is preserved in the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa. Confusion of dalet (вАП„УвАО) and resh (вАП„®вАО) is a well-attested scribal error.
n2303323: tn Heb вАЬhe does not regard human beings.вАЭ
n2303325: tn Or вАЬmournsвАЭ (BDB 5 s.v. I вАП„Р÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО). HALOT 6-7 lists homonyms I вАП„Р„С„ЬвАО (вАЬmournвАЭ) and II вАП„Р„С„ЬвАО (вАЬdry upвАЭ). They propose the second here on the basis of parallelism. See 24:4.
n2303326: tn Heb вАЬLebanon is ashamed.вАЭ The Hiphil is exhibitive, expressing the idea, вАЬexhibits shame.вАЭ In this context the statement alludes to the withering of vegetation.
