n2303327: sn Sharon was a fertile plain along the Mediterranean coast. See 35:2.
n2303328: tn The rift valley (вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО,  њaravah) is a geographic feature extending from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. Especially in the vicinity of the Dead Sea and then ranging southward, it is very dry with little vegetation.
n2303329: sn Both of these areas were known for their trees and vegetation. See 2:13; 35:2.
n2303330: tn Heb вАЬshake off [their leaves]вАЭ (so ASV, NRSV); NAB вАЬare stripped bare.вАЭ
n2303331: tn Or вАЬlift myself upвАЭ (KJV); NLT вАЬshow my power and might.вАЭ
n2303332: tn The second person verb and pronominal forms in this verse are plural. The hostile nations are the addressed, as the next verse makes clear.
n2303333: sn The hostile nationsвАЩ plans to destroy GodвАЩs people will come to nothing; their hostility will end up being self-destructive.
n2303334: tn Heb вАЬwill be a burning to lime.вАЭ See Amos 2:1.
n2303335: tn Or вАЬtremblingвАЭ (ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬshake with fear.вАЭ
n2303336: tn Or вАЬthe defiledвАЭ; TEV вАЬThe sinful people of ZionвАЭ; NLT вАЬThe sinners in Jerusalem.вАЭ
n2303337: tn The words вАЬthey sayвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2303338: tn Or вАЬperpetualвАЭ; or вАЬeverlastingвАЭ (KJV, ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV).
n2303340: tn Or, possibly, вАЬjustlyвАЭ; NAB вАЬwho practices virtue.вАЭ
n2303341: tn Heb вАЬ[who] shakes off his hands from grabbing hold of a bribe.вАЭ
n2303342: tn Heb вАЬ[who] shuts his ear from listening to bloodshed.вАЭ
n2303343: tn Heb вАЬ[who] closes his eyes from seeing evil.вАЭ
n2303344: tn Heb вАЬhe [in the] exalted places will live.вАЭ
n2303345: tn Heb вАЬmountain strongholds, cliffs [will be] his elevated place.вАЭ
n2303346: tn Heb вАЬyour eyes will see a king in his beautyвАЭ; NIV, NRSV вАЬthe king.вАЭ
n2303347: tn Heb вАЬa land of distances,вАЭ i.e., an extensive land.
n2303348: tn Heb вАЬyour heart will meditate on terror.вАЭ
n2303349: tn The words вАЬand you will ask yourselvesвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification and stylistic reasons.
n2303350: sn The people refer to various Assyrian officials who were responsible for determining the amount of taxation or tribute Judah must pay to the Assyrian king.
n2303351: tn The Hebrew form вАП„†„Х÷є„Ґ÷Є„ЦвАО (no њaz) is a Niphal participle derived from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЦвАО (ya њaz, an otherwise unattested verb) or from вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Є„ЦвАО ( њazaz, вАЬbe strong,вАЭ unattested elsewhere in the Niphal). Some prefer to emend the form to вАП„Ь„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„ЦвАО (lo њez) which occurs in Ps 114:1 with the meaning вАЬspeak a foreign language.вАЭ See HALOT 809 s.v. вАП„Ґ„Ц„ЦвАО, 533 s.v. вАП„Ь„Ґ„ЦвАО. In this case, one might translate вАЬpeople who speak a foreign language.вАЭ
n2303352: tn Heb вАЬa people too deep of lip to hear.вАЭ The phrase вАЬdeep of lipвАЭ must be an idiom meaning вАЬlips that speak words that are unfathomable [i.e., incomprehensible].вАЭ
n2303353: tn Heb вАЬderision of tongue there is no understanding.вАЭ The Niphal of вАП„Ь÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ТвАО (la њag) occurs only here. In the Qal and Hiphil the verb means вАЬto deride, mock.вАЭ A related noun is used in 28:11.
n2303356: tn Or вАЬthat does not travelвАЭ; NASB вАЬwhich shall not be folded.вАЭ
n2303357: tn Heb вАЬBut there [as] a mighty one [will be] the Lord for us.вАЭ
n2303358: tn Heb вАЬa place of rivers, streams wide of hands [i.e., on both sides].вАЭ
n2303359: tn Heb вАЬa ship of rowing will not go into it.вАЭ
n2303360: tn Heb вАЬand a mighty ship will not pass through it.вАЭ
n2303361: tn The words вАЬthough at this timeвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The first half of the verse is addressed to Judah and contrasts the nationвАЩs present weakness with its future prosperity. Judah is compared to a ship that is incapable of sailing.
n2303362: tn Heb вАЬthey do not fasten the base of their mast.вАЭ On вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ken, вАЬbaseвАЭ) see BDB 487 s.v. III вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО and HALOT 483 s.v. III вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО.
n2303364: tn Heb вАЬthen there will be divided up loot of plunder [in] abundance.вАЭ
n2303365: sn JudahвАЩs victory over its enemies will be so thorough there will be more than enough plunder for everyone, even slow-moving lame men who would normally get left out in the rush to gather the loot.
n2303366: tn The words вАЬof ZionвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n230341: tn Heb вАЬthe world and its offspringвАЭ; NASB вАЬthe world and all that springs from it.вАЭ
n230342: tn Heb вАЬwill be cast asideвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬthrown out.вАЭ
n230343: tn Heb вАЬ[as for] their corpses, their stench will arise.вАЭ
n230344: tn Heb вАЬhills will dissolve from their blood.вАЭ
n230345: tc Heb вАЬand all the host of heaven will rot.вАЭ The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa inserts вАЬand the valleys will be split open,вАЭ but this reading may be influenced by Mic 1:4. On the other hand, the statement, if original, could have been omitted by homoioarcton, a scribeвАЩs eye jumping from the conjunction prefixed to вАЬthe valleysвАЭ to the conjunction prefixed to the verb вАЬrot.вАЭ
n230346: tn Heb вАЬlike the withering of a leaf from a vine, and like the withering from a fig tree.вАЭ
n230347: tn The words вАЬhe saysвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The Lord speaks at this point.
n230348:
tn Heb вАЬindeed [or вАЬforвАЭ] my sword is drenched in the heavens.вАЭ The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„™„®„Р„ФвАО (вАЬ[my sword] appeared [in the heavens]вАЭ), but this is apparently an attempt to make sense out of a difficult metaphor. Cf. NIV вАЬMy sword has drunk its fill in the heavens.вАЭ
sn In v. 4 the вАЬhost of the heavenвАЭ refers to the heavenly luminaries (stars and planets, see, among others, Deut 4:19; 17:3; 2 Kgs 17:16; 21:3, 5; 23:4-5; 2 Chr 33:3, 5) that populate the divine/heavenly assembly in mythological and prescientific Israelite thought (see Job 38:7; Isa 14:13). As in 24:21, they are viewed here as opposing God and being defeated in battle.
n230349: sn Edom is mentioned here as epitomizing the hostile nations that oppose God.
n2303410: tn The verb is a rare Hotpaal passive form. See GKC 150 ¬І54.h.
n2303411: tn The words вАЬit dripsвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2303412: tn The words вАЬand is coveredвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2303413: tn Heb вАЬfor there is a sacrifice to the Lord.вАЭ
n2303414: sn The LordвАЩs judgment of Edom is compared to a bloody sacrificial scene.
n2303415: tn Heb вАЬgreatвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2303416: tn Heb вАЬwill go downвАЭ; NAB вАЬshall be struck down.вАЭ
n2303417: tn Heb вАЬand bulls along with strong ones.вАЭ Perhaps this refers to the leaders.
n2303418: tn Heb вАЬfor a day of vengeance [is] for the Lord.вАЭ
n2303419: tn Heb вАЬa year of repayment for the strife of Zion.вАЭ The translation assumes that вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) refers to EdomвАЩs hostility toward Zion. Another option is to understand вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) as referring to the LordвАЩs taking up ZionвАЩs cause. In this case one might translate, вАЬa time when he will repay Edom and vindicate Zion.вАЭ
n2303420: tn Heb вАЬherвАЭ; the referent (Edom) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2303422: tn вАП„І÷Є„Р÷Ј„™вАО (qa Њat) refers to some type of bird (cf. Lev 11:18; Deut 14:17) that was typically found near ruins (see Zeph 2:14). вАП„І÷і„§÷Љ„Х÷є„УвАО (qippod) may also refer to a type of bird (NAB вАЬhoot owlвАЭ; NIV вАЬscreech owlвАЭ; TEV вАЬravensвАЭ), but some have suggested a rodent may be in view (cf. NCV вАЬsmall animalsвАЭ; ASV вАЬporcupineвАЭ; NASB, NRSV вАЬhedgehogвАЭ).
n2303424: tn The Hebrew text has вАП„Щ÷Ј„†÷∞„©„Б„Х÷є„£ „Х÷∞„Ґ÷є„®÷µ„СвАО (yanshof ve њorev). Both the вАП„Щ÷Ј„†÷∞„©„Б„Х÷є„£вАО (вАЬowlвАЭ; see Lev 11:17; Deut 14:16) and вАП„Ґ÷є„®÷µ„СвАО (вАЬravenвАЭ; Lev 11:15; Deut 14:14) were types of wild birds.
n2303425: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2303426: tn Heb вАЬstones,вАЭ i.e., the stones used in a plumb bob.
n2303427: sn The metaphor in v. 11b emphasizes that God has carefully planned EdomвАЩs demise.
n2303428: tn Heb вАЬwill be nothingвАЭ; NCV, TEV, NLT вАЬwill all be gone.вАЭ
n2303429: tn The words вАЬwill growвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2303430: tc Heb вАЬand she will be a settlement for wild dogs, a dwelling place for ostriches.вАЭ The translation assumes an emendation of вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷і„Щ„®вАО (khatsir, вАЬgrassвАЭ) to вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷µ„®вАО (khatser, вАЬsettlementвАЭ). One of the Qumran scrolls of Isaiah (1QIsaa) supports this emendation (cf. HALOT 344 s.v. II вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷і„Щ„®вАО)
n2303431: tn Heb вАЬwill meetвАЭ (so NIV); NLT вАЬwill mingle there.вАЭ
n2303432: tn Heb вАЬand a goat will call to its neighbor.вАЭ
n2303433: tn The precise meaning of вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„Ь÷і„Щ„™вАО (lilit) is unclear, though in this context the word certainly refers to some type of wild animal or bird. The word appears to be related to вАП„Ь÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (laylah, вАЬnightвАЭ). Some interpret it as the name of a female night demon, on the basis of an apparent Akkadian cognate used as the name of a demon. Later Jewish legends also identified Lilith as a demon. Cf. NRSV вАЬLilith.вАЭ
n2303434: tn Heb вАЬand will find for themselves a resting place.вАЭ
n2303435: tn Hebrew вАП„І÷і„§÷Љ„Х÷є„ЦвАО (qippoz) occurs only here; the precise meaning of the word is uncertain.
n2303436: tn For this proposed meaning for Hebrew вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ШвАО (malat), see HALOT 589 s.v. I вАП„Ю„Ь„ШвАО.
n2303437: tn Heb вАЬand brood [over them] in her shadow.вАЭ
n2303438: tn The precise meaning of вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (dayyah) is uncertain, though the term appears to refer to some type of bird of prey, perhaps a vulture.
n2303439:
tn Heb вАЬSeek from upon the scroll of the Lord and read.вАЭ
sn It is uncertain what particular scroll is referred to here. Perhaps the phrase simply refers to this prophecy and is an admonition to pay close attention to the details of the message.
n2303440: tn Heb вАЬone from these will not be missing.вАЭ вАП„Ф÷µ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (hennah, вАЬtheseвАЭ) is feminine plural in the Hebrew text. It may refer only to the birds mentioned in v. 15b or may include all of the creatures listed in vv. 14b-15 (all of which are identified with feminine nouns).
n2303441: tn Heb вАЬeach its mate they will not lack.вАЭ
n2303442: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬfor a mouth, it has commanded.вАЭ The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa and a few medieval mss have вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (pihu, вАЬhis mouth [has commanded]вАЭ), while a few other medieval mss read вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (pi yehvah, вАЬthe mouth of the Lord [has commanded]вАЭ).
n2303443: tn Heb вАЬand his spirit, he gathers them.вАЭ The pronominal suffix (вАЬthemвАЭ) is feminine plural, referring to the birds mentioned in v. 15b or to all of the creatures listed in vv. 14b-15 (all of which are identified with feminine nouns).
n2303444: tn Heb вАЬand he causes the lot to fall for them.вАЭ Once again the pronominal suffix (вАЬthemвАЭ) is feminine plural, referring to the birds mentioned in v. 15b or to all of the creatures listed in vv. 14b-15 (all of which are identified with feminine nouns).
n2303445: tn Heb вАЬand his hand divides for them with a measuring line.вАЭ The pronominal suffix (вАЬthemвАЭ) now switches to masculine plural, referring to all the animals and birds mentioned in vv. 11-15, some of which were identified with masculine nouns. This signals closure for this portion of the speech, which began in v. 11. The following couplet (v. 17b) forms an inclusio with v. 11a through verbal repetition.
n2303446: tn Heb вАЬwill possess itвАЭ (so NIV); NCV вАЬthey will own that land forever.вАЭ
n230351: tn The final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) on the verb вАП„Щ÷∞„©÷ї„В„©„В„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (yesusum) is dittographic (note the initial mem on the following noun вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„С÷Є÷Љ„®вАО [midbar]). The ambiguous verbal form is translated as a jussive because it is parallel to the jussive form вАП„™÷Є„Т÷µ„ЬвАО (tagel). The jussive is used rhetorically here, not as a literal command or prayer.
n230352: tn This verse employs three terms for desolate paces: вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„С÷Є÷Љ„®вАО (midbar, вАЬwildernessвАЭ), вАП„¶÷і„Щ÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (tsiyyah, вАЬdry place, desertвАЭ), and вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО ( њaravah, вАЬrift valleyвАЭ). A midbar is an area that receives less than twelve inches of rain per year. It may have some pasturage (if receiving six to twelve inches of rain), but often has desert-like qualities. A tsiyyah is not a sandy desert per se, but of the three terms most clearly indicates a dry, desert region. The rift valley includes the Jordan Valley, but it still has a reputation as a dry, desolate place from its conditions near the Dead Sea and southward.
n230353: tn The ambiguous verb form вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷∞„®÷Ј„ЧвАО (tifrakh) is translated as a jussive because it is parallel to the jussive form вАП„™÷Є„Т÷µ„ЬвАО (tagel).
n230354: tn Heb вАЬand let it rejoice, yes [with] rejoicing and shouting.вАЭ вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь÷Ј„™вАО (gilat) may be an archaic feminine nominal form (see GKC 421 ¬І130.b).
n230355: tn Or вАЬgloryвАЭ (KJV, NIV, NRSV); also a second time later in this verse.
n230356: tn Heb вАЬstaggering kneesвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬfeeble kneesвАЭ; NIV вАЬknees that give way.вАЭ
n230357: tn Heb вАЬSay to the hasty of heart,вАЭ i.e., those whose hearts beat quickly from fear.
n230358: tn The jussive form вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷є„©„Б÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„Ы÷ґ„ЭвАО (veyosha њakhem), which is subordinated to the preceding imperfect with vav conjunctive, indicates purpose.
n230359: tn The rift valley (вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО,  њaravah) extends from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. Near the Dead Sea and southward its conditions are very dry and desolate. Other translations render it as вАЬdesertвАЭ (KJV, NIV, NRSV), вАЬwastelandsвАЭ (NLT), or by its Hebrew name, вАЬthe ArabahвАЭ (NASB).
n2303510: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬand there will be there a road and a way, and the Way of Holiness it will be called.вАЭ вАП„Х÷∞„У÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (vederekh, вАЬand a/the wayвАЭ) is accidentally duplicated; the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa does not reflect the repetition of the phrase.
n2303511: tn The precise meaning of this line is uncertain. The text reads literally вАЬand it is for them, the one who walks [on the] way.вАЭ In this context those authorized to use the Way of Holiness would be morally upright people who are the recipients of GodвАЩs deliverance, in contrast to the morally impure and foolish who are excluded from the new covenant community.
n2303512: tn In this context вАЬfoolsвАЭ are those who are morally corrupt, not those with limited intellectual capacity.
n2303513: tn Heb вАЬwill go up on itвАЭ; TEV вАЬwill pass that way.вАЭ
n2303514: tn Heb вАЬand the redeemed will walk, the ransomed of the Lord will return.вАЭ
n2303515: tn Heb вАЬ[will be] on their head[s].вАЭ вАЬJoyвАЭ may be likened here to a crown (cf. 2 Sam 1:10). The statement may also be an ironic twist on the idiom вАЬearth/dust on the headвАЭ (cf. 2 Sam 1:2; 13:19; 15:32; Job 2:12), referring to a mourning practice.
n2303516: tn Heb вАЬwill overtakeвАЭ (NIV); NLT вАЬthey will be overcome with.вАЭ
n2303517: tn Heb вАЬgrief and groaning will fleeвАЭ; KJV вАЬsorrow and sighing shall flee away.вАЭ
n230361: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n230362: sn For a discussion of this title see M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 229-30.
n230364: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the chief adviser) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n230365: tn Heb вАЬthe field of the washerвАЭ; traditionally вАЬthe fullerвАЩs fieldвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV).
n230366: tn Heb вАЬWhat is this object of trust in which you are trusting?вАЭ
n230367: tn Heb вАЬyou say only a word of lips, counsel and might for battle.вАЭ SennacheribвАЩs message appears to be in broken Hebrew at this point. The phrase вАЬword of lipsвАЭ refers to mere or empty talk in Prov 14:23.
n230368: tn Heb вАЬHow can you turn back the face of an official [from among] the least of my masterвАЩs servants and trust in Egypt for chariots and horsemen?вАЭ In vv. 8-9 the chief adviser develops further the argument begun in v. 6. His reasoning seems to be as follows: вАЬIn your weakened condition you obviously need military strength. Agree to the kingвАЩs terms, and I will personally give you more horses than you are capable of outfitting. If I, a mere minor official, am capable of giving you such military might, just think what power the king has. There is no way the Egyptians can match our strength. It makes much better sense to deal with us.вАЭ
n230369: sn In v. 10 the chief adviser develops further the argument begun in v. 7. He claims that Hezekiah has offended the Lord and that the Lord has commissioned Assyria as his instrument of discipline and judgment.
n2303610: sn Aramaic was the diplomatic language of the Assyrian empire.
n2303611: tn Or вАЬin HebrewвАЭ (NIV, NCV, NLT); NAB, NASB вАЬin Judean.вАЭ
n2303612: tn Heb вАЬTo your master and to you did my master send me to speak these words?вАЭ The rhetorical question expects a negative answer.
n2303613:
tn Heb вАЬ[Is it] not [also] to the menвА¶?вАЭ The rhetorical question expects the answer, вАЬYes, it is.вАЭ
sn The chief adviser alludes to the horrible reality of siege warfare, when the starving people in the besieged city would resort to eating and drinking anything to stay alive.
n2303614: tn The Hebrew text includes вАЬand he said.вАЭ
n2303615: tn Heb вАЬmake with me a blessing and come out to me.вАЭ
n2303616: tn Heb вАЬHave the gods of the nations rescued, each his land, from the hand of the king of Assyria?вАЭ The rhetorical question expects the answer, вАЬOf course not!вАЭ
n2303617: tn The rhetorical questions suggest the answer, вАЬNowhere. They seem to have disappeared in the face of AssyriaвАЩs might.вАЭ
n2303619: tn Heb вАЬthat they rescued Samaria from my hand?вАЭ But this gives the impression that the gods of Sepharvaim were responsible for protecting Samaria, which is obviously not the case. The implied subject of the plural verb вАЬrescuedвАЭ must be the generic вАЬgods of the nations/landsвАЭ (vv. 18, 20).
n2303620: tn Heb вАЬthat the Lord might rescue Jerusalem from my hand?вАЭ The logic runs as follows: Since no god has ever been able to withstand the Assyrian onslaught, how can the people of Jerusalem possibly think the Lord will rescue them?
n2303621: sn As a sign of grief and mourning.
n230371: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n230372: tn Heb вАЬelders of the priestsвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB); NCV вАЬthe older priestsвАЭ; NRSV, TEV, CEV вАЬthe senior priests.вАЭ
n230373: tn In the Hebrew text this verse begins with вАЬthey said to himвАЭ (cf. NRSV).
n230374: tn Or вАЬrebukeвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV), or вАЬcorrection.вАЭ
n230375: tn Or вАЬcontemptвАЭ; NAB, NIV, NRSV вАЬdisgrace.вАЭ
n230376: tn Heb вАЬwhen sons come to the cervical opening and there is no strength to give birth.вАЭ
n230377: tn Heb вАЬall the words of the chief adviser whom his master, the king of Assyria, sent to taunt the living God.вАЭ
n230378: tn Heb вАЬand rebuke the words which the Lord your God hears.вАЭ
n230379: tn Heb вАЬand lift up a prayer on behalf of the remnant that is found.вАЭ
n2303710: tn Heb вАЬby which the servants of the king of Assyria have insulted me.вАЭ
n2303711: tn Heb вАЬI will put in him a spirit.вАЭ The precise sense of вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh, вАЬspiritвАЭ) is uncertain in this context. It may refer to a spiritual being who will take control of his mind (see 1 Kgs 22:19), or it could refer to a disposition of concern and fear. In either case the LordвАЩs sovereignty over the king is apparent.
n2303712: tn Heb вАЬcause him to fallвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NAB), that is, вАЬkill him.вАЭ
n2303713: tn Heb вАЬand the chief adviser returned and he found the king of Assyria fighting against Libnah, for he heard that he had departed from Lachish.вАЭ
n2303714: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the king) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2303715: tn Heb вАЬCushвАЭ (so NASB); NIV, NCV вАЬthe Cushite king of Egypt.вАЭ
n2303716: tn Heb вАЬheard concerning Tirhakah king of Cush, вАШHe has come out to fight with you.вАЩвАЭ
n2303717: tn The Hebrew text has, вАЬand he heard and he sent,вАЭ but the parallel in 2 Kgs 19:9 has вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„С „Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (vayyashav vayyishlakh, вАЬand he returned and he sentвАЭ), i.e., вАЬhe again sent.вАЭ
n2303718: tn Heb вАЬLook, you have heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all the lands, annihilating them.вАЭ
n2303719: tn Heb вАЬand will you be rescued?вАЭ The rhetorical question expects the answer, вАЬNo, of course not!вАЭ
n2303720: tn Heb вАЬfathersвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB); NIV вАЬforefathersвАЭ; NCV вАЬancestors.вАЭ
n2303721: tn Heb вАЬDid the gods of the nations whom my fathers destroyed rescue themвАФGozan and Haran, and Rezeph and the sons of Eden who are in Telassar?вАЭ
n2303722: sn Lair was a city located in northeastern Babylon. See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 235.
n2303723: tc The Hebrew text has the plural, вАЬletters.вАЭ The final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) may be dittographic (note the initial mem on the form that immediately follows). Some Greek and Aramaic witnesses have the singular. If so, one still has to deal with the yod that is part of the plural ending. J. N. Oswalt refers to various commentators who have suggested ways to understand the plural form (Isaiah [NICOT], 1:652).
n2303724: tn In the parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:14 the verb has the plural suffix, вАЬthem,вАЭ but this may reflect a later harmonization to the preceding textual reading of вАЬletters.вАЭ
n2303725: sn The cherubim (singular вАЬcherubвАЭ) refer to the images of winged angelic creatures that were above the ark of the covenant.
n2303726: tn Or вАЬthe heavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2303727: tn Heb вАЬHear all the words of Sennacherib which he sent to taunt the living God.вАЭ
n2303728: tn The Hebrew text here has вАЬall the lands,вАЭ but the parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:17 has вАЬthe nations.вАЭ
n2303729: tn Heb вАЬand they put their gods in the fire.вАЭ
n2303731: tn The parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:19 reads, вАЬthat you, Lord, are the only God.вАЭ
n2303732: tn The parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:20 includes a verb, вАЬWhat you have prayed вА¶ I have heard.вАЭ
n2303733: tn Heb вАЬthis is the word which the Lord has spoken about him.вАЭ
n2303734: sn Zion (Jerusalem) is pictured here as a young, vulnerable daughter whose purity is being threatened by the would-be Assyrian rapist. The personification hints at the reality which the young girls of the city would face if the Assyrians conquered it.
n2303735: sn Shaking the head was a mocking gesture of derision.
n2303736: tn Heb вАЬand lifted your eyes on high?вАЭ Cf. NIV вАЬlifted your eyes in prideвАЭ; NRSV вАЬhaughtily lifted your eyes.вАЭ
n2303737: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2303738: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2303739: tn Heb вАЬthe height of its extremityвАЭ; ASV вАЬits farthest height.вАЭ
n2303740: tc The Hebrew text has simply, вАЬI dug and drank water.вАЭ But the parallel text in 2 Kgs 19:24 has вАЬforeign waters.вАЭ вАП„Ц÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (zarim, вАЬforeignвАЭ) may have accidentally dropped out of the Isaianic text by homoioteleuton (cf. NCV, NIV, NLT). Note that the preceding word, вАП„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (mayim, вАЬwater) also ends in mem (вАП„ЭвАО). The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАЬforeign watersвАЭ for this line. However, in several other passages the 1QIsaa scroll harmonizes with 2 Kgs 19 against the MT (Isa 36:5; 37:9, 20). Since the addition of вАЬforeignвАЭ to this text in Isaiah by a later scribe would be more likely than its deletion, the MT reading should be accepted.
n2303741: tn Having quoted the Assyrian kingвАЩs arrogant words in vv. 23-24, the Lord now speaks to the king.
n2303742: tn Heb вАЬHave you not heard?вАЭ The rhetorical question expresses the LordвАЩs amazement that anyone might be ignorant of what he is about to say.
n2303744: tn Heb вАЬand it is to cause to crash into heaps of ruins fortified cities.вАЭ The subject of the third feminine singular verb вАП„™÷∞„Ф÷і„ЩвАО (tehi) is the implied plan, referred to in the preceding lines with third feminine singular pronominal suffixes.
n2303745: tn Heb вАЬshort of handвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬof small powerвАЭ; NASB вАЬshort of strength.вАЭ
n2303746: tn Heb вАЬthey are plants in the field and green vegetation.вАЭ The metaphor emphasizes how short-lived these seemingly powerful cities really were. See Ps 90:5-6; Isa 40:6-8, 24.
n2303747: tn Heb вАЬ[they are] grass on the rooftops.вАЭ See the preceding note.
n2303748: tc The Hebrew text has вАЬscorched before the standing grainвАЭ (perhaps meaning вАЬbefore it reaches maturityвАЭ), but it is preferable to emend вАП„І÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (qamah, вАЬstanding grainвАЭ) to вАП„І÷Є„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (qadim, вАЬeast windвАЭ) with the support of 1Q Isaa; cf. J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:657, n. 8.
n2303749: tc Heb вАЬyour going out and your coming in and how you have raged against me.вАЭ Several scholars have suggested that this line is probably dittographic (note the beginning of the next line). However, most English translations include the statement in question at the end of v. 28 and the beginning of v. 29. Interestingly, the LXX does not have this clause at the end of v. 28 and the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa does not have it at the beginning of v. 29. In light of this ambiguous manuscript evidence, it appears best to retain the clause in both verses.
n2303750: tc Heb вАЬand your complacency comes up into my ears.вАЭ The parallelism is improved if вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„†÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (sha Њananekha, вАЬyour complacencyвАЭ) is emended to вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„†÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (she Њonekha, вАЬyour uproarвАЭ). See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 237-38. However, the LXX seems to support the MT, and SennacheribвАЩs cavalier dismissal of Yahweh depicts an arrogant complacency (J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 1:658, n. 10).
n2303751: sn The word-picture has a parallel in Assyrian sculpture. See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 238.
n2303752: tn At this point the word concerning the king of Assyria (vv. 22-29) ends, and the Lord again addresses Hezekiah and the people directly (see v. 21).
n2303753: tn Heb вАЬand this is your sign.вАЭ In this case the вАП„Р„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њot, вАЬsignвАЭ) is a future reminder of GodвАЩs intervention designated before the actual intervention takes place. For similar вАЬsignsвАЭ see Exod 3:12 and Isa 7:14-25.
n2303754: sn This refers to crops that grew up on their own (that is, without cultivation) from the seed planted in past years.
n2303755: tn Heb вАЬand in the second yearвАЭ (so ASV).
n2303756: tn Heb вАЬin the third yearвАЭ (so KJV, NAB).
n2303757: tn The four plural imperatival verb forms in v. 30b are used rhetorically. The Lord commands the people to plant, harvest, etc. to emphasize the certainty of restored peace and prosperity.
n2303758: tn Heb вАЬThe remnant of the house of Judah that is left will add roots below and produce fruit above.вАЭ
n2303759: tn Traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hosts.вАЭ In this context the LordвАЩs вАЬzealвАЭ refers to his intense devotion to and love for his people that prompts him to protect and restore them.
n2303760: tn Heb вАЬthereвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV). In terms of English style вАЬhereвАЭ is expected in collocation with вАЬthisвАЭ in the previous line.
n2303761: tn Heb вАЬ[with] a shieldвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, NRSV).
n2303762: tn Heb вАЬfor my sake and for the sake of David my servant.вАЭ
n2303763: tn The word вАЬtroopsвАЭ is supplied in the translation for smoothness and clarity.
n2303764: tn This refers to the Israelites and/or the rest of the Assyrian army.
n2303765: tn Heb вАЬlook, all of them were dead bodiesвАЭ; NLT вАЬthey found corpses everywhere.вАЭ
n2303766: tn Heb вАЬand Sennacherib king of Assyria departed and went and returned and lived in Nineveh.вАЭ
n2303767: sn The assassination of King Sennacherib probably took place in 681 b.c.
n2303768: tn The verb that introduces this verse serves as a discourse particle and is untranslated; see note on вАЬin the futureвАЭ in 2:2.
n2303769: sn No such Mesopotamian god is presently known. Perhaps the name Nisroch is a textual variation of Nusku, the Mesopotamian god of light and fire. Other proposals have tried to relate the name to Ashur, the chief god of the Assyria, or to Ninurta, the Assyrian god of war.
n2303770: sn Extra-biblical sources also mention the assassination of Sennacherib, though they refer to only one assassin. See M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 239-40.
n230381: tn Heb вАЬwas sick to the point of dyingвАЭ; NRSV вАЬbecame sick and was at the point of death.вАЭ
n230382: tn Heb вАЬwalked before you.вАЭ For a helpful discussion of the background and meaning of this Hebrew idiom, see M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 254.
n230383: tn Heb вАЬand with a complete heartвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬwith a perfect heart.вАЭ
n230384: tn Heb вАЬand that which is good in your eyes I have done.вАЭ
n230385: tn Heb вАЬwept with great weepingвАЭ; NCV вАЬcried loudlyвАЭ; TEV вАЬbegan to cry bitterly.вАЭ
n230387: tn The words вАЬIsaiah repliedвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. In the present form of the Hebrew text v. 7 is joined directly to v. 6, but vv. 21-22, if original to Isaiah 38, must be inserted here. See 2 Kgs 20:7-8.
n230388:
tn Heb вАЬthe shadow on the steps which it [the sun] had gone down, on the steps of Ahaz, with the sun, back ten steps.вАЭ
sn These steps probably functioned as a type of sundial. See HALOT 614 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„Ь÷Є„ФвАО and M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 256.
n230389: tn Heb вАЬand the sun returned ten steps on the steps which it had gone down.вАЭ
n2303810: tn Or вАЬI saidвАЭ (KJV, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2303811: tn The precise meaning of the phrase вАП„С÷Љ÷і„У÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ „Щ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЩвАО (bidmi yamay, вАЬin the [?] of my daysвАЭ) is uncertain. According to HALOT 226 s.v. вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„ЩвАО this word is a hapax legomenon meaning вАЬhalf.вАЭ Others derive the form from вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ю÷і„ЩвАО (dami, вАЬquiet, rest, peacefulnessвАЭ).
n2303812: tn The precise meaning of the verb is uncertain. The Pual of of вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) occurs only here and in Exod 38:21, where it appears to mean вАЬpassed in reviewвАЭ or вАЬmustered.вАЭ Perhaps the idea is, вАЬI have been called away for the remainder of my years.вАЭ To bring out the sense more clearly, one can translate, вАЬI am deprived of the rest of my years.вАЭ
n2303813: tn The Hebrew text has вАП„Щ÷Є„Ф÷Љ „Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (yah yah, the abbreviated form of вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО [yehvah] repeated), but this probably should be emended to вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО.
n2303814: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Ч÷Є„У÷ґ„ЬвАО (khadel), which appears to be derived from a verbal root meaning вАЬto cease, refrain.вАЭ But the form has probably suffered an error of transmission; the original form (attested in a few medieval Hebrew mss) was likely вАП„Ч÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„УвАО (kheled, вАЬworldвАЭ).
n2303815: tn According to HALOT 217 s.v. вАП„У÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО this noun is a hapax legomenon meaning вАЬdwelling place,вАЭ derived from a verbal root meaning вАЬliveвАЭ (see Ps 84:10). For an interpretation that understands the form as the well-attested noun meaning вАЬgeneration,вАЭ see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:679, n. 4.
n2303816: tn The verb form appears to be a Niphal from вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (galah), which normally means вАЬuncovered, revealedвАЭ in the Niphal. Because of the following reference to a shepherdвАЩs tent, some prefer to emend the form to вАП„Х÷∞„†÷Є„Т÷Ј„ЬвАО, a Niphal from вАП„Т÷Є„Ь÷Є„ЬвАО (galal, вАЬrollвАЭ) and translate вАЬis rolled [or вАЬfoldedвАЭ] up.вАЭ
n2303817: tn Heb вАЬI rolled up, like a weaver, my lifeвАЭ (so ASV).
n2303818: sn For a discussion of the imagery employed here, see J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah (NICOT), 1:684.
n2303819: tn Heb вАЬfrom day to night you bring me to an end.вАЭ
n2303820: tn The verb form in the Hebrew text is a Piel from вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷Ј„ФвАО (shavah). There are two homonyms вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷Ј„ФвАО, one meaning in the Piel вАЬlevel, smooth out,вАЭ the other вАЬset, place.вАЭ Neither fits in v. 13. It is likely that the original reading was вАП„©„Б÷і„Х÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (shivva њti, вАЬI cry outвАЭ) from the verbal root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷Ј„ҐвАО (shava њ), which occurs exclusively in the Piel.
n2303821: tn Heb вАЬfrom day to night you bring me to an end.вАЭ
n2303822: tn Or вАЬmoanвАЭ (ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV); KJV, CEV вАЬmourn.вАЭ
n2303823: tn Heb вАЬmy eyes become weak, toward the height.вАЭ
n2303824: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here and in v. 16 is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2303825: tn Heb вАЬstand surety for me.вАЭ Hezekiah seems to be picturing himself as a debtor who is being exploited; he asks that the Lord might relieve his debt and deliver him from the oppressive creditor.
n2303826: tn Heb вАЬand he has spoken and he has acted.вАЭ
n2303827: tn Heb вАЬbecause of the bitterness of my soul.вАЭ
n2303828: tn The translation offered here is purely speculative. The text as it stands is difficult and obscure. It reads literally, вАЬO Lord, on account of them [the suffix is masculine plural], they live, and to all in them [the suffix is feminine plural], life of my spirit.вАЭ
n2303829: tn The prefixed verbal form could be taken as indicative, вАЬyou restore my health,вАЭ but the following imperatival form suggests it be understood as an imperfect of request.
n2303830: tn Heb вАЬLook, for peace bitterness was to me bitterвАЭ; NAB вАЬthus is my bitterness transformed into peace.вАЭ
n2303831: tc The Hebrew text reads, вАЬyou loved my soul,вАЭ but this does not fit syntactically with the following prepositional phrase. вАП„Ч÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„І÷∞„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (khashaqta, вАЬyou lovedвАЭ), may reflect an aural error; most emend the form to вАП„Ч÷Є„©„В÷Ј„Ы÷∞„™вАО, (khasakht, вАЬyou held backвАЭ).
n2303832: tn вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (beli) most often appears as a negation, meaning вАЬwithout,вАЭ suggesting the meaning вАЬnothingness, oblivion,вАЭ here. Some translate вАЬdecayвАЭ or вАЬdestruction.вАЭ
n2303833: tn Heb вАЬfor you threw behind your back all my sins.вАЭ
n2303834: tn Or вАЬForвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2303835: tn The negative particle is understood by ellipsis in this line. See GKC 483 ¬І152.z.
n2303836: tn The infinitive construct is used here to indicate that an action is imminent. See GKC 348-49 ¬І114.i, and IBHS 610 ¬І36.2.3g.
n2303837: tn Heb вАЬand music [or perhaps, вАЬstringed instrumentsвАЭ] we will play.вАЭ
n2303838:
tn Heb вАЬall the days of our lives in the house of the Lord.вАЭ
sn Note that vv. 21-22 have been placed between vv. 6-7, where they logically belong. See 2 Kgs 20:7-8.
n2303839: tc If original to Isaiah 38, vv. 21-22 have obviously been misplaced in the course of the textвАЩs transmission, and would most naturally be placed here, between Isa 38:6 and 38:7. See 2 Kgs 20:7-8, where these verses are placed at this point in the narrative, not at the end. Another possibility is that these verses were not in the original account, and a scribe, familiar with the 2 Kgs version of the story, appended vv. 21-22 to the end of the account in Isaiah 38.
n230391: tn Heb вАЬwas happy withвАЭ; NAB, NASB вАЬwas pleasedвАЭ; NIV вАЬreceived the envoys gladly.вАЭ
n230392: tn Heb вАЬthere was nothing which Hezekiah did not show them in his house and in all his kingdom.вАЭ
n230393: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (Isaiah) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n230395: tn Heb вАЬSome of your sons, who go out from you, whom you father.вАЭ
n230396: tn Heb вАЬgoodвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB вАЬfavorable.вАЭ
n230397: tn Heb вАЬand he said.вАЭ The verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО ( Њamar, вАЬsayвАЭ) is sometimes used of what one thinks (that is, says to oneself).
n230398: tn Or вАЬsurelyвАЭ; cf. CEV вАЬAt least.вАЭ
n230401: tn The pronominal suffix is second masculine plural. The identity of the addressee is uncertain: (1) GodвАЩs people may be addressed, or (2) the unidentified heralds commanded to comfort Jerusalem.
n230402: tn Heb вАЬspeak to the heart of Jerusalem.вАЭ Jerusalem is personified as a woman.
n230404: tn Heb вАЬthat she is filled [with] her warfare.вАЭ Some understand вАП„¶÷Є„С÷Є„РвАО (tsavah, вАЬwarfareвАЭ) as meaning вАЬhard serviceвАЭ or вАЬcompulsory laborвАЭ in this context.
n230405: tn Heb вАЬthat her punishment is accepted [as satisfactory].вАЭ
n230406: tn Heb вАЬfor she has received from the hand of the Lord double.вАЭ The principle of the double portion in punishment is also seen in Jer 16:18; 17:18 and Rev 18:6. For examples of the double portion in Israelite law, see Exod 22:4, 7, 9 (double restitution by a thief) and Deut 21:17 (double inheritance portion for the firstborn).
n230407: tn Heb вАЬmake level a built road.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„®вАО (yashar) in the Piel means вАЬto make smooth, or straight.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„°÷і„Ь÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (mesillah) typically refers to a main road, possibly paved with stones or made level with fill (see HALOT 606 s.v. and The Concise DCH 230 s.v.).
n230408: sn Most translations render this as вАЬdesertвАЭ (KJV, NASB, ESV, NRSV, NIV 2011, Holman), вАЬwildernessвАЭ (NIV 1984), or вАЬwastelandвАЭ (NLV). The rift valley (вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО,  њaravah), which extends from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba, is quite arid and desert-like in the areas near the Dead Sea and southward (see the note at Num 22:1). But the point here has more to do with preparation for a royal visit. To come to Jerusalem from the east requires coming through the rift valley (or Jordan Valley). Thematically, God is typically portrayed as coming to Israel from the east. Similarly in the Gospel accounts Jesus approaches Jerusalem from the east.
n230409: tn Or вАЬglory.вАЭ The LordвАЩs вАЬgloryвАЭ is his theophanic radiance and royal splendor (see Isa 6:3; 24:23; 35:2; 60:1; 66:18-19).
n2304010: tn Heb вАЬfleshвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB); NAB, NIV вАЬmankindвАЭ; TEV вАЬthe whole human race.вАЭ
n2304012: tn Heb вАЬthe mouth of the Lord has spokenвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304013: tn Heb вАЬand he says.вАЭ Apparently a second вАЬvoiceвАЭ responds to the command of the first вАЬvoice.вАЭ
n2304014: tn The words вАЬthe first voice respondsвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The first voice tells the second one what to declare.
n2304015: tn Heb вАЬall flesh is grass.вАЭ The point of the metaphor is explained in v. 7.
n2304016: tn Heb вАЬand all his loyalty.вАЭ The antecedent of the third masculine suffix is вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„©„В÷Є„®вАО (basar, вАЬfleshвАЭ), which refers collectively to mankind. The LXX, apparently understanding the antecedent as вАЬgrass,вАЭ reads вАЬglory,вАЭ but вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed) rarely, if ever, has this nuance. The normal meaning of вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (вАЬfaithfulness, loyalty, devotionвАЭ) fits very well in the argument. Human beings and their faithfulness (verbal expressions of faithfulness are specifically in view; cf. NRSV вАЬconstancyвАЭ) are short-lived and unreliable, in stark contrast to the decrees and promises of the eternal God.
n2304017: tn The Hebrew text has вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷Ј „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ruakh yehvah), which in this context probably does not refer to the LordвАЩs personal Spirit. The phrase is better translated вАЬthe breath of the Lord,вАЭ or вАЬthe wind of [i.e., sent by] the Lord.вАЭ The LordвАЩs sovereign control over nature, including the hot desert winds that dry up vegetation, is in view here (cf. Ps 147:18; Isa 59:19).
n2304018: tn Heb вАЬthe peopleвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304019: tn Heb вАЬbut the word of our God stands forever.вАЭ In this context the divine вАЬwordвАЭ specifically refers to his decreed promise assuring Jerusalem that her suffering is over and his glorious return imminent (vv. 1-5).
n2304020: tn The second feminine singular imperatives are addressed to personified Zion/Jerusalem, who is here told to ascend a high hill and proclaim the good news of the LordвАЩs return to the other towns of Judah. Isa 41:27 and 52:7 speak of a herald sent to Zion, but the masculine singular form вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷Ј„©„В÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (mevasser) is used in these verses, in contrast to the feminine singular form вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷Ј„©„В÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„™вАО (mevasseret) employed in 40:9, where Zion is addressed as a herald.
n2304021: tn Heb вАЬcomes as a strong oneвАЭ; ASV вАЬwill come as a mighty one.вАЭ The preposition вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО (bet) here carries the nuance вАЬin the capacity of.вАЭ It indicates that the Lord possesses the quality expressed by the noun. See GKC 379 ¬І119.i and HALOT 104 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО.
n2304022: tn Heb вАЬhis arm rules for himвАЭ (so NIV, NRSV). The LordвАЩs вАЬarmвАЭ symbolizes his military power (see Isa 51:9-10; 63:5).
n2304023: tn As the Lord returns to Jerusalem as a victorious warrior, he brings with him the spoils of victory, called here his вАЬrewardвАЭ and вАЬprize.вАЭ These terms might also be translated вАЬwagesвАЭ and вАЬrecompense.вАЭ Verse 11 indicates that his rescued people, likened to a flock of sheep, are his reward.
n2304024: tn Heb вАЬin his bosomвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV), an expression which reflects closeness and protective care.
n2304025: tn The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ю„Щ „Щ„ЭвАО (вАЬwaters of the seaвАЭ), a reading followed by NAB.
n2304026: tn Heb вАЬwith a span.вАЭ A вАЬspanвАЭ was the distance between the ends of the thumb and the little finger of the spread handвАЭ (BDB 285 s.v. вАП„Ц÷ґ„®÷ґ„™вАО).
n2304027: tn Or вАЬthe heavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2304028: tn Heb вАЬor weighed by a third part [of a measure].вАЭ
n2304029: sn The implied answer to the rhetorical questions of v. 12 is, вАЬNo one but the Lord.вАЭ The Lord, and no other, created the world. Like a merchant weighing out silver or commodities on a scale, the Lord established the various components of the physical universe in precise proportions.
n2304030: tn Perhaps the verb is used metonymically here in the sense of вАЬadvisesвАЭ (note the following line).
n2304031: tn In this context вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh) likely refers to the LordвАЩs вАЬmind,вАЭ or mental faculties, rather than his personal Spirit (see BDB 925 s.v. 6).
n2304032: tn Heb вАЬor [as] the man of his counsel causes him to know?вАЭ
n2304033: tn Heb вАЬWith whom did he consult, so that he gave discernment to him?вАЭ
n2304034: tn Heb вАЬand taught him.вАЭ The vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive with prefixed verbal form continues the previous line. The translation employs an interrogative pronoun for stylistic reasons.
n2304035: tn The phrase вАП„Р÷є„®÷Ј„Ч „Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО ( Њorakh mishpat) could be translated вАЬpath of justiceвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV), but in this context, where creative ability and skill is in view, the phrase is better understood in the sense of вАЬthe way that is proper or fittingвАЭ (see BDB 1049 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО 6); cf. NIV, NCV вАЬthe right way.вАЭ
n2304036:
tn Heb вАЬor the way of understanding causes him to know?вАЭ
sn The implied answer to the rhetorical questions in vv. 13-14 is, вАЬNo one.вАЭ In contrast to Marduk, the creator-god of Mesopotamian myths who receives help from the god of wisdom, the Lord neither needs nor receives any such advice or help. See R. Whybray, Heavenly Counsellor (SOTSMS), 64-77.
n2304039: tn The words вАЬfor a sacrificeвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2304040: sn The point is that not even the Lebanon forest could supply enough wood and animals for an adequate sacrifice to the Lord.
n2304041: tn Heb вАЬ[as derived] from nothing and unformed.вАЭ
n2304043: tn The first two words of the verse (вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷∞„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Я „™÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО, hamesukkan terumah) are problematic. Some take вАП„Ю÷∞„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО as an otherwise unattested Pual participle from вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЯвАО (sakhan, вАЬbe poorвАЭ) and translate вАЬthe one who is impoverished.вАЭ вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (terumah, вАЬcontributionвАЭ) can then be taken as an adverbial accusative, вАЬwith respect to a contribution,вАЭ and the entire line translated, вАЬthe one who is too impoverished for such a contribution [i.e., the metal idol of v. 19?] selects wood that will not rot.вАЭ However, вАП„Ю÷∞„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО is probably the name of a tree used in idol manufacturing (cognate with Akkadian musukkanu, cf. H. R. Cohen, Biblical Hapax Legomena [SBLDS], 133). вАП„Ю÷∞„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО may be a scribal interpretive addition attempting to specify вАП„Ґ÷µ„•вАО ( њets) or вАП„Ґ÷µ„•вАО may be a scribal attempt to categorize вАП„Ю÷∞„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО. How an idol constitutes a вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (вАЬcontributionвАЭ) is not entirely clear.
n2304044: tn Or вАЬset upвАЭ (ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); KJV, NASB вАЬto prepare.вАЭ
n2304045: tn Heb вАЬthe circle of the earthвАЭ (so KJV, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2304046: tn The words вАЬbefore himвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2304047: tn The otherwise unattested noun вАП„У÷Љ÷є„ІвАО (doq), translated here вАЬthin curtain,вАЭ is apparently derived from the verbal root вАП„У„І„ІвАО (вАЬcrushвАЭ) from which is derived the adjective вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„ІвАО (daq, вАЬthinвАЭ; see HALOT 229 s.v. вАП„У„І„ІвАО). The nuance вАЬcurtainвАЭ is implied from the parallelism (see вАЬtentвАЭ in the next line).
n2304048: tn The meaning of the otherwise unattested verb вАП„Ю÷Є„™÷Ј„ЧвАО (matakh, вАЬspread outвАЭ) is determined from the parallelism (note the corresponding verb вАЬstretch outвАЭ in the previous line) and supported by later Hebrew and Aramaic cognates. See HALOT 654 s.v. *вАП„Ю„™„ФвАО.
n2304049: tn Heb вАЬlike a tent [in which] to liveвАЭ; NAB, NASB вАЬlike a tent to dwell (live NIV, NRSV) in.вАЭ
n2304050: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304051: tn Heb вАЬLift on high your eyes and see.вАЭ
n2304052: tn The words вАЬheavenly lightsвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. See the following lines.
n2304053: tn Heb вАЬthe one who brings out by number their host.вАЭ The stars are here likened to a huge army that the Lord leads out. Perhaps the next line pictures God calling roll. If so, the final line may be indicating that none of them dares вАЬgo AWOL.вАЭ (вАЬAWOLвАЭ is a military acronym for вАЬabsent without leave.вАЭ)
n2304054: tn Heb вАЬmy way is hidden from the LordвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304055: tn Heb вАЬand from my God my justice passes awayвАЭ; NRSV вАЬmy right is disregarded by my God.вАЭ
n2304056: tn Heb вАЬthe ends of the earth,вАЭ but this is a merism, where the earthвАЩs extremities stand for its entirety, i.e., the extremities and everything in between them.
n2304057: sn Exiled IsraelвАЩs complaint (v. 27) implies that God might be limited in some way. Perhaps he, like so many of the pagan gods, has died. Or perhaps his jurisdiction is limited to Judah and does not include Babylon. Maybe he is unable to devise an adequate plan to rescue his people, or is unable to execute it. But v. 28 affirms that he is not limited temporally or spatially nor are his power and wisdom restricted in any way. He can and will deliver his people, if they respond in hopeful faith (v. 31a).
n2304058: tn Heb вАЬstumbling they stumble.вАЭ The verbal idea is emphasized by the infinitive absolute.
n2304059: tn The word вАЬhelpвАЭ in the phrase вАЬfor the LordвАЩs helpвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarification, as is the possessive on вАЬLord.вАЭ
n2304060: tn Heb вАЬthey rise up [on] wings like eaglesвАЭ (TEV similar).
n230411: tn Or вАЬislandsвАЭ (KJV, NIV, CEV); TEV вАЬdistant landsвАЭ; NLT вАЬlands beyond the sea.вАЭ
n230412: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat) could be translated вАЬjudgment,вАЭ but here it seems to refer to the dispute or debate between the Lord and the nations.
n230413: sn The expression this one from the east refers to the Persian conqueror Cyrus, as later texts indicate (see 44:28-45:6; 46:11; 48:14-16).
n230414: tn The interrogative particle is understood by ellipsis.
n230415: tn Heb вАЬ[in] righteousness called him to his foot.вАЭ
n230416: tn Heb вАЬhe [the Lord] places before him [Cyrus] nations.вАЭ
n230417: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„®÷∞„У÷Љ÷∞вАО (yard) is an otherwise unattested Hiphil form from вАП„®÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (radah, вАЬruleвАЭ). But the Hiphil makes no sense with вАЬkingsвАЭ as object; one must understand an ellipsis and supply вАЬhimвАЭ (Cyrus) as the object. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Щ„Х÷є„®÷і„Щ„УвАО (yorid), which appears to be a Hiphil form from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО (yarad, вАЬgo downвАЭ). Others suggest reading вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷є„УвАО (yarod), a Qal form from вАП„®÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (radad, вАЬbeat downвАЭ).
n230418: sn The point is that they are powerless before CyrusвАЩ military power and scatter before him.
n230419: tn Heb вАЬ[in] peaceвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬsafelyвАЭ; NASB вАЬin safetyвАЭ; NIV вАЬunscathed.вАЭ
n2304110: tn Heb вАЬa way with his feet he does not come [or вАЬenterвАЭ].вАЭ One could translate, вАЬby a way he was not [previously] entering with his feet.вАЭ This would mean that he is advancing into new territory and expanding his conquests. The present translation assumes this is a hyperbolic description of his speedy advance. He moves so quickly he does not enter the way with his feet, i.e., his feet donвАЩt even touch the ground. See C. R. North, Second Isaiah, 94.
n2304111: tn Heb вАЬWho acts and accomplishes?вАЭ; NASB вАЬWho has performed and accomplished it.вАЭ
n2304112: tn The interrogative particle is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n2304113: tn Heb вАЬI, the Lord, [am with] the first, and with the last ones I [am] he.вАЭ
n2304114: tn Or вАЬislandsвАЭ (NIV, CEV); NCV вАЬfaraway placesвАЭ; NLT вАЬlands beyond the sea.вАЭ
n2304115: tn Heb вАЬthe ends of the earth,вАЭ but this is a merism, where the earthвАЩs extremities stand for its entirety, i.e., the extremities and everything in between them.
n2304116: tn Heb вАЬeach his neighbor helpsвАЭ; NCV вАЬThe workers help each other.вАЭ
n2304117: tn The verb вАЬencouragesвАЭ is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n2304118: tn Heb вАЬsaying of the welding, вАШIt is good.вАЩвАЭ
n2304119: tn Or perhaps, вАЬcovenantal partnerвАЭ (see 1 Kgs 5:15 HT [5:1 ET]; 2 Chr 20:7).
n2304120: tn Heb вАЬwhom I have taken hold of [i.e., to lead back].вАЭ
n2304121: tn According to BDB (1043 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО), the verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„©„Б÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„ҐвАО (tishta њ) in the second line of the poetic couplet is a Hitpael form from the root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (sha њah, вАЬgaze,вАЭ with metathesis of the stem prefix and the first root letter). Taking the Hitpael as iterative, one may then translate вАЬdo not anxiously look about.вАЭ However, the alleged Hitpael form of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (sha њah) only occurs here and in verse 23. HALOT 1671 s.v. вАП„©„Б„™„ҐвАО proposes that the verb is instead a Qal form from the root вАП„©„Б„™„ҐвАО (вАЬfearвАЭ). Its attestation in cognate Semitic languages, including Ugaritic (discovered after the publishing of BDB), suggests the existence of this root. The poetic structure of v. 10 also supports the proposal, for the form in question is in synonymous parallelism to вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷µ„РвАО (yare Њ, вАЬfearвАЭ).
n2304122: tn The вАЬright handвАЭ is a symbol of the LordвАЩs power to deliver (Exod 15:6, 12) and protect (Ps 63:9 HT [63:8 ET]). Here вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО (tsedeq) has its well-attested nuance of вАЬvindicated righteousness,вАЭ i.e., вАЬvictory, deliveranceвАЭ (see 45:8; 51:5, and BDB 841-42 s.v.).
n2304123: tn Heb вАЬthe men of your strifeвАЭ; NASB вАЬthose who contend with you.вАЭ
n2304124: tn Heb вАЬlike nothingвАЭ; NAB вАЬcome to nought.вАЭ
n2304125: tn Heb вАЬthe men of your struggleвАЭ; NASB вАЬthose who quarrel with you.вАЭ
n2304126: tn Heb вАЬthe men of your battleвАЭ; NAB вАЬwho do battle with you.вАЭ
n2304127: tn Heb вАЬO worm JacobвАЭ (NAB, NIV). The worm metaphor suggests that Jacob is insignificant and despised.
n2304128: tn On the basis of the parallelism (note вАЬwormвАЭ) and an alleged Akkadian cognate, some read вАЬlouseвАЭ or вАЬweevil.вАЭ Cf. NAB вАЬO maggot IsraelвАЭ; NRSV вАЬyou insect Israel.вАЭ
n2304129: tn Heb вАЬyour kinsman redeemer.вАЭ A вАП„Т÷Љ÷є„Р÷µ„ЬвАО (go Њel, вАЬkinsman redeemerвАЭ) was a protector of the extended familyвАЩs interests.
n2304130: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304131: tn Heb вАЬintoвАЭ (so NIV); ASV вАЬhave made thee to be.вАЭ
n2304133: sn The mountains and hills symbolize hostile nations that are obstacles to IsraelвАЩs restoration.
n2304134: tn Heb вАЬwill answer themвАЭ (so ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2304135: sn The rift valley (вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО,  њaravah) is known for its arid, desert-like conditions in the area of the Dead Sea and southward (although it also includes the Jordan Valley, extending from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba). The point here is the contrast from its normal arid conditions to being productive with trees, which implies being watered. Similarly, the wilderness (вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„®вАО, midbar) in the first line is an area that receives less than twelve inches of rainfall annually and so cannot support trees.
n2304136: tn The words вАЬI will do thisвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The Hebrew text has here simply, вАЬin order that.вАЭ
n2304137: tn Heb вАЬtheyвАЭ; NAB, NRSV вАЬthat all may seeвАЭ; CEV, NLT вАЬEveryone will see.вАЭ
n2304139: tn Or вАЬcreated itвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); TEV вАЬhas made it happen.вАЭ
n2304141: sn Apparently this challenge is addressed to the pagan idol gods, see vv. 23-24.
n2304142: tn Heb вАЬAs for the former things, tell us what they are!вАЭ
n2304143: tn Heb вАЬso we might set [them to] our heart.вАЭ
n2304145: tn Heb вАЬDeclare the coming things, with respect to the end.вАЭ
n2304146:
tc The translation assumes the Qere (вАП„Х÷∞„†÷і„®÷∞„Р÷ґ„ФвАО [venir Њeh], from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷µ„РвАО [yare Њ], вАЬbe afraidвАЭ).
tn Heb вАЬso we might be frightened and afraid together.вАЭ On the meaning of the verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„™÷Є„ҐвАО (shata њ), see the note at v. 10.
n2304147: tn Heb вАЬan object of disgust [is he who] chooses you.вАЭ
n2304148: sn That is, Cyrus the Persian. See the note at v. 2.
n2304149: tn Heb вАЬ[one] from the rising of the sun [who] calls in my name.вАЭ
n2304150: tn The Hebrew text has вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„С÷є„РвАО (veyavo Њ, вАЬand he comesвАЭ), but this likely needs to be emended to an original вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„С÷Є„°вАО (vayyavas), from вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„°вАО (bus, вАЬstep onвАЭ).
n2304151: tn The words вАЬwho announced itвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The interrogative particle and verb are understood by ellipsis (see the preceding line).
n2304152: tn The Hebrew text reads simply, вАЬFirst to Zion, вАШLook here they are!вАЩвАЭ The words вАЬI decreedвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2304154: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Р÷Є„Х÷ґ„ЯвАО ( Њaven, вАЬdeception,вАЭ i.e., вАЬfalseвАЭ), but the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Р„Щ„ЯвАО (вАЬnothingвАЭ), which forms a better parallel with вАП„Р÷ґ„§÷ґ„°вАО ( Њefes, вАЬnothingвАЭ) in the next line. See also 40:17 and 41:12.
n2304155: tn Heb вАЬtheir statues are wind and nothingвАЭ; NASB вАЬwind and emptinessвАЭ; NIV вАЬwind and confusion.вАЭ
n230421: sn Verses 1-7 contain the first of IsaiahвАЩs вАЬservant songs,вАЭ which describe the ministry of a special, ideal servant who accomplishes GodвАЩs purposes for Israel and the nations. This song depicts the servant as a just king who brings justice to the earth and relief for the oppressed. The other songs appear in 49:1-13; 50:4-11; and 52:13-53:12.
n230422: tn Heb вАЬhe will bring out justiceвАЭ (cf. ASV, NASB, NRSV).
n230423: sn Like the ideal king portrayed in Isa 11:1-9, the servant is energized by the divine spirit and establishes justice on the earth.
n230424: tn Heb вАЬhe will not cause his voice to be heard in the street.вАЭ
n230425: sn The вАЬcrushed reedвАЭ and вАЬdim wickвАЭ symbolize the weak and oppressed who are on the verge of extinction.
n230426: tn Heb вАЬfaithfully he will bring out justiceвАЭ (cf. NASB, NRSV).
n230427: tn For rhetorical effect the terms used to describe the вАЬcrushed (вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Ј„•вАО, ratsats) reedвАЭ and вАЬdim (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ф÷Є„ФвАО, kahah) wickвАЭ in v. 3 are repeated here.
n230428: tn Or вАЬislandsвАЭ (NIV); NLT вАЬdistant lands beyond the sea.вАЭ
n230429: tn Or вАЬhis lawвАЭ (KJV, ASV, NASB, NIV) or вАЬhis instructionвАЭ (NLT).
n2304210: tn Heb вАЬthe God.вАЭ The definite article here indicates distinctiveness or uniqueness.
n2304211: tn Heb вАЬand its offspringвАЭ (so NASB); NIV вАЬall that comes out of it.вАЭ
n2304212: tn Heb вАЬand spirit [i.e., вАЬbreathвАЭ] to the ones walking in itвАЭ (NAB, NASB, and NRSV all similar).
n2304213: tn Heb вАЬcall you in righteousness.вАЭ The pronoun вАЬyouвАЭ is masculine singular, referring to the servant. See the note at 41:2.
n2304214: tn The translation assumes the verb is derived from the root вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (natsar, вАЬprotectвАЭ). Some prefer to derive it from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (yatsar, вАЬformвАЭ).
n2304215: tn Heb вАЬa covenant of people.вАЭ A person cannot literally be a covenant; вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷і„Щ„™вАО (berit) is probably metonymic here, indicating a covenant mediator. The precise identity of вАП„Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО ( њam, вАЬpeopleвАЭ) is uncertain. In v. 5 вАП„Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО refers to mankind, and the following reference to вАЬnationsвАЭ also favors this. But in 49:8, where the phrase вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷і„Щ„™ „Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО occurs again, Israel seems to be in view.
n2304216: sn Light here symbolizes deliverance from bondage and oppression; note the parallelism in 49:6b and in 51:4-6.
n2304217: tn Or вАЬthe GentilesвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NIV); the same Hebrew word can be translated вАЬnationsвАЭ or вАЬGentilesвАЭ depending on the context.
n2304218: sn This does not refer to literal physical healing of the blind. As the next two lines suggest, this refers metonymically to freeing captives from their dark prisons where their eyes have grown unaccustomed to light.
n2304219: sn This does not refer to hardened, dangerous criminals, who would have been executed for their crimes in ancient Near Eastern society. This verse refers to political prisoners or victims of social injustice.
n2304220: tn Heb вАЬthe former things, look, they have come.вАЭ
n2304221: tn Heb вАЬbefore they sprout up, I cause you to hear.вАЭ The pronoun вАЬyouвАЭ is plural, referring to the people of Israel. In this verse вАЬthe former thingsвАЭ are the LordвАЩs earlier predictive oracles which have come to pass, while вАЬthe new thingsвАЭ are predicted events that have not yet begun to take place. вАЬThe former thingsвАЭ are earlier events in IsraelвАЩs history which God announced beforehand, such as the Exodus (see 43:16-18). вАЬThe new thingsвАЭ are the predictions about the servant (42:1-7). and may also include CyrusвАЩ conquests (41:25-27).
n2304222: tn Heb вАЬhis praise.вАЭ The phrase stands parallel to вАЬnew songвАЭ in the previous line.
n2304223: tn Heb вАЬand its fullnessвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬand all that is in it.вАЭ
n2304224: tn Or вАЬislandsвАЭ (NASB, NIV); NLT вАЬdistant coastlands.вАЭ
n2304225: tn Heb вАЬLet them ascribe to the Lord glory.вАЭ
n2304226: tn Heb вАЬand his praise in the coastlands [or вАЬislandsвАЭ] let them declare.вАЭ
n2304227: tn Heb вАЬlike a man of war he stirs up zealвАЭ (NIV similar).
n2304228: tn Or perhaps, вАЬhe triumphs over his enemiesвАЭ (cf. NIV); NLT вАЬwill crush all his enemies.вАЭ
n2304229: tn Heb вАЬsilentвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, TEV, NLT); CEV вАЬhave held my temper.вАЭ
n2304230: sn The imagery depicts the Lord as a warrior who is eager to fight and can no longer hold himself back from the attack.
n2304231: tn Heb вАЬI will dry up the mountains and hills.вАЭ The вАЬmountains and hillsвАЭ stand by synecdoche for the trees that grow on them. Some prefer to derive the verb from a homonymic root and translate, вАЬI will lay waste.вАЭ
n2304232: tc The Hebrew text reads, вАЬI will turn streams into coastlands [or вАЬislandsвАЭ].вАЭ Scholars who believe that this reading makes little sense have proposed an emendation of вАП„Р÷і„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њiyyim, вАЬislandsвАЭ) to вАП„¶÷і„Щ÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (tsiyyot, вАЬdry placesвАЭ; cf. NCV, NLT, TEV). However, since all the versions support the MT reading, there is insufficient grounds for an emendation here. Although the imagery of changing rivers into islands is somewhat strange, J. N. Oswalt describes this imagery against the backdrop of rivers of the Near East. The receding of these rivers at times occasioned the appearance of previously submerged islands (Isaiah [NICOT], 2:126).
n2304233: sn The imagery of this verse, which depicts the Lord bringing a curse of infertility to the earth, metaphorically describes how the Lord will destroy his enemies.
n2304234: tn Heb вАЬa way they do not knowвАЭ (so NASB); NRSV вАЬa road they do not know.вАЭ
n2304235: tn Heb вАЬin paths they do not know I will make them walk.вАЭ
n2304236: tn Heb вАЬand the rough ground into a level place.вАЭ
n2304237: tn Heb вАЬbe ashamed with shameвАЭ; ASV, NASB вАЬbe utterly put to shame.вАЭ
n2304238: tn Heb вАЬlook to seeвАЭ; NAB, NCV вАЬlook and seeвАЭ; NRSV вАЬlook up and see.вАЭ
n2304239: tc The precise meaning of вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б÷ї„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (meshullam) in this context is uncertain. In later biblical Hebrew the form (which appears to be a Pual participle from the root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО, shalam) occurs as a proper name, Meshullam. The Pual of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (вАЬbe completeвАЭ) is attested with the meaning вАЬrepaid, requited,вАЭ but that makes little sense here. BDB 1023 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО relates the form to the denominative verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (вАЬbe at peaceвАЭ) and paraphrases вАЬone in a covenant of peaceвАЭ (J. N. Oswalt suggests вАЬthe covenanted oneвАЭ; Isaiah [NICOT], 2:128, n. 59) Some emend the form to вАП„Ю÷є„©„Б÷∞„Ь÷Є„ЭвАО (moshelam, вАЬtheir rulerвАЭ) or to вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б÷ї„Ь÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷і„ЩвАО (meshullakhi, вАЬmy sent [or вАЬcommissionedвАЭ] oneвАЭ), which fits nicely in the parallelism (note вАЬmy messengerвАЭ in the previous line). The translation above assumes an emendation to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷є „©„Б÷є„Ь÷∞„Ю÷і„ЩвАО (kemo sholemi, вАЬlike my allyвАЭ). Isaiah uses вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷євАО in 30:22 and perhaps 51:5; for вАП„©„Б÷є„Ь÷∞„Ю„ЩвАО (вАЬmy allyвАЭ) see Ps 7:5 HT (7:4 ET).
n2304240: tn Heb вАЬWho is blind but my servant, and deaf like my messenger I send? Who is blind like my commissioned one, blind like the servant of the Lord?вАЭ The point of the rhetorical questions is that no one is as blind/deaf as this servant. In this context the LordвАЩs вАЬservantвАЭ is exiled Israel (cf. 41:8-9), which is spiritually blind and deaf and has failed to fulfill GodвАЩs purpose for it. This servant stands in contrast to the ideal вАЬIsraelвАЭ of the servant songs.
n2304241: tn The consonantal text (Kethib) has a perfect, second person masculine singular; the marginal reading (Qere) has an infinitive absolute, which functions here as a finite verb.
n2304242: tn Heb вАЬbut you do not guard [i.e., retain in your memory]вАЭ; NIV вАЬbut have paid no attention.вАЭ
n2304243: tn Heb вАЬThe Lord was pleased for the sake of his righteousness [or вАЬjusticeвАЭ], he was magnifying [the] law and was making [it] glorious.вАЭ The Lord contrasts his good intentions for the people with their present crisis (v. 22). To demonstrate his just character and attract the nations, the Lord wanted to showcase his law among and through Israel (Deut 4:5-8). But Israel disobeyed (v. 24) and failed to carry out their commission.
n2304244: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ч„Х÷Љ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (bakhurim, вАЬyoung menвАЭ), but the text should be emended to вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ф„Х÷є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (behorim, вАЬin holesвАЭ).
n2304245: tn Heb вАЬand made to be hiddenвАЭ; NAB, NASB, NIV, TEV вАЬhidden away in prisons.вАЭ
n2304246: tn Heb вАЬthey became loot, and there was no one rescuing, plunder, and there was no one saying, вАШBring backвАЩ.вАЭ
n2304247: tn The interrogative particle is understood in the second line by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n2304248: tn Heb вАЬWho gave to the robber Jacob, and Israel to the looters?вАЭ In the first line the consonantal text (Kethib) has вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б„Х÷є„°÷ґ„ФвАО (meshoseh), a Polel participle from вАП„©„Б÷Є„°÷Є„ФвАО (shasah, вАЬplunderвАЭ). The marginal reading (Qere) is вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б÷і„Щ„°÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (meshissah), a noun meaning вАЬplunder.вАЭ In this case one could translate вАЬWho handed Jacob over as plunder?вАЭ
n2304249: tn Heb вАЬthey were not willing in his ways to walk, and they did not listen to his law.вАЭ
n2304250: tn The Hebrew third masculine singular pronoun, representing the nation, has been rendered as the third plural throughout this verse.
n2304251: tn Heb вАЬstrengthвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NAB вАЬfuryвАЭ; NASB вАЬfiercenessвАЭ; NIV вАЬviolence.вАЭ
n2304252: tn Heb вАЬand it blazed against him all around.вАЭ The subject of the third feminine singular verb вАЬblazedвАЭ is the divine вАП„Ч÷µ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (khemah, вАЬangerвАЭ) mentioned in the previous line.
n2304253: sn It is not that he did not know about the war, but he did not attribute this to GodвАЩs wrath.
n2304254: tn Heb вАЬbut he did not set [it] upon [his] heart.вАЭ The Hebrew word вАЬheartвАЭ also refers to the mind.
n230431: tn Or вАЬredeem.вАЭ See the note at 41:14. Cf. NCV вАЬsaved youвАЭ; CEV вАЬrescued youвАЭ; NLT вАЬransomed you.вАЭ
n230432: tn The verb is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n230433: tn Heb вАЬburnвАЭ (so NASB); NAB, NRSV, NLT вАЬconsumeвАЭ; NIV вАЬset you ablaze.вАЭ
n230434: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n230435: sn Seba is not the same as Sheba in southern Arabia; cf. Gen 1:10; 1 Chr 1:9.
n230436: tn Heb вАЬSince you are precious in my eyes and you are honored.вАЭ
n230437: tn Heb вАЬeveryone who is called by my nameвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n230438: tn Heb вАЬand the former things he was causing us to hear?вАЭ
n230439: tn Or вАЬknowвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304310: tn Heb вАЬand after me, there will not beвАЭ; NASB вАЬthere will be none after Me.вАЭ
n2304311: tn Heb вАЬhandвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬNo one can oppose what I do.вАЭ
n2304312: tn Or вАЬkinsman redeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n2304313: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304314: tn Heb вАЬand I bring down [as] fugitives all of them.вАЭ
n2304315: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬas for the Babylonians, in ships their joyful shout.вАЭ This might be paraphrased, вАЬeven the Babylonians in the ships [over which] they joyfully shouted.вАЭ The point would be that the Lord caused the Babylonians to flee for safety in the ships in which they took such great pride. A slight change in vocalization yields the reading вАЬinto mourning songs,вАЭ which provides a good contrast with вАЬjoyful shout.вАЭ The prefixed bet (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО) would indicate identity.
n2304316: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304317: tn Heb вАЬled out chariots and horses.вАЭ The words вАЬto destructionвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The verse refers to the destruction of the Egyptians at the Red Sea.
n2304318: tn Heb вАЬlay downвАЭ; NAB вАЬlie prostrate togetherвАЭ; CEV вАЬlie deadвАЭ; NRSV вАЬthey lie down.вАЭ
n2304319: tn Heb вАЬthe former thingsвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬforget all that.вАЭ
n2304320: tn Heb вАЬsprouts upвАЭ; NASB вАЬwill spring forth.вАЭ
n2304321: tn Or вАЬknowвАЭ (KJV, ASV); NASB вАЬbe aware ofвАЭ; NAB, NIV, NRSV вАЬperceive.вАЭ
n2304322: tn The Hebrew text has вАЬstreams,вАЭ probably under the influence of v. 20. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„†„™„Щ„С„Х„™вАО (вАЬpathsвАЭ).
n2304324: tn Heb вАЬ[so] they might declare my praise.вАЭ
n2304325: tn Or вАЬstriveвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬbeen weary of me.вАЭ
n2304326: tn Heb вАЬwith.вАЭ The words вАЬby demandingвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2304327: tn That is, вАЬcalamusвАЭ (so NIV); NCV, TEV, NLT вАЬincenseвАЭ; CEV вАЬspices.вАЭ
n2304328: tn Heb вАЬyou did not saturate meвАЭ; NASB вАЬNeither have you filled Me.вАЭ
n2304329: sn In vv. 22-24 the Lord appears to be condemning his people for failure to bring the proper sacrifices. However, this is problematic. If this refers to the nationвАЩs behavior while in exile, such cultic service was impossible and could hardly be expected by the Lord. If this refers to the nationвАЩs conduct before the exile, it contradicts other passages that depict Israel as bringing excessive sacrifices (see, e.g., Isa 1:11-14; Jer 6:20; Amos 4:4-5; 5:21-23). Rather than being a condemnation of IsraelвАЩs failure to bring sacrifices, these verses are better taken as a highly rhetorical comment on the worthlessness of IsraelвАЩs religious ritual. They may have brought sacrifices, but not to the Lord, for he did not accept them or even want them. See C. R. North, Second Isaiah, 127, and R. Whybray, Isaiah 40-66 (NCBC), 91.
n2304330: tn Heb вАЬyou, tell in order that you may be rightвАЭ; NAB вАЬprove your innocence.вАЭ
n2304331: tn Heb вАЬyour first father.вАЭ This could refer to Abraham (see 51:2), but elsewhere in Isaiah he does not appear in a negative light (see 29:22; 41:8; 63:16). A more likely candidate is Jacob/Israel, also referred to as the nationвАЩs вАЬfatherвАЭ elsewhere (see 58:14; 63:16).
n2304332: tn On the meaning of the term вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО (lits), see HALOT 590 s.v. вАП„Ю÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО. This may refer to the nationвАЩs prophets, priests, and/or kings.
n2304333: tn The word вАЬsubjectedвАЭ is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n230441: sn Jeshurun is a poetic name for Israel; it occurs here and in Deut 32:15; 33:5, 26.
n230442: tn Heb вАЬthe thirsty.вАЭ Parallelism suggests that dry ground is in view (see вАЬdry landвАЭ in the next line.)
n230443: tn Heb вАЬand streamsвАЭ; KJV вАЬfloods.вАЭ The verb вАЬcauseвА¶to flowвАЭ is supplied in the second line for clarity and for stylistic reasons.
n230444: tn The Hebrew term вАП„С÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО (ven) is usually taken as a preposition, in which case one might translate, вАЬamong the grass.вАЭ But вАП„С÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО is probably the name of a tree (cf. C. R. North, Second Isaiah, 133). If one alters the preposition bet (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО) to kaf (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞вАО), one can then read, вАЬlike a binu-tree.вАЭ (The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa supports this reading.) This forms a nice parallel to вАЬlike poplarsвАЭ in the next line. вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷і„Щ„®вАО (khatsir) is functioning as an adverbial accusative of location.
n230445: tn The Hebrew text has a Qal verb form, вАЬand another will call by the name of Jacob.вАЭ With support from Symmachus (an ancient Greek textual witness), some read the Niphal, вАЬand another will be called by the name of Jacob.вАЭ
n230446: tn Heb вАЬand by the name of Israel he will title.вАЭ Some, with support from several ancient versions, prefer to change the Piel (active) verb form to a Pual (passive), вАЬand he will be titled by the name of Israel.вАЭ
n230447: tn Heb вАЬhis kinsman redeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n230448: tn Heb вАЬlet him callвАЭ or вАЬlet him proclaimвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV); NAB вАЬLet him stand up and speak.вАЭ
n230449: tc The Hebrew text reads, вАЬfrom (the time) I established an ancient people, and the coming things.вАЭ Various emendations have been proposed. One of the options assumes the reading вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„Ґ÷і„Щ„Э „Ю÷µ„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„Э „Р„Х÷є„™÷і„Щ÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (mashmi њim me њolam  Њotiyyot); This literally reads вАЬthe ones causing to hear from antiquity coming things,вАЭ but more idiomatically would read вАЬas for those who predict from antiquity what will happenвАЭ (cf. NAB, NEB, REB). The emendation directs the attention of the reader to those who claim to be able to predict the future, challenging them to actually do what they claim they can do. The MT presents Yahweh as an example to whom these alleged вАЬpredictors of the futureвАЭ can compare themselves. Since the ancient versions are unanimous in their support of the MT, the emendations should be set aside.
n2304410: tn Heb and those things which are coming let them declare for themselves.вАЭ
n2304411: tn BDB 923 s.v. вАП„®÷Є„Ф÷Є„ФвАО derives this verb from an otherwise unattested root, while HALOT 403 s.v. вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО defines it as вАЬbe stupefiedвАЭ on the basis of an Arabic cognate. The form likely needs to be emended to вАП„™„Щ„®„Р„ХвАО, the reading attested in the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa.
n2304412: tn Heb вАЬrockвАЭ or вАЬrocky cliff,вАЭ a title that depicts God as a protective refuge in his role as sovereign king; thus the translation вАЬsheltering rock.вАЭ
n2304413: tn The rhetorical question is sarcastic. The sense is, вАЬWho is foolish enoughвА¶?вАЭ
n2304414: tn The pronoun вАЬhisвАЭ probably refers to the one who forms/casts an idol (v. 10), in which case it refers to the craftsmanвАЩs associates in the idol-manufacturing guild.
n2304415: sn The point seems to be this: if the idols are the mere products of human hands, then those who trust in them will be disappointed, for man-made gods are incapable of helping their вАЬcreators.вАЭ
n2304416: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Є„УвАО (ma њatsad), which refers to some type of tool used for cutting, occurs only here and in Jer 10:3. See HALOT 615 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Є„УвАО.
n2304417: tn Some English versions take the pronoun вАЬitвАЭ to refer to an idol being fashioned by the blacksmith (cf. NIV, NCV, CEV). NLT understands the referent to be вАЬa sharp tool,вАЭ which is then used by the carpenter in the following verse to carve an idol from wood.
n2304418: tn Heb вАЬand there is no strengthвАЭ; NASB вАЬhis strength fails.вАЭ
n2304419: tn Heb вАЬstretches out a lineвАЭ (ASV similar); NIV вАЬmeasures with a line.вАЭ
n2304420: tn Heb вАЬhe makes an outline with the [?].вАЭ The noun вАП„©„В÷ґ„®÷ґ„УвАО (shered) occurs only here; it apparently refers to some type of tool or marker. Cf. KJV вАЬwith a lineвАЭ; ASV вАЬwith a pencilвАЭ; NAB, NRSV вАЬwith a stylusвАЭ; NASB вАЬwith red chalkвАЭ; NIV вАЬwith a marker.вАЭ
n2304421: tn Heb вАЬworksвАЭ (so NASB) or вАЬfashionsвАЭ (so NRSV); NIV вАЬhe roughs it out.вАЭ
n2304422: tn Heb вАЬhe makes it like the pattern of a manвАЭ; NAB вАЬlike a man in appearance.вАЭ
n2304423: tn Heb вАЬlike the glory of man to sit [in] a houseвАЭ; NIV вАЬthat it may dwell in a shrine.вАЭ
n2304424: tn It is not certain what type of tree this otherwise unattested noun refers to. Cf. ASV вАЬa holm-treeвАЭ (NRSV similar).
n2304425: tn Heb вАЬstrengthens for himself,вАЭ i.e., вАЬsecures for himselfвАЭ (see BDB 55 s.v. вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷µ„•вАО Pi.2).
n2304426: tn Some prefer to emend вАП„Р÷є„®÷ґ„ЯвАО ( Њoren) to вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЦвАО ( Њerez, вАЬcedarвАЭ), but the otherwise unattested noun appears to have an Akkadian cognate, meaning вАЬcedar.вАЭ See H. R. Cohen, Biblical Hapax Legomena (SBLDS), 44-45. HALOT 90 s.v. I вАП„Р÷є„®÷ґ„ЯвАО offers the meaning вАЬlaurel.вАЭ
n2304427: tn Heb вАЬand it becomes burning [i.e., firewood] for a manвАЭ; NAB вАЬto serve man for fuel.вАЭ
n2304430: tn Heb вАЬfor their eyes are smeared over so they cannot see, so their heart cannot be wise.вАЭ
n2304431: tn There is no formal interrogative sign here, but the context seems to indicate these are rhetorical questions. See GKC 473 ¬І150.a.
n2304432: tn Or perhaps, вАЬhe eats on an ash heap.вАЭ
n2304433: tn Heb вАЬIs it not a lie in my right hand?вАЭ
n2304434: tc The verb in the Hebrew text is a Niphal imperfect with a pronominal suffix. Although the Niphal ordinarily has the passive sense, it can have a reflexive nuance as well (see above translation). Some have suggested an emendation to a Qal form: вАЬDo not forget meвАЭ (all the ancient versions, NEB, REB; see GKC 369 ¬І117.x). вАЬDo not forget meвАЭ would make a good parallel with вАЬremember these thingsвАЭ in the first line. Since the MT is the harder reading and fits with IsraelвАЩs complaint that God had forgotten her (Isa 40:27), the MT reading should be retained (NASB, NKJV, NRSV, ESV). The passive has been rendered as an active in the translation in keeping with contemporary English style (so also NIV, NCV, TEV, NLT).
n2304435: tn Heb вАЬI blot out like a cloud your rebellious deeds, and like a cloud your sins.вАЭ вАЬRebellious deedsвАЭ and вАЬsinsвАЭ stand by metonymy for the guilt they produce. Both вАП„Ґ÷Є„СвАО ( њav) and вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ЯвАО ( њanan) refer to the clouds in the sky. It is tempting for stylistic purposes to translate the second with вАЬfogвАЭ or вАЬmistвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬcloudвА¶mistвАЭ; NIV вАЬcloudвА¶morning mistвАЭ; NLT вАЬmorning mistsвА¶cloudsвАЭ), but this distinction between the synonyms is unwarranted here. The point of the simile seems to be this: The Lord forgives their sins, causing them to vanish just as clouds disappear from the sky (see Job 7:9; 30:15).
n2304437: tn Heb вАЬactsвАЭ; NASB, NRSV вАЬhas done itвАЭ; NLT вАЬhas done this wondrous thing.вАЭ
n2304438: tn Heb вАЬlower regions.вАЭ This refers to Sheol and forms a merism with вАЬskyвАЭ in the previous line. See Pss 63:9; 71:20.
n2304439: tn Heb вАЬO forest and all the trees in itвАЭ; NASB, NRSV вАЬand every tree in it.вАЭ
n2304441: tn That is, by delivering Israel. Cf. NCV вАЬshowed his glory when he saved IsraelвАЭ; TEV вАЬhas shown his greatness by saving his people Israel.вАЭ
n2304442: tn Heb вАЬyour redeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n2304443: tn The consonantal text (Kethib) has вАЬWho [was] with me?вАЭ The marginal reading (Qere) is вАЬfrom with me,вАЭ i.e., вАЬby myself.вАЭ See BDB 87 s.v. II вАП„Р÷µ„™вАО 4.c.
n2304444: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (baddim), perhaps meaning вАЬempty talkersвАЭ (BDB 95 s.v. III вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„УвАО). In the four other occurrences of this word (Job 11:3; Isa 16:6; Jer 48:30; 50:36) the context does not make the meaning of the term very clear. Its primary point appears to be that the words spoken are meaningless or false. In light of its parallelism with вАЬomen readers,вАЭ some have proposed an emendation to вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (barim, вАЬseersвАЭ). The Mesopotamian baru-priests were divination specialists who played an important role in court life. See R. Wilson, Prophecy and Society in Ancient Israel, 93-98. Rather than supporting an emendation, J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 2:189, n. 79) suggests that Isaiah used вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО purposively as a derisive wordplay on the Akkadian word baru (in light of the close similarity of the d and r consonants).
n2304445: tn Or вАЬmakes fools ofвАЭ (NIV, NRSV); NAB and NASB both similar.
n2304446: tn Heb вАЬwho turns back the wiseвАЭ (so NRSV); NIV вАЬoverthrows the learning of the wiseвАЭ; TEV вАЬThe words of the wise I refute.вАЭ
n2304447: tn Heb вАЬtheir knowledgeвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV).
n2304448: tn Heb вАЬthe word of his servant.вАЭ The following context indicates that the LordвАЩs prophets are in view.
n2304449: tn Heb вАЬcounsel.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„Ґ÷µ„¶÷Є„ФвАО ( њetsah) probably refers here to the divine plan as announced by the prophets. See HALOT 867 s.v. I вАП„Ґ÷µ„¶÷Є„ФвАО.
n2304451: tn Heb вАЬsays to.вАЭ It is possible that the sentence is not completed, as the description of Cyrus and his God-given role is developed in the rest of the verse. 45:1 picks up where 44:28a leaves off with the LordвАЩs actual words to Cyrus finally being quoted in 45:2.
