n2304452: tn Heb вАЬmy shepherd.вАЭ The shepherd motif is sometimes applied, as here, to a royal figure who is responsible for the well-being of the people whom he rules.
n2304453: tn Heb вАЬthat he might bring to completion all my desire.вАЭ
n2304454: tn Heb вАЬand [concerning the] temple, you will be founded.вАЭ The preposition -вАП„Ь÷∞вАО (le) is understood by ellipsis at the beginning of the second line. The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„Х÷Љ÷Є„°÷µ„УвАО (tivvased, вАЬyou will be foundedвАЭ) is second masculine singular and is probably addressed to the personified temple (вАП„Ф÷µ„Щ„Ы÷Є„ЬвАО [hekhal, вАЬtempleвАЭ] is masculine).
n230451: tn Heb вАЬanointedвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NCV вАЬhis appointed king.вАЭ
n230452: sn The вАЬright handвАЭ is a symbol of activity and strength; the Lord directs CyrusвАЩ activities and assures his success.
n230453: tn Heb вАЬand the belts of kings I will loosenвАЭ; NRSV вАЬstrip kings of their robesвАЭ; NIV вАЬstrip kings of their armor.вАЭ
n230454: tc The form вАП„Ф÷≤„У„Х÷Љ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (hadurim) is difficult. It may need to be emended to an original вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (hararim, вАЬmountainsвАЭ), the reduplicated form of вАП„Ф÷Є„®вАО (har, вАЬmountainвАЭ). 1QIsaa and the LXX support an unambiguous text of вАЬmountains.вАЭ
n230455: tn That is, on the gates. Cf. CEV вАЬbreak the iron bars on bronze gates.вАЭ
n230456: tn Heb вАЬtreasures of darknessвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); TEV вАЬtreasures from dark, secret places.вАЭ
n230457: tn Or вАЬknowвАЭ (NCV, NRSV, TEV, NLT); NIV вАЬacknowledge.вАЭ The common verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) means вАЬto know.вАЭ Among homophonous roots DCH includes вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО II meaning вАЬbe submissive, humbled; be quiet, at restвАЭ (cf. Job 21:19; Prov 5:6; Jer 14:18; Hos 9:7).
n230458: tn Heb вАЬand there is none besides.вАЭ On the use of вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„УвАО ( њod) here, see BDB 729 s.v. 1.c.
n230459: tn Heb вАЬgird youвАЭ (so NASB) or вАЬstrengthen youвАЭ (so NIV).
n2304510: tn Or вАЬknowвАЭ (NAB, NCV, NRSV, TEV, CEV, NLT); NIV вАЬhave not acknowledged.вАЭ
n2304511: tn The words вАЬI do thisвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2304512: tn Heb вАЬtheyвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); TEV, CEV вАЬeveryoneвАЭ; NLT вАЬall the world.вАЭ
n2304513: tn The words вАЬI amвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text the participle at the beginning of v. 7 stands in apposition to вАЬthe LordвАЭ in v. 6.
n2304514: tn On the surface v. 7a appears to describe GodвАЩs sovereign control over the cycle of day and night, but the following statement suggests that вАЬlightвАЭ and вАЬdarknessвАЭ symbolize вАЬdeliveranceвАЭ and вАЬjudgment.вАЭ
n2304515: sn This verse affirms that God is ultimately sovereign over his world, including mankind and nations. In accordance with his sovereign will, he can cause wars to cease and peace to predominate (as he was about to do for his exiled people through Cyrus), or he can bring disaster and judgment on nations (as he was about to do to Babylon through Cyrus).
n2304516: tn Heb вАЬlet the clouds drip withвАЭ; KJV вАЬlet the skies pour down.вАЭ
n2304517: tn Heb вАЬopen upвАЭ (so NASB); NIV, NLT вАЬopen wide.вАЭ
n2304518: tc The plural verb should be emended to a singular form. The vav (вАП„ХвАО) ending is probably virtually dittographic (note the yod at the beginning of the following word).
n2304519: tc The Hiphil verb form (вАП„™÷Ј„¶÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО, tatsmiakh) should probably be emended to a Qal (вАП„™÷і„¶÷∞„Ю÷Ј„ЧвАО, titsmakh). The вАП„Щ„ЧвАО sequence at the end of the form is probably due to dittography (note the following вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО, yakhad).
n2304520: tn The masculine singular pronominal suffix probably refers back to вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ҐвАО (yasha њ, вАЬsalvationвАЭ).
n2304521: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] the one who argues with the one who formed him.вАЭ
n2304522: tn The words вАЬone who is like a mereвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and clarification.
n2304523: tn Heb вАЬShould the clay say to the one who forms it,вА¶?вАЭ The rhetorical question anticipates a reply, вАЬOf course not!вАЭ
n2304524: tn The words вАЬin the worldвАЭ are supplied in the translation to approximate in English idiom the force of the sarcastic question.
n2304525: tn Heb вАЬyour work, there are no hands for it,вАЭ i.e., вАЬyour work looks like something made by a person who has no hands.вАЭ
n2304526: tn Heb вАЬWoe [to] one who saysвАЭ (NASB and NIV both similar); NCV вАЬHow terrible it will be.вАЭ
n2304527: tn See the note at v. 9. This phrase occurs a second time later in this verse.
n2304528: sn Verses 9-10 may allude to the exilesвАЩ criticism that the Lord does not appear to know what he is doing.
n2304529: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304530: tc The Hebrew text reads вАЬthe one who formed him, the coming things.вАЭ Among various suggestions, some have proposed an emendation of вАП„Щ÷є„¶÷∞„®„Х÷євАО (yotsero, вАЬthe one who formed himвАЭ) to вАП„Щ÷є„¶÷µ„®вАО (yotser, вАЬthe one who formsвАЭ; the suffixed form in the Hebrew text may be influenced by vv. 9-10, where the same form appears twice) and takes вАЬcoming thingsвАЭ as the object of the participle (either objective genitive or accusative): вАЬthe one who brings the future into being.вАЭ
n2304531: tn Heb вАЬAsk me.вАЭ The rhetorical command sarcastically expresses the LordвАЩs disgust with those who question his ways.
n2304532: tn Heb вАЬDo you command me aboutвА¶?вАЭ The rhetorical question sarcastically expresses the LordвАЩs disgust with those who question his ways.
n2304533: tn The words вАЬwho liveвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2304534: tn Heb вАЬI, even my handsвАЭ; NASB вАЬI stretched outвА¶with My handsвАЭ; NRSV вАЬit was my hands that stretched out.вАЭ The same construction occurs at the beginning of v. 13.
n2304535: tn Or вАЬthe heavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2304536: tn Heb вАЬand to all their host I commanded.вАЭ See the notes at 40:26.
n2304537: tn Heb вАЬI stir him up in righteousnessвАЭ; NASB вАЬI have aroused him.вАЭ See the note at 41:2. Cyrus (cf. 44:28) is in view here.
n2304538: tn Heb вАЬlabor,вАЭ which stands metonymically for the fruits of labor, either вАЬmonetary profit,вАЭ or вАЬproducts.вАЭ
n2304539: tn Or perhaps, вАЬmerchandiseвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NAB вАЬthe gain of EthiopiaвАЭ; CEV вАЬthe treasures of Ethiopia.вАЭ
n2304540: tn Heb вАЬthey will pass over to youвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬwill come over to youвАЭ; CEV вАЬwill belong to you.вАЭ
n2304541: sn Restored Israel is depicted here in typical ancient Near Eastern fashion as an imperial power that receives riches and slaves as tribute.
n2304542: sn IsraelвАЩs vassals are portrayed as so intimidated and awed that they treat Israel as an intermediary to God or sub-deity.
n2304543: tn Or perhaps, вАЬamong.вАЭ Cf. KJV, ASV вАЬSurely God is in thee.вАЭ
n2304544: tn Heb вАЬthere is no otherвАЭ (so NIV, NRSV). The same phrase occurs at the end of v. 18, in v. 21, and at the end of v. 22.
n2304545: tn вАЬtogether they will walk in humiliation, the makers of images.вАЭ
n2304546: tn Heb вАЬIsrael will be delivered by the Lord [with] a permanent deliverance.вАЭ
n2304547: tn Heb вАЬyou will not be ashamed and you will not be humiliated for ages of future time.вАЭ
n2304548: tn Heb вАЬhe [is] the God.вАЭ The article here indicates uniqueness.
n2304549: tn Or вАЬunformed.вАЭ Gen 1:2 describes the world as вАЬunformedвАЭ (вАП„™÷є„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО, tohu) prior to GodвАЩs creative work, but God then formed the world and made it fit for habitation.
n2304550: tn Heb вАЬin a place of a land of darknessвАЭ (ASV similar); NASB вАЬin some dark land.вАЭ
n2304551: tn вАЬIn vainвАЭ translates вАП„™÷є„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (tohu), used here as an adverbial accusative: вАЬfor nothing.вАЭ
n2304552: tn The translation above assumes that вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО (tsedeq) and вАП„Ю÷µ„Щ„©„Б÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mesharim) are adverbial accusatives (see 33:15). If they are taken as direct objects, indicating the content of what is spoken, one might translate, вАЬwho proclaims deliverance, who announces justice.вАЭ
n2304553: tn Heb вАЬDeclare! Bring near!вАЭ; NASB вАЬDeclare and set forth your case.вАЭ See 41:21.
n2304554: tn Or вАЬa righteous God and delivererвАЭ; NASB, NIV, NRSV вАЬa righteous God and a Savior.вАЭ
n2304555: tn The Niphal imperative with prefixed vav (вАП„ХвАО) indicates purpose after the preceding imperative. The Niphal probably has a tolerative sense, вАЬallow yourselves to be delivered, accept help.вАЭ
n2304556: tn Heb вАЬI swear by myselfвАЭ; KJV, NASB вАЬhave sworn.вАЭ
n2304557: tn Heb вАЬa word goes out from my mouth [in] truth and will not return.вАЭ
n2304558: tn Heb вАЬswearвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬconfess allegiance.вАЭ
n2304559: tn Heb вАЬвАШYes, in the Lord,вАЩ one says about me, вАШis deliverance and strength.вАЩвАЭ
n2304560: tn Heb вАЬwill come to him and be ashamed.вАЭ
n2304561: tn Heb вАЬIn the Lord all the offspring of Israel will be vindicated and boast.вАЭ
n230461: sn Bel was the name of a Babylonian god. The name was originally associated with Enlil, but later was applied to Marduk. See HALOT 132 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЬвАО.
n230462: sn Nebo is a variation of the name of the Babylonian god Nabu.
n230463: tn Heb вАЬtheir images belong to animals and beastsвАЭ; NIV вАЬtheir idols are borne by beasts of burdenвАЭ; NLT вАЬare being hauled away.вАЭ
n230464: tn Heb вАЬyour loads are carried [as] a burden by a weary [animal].вАЭ
n230465: tn Heb вАЬ[the] burden,вАЭ i.e., their images, the heavy burden carried by the animals.
n230466: tn Heb вАЬTheir soul/life has gone into captivity.
n230467: sn The downfall of Babylon is depicted here. The idols are carried off by the victorious enemy; the gods are likened to defeated captives who cower before the enemy and are taken into exile.
n230468: tn Heb вАЬhouse of JacobвАЭ; TEV вАЬdescendants of Jacob.вАЭ
n230469: tn Heb вАЬand all the remnant of the house of Israel.вАЭ
n2304610: tn Heb вАЬfrom the wombвАЭ (so NRSV); KJV вАЬfrom the bellyвАЭ; NAB вАЬfrom your infancy.вАЭ
n2304611: tn Heb вАЬwho have been lifted up from the womb.вАЭ
n2304612: tn Heb вАЬuntil old age, I am heвАЭ (NRSV similar); NLT вАЬI will be your God throughout your lifetime.вАЭ
n2304613: sn Unlike the weary idol gods, whose images must be carried by animals, the Lord carries his weary people.
n2304614: tn Heb вАЬthe reed,вАЭ probably referring to the beam of a scales. See BDB 889 s.v. вАП„І÷Є„†÷ґ„ФвАО 4.c.
n2304615: tn Or perhaps, вАЬcannot,вАЭ here and in the following two lines. The imperfect forms can indicate capability.
n2304616: tn The meaning of the verb вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„©„БвАО ( Њashash, which appears here in the Hitpolel stem) is uncertain. BDB 84 s.v. вАП„Р„©„Б„©„БвАО relates it to a root meaning вАЬfound, establishвАЭ in Arabic; HALOT 100 s.v. II вАП„Р„©„Б„©„БвАО gives the meaning вАЬpluck up courage.вАЭ The imperative with vav (вАП„ХвАО) may indicate purpose following the preceding imperative.
n2304617: tn Heb вАЬreturn [it], rebels, to heartвАЭ; NRSV вАЬrecall it to mind, you transgressors.вАЭ
n2304618: tn Heb вАЬremember the former things, from antiquityвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬthe former things of old.вАЭ
n2304619: tn Heb вАЬand there is no otherвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304620: tn Or вАЬfrom long agoвАЭ; KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV вАЬfrom ancient times.вАЭ
n2304621: tn Or, more generally, вАЬa bird of preyвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV; see 18:6).
n2304622: tn Heb вАЬspokenвАЭ; KJV вАЬI have spoken it.вАЭ
n2304623: tn Heb вАЬstrong of heart [or, mind]вАЭ; KJV вАЬstoutheartedвАЭ; NAB вАЬfaintheartedвАЭ; NIV вАЬstubborn-hearted.вАЭ
n2304624: tn Heb вАЬwho are far from righteousness [or perhaps, вАЬdeliveranceвАЭ].вАЭ
n2304625: tn Heb вАЬmy salvation.вАЭ The verb вАЬI am bringing nearвАЭ is understood by ellipsis (note the previous line).
n2304626: tn Heb вАЬI will place in Zion salvationвАЭ; NASB вАЬI will grant salvation in Zion.вАЭ
n2304627: tn Heb вАЬto Israel my splendorвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬfor Israel my glory.вАЭ
n230471: tn вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„™„Х÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„ФвАО (betulah) often refers to a virgin, but the phrase вАЬvirgin daughterвАЭ is apparently stylized (see also 23:12; 37:22). In the extended metaphor of this chapter, where Babylon is personified as a queen (vv. 5, 7), she is depicted as being both a wife and mother (vv. 8-9).
n230473: tn Heb In this context вАЬshameвАЭ is a euphemism referring to their naked bodies.
n230474: tn Heb вАЬI will not meet a man.вАЭ The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (pagaвАШ) apparently carries the nuance вАЬmeet with kindnessвАЭ here (cf. 64:5, and see BDB 803 s.v. Qal.2).
n230475:
tc The Hebrew text reads, вАЬOur redeemerвАФthe Lord of armies [traditionally, вАЬthe Lord of hostsвАЭ] is his name, the Holy One of Israel.вАЭ The ancient Greek version adds вАЬsaysвАЭ before вАЬour redeemer.вАЭ вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО ( Њamar) may have accidentally dropped from the text by virtual haplography. Note that the preceding word вАП„Р÷Є„У÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њadam) is graphically similar.
sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n230476: tn Heb вАЬdarkness,вАЭ which may indicate a place of hiding where a fugitive would seek shelter and protection.
n230479: tn Heb вАЬon the old you made very heavy your yoke.вАЭ
n2304710: tn Heb вАЬForever I [will be] permanent queenвАЭ; NIV вАЬthe eternal queenвАЭ; CEV вАЬqueen forever.вАЭ
n2304711: tn Heb вАЬyou did not set these things upon your heart [or вАЬmindвАЭ].вАЭ

n2304713: tn Or perhaps, вАЬvoluptuous oneвАЭ (NAB); NAB вАЬyou sensual oneвАЭ; NLT вАЬYou are a pleasure-crazy kingdom.вАЭ
n2304714: tn Heb вАЬthe one who says in her heart.вАЭ
n2304715: tn Heb вАЬI [am], and besides me there is no other.вАЭ See Zeph 2:15.
n2304716: tn Heb вАЬI will not live [as] a widow, and I will not know loss of children.вАЭ
n2304717: tn Heb вАЬloss of children and widowhood.вАЭ In the Hebrew text the phrase is in apposition to вАЬboth of theseвАЭ in line 1.
n2304718: tn Heb вАЬaccording to their fullness, they will come upon you.вАЭ
n2304719: tn For other examples of the preposition bet (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО) having the sense of вАЬalthough, despite,вАЭ see BDB 90 s.v. III.7.
n2304720: sn Reference is made to incantations and amulets, both of which were important in Mesopotamian religion. They were used to ward off danger and demons.
n2304721: tn Heb вАЬyou trusted in your evilвАЭ; KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV вАЬwickedness.вАЭ
n2304722: tn Or вАЬsaidвАЭ; NAB вАЬsaid to yourselfвАЭвАЩ NASB вАЬsaid in your heart.вАЭ
n2304723: tn The words вАЬself-professedвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2304725: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ч÷∞„®÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (shakhrah), which is either a suffixed noun (вАЬits dawning,вАЭ i.e., origin) or infinitive (вАЬto look early for itвАЭ). Some have suggested an emendation to вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ч÷≤„У÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (shakhadah), a suffixed infinitive from вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (shakhad, вАЬ[how] to buy it offвАЭ; see BDB 1005 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО). This forms a nice parallel with the following couplet. The above translation is based on a different etymology of the verb in question. HALOT 1466 s.v. III вАП„©„Б„Ч„®вАО references a verbal root with these letters (вАП„©„Б„Ч„УвАО) that refers to magical activity.
n2304726: tn Heb вАЬyou will not knowвАЭ; NIV вАЬyou cannot foresee.вАЭ
n2304727: tn Heb вАЬstandвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NASB, NRSV вАЬStand fast.вАЭ
n2304728: tn The word вАЬtrustingвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarification. See v. 9.
n2304729: tn Heb вАЬin that which you have toiled.вАЭ
n2304730: tn Heb вАЬmaybe you will be able to profit.вАЭ
n2304731: tn Heb вАЬmaybe you will cause to tremble.вАЭ The object вАЬdisasterвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarification. See the note at v. 9.
n2304732: tn Heb вАЬyou are tired because of the abundance of your advice.вАЭ
n2304733: tn Heb вАЬlet them stand and rescue youвАФthe ones who see omens in the sky, who gaze at the stars, who make known by monthsвАФfrom those things which are coming upon you.вАЭ
n2304734: tn Heb вАЬhand,вАЭ here a metaphor for the strength or power of the flames.
n2304735: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬthere is no coal [for?] their food, light to sit before it.вАЭ Some emend вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ч÷∞„Ю÷Є„ЭвАО (lakhmam, вАЬtheir foodвАЭ) to вАП„Ь÷∞„Ч÷ї„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (lekhummam, вАЬto warm themвАЭ; see HALOT 328 s.v. вАП„Ч„Ю„ЭвАО). This statement may allude to Isa 44:16, where idolaters are depicted warming themselves over a fire made from wood, part of which was used to form idols. The fire of divine judgment will be no such campfire; its flames will devour and destroy.
n2304736: tn Heb вАЬSo they will be to youвАЭ; NIV вАЬThat is all they can do for you.вАЭ
n2304737: tn Heb вАЬthat for which you toiled, your traders from your youth.вАЭ The omen readers and star gazers are likened to merchants with whom Babylon has had an ongoing economic relationship.
n2304738: tn Heb вАЬeach to his own side, they err.вАЭ
n230481: tn Heb вАЬhouse of JacobвАЭ; TEV, CEV вАЬpeople of Israel.вАЭ
n230482: tc The Hebrew text reads literally вАЬand from the waters of Judah came out.вАЭ вАП„Ю÷і„Ю÷Љ÷µ„ЩвАО (mimme) could be a variation from an original вАП„Ю÷і„Ю÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷µ„ЩвАО (mimme њe, вАЬfrom the inner parts ofвАЭ). The translation above as well as several other translations treat the text this way or understand that this is what the Hebrew phrase figuratively means (cf. HCSB, NASB, NIV, NLT, NRSV). Some translations (ESV, NKJV) retain the MT reading and render it literally as вАЬwaters.вАЭ The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa, which corrects a similar form to вАЬfrom inner parts ofвАЭ in 39:7, does not do it here.
n230483: tn Heb вАЬcause to rememberвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬmake mention of.вАЭ
n230484: tn Heb вАЬnot in truth and not in righteousness.вАЭ
n230485: tn Heb вАЬthey call themselves [or вАЬare calledвАЭ] from the holy city.вАЭ The precise meaning of the statement is uncertain. The Niphal of вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО (qara Њ) is combined with the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) only here. When the Qal of вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО is used with вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО, the preposition often indicates the place from which one is summoned (see 46:11). So one could translate, вАЬfrom the holy city they are summoned,вАЭ meaning that they reside there.
n230486: tn Heb вАЬlean onвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV); NAB, NIV вАЬrely on.вАЭ
n230487: tn Heb вАЬthe former things beforehand I declared.вАЭ
n230488: tn Heb вАЬand from my mouth they came forth, and I caused them to be heard.вАЭ
n230489: tn The words вАЬI did thisвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text v. 4 is subordinated to v. 3.
n2304810: sn The image is that of a person who has tensed the muscles of the face and neck as a sign of resolute refusal.
n2304811: tn Heb вАЬgaze [at] all of itвАЭ; KJV вАЬsee all this.вАЭ
n2304812: tn Heb вАЬ[as for] you, will you not declare?вАЭ
n2304813: tn Heb вАЬand hidden things, and you do not know them.вАЭ
n2304814: tn Heb вАЬare createdвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬThey are brand new.вАЭ
n2304816: tn Heb вАЬbeforehand your ear did not open.вАЭ
n2304817: tn Heb вАЬdeceiving, you deceive.вАЭ The infinitive absolute precedes the finite verb for emphasis.
n2304818: tn Or вАЬcalledвАЭ (KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304819: tn Heb вАЬfor the sake of my nameвАЭ (so NAB, NASB); NLT вАЬfor my own sake.вАЭ
n2304820: tn Heb вАЬand my praise.вАЭ вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (lema њan, вАЬfor the sake ofвАЭ) is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n2304821: tn Heb вАЬI restrain [myself] concerning you not to cut you off.вАЭ
n2304822: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ч÷Ј„®÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (bekhartikha, вАЬI have chosen youвАЭ), but the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads correctly вАП„С„Ч„†„™„Щ„Ы„ФвАО (вАЬI have tested youвАЭ). The metallurgical background of the imagery suggests that purification through testing is the idea.
n2304823: tn The Hebrew text repeats вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷і„ЩвАО (lema њani, вАЬfor my sakeвАЭ) for emphasis.
n2304824: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬfor how can it be defiled?вАЭ The subject of the verb is probably вАЬnameвАЭ (v. 9).
n2304826: tn Heb вАЬI [am] he, I [am the] first, also I [am the] last.вАЭ
n2304827: sn This probably refers to the idol gods (see v. 5).
n2304828:
tn Or вАЬfriend,вАЭ or вАЬcovenant partner.вАЭ
sn The LordвАЩs ally is a reference to Cyrus.
n2304830: tn Heb вАЬand his way will be prosperous.вАЭ
n2304831: tn Heb вАЬfrom the time of its occurring.вАЭ
n2304832: sn The speaker here is not identified specifically, but he is probably Cyrus, the LordвАЩs вАЬallyвАЭ mentioned in vv. 14-15.
n2304833: tn Heb вАЬyour redeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n2304834: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304835: tn Heb вАЬpaid attention toвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV); TEV вАЬhad listened to.вАЭ
n2304836: tn Heb вАЬlike a river your peace would have been.вАЭ вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom) probably refers here to the peace and prosperity which God promised in return for obedience to the covenant.
n2304837: tn Heb вАЬand your righteousness like the waves of the sea.вАЭ вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsedaqah) probably refers here to divine deliverance from enemies. See v. 19.
n2304838: tn Heb вАЬlike sandвАЭ; NCV вАЬas many as the grains of sand.вАЭ
n2304839: tn Heb вАЬand the issue from your inner parts.вАЭ
n2304840: tn Heb вАЬand his name would not be cut off and would not be destroyed from before me.вАЭ
n2304841: tn Heb вАЬto the end of the earthвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV).
n2304843: sn The translation above (present tense) assumes that this verse describes GodвАЩs provision for returning Babylonian exiles (see v. 20; 35:6; 49:10) in terms reminiscent of the Exodus from Egypt (see Exod 17:6).
n230491:
tn Or вАЬislandsвАЭ (NASB, NIV); NLT вАЬin far-off lands.вАЭ
sn The LordвАЩs special servant, introduced in chap. 42, speaks here of his commission.
n230493: tn Heb вАЬfrom the inner parts of my mother he mentioned my name.вАЭ
n230494: tn Or perhaps, вАЬpolishedвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); NASB вАЬa select arrow.вАЭ
n230495: sn The figurative language emphasizes the servantвАЩs importance as the LordвАЩs effective instrument. The servantвАЩs mouth, which stands metonymically for his words, is compared to a sharp sword because he will be an effective spokesman on GodвАЩs behalf (see 50:4). The Lord holds his hand on the servant, ready to draw and use him at the appropriate time. The servant is like a sharpened arrow reserved in a quiver for just the right moment.
n230496: sn This verse identifies the servant as Israel. This seems to refer to the exiled nation (cf. 41:8-9; 44:1-2, 21; 45:4; 48:20), but in vv. 5-6 this servant says he has been commissioned to reconcile Israel to God, so he must be distinct from the exiled nation. This servant is an ideal вАЬIsraelвАЭ who, like Moses of old, mediates a covenant for the nation (see v. 8), leads them out of bondage (v. 9a), and carries out GodвАЩs original plan for Israel by positively impacting the pagan nations (see v. 6b). By living according to GodвАЩs law, Israel was to be a model of GodвАЩs standards of justice to the surrounding nations (Deut 4:6-8). The sinful nation failed, but the servant, the ideal вАЬIsrael,вАЭ will succeed by establishing justice throughout the earth.
n230497: tn Or вАЬsaidвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬI replied.вАЭ
n230498: tn Heb вАЬfor nothing and emptiness.вАЭ Synonyms are combined to emphasize the common idea.
n230499: tn Heb вАЬBut my justice is with the Lord, and my reward [or вАЬwageвАЭ] with my God.вАЭ
n2304911: tn The words вАЬhe did thisвАЭ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text the infinitive construct of purpose is subordinated to the previous statement.
n2304912: tn The vav (вАП„ХвАО) + imperfect is translated here as a result clause; one might interpret it as indicating purpose, вАЬand so I might be honored.вАЭ
n2304913: tn Heb вАЬand my God is [perhaps, вАЬhaving beenвАЭ] my strength.вАЭ The disjunctive structure (vav [вАП„ХвАО] + subject + verb) is interpreted here as indicating a causal circumstantial clause.
n2304915: sn The question is purely rhetorical; it does not imply that the servant was dissatisfied with his commission or that he minimized the restoration of Israel.
n2304917: tn Heb вАЬbeвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); CEV вАЬyou must take.вАЭ
n2304919: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2304920: tc The Hebrew text reads literally вАЬto [one who] despises life.вАЭ It is preferable to read with the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa вАП„Ь„С„Ц„Х„ЩвАО, which should be vocalized as a passive participle, вАП„Ь÷і„С÷∞„Ц„Х÷Љ„ЩвАО (livzuy, вАЬto the one despised with respect to lifeвАЭ [вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО is a genitive of specification]). The consonantal sequence вАП„Х„ЩвАО was probably misread as вАП„ФвАО in the MT tradition. The contextual argument favors the 1QIsaa reading. As J. N. Oswalt (Isaiah [NICOT], 2:294) points out, the three terse phrases вАЬconvey a picture of lowliness, worthlessness, and helplessness.вАЭ
n2304921: tn MTвАЩs Piel participle (вАЬto the one who rejectsвАЭ) does not fit contextually. The form should be revocalized as a Pual, вАЬto the one rejected.вАЭ
n2304922: tn Parallelism (see вАЬrulers,вАЭ вАЬkings,вАЭ вАЬprincesвАЭ) suggests that the singular вАП„Т÷Љ„Х÷є„ЩвАО (goy) be emended to a plural or understood in a collective sense (see 55:5).
n2304923: tn For this sense of вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (qum), see Gen 19:1; 23:7; 33:10; Lev 19:32; 1 Sam 20:41; 25:41; 1 Kgs 2:19; Job 29:8.
n2304924: tn The translation assumes the verb is derived from the root вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Є„®вАО (natsar, вАЬprotectвАЭ). Some prefer to derive it from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Є„®вАО (yatsar, вАЬformвАЭ).
n2304925: tn Heb вАЬa covenant of people.вАЭ A person cannot literally be a covenant; вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷і„Щ„™вАО (berit) is probably metonymic here, indicating a covenant mediator. Here вАП„Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО ( њam, вАЬpeopleвАЭ) appears to refer to Israel. See the note at 42:6.
n2304926: tn The Hiphil of вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (qum, вАЬariseвАЭ) is probably used here in the sense of вАЬrebuild.вАЭ
n2304927: tn The вАЬlandвАЭ probably stands by metonymy for the ruins within it.
n2304928: tn Heb вАЬto say.вАЭ In the Hebrew text the infinitive construct is subordinated to what precedes.
n2304929: tn Heb вАЬin darknessвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬthe prisoners of darkness.вАЭ
n2304931: tn Heb вАЬand the heat and the sun will not strike them.вАЭ In Isa 35:7, its only other occurrence in the OT, вАП„©„Б÷Є„®÷Є„СвАО (sharav) stands parallel to вАЬparched groundвАЭ and in contrast to вАЬpool.вАЭ In later Hebrew and Aramaic it refers to вАЬdry heat, heat of the sunвАЭ (Jastrow 1627 s.v.). Here it likely has this nuance and forms a hendiadys with вАЬsun.вАЭ
n2304932:
tc The MT reads вАЬSinimвАЭ here; the Dead Sea Scrolls read вАЬSyene,вАЭ a location in Egypt associated with modern Aswan. A number of recent translations adopt this reading: вАЬSyeneвАЭ (NAB, NRSV); вАЬAswanвАЭ (NIV); вАЬEgyptвАЭ (NLT).
sn The precise location of the land of Sinim is uncertain, but since the north and west are mentioned in the previous line, it was a probably located in the distant east or south.
n2304933: tn Or вАЬO heavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2304934: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2304935: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬLordвАЭ here is вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay).
n2304936: tn Heb вАЬher sucklingвАЭ; NASB вАЬher nursing child.вАЭ
n2304937: tn Heb вАЬso as not to have compassion on the son of her womb?вАЭ
n2304939: sn The argument of v. 15 seems to develop as follows: The Lord has an innate attachment to Zion, just like a mother does for her infant child. But even if mothers were to suddenly abandon their children, the Lord would never forsake Zion. In other words, the LordвАЩs attachment to Zion is like a motherвАЩs attachment to her infant child, but even stronger.
n2304940: tn Heb вАЬyou.вАЭ Here the pronoun is put by metonymy for the personвАЩs name.
n2304941: tn Heb вАЬLift up around your eyes and see.вАЭ
n2304942: tn Heb вАЬIndeed your ruins and your desolate places, and the land of your destruction.вАЭ This statement is abruptly terminated in the Hebrew text and left incomplete.
n2304944: tn Heb вАЬdraw near to me so I can dwell.вАЭ
n2304945: tn Heb вАЬand you will say in your heart.вАЭ
n2304946: tn Or вАЬexiled and thrust awayвАЭ; NIV вАЬexiled and rejected.вАЭ
n2304947: tn Heb вАЬyour,вАЭ but Zion here stands by metonymy for her children (see v. 22b).
n2304949: tn Or вАЬat your feetвАЭ (NAB, NIV); NLT вАЬfrom your feet.вАЭ
n2304950: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„¶÷Є„У÷і„Щ„ІвАО (tsadiq, вАЬa righteous [one]вАЭ), but this makes no sense in the parallelism. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads correctly вАП„Ґ„®„Щ„•вАО (вАЬviolent [one], tyrantвАЭ; see v. 25).
n2304951: sn Verse 26a depicts siege warfare and bloody defeat. The besieged enemy will be so starved they will eat their own flesh. The bloodstained bodies lying on the blood-soaked battle site will look as if they collapsed in drunkenness.
n2304953: tn Heb вАЬyour redeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n2304954: tn Or вАЬthe Mighty One of Jacob.вАЭ See 1:24.
n230501: sn The Lord challenges the exiles (ZionвАЩs children) to bring incriminating evidence against him. The rhetorical questions imply that Israel accused the Lord of divorcing his wife (Zion) and selling his children (the Israelites) into slavery to pay off a debt.
n230502: sn The Lord admits that he did sell the Israelites, but it was because of their sins, not because of some debt he owed. If he had sold them to a creditor, they ought to be able to point him out, but the preceding rhetorical question implies they would not be able to do so.
n230503: sn The Lord admits he did divorce Zion, but that too was the result of the nationвАЩs sins. The force of the earlier rhetorical question comes into clearer focus now. The question does not imply that a certificate does not exist and that no divorce occurred. Rather, the question asks for the certificate to be produced so the accuser can see the reason for the divorce in black and white. The Lord did not put Zion away arbitrarily.
n230504: sn The present-tense translation of the verbs assumes that the Lord is questioning why Israel does not attempt to counter his arguments. Another possibility is to take the verbs as referring to past events: вАЬWhy did no one meet me when I came? Why did no one answer when I called?вАЭ In this case the Lord might be asking why Israel rejected his calls to repent and his offer to deliver them.
n230508: tn Heb вАЬthe fish stink from lack of water and die from thirst.вАЭ
n230509:
tn Heb вАЬhas given to me a tongue of disciples.вАЭ
sn Verses 4-11 contain the third of the so-called servant songs, which depict the career of the LordвАЩs special servant, envisioned as an ideal Israel (49:3) who rescues the exiles and fulfills GodвАЩs purposes for the world. Here the servant alludes to opposition (something hinted at in 49:4), but also expresses his determination to persevere with the LordвАЩs help.
n2305010: tc Heb вАЬto know [?] the weary with a word.вАЭ Comparing it with Arabic and Aramaic cognates yields the meaning of вАЬhelp, sustain.вАЭ Nevertheless, the meaning of вАП„Ґ„Х÷Љ„™вАО ( њut) is uncertain. The word occurs only here in the OT (see BDB 736 s.v.). Various scholars have suggested an emendation to вАП„Ґ÷Є„†„Х÷є„™вАО ( њanot) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО ( њanah, вАЬanswerвАЭ): вАЬso that I know how to respond kindly to the weary.вАЭ Since the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa and the Vulgate support the MT reading, that reading is retained.
n2305011: tn Heb вАЬhe arouses for me an ear, to hear like disciples.вАЭ
n2305012: tn Or perhaps, вАЬmakes me obedient.вАЭ The text reads literally, вАЬhas opened for me an ear.вАЭ
n2305014: tn Heb вАЬTherefore I set my face like flint.вАЭ
n2305016: tn Heb вАЬWho is the master of my judgment?вАЭ
n2305017: tn Heb вАЬlet him approach meвАЭ; NAB, NIV вАЬLet him confront me.вАЭ
n2305018: tn Heb вАЬ[who] listens to the voice of his servant?вАЭ The interrogative is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n2305019: tn The plural indicates degree. Darkness may refer to exile and/or moral evil.
n2305020: tc Several more recent commentators have proposed an emendation of вАП„Ю÷∞„Р÷Ј„Ц÷Љ÷∞„®÷µ„ЩвАО (me Њazzere, вАЬwho put onвАЭ) to вАП„Ю÷∞„Р÷і„Щ„®÷і„ЩвАО (me Њiri, вАЬwho lightвАЭ). However, both Qumran scrolls of Isaiah and the Vulgate support the MT reading (cf. NIV, ESV).
n2305021: tn On the meaning of вАП„Ц÷і„Щ„І„Х÷є„™вАО (ziqot, вАЬflaming arrowsвАЭ), see HALOT 268 s.v. вАП„Ц÷і„Щ„І„Х÷є„™вАО.
n2305022: tn The imperative is probably rhetorical and has a predictive force.
n2305024: sn Perhaps the servant here speaks to his enemies and warns them that they will self-destruct.
n2305025: tn Heb вАЬfrom my handвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2305026: sn The imagery may be that of a person who becomes ill and is forced to lie down in pain on a sickbed. Some see this as an allusion to a fiery place of damnation because of the imagery employed earlier in the verse.
n230511: tn Or вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NAB вАЬjusticeвАЭ; NLT вАЬhope for deliverance.вАЭ
n230513: sn The вАЬrockвАЭ and вАЬquarryвАЭ refer here to Abraham and Sarah, the progenitors of the nation.
n230514: sn Although Abraham and Sarah are distant ancestors of the people the prophet is addressing, they are spoken of as the immediate parents.
n230515: tn Heb вАЬoneвАЭ; NLT вАЬwas aloneвАЭ; TEV вАЬwas childless.вАЭ
n230516: tn вАЬBlessвАЭ may here carry the sense of вАЬendue with potency, reproductive power.вАЭ See Gen 1:28.
n230518: sn The rift valley (вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО,  њaravah) is known for its dry, desert-like conditions in the area of the Dead Sea and southward (although it also includes the Jordan Valley to the north). The wilderness (вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„®вАО, midbar) is an area that receives less than twelve inches of rain per year and so can only support meager vegetation at best. The imagery here focuses on the transformation from arid and lifeless to watered and luxuriant.
n230519: tn Heb вАЬfound inвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2305111: tn Heb вАЬinstruction [or вАЬa lawвАЭ] will go out from me.вАЭ
n2305112: tn Heb вАЬand my justice for a light to the nations I will cause to rest.вАЭ
n2305115: tn Heb вАЬand my arms will judge [on behalf of] nations.вАЭ
n2305118: tn Heb вАЬwill be torn in pieces.вАЭ The perfect indicates the certitude of the event, from the LordвАЩs rhetorical perspective.
n2305119: tn Heb вАЬmy deliverance.вАЭ The same Hebrew word can also be translated вАЬsalvationвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); cf. CEV вАЬvictory.вАЭ
n2305122: tn Heb вАЬpeople (who have) my law in their heart.вАЭ
n2305123: tn Heb вАЬmy vindicationвАЭ; many English versions вАЬmy righteousnessвАЭ; NRSV, TEV вАЬmy deliveranceвАЭ; CEV вАЬmy victory.вАЭ
n2305124: tn The arm of the Lord is a symbol of divine military power. Here it is personified and told to arouse itself from sleep and prepare for action.
n2305125: tn Heb вАЬAre you not the one who smashed?вАЭ The feminine singular forms agree grammatically with the feminine noun вАЬarm.вАЭ The Hebrew text hasвАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„¶÷ґ„С÷ґ„™ вАО (hammakhtsevet), from the verbal root вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷Ј„СвАО (khatsav, вАЬhew, chopвАЭ). The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has, probably correctly, вАП„Ф„Ю„Ч„¶„™вАО, from the verbal root вАП„Ю÷Є„Ч÷Ј„•вАО (makhats, вАЬsmashвАЭ) which is used in Job 26:12 to describe GodвАЩs victory over вАЬthe Proud One.вАЭ
n2305126: tn This title (вАП„®÷Ј„Ф÷Ј„СвАО, rahav, вАЬproud oneвАЭ) is sometimes translated as a proper name: вАЬRahabвАЭ (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV). It is used here of a symbolic sea monster, known elsewhere in the Bible and in Ugaritic myth as Leviathan. This sea creature symbolizes the forces of chaos that seek to destroy the created order. In the Bible вАЬthe Proud OneвАЭ opposes GodвАЩs creative work, but is defeated (see Job 26:12; Ps 89:10). Here the title refers to PharaohвАЩs Egyptian army that opposed Israel at the Red Sea (see v. 10, and note also Isa 30:7 and Ps 87:4, where the title is used of Egypt).
n2305127: tn The words вАЬdid you notвАЭ are understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line). The rhetorical questions here and in v. 10 expect the answer, вАЬYes, you certainly did!вАЭ
n2305128: tn Hebrew вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (tannin) is another name for the symbolic sea monster. See the note at 27:1. In this context the sea creature represents Egypt. See the note on the title вАЬProud OneвАЭ earlier in this verse.
n2305129: tn The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬAre you not the one who dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep, who madeвА¶?вАЭ
n2305130: tn Heb вАЬthe redeemedвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, NIV, NRSV); KJV вАЬthe ransomed.вАЭ
n2305131: tn Heb вАЬ[will be] on their head[s].вАЭ вАЬJoyвАЭ may be likened here to a crown (cf. 2 Sam 1:10). The statement may also be an ironic twist on the idiom вАЬearth/dust on the headвАЭ (cf. 2 Sam 1:2; 13:19; 15:32; Job 2:12), referring to a mourning practice.
n2305132: tn Heb вАЬovertakeвАЭ (so NIV); NASB вАЬthey will obtain.вАЭ
n2305134: tc The plural suffix should probably be emended to the second masculine singular (which is used in v. 13). The final mem (вАП„ЭвАО) is probably dittographic; note the mem at the beginning of the next word.
n2305135: tn Heb вАЬWho are you that you are afraid of man who dies, and of the son of man who [as] grass is given up?вАЭ The feminine singular forms should probably be emended to the masculine singular (see v. 13). They have probably been influenced by the construction вАП„Р÷Ј„™÷Љ÷∞÷Њ„Ф÷і„Щ„РвАО ( Њat-hi Њ) in vv. 9-10.
n2305137: tn Or вАЬthe heavensвАЭ (also in v. 16). The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2305138: tn Heb вАЬand that you tremble constantly all the day.вАЭ
n2305139: tn The question anticipates the answer, вАЬReady to disappear!вАЭ See v. 14.
n2305140: tn Heb вАЬwho is stooped overвАЭ (under a burden).
n2305141: tn Heb вАЬthe pitвАЭ (so KJV); ASV, NAB вАЬdie and go down into the pitвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬdungeonвАЭ; NCV вАЬprison.вАЭ
n2305142: tn Heb вАЬhe will not lack his bread.вАЭ
n2305143: tn The addressee (second masculine singular, as in vv. 13, 15) in this verse is unclear. The exiles are addressed in the immediately preceding verses (note the critical tone of vv. 12-13 and the reference to the exiles in v. 14). However, it seems unlikely that they are addressed in v. 16, for the addressee appears to be commissioned to tell Zion, who here represents the restored exiles, вАЬyou are my people.вАЭ The addressee is distinct from the exiles. The language of v. 16a is reminiscent of 49:2 and 50:4, where the LordвАЩs special servant says he is GodвАЩs spokesman and effective instrument. Perhaps the Lord, having spoken to the exiles in vv. 1-15, now responds to this servant, who spoke just prior to this in 50:4-11.
n2305144: tn Heb вАЬI place my words in your mouth.вАЭ
n2305145: tn Heb вАЬwith the shadow of my hand.вАЭ
n2305146: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Ь÷і„†÷∞„Ш÷є„Ґ÷ЈвАО (lintoa њ, вАЬto plantвАЭ). Several scholars prefer to emend this form to вАП„Ь÷і„†÷∞„Ш÷є„™вАО (lintot) from вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (natah, вАЬto stretch outвАЭ); see v. 13, as well as 40:22; 42:5; 44:24; 45:12; cf. NAB, NCV, NRSV. However, since the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa, LXX (and Aquila and Symmachus), and Vulgate support the MT reading, there is no need to emend the form. The interpretation is clear enough: Yahweh fixed the sky in its place.
n2305147: tn The infinitives in v. 16b are most naturally understood as indicating the purpose of the divine actions described in v. 16a. The relationship of the third infinitive to the commission is clear enoughвАФthe Lord has made the addressee (his special servant?) his spokesman so that the latter might speak encouraging words to those in Zion. But how do the first two infinitives relate? The text seems to indicate that the Lord has commissioned the addressee so that the latter might create the universe! Perhaps creation imagery is employed metaphorically here to refer to the transformation that Jerusalem will experience (see 65:17-18).
n2305148: tn Heb вАЬ[you] who have drunk from the hand of the Lord the cup of his anger.вАЭ
n2305149: tn Heb вАЬthe goblet, the cup [that causes] staggering, you drank, you drained.вАЭ
n2305150: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њanakhamekh), a first person form, but the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa reads correctly вАП„Щ÷∞„†÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (yenakhamekh), a third person form.
n2305151: tn Heb вАЬthose who are full of the anger of the Lord, the shout [or вАЬrebukeвАЭ] of your God.вАЭ
n2305152: tn Or вАЬLord,вАЭ from вАП„Р÷≤„У„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( Њadon).
n2305153: tn Many translations say вАЬpleads the cause of his peopleвАЭ (KJV, NRSV, ESV) or similarly (NASB, NIV). The verb вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv, вАЬto contend, dispute, conduct a law suitвАЭ) normally conveys that notion with the cognate direct object вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv, вАЬcause, dispute, legal caseвАЭ), but that is lacking here. Instead вАЬhis peopleвАЭ are the direct object, an unusual construction. The verb вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО typically uses a preposition to indicate whether the action is done for or against someone. The syntax here may reflect Isa 3:13 where God is said to judge his people. There вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО occurs without a direct object, but вАЬhis peopleвАЭ are supplied by parallelism in the second half of the line. The immediate context here is about the reversal of judgment, so referring to God as the one who judges his people but now takes his cup of judgement away would fit well.
n2305154: tn Heb вАЬthe cup of [= that causes] staggeringвАЭ (so ASV, NAB, NRSV); NASB вАЬthe cup of reeling.вАЭ
n2305155: tn Heb вАЬthe goblet of the cup of my anger.вАЭ
n2305156: tn That is, to make them drink it.
n230522: tn Heb вАЬShake yourself free from the dirt.вАЭ
n230523: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„©„В÷Љ÷∞„С÷і„ЩвАО (shevi), which some understand as a feminine singular imperative from вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО (yashav, вАЬsitвАЭ). The LXX, Vulgate, Syriac, and the Targum support the MT reading (the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa does indirectly). Some interpret this to mean вАЬtake your throneвАЭ: The Lord exhorts Jerusalem to get up from the dirt and sit, probably with the idea of sitting in a place of honor (J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 2:361)(cf. NLT, NIV, TNIV, HCSB). However, the form likely needs to be emended to вАП„©„Б÷∞„С÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (sheviyyah, вАЬcaptiveвАЭ), which appears in the parallel line (cf. NASB, RSV, NRSV).
n230524: tn Heb вАЬand now what [following the marginal reading (Qere)] to me here?вАЭ
n230525: tn The verb appears to be a Hiphil form from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (yalal, вАЬhowlвАЭ), perhaps here in the sense of вАЬmock.вАЭ Some emend the form to вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„Ь„Х÷євАО (yeholalo) and understand a Polel form of the root вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО meaning here вАЬmock, taunt.вАЭ
n230526: tn The verb is apparently a Hitpolal form (with assimilated tav, вАП„™вАО) from the root вАП„†÷Є„Р÷Ј„•вАО (na Њats), but GKC 151-52 ¬І55.b explains it as a mixed form, combining Pual and Hitpolel readings.
n230527: tn The verb is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n230528: tn Heb вАЬin that dayвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2305210: tn Or вАЬhas become king.вАЭ When a new king was enthroned, his followers would give this shout. For other examples of this enthronement formula (Qal perfect third person masculine singular вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО [malakh], followed by the name of the king), see 2 Sam 15:10; 1 Kgs 1:11, 13, 18; 2 Kgs 9:13. The Lord is an eternal king, but here he is pictured as a victorious warrior who establishes his rule from Zion.
n2305211: tn вАП„І„Х÷є„ЬвАО (qol, вАЬvoiceвАЭ) is used at the beginning of the verse as an interjection.
n2305212: tn Heb вАЬeye in eyeвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬeye to eyeвАЭ; NAB вАЬdirectly, before their eyes.вАЭ
n2305213: tn Or вАЬredeems.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n2305215: tn Heb вАЬhis holy arm.вАЭ This is a metonymy for his power.
n2305216: tn Heb вАЬthe remote regions,вАЭ which here stand for the extremities and everything in between.
n2305217: tn Heb вАЬthe deliverance of our God.вАЭ вАЬGodвАЭ is a subjective genitive here.
n2305218: tn Heb вАЬthe vessels of the LordвАЭ (so KJV, NAB).
n2305219: tn Heb вАЬor go in flightвАЭ; NAB вАЬleave in headlong flight.вАЭ
n2305220: tn Heb вАЬact wisely,вАЭ which by metonymy means вАЬsucceed.вАЭ
n2305221: tn This piling up of synonyms emphasizes the degree of the servantвАЩs coming exaltation.
n2305222: tn Some witnesses read вАЬhim,вАЭ which is more consistent with the context, where the servant is spoken about, not addressed. However, it is possible that the Lord briefly addresses the servant here. The present translation assumes the latter view and places the phrase in parentheses.
n2305223: tn Heb вАЬsuch was the disfigurement.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (mishkhat) occurs only here. It may be derived from the verbal root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (shakhat, вАЬbe ruinedвАЭ; see BDB 1007-8 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО). The construct form appears here before a prepositional phrase (cf. GKC 421 ¬І130.a).
n2305224: tn Heb вАЬfrom a man his appearance.вАЭ The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) here carries the sense вАЬaway from,вАЭ i.e., вАЬso as not to be.вАЭ See BDB 583 s.v.
n2305225: tn Heb вАЬand his form from the sons of men.вАЭ The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) here carries the sense вАЬaway from,вАЭ i.e., вАЬso as not to be.вАЭ
n2305226: tn This statement completes the sentence begun in v. 14a. The introductory вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ken) answers to the introductory вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Р÷≤„©„Б÷ґ„®вАО (ka Њasher) of v. 14a. Verses 14b-15a are parenthetical, explaining why many were horrified.
n2305227: tn Traditionally the verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ц÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (yazzeh, a Hiphil stem) has been understood as a causative of вАП„†÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО (nazah, вАЬspurt, spatterвАЭ) and translated вАЬsprinkle.вАЭ In this case the passage pictures the servant as a priest who вАЬsprinklesвАЭ (or spiritually cleanses) the nations. Though the verb вАП„†÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО does occur in the Hiphil with the meaning вАЬsprinkle,вАЭ the usual interpretation is problematic. In all other instances where the object or person sprinkled is indicated, the verb is combined with a preposition. This is not the case in Isaiah 52:15, unless one takes the following вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„Щ„ХвАО ( њalayv, вАЬon himвАЭ) with the preceding line. But then one would have to emend the verb to a plural, make the nations the subject of the verb вАЬsprinkle,вАЭ and take the servant as the object. Consequently some interpreters doubt the cultic idea of вАЬsprinklingвАЭ is present here. Some emend the text; others propose a homonymic root meaning вАЬspring, leap,вАЭ which in the Hiphil could mean вАЬcause to leap, startleвАЭ and would fit the parallelism of the verse nicely.
n2305228: tn Heb вАЬBecause of him kings will shut their mouths,вАЭ i.e., be speechless.
n230531: tn The perfect has a hypothetical force in this rhetorical question. For another example, see Gen 21:7.
n230532: sn The speaker shifts here from God to an unidentified group (note the first person plural pronouns throughout vv. 1-6). The content of the speech suggests that the prophet speaks here as representative of the sinful nation Israel. The group acknowledges its sin and recognizes that the servant suffered on their behalf.
n230533: tn The first half of v. 1 is traditionally translated, вАЬWho has believed our report?вАЭ or вАЬWho has believed our message?вАЭ as if the group speaking is lamenting that no one will believe what they have to say. But that doesnвАЩt seem to be the point in this context. Here the group speaking does not cast itself in the role of a preacher or evangelist. No, they are repentant sinners, who finally see the light. The phrase вАЬour reportвАЭ can mean (1) the report which we deliver, or (2) the report which was delivered to us. The latter fits better here, where the report is most naturally taken as the announcement that has just been made in 52:13-15.
n230534: tn Heb вАЬto whomвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n230535: tn Heb вАЬthe arm of the Lord.вАЭ The вАЬarm of the LordвАЭ is a metaphor of military power; it pictures the Lord as a warrior who bares his arm, takes up his weapon, and crushes his enemies (cf. 51:9-10; 63:5-6). But Israel had not seen the LordвАЩs military power at work in the servant.
n230536: tn Heb вАЬbefore him.вАЭ Some suggest an emendation to вАЬbefore us.вАЭ If the third singular suffix of the Hebrew text is retained, it probably refers to the Lord (see v. 1b). For a defense of this reading, see R. Whybray, Isaiah 40-66 (NCBC), 173-74.
n230537: sn The metaphor in this verse suggests insignificance.
n230538: tn Heb вАЬthat we might see him.вАЭ The vav conjunctive prefixed to the imperfect introduces a result clause here. See GKC 504-5 ¬І166.a.
n230539: tn Heb вАЬthat we should desire him.вАЭ The vav conjunctive prefixed to the imperfect introduces a result clause here. See GKC 504-5 ¬І166.a.
n2305310: tn Heb вАЬlacking of men.вАЭ If the genitive is taken as specifying (вАЬlacking with respect to menвАЭ), then the idea is that he lacked company because he was rejected by people. Another option is to take the genitive as indicating genus or larger class (i.e., вАЬone lacking among menвАЭ). In this case one could translate, вАЬhe was a transientвАЭ (cf. the use of вАП„Ч÷Є„У÷µ„ЬвАО [khadel] in Ps 39:5 HT [39:4 ET]).
n2305311: tn Heb вАЬlike a hiding of the face from him,вАЭ i.e., вАЬlike one before whom the face is hiddenвАЭ (see BDB 712 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Ј„°÷∞„™÷Љ÷µ„®вАО).
n2305312: sn The servant is likened to a seriously ill person who is shunned by others because of his horrible disease.
n2305313: sn Illness and pain stand by metonymy (or perhaps as metaphors) for sin and its effects, as vv. 11-12 make clear.
n2305314: tn The words вАЬfor something he had doneвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification. The group now realizes he suffered because of his identification with them, not simply because he was a special target of divine anger.
n2305315: tn The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) has a causal sense (translated вАЬbecause ofвАЭ) here and in the following clause.
n2305316: tn Heb вАЬthe punishment of our peace [was] on him.вАЭ вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom, вАЬpeaceвАЭ) is here a genitive of result, i.e., вАЬpunishment that resulted in our peace.вАЭ
n2305317: sn Continuing to utilize the imagery of physical illness, the group acknowledges that the servantвАЩs willingness to carry their illnesses (v. 4) resulted in their being healed. Healing is a metaphor for forgiveness here.
n2305318: tn Elsewhere the Hiphil of вАП„§÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (paga њ) means вАЬto intercede verballyвАЭ (Jer 15:11; 36:25) or вАЬto intervene militarilyвАЭ (Isa 59:16), but neither nuance fits here. Apparently here the Hiphil is the causative of the normal Qal meaning, вАЬencounter, meet, touch.вАЭ The Qal sometimes refers to a hostile encounter or attack; when used in this way the object is normally introduced by the preposition вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО (bet, see Josh 2:16; Judg 8:21; 15:12, etc.). Here the causative Hiphil has a double objectвАФthe Lord makes вАЬsinвАЭ attack вАЬhimвАЭ (note that the object attacked is introduced by the preposition вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО. In their sin the group was like sheep who had wandered from GodвАЩs path. They were vulnerable to attack; the guilt of their sin was ready to attack and destroy them. But then the servant stepped in and took the full force of the attack.
n2305319: tn The translation assumes the Niphal is passive; another option is take the clause (note the subject + verb pattern) as concessive and the Niphal as reflexive, вАЬthough he humbled himself.вАЭ
n2305320: sn This verse emphasizes the servantвАЩs silent submission. The comparison to a sheep does not necessarily suggest a sacrificial metaphor. Sheep were slaughtered for food as well as for sacrificial rituals, and вАП„Ш÷ґ„С÷Ј„ЧвАО (tevakh) need not refer to sacrificial slaughter (see Gen 43:16; Prov 7:22; 9:2; Jer 50:27; note also the use of the related verb in Exod 21:37 HT [22:1 ET]; Deut 28:31; 1 Sam 25:11).
n2305321: tn The precise meaning of this line is uncertain. The present translation assumes that вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) here has an instrumental sense (вАЬby, throughвАЭ) and understands вАП„Ґ÷є„¶÷ґ„® „Х÷Љ„Ю÷і„Ю÷Љ÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО ( њotser umimmishpat, вАЬcoercion and legal decisionвАЭ) as a hendiadys meaning вАЬcoercive legal decision,вАЭ thus вАЬan unjust trial.вАЭ Other interpretive options include: (1) вАЬwithout [for this sense of вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО, see BDB 578 s.v. 1.b] hindrance and proper judicial process,вАЭ i.e., вАЬunfairly and with no one to defend him,вАЭ (2) вАЬfrom [in the sense of вАЬafter,вАЭ see BDB 581 s.v. 4.b] arrest and judgment.вАЭ
n2305322: tn Heb вАЬand his generation, who considers?вАЭ (NASB similar). Some understand вАЬhis generationвАЭ as a reference to descendants. In this case the question would suggest that he will have none. However, вАП„Р÷ґ„™вАО ( Њet) may be taken here as specifying a new subject (see BDB 85 s.v. I вАП„Р÷µ„™вАО 3). If вАЬhis generationвАЭ refers to the servantвАЩs contemporary generation, one may then translate, вАЬAs for his contemporary generation, who took note?вАЭ The point would be that few were concerned about the harsh treatment he received.
n2305323: sn The вАЬland of the livingвАЭ is an idiom for the sphere where people live, in contrast to the underworld realm of the dead. See, for example, Ezek 32:23-27.
n2305324: tn The Hebrew text reads вАЬmy people,вАЭ a reading followed by most English versions, but this is problematic in a context where the first person plural predominates, and where God does not appear to speak again until v. 11b. Therefore, it is preferable to read with the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa вАП„Ґ„Ю„ХвАО (вАЬhis peopleвАЭ). In this case, the group speaking in these verses is identified as the servantвАЩs people (compare вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷µ„†„Х÷ЉвАО [pesha њenu, вАЬour rebellious deedsвАЭ] in v. 5 with вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„©„Б÷Ј„Ґ „Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО [pesha њ  њammi, вАЬthe rebellion of his peopleвАЭ] in v. 8).
n2305325: tn Heb вАЬone assigned his grave with criminals.вАЭ The subject of the singular is impersonal; English typically uses вАЬtheyвАЭ in such constructions.
n2305326: tn This line reads literally, вАЬand with the rich in his death.вАЭ вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷є„™÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (bemotayv) combines a preposition, a plural form of the noun вАП„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (mot), and a third masculine singular suffix. The plural of the noun is problematic and the вАП„Щ„ХвАО may be the result of virtual dittography. The form should probably be emended to вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Є„™„Х÷євАО (bamato, singular noun). The relationship between this line and the preceding one is uncertain. The parallelism appears to be synonymous (note вАЬhis graveвАЭ and вАЬin his deathвАЭ), but вАЬcriminalsвАЭ and вАЬthe richвАЭ hardly make a compatible pair in this context, for they would not be buried in the same kind of tomb. Some emend вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„®вАО ( њashir, вАЬrichвАЭ) to вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„В÷µ„Щ „®÷Є„ҐвАО ( њase ra њ, вАЬdoers of evilвАЭ) but the absence of the ayin (вАП„ҐвАО) is not readily explained in this graphic environment. Others suggest an emendation to вАП„©„В÷∞„Ґ÷і„Щ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (se њirim, вАЬhe-goats, demonsвАЭ), but the meaning in this case is not entirely transparent and the proposal assumes that the form suffered from both transposition and the inexplicable loss of a final mem. Still others relate вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„®вАО ( њashir) to an alleged Arabic cognate meaning вАЬmob.вАЭ See HALOT 896 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„®вАО. Perhaps the parallelism is antithetical, rather than synonymous. In this case, the point is made that the servantвАЩs burial in a rich manвАЩs tomb, in contrast to a criminalвАЩs burial, was appropriate, for he had done nothing wrong.
n2305327: tn If the second line is antithetical, then вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal) is probably causal here, explaining why the servant was buried in a rich manвАЩs tomb, rather than that of criminal. If the first two lines are synonymous, then вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО is probably concessive: вАЬeven thoughвА¶.вАЭ
n2305328:
tn The meaning of this line is uncertain. It reads literally, вАЬif you/she makes, a reparation offering, his life.вАЭ The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tasim) could be second masculine singular, in which case it would have to be addressed to the servant or to God. However, the servant is only addressed once in this servant song (see 52:14a), and God either speaks or is spoken about in this servant song; he is never addressed. Furthermore, the idea of God himself making a reparation offering is odd. If the verb is taken as third feminine singular, then the feminine noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) at the end of the line is the likely subject. In this case one can take the suffixed form of the noun as equivalent to a pronoun and translate, вАЬif he [literally, вАЬhis lifeвАЭ] makes a reparation offering.вАЭ
sn What constitutes the servantвАЩs reparation offering? Some might think his suffering, but the preceding context views this as past, while the verb here is imperfect in form. The offering appears to be something the servant does after his suffering has been completed. Perhaps the background of the language can be found in the Levitical code, where a healed leper would offer a reparation offering as part of the ritual to achieve ceremonial cleanliness (see Lev 14). The servant was pictured earlier in the song as being severely ill. This illness (a metaphor for the effects of the peopleвАЩs sin) separated him from God. However, here we discover the separation is not final; once reparation is made, so to speak, he will again experience the LordвАЩs favor.
n2305329: sn The idiomatic and stereotypical language emphasizes the servantвАЩs restoration to divine favor. Having numerous descendants and living a long life are standard signs of divine blessing. See Job 42:13-16.
n2305330: tn Heb вАЬhe will be satisfied by his knowledge,вАЭ i.e., вАЬwhen he knows.вАЭ The preposition is understood as temporal and the suffix as a subjective genitive. Some take вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„У÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ„Х÷євАО (beda њto, вАЬby his knowledgeвАЭ) with what follows and translate вАЬby knowledge of him,вАЭ understanding the preposition as instrumental and the suffix as objective.
n2305331: sn The song ends as it began (cf. 52:13-15), with the Lord announcing the servantвАЩs vindication and exaltation.
n2305332:
tn Heb вАЬhe will acquit, a righteous one, my servant, many.вАЭ вАП„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ІвАО (tsaddiq) may refer to the servant, but more likely it is dittographic (note the preceding verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„¶÷∞„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ІвАО, yatsdiq). The precise meaning of the verb (the Hiphil of вАП„¶÷Є„У÷Ј„ІвАО, tsadaq) is debated. Elsewhere the Hiphil is used at least six times in the sense of вАЬmake righteousвАЭ in a legal sense, i.e., вАЬpronounce innocent, acquitвАЭ (see Exod 23:7; Deut 25:1; 1 Kgs 8:32 = 2 Chr 6:23; Prov 17:15; Isa 5:23). It can also mean вАЬrender justiceвАЭ (as a royal function, see 2 Sam 15:4; Ps 82:3), вАЬconcedeвАЭ (Job 27:5), вАЬvindicateвАЭ (Isa 50:8), and вАЬlead to righteousnessвАЭ (by teaching and example, Dan 12:3). The preceding context and the next line suggest a legal sense here. Because of his willingness to carry the peopleвАЩs sins, the servant is able to вАЬacquitвАЭ them.
sn Some (e.g., H. M. Orlinsky, вАЬThe So-called вАШSuffering ServantвАЩ in Isaiah 53, 22, вАЭ VTSup 14 [1967]: 3-133) object to this legal interpretation of the language, arguing that it would be unjust for the righteous to suffer for the wicked and for the wicked to be declared innocent. However, such a surprising development is consistent with the ironic nature of this song. It does seem unfair for the innocent to die for the guilty. But what is God to do when all have sinned and wandered off like stray sheep (cf. v. 6)? Covenant law demands punishment, but punishment in this case would mean annihilation of what God has created. GodвАЩs justice, as demanded by the law, must be satisfied. To satisfy his justice, he does something seemingly unjust. He punishes his sinless servant, the only one who has not strayed off! In the progress of biblical revelation, we discover that the sinless servant is really God in the flesh, who offers himself because he is committed to the world he has created. If his justice can only be satisfied if he himself endures the punishment, then so be it. What appears to be an act of injustice is really love satisfying the demands of justice!
n2305333: tn The circumstantial clause (note the vav [вАП„ХвАО] + object + subject + verb pattern) is understood as causal here. The prefixed verb form is either a preterite or an imperfect used in a customary manner.
n2305334: tn Scholars have debated the precise meaning of the term вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (rabbim) that occurs five times in this passage (Isa 52:14, 15; 53:11, 12 [2x]). Its two broad categories of translation are вАЬmuchвАЭ/вАЬmanyвАЭ and вАЬgreatвАЭ (HALOT 1171-72 s.v. I вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО). Unlike other Hebrew terms for might or strength, this term is linked with numbers or abundance. In all sixteen uses outside of Isaiah 52:13-53:12 (articular and plural) it signifies an inclusive meaning: вАЬthe majorityвАЭ or вАЬthe multitudeвАЭ (J. Jeremias, TDNT 6:536-37). This term occurs in parallelism with вАП„Ґ÷≤„¶„Х÷Љ„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њatsumim), which normally signifies вАЬnumerousвАЭ or вАЬlargeвАЭ or вАЬpowerfulвАЭ (through large numbers). Like вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (rabbim), it refers to greatness in numbers (cf. Deut 4:38; 7:1; 9:1; 11:23). It emphasizes the multitudes with whom the Servant will share the spoil of his victory. As J. Olley wrote: вАЬYahweh has won the victory and vindicates his Servant, giving to him many subservient people, together with their spoils. These numerous peoples in turn receive blessing, sharing in the вАЬpeaceвАЭ resulting from YahwehвАЩs victory and the ServantвАЩs sufferingвАЭ (John W. Olley, вАЬвАШThe ManyвАЩ: How Is Isa 53, 12a to Be Understood,вАЭ Bib 68 [1987]: 330-56).
n2305335: sn The servant is compared here to a warrior who will be richly rewarded for his effort and success in battle.
n2305336: tn Heb вАЬbecause he laid bare his lifeвАЭ; traditionally, ASV вАЬbecause he (+ hath KJV) poured out his soul (life NIV) unto death.вАЭ
n2305337: tn The Hiphil of вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (paga њ) can mean вАЬcause to attackвАЭ (v. 6), вАЬurge, plead verballyвАЭ (Jer 15:11; 36:25), or вАЬintervene militarilyвАЭ (Isa 59:16). Perhaps the third nuance fits best here, for military imagery is employed in the first two lines of the verse.
n230541: tn Heb вАЬthe curtains of your dwelling places let them stretch out.вАЭ
n230543: tn Or вАЬtake possession ofвАЭ; NAB вАЬshall dispossess.вАЭ
n230545: tn Another option is to translate, вАЬthe disgrace of our widowhoodвАЭ (so NRSV). However, the following context (vv. 6-7) refers to ZionвАЩs husband, the Lord, abandoning her, not dying. This suggests that an вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО ( Њalmanah) was a woman who had lost her husband, whether by death or abandonment.
n230546: tn Or вАЬredeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n230547: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n230548: tn Heb вАЬlike a woman abandoned and grieved in spirit.вАЭ
n2305410: tn According to BDB 1009 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ш÷ґ„£вАО the noun вАП„©„Б÷ґ„¶÷ґ„£вАО here is an alternate form of вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ш÷ґ„£вАО (shetef, вАЬfloodвАЭ). Some relate the word to an alleged Akkadian cognate meaning вАЬstrength.вАЭ
n2305411: tn Heb вАЬI hid my face from you.вАЭ
n2305412: tn Or вАЬredeemer.вАЭ See the note at 41:14.
n2305413: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬFor [or вАЬindeedвАЭ] the waters of Noah [is] this to me.вАЭ вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ÷Њ„Ю÷µ„ЩвАО (ki-me, вАЬfor the waters ofвАЭ) should be emended to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ю÷µ„ЩвАО (kime, вАЬlike the days ofвАЭ), which is supported by the Qumran scroll 1QIsaa and all the ancient versions except LXX.
n2305414: tn Heb вАЬthe waters of NoahвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV).
n2305415: tn Heb вАЬpeaceвАЭ (so many English versions); NLT вАЬof blessing.вАЭ
n2305417: tn Perhaps, вАЬrubiesвАЭ (so ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2305418: tn On the meaning of вАП„Р÷ґ„І÷∞„У÷Љ÷Є„ЧвАО ( Њeqdakh), which occurs only here, see HALOT 82 s.v.
n2305419: tn Heb вАЬborderвАЭ (so ASV); NASB вАЬyour entire wall.вАЭ
n2305421: tn Heb вАЬand great [will be] the peace of your sons.вАЭ
n2305422: tn Heb вАЬin righteousness [or вАЬvindicationвАЭ] you will be established.вАЭ The precise meaning of вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsedaqah) here is uncertain. It could mean вАЬrighteousness, justice,вАЭ indicating that the city will be a center for justice. But the context focuses on deliverance, suggesting that the term means вАЬdeliverance, vindicationвАЭ here.
n2305423: tn Heb вАЬBe far from oppression!вАЭ The imperative is used here in a rhetorical manner to express certainty and assurance. See GKC 324 ¬І110.c.
n2305424: tn Heb вАЬfrom terror.вАЭ The rhetorical command, вАЬbe farвАЭ is understood by ellipsis here. Note the preceding context.
n2305425: tn Heb вАЬit,вАЭ i.e., the вАЬterrorвАЭ just mentioned.
n2305426: tn The infinitive absolute precedes the finite verb here for emphasis.
n2305427: tn Heb вАЬwill fall over you.вАЭ The expression вАП„†÷Є„§÷Ј„Ь „Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (nafal  њal) can mean вАЬattack,вАЭ but here it means вАЬfall over to,вАЭ i.e., вАЬsurrender to.вАЭ
n2305428: tn Heb вАЬwho brings out an implement for his work.вАЭ
n2305429: tn Heb вАЬand every tongue that rises up for judgment with you will prove to be guilty.вАЭ
n2305430: tn Heb вАЬthis is the inheritance of the servants of the Lord, and their vindication from me.вАЭ
n230551: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ф„Х÷є„ЩвАО (hoy, вАЬwoe, ahвАЭ) was used in funeral laments and is often prefixed to judgment oracles for rhetorical effect. But here it appears to be a simple interjection, designed to grab the audienceвАЩs attention. Perhaps there is a note of sorrow or pity. See BDB 223 s.v.
n230552: sn The statement is an oxymoron. Its ironic quality adds to its rhetorical impact. The statement reminds one of the norm (one must normally buy commodities) as it expresses the astounding offer. One might paraphrase the statement: вАЬCome and take freely what you normally have to pay for.вАЭ
n230554: tn The interrogative particle and the verb вАЬspendвАЭ are understood here by ellipsis (note the preceding line).
n230555: tn Heb вАЬyour labor,вАЭ which stands by metonymy for that which one earns.
n230556: tn The infinitive absolute follows the imperative and lends emphasis to the exhortation.
n230558:
tn Heb вАЬLet your appetite delight in fine food.вАЭ
sn Nourishing, fine food here represents the blessings God freely offers. These include forgiveness, a new covenantal relationship with God, and national prominence (see vv. 3-6).
n230559:
tn The jussive with vav (вАП„ХвАО) conjunctive following the imperative indicates purpose/result.
sn To live here refers to covenantal blessing, primarily material prosperity and national security (see vv. 4-5, 13, and Deut 30:6, 15, 19-20).
n2305511: tn Heb вАЬthe reliable expressions of loyalty of David.вАЭ The syntactical relationship of вАП„Ч÷Ј„°÷∞„У÷µ„ЩвАО (khasde, вАЬexpressions of loyaltyвАЭ) to the preceding line is unclear. If the term is appositional to вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷і„Щ„™вАО (berit, вАЬcovenantвАЭ), then the Lord here transfers the promises of the Davidic covenant to the entire nation. Another option is to take вАП„Ч÷Ј„°÷∞„У÷µ„ЩвАО (khasde) as an adverbial accusative and to translate вАЬaccording to the reliable covenantal promises.вАЭ In this case the new covenantal arrangement proposed here is viewed as an extension or perhaps fulfillment of the Davidic promises. A third option, the one reflected in the above translation, is to take the last line as comparative. In this case the new covenant being proposed is analogous to the Davidic covenant. Verses 4-5, which compare DavidвАЩs international prominence to what Israel will experience, favors this view. In all three of these interpretations, вАЬDavidвАЭ is an objective genitive; he is the recipient of covenantal promises. A fourth option would be to take David as a subjective genitive and understand the line as giving the basis for the preceding promise: вАЬThen I will make an unconditional covenantal promise to you, because of DavidвАЩs faithful acts of covenantal loyalty.вАЭ
n2305512: sn Ideally the Davidic king was to testify to the nations of GodвАЩs greatness (cf. Pss 18:50 HT [18:49 ET]; 22:28 HT [22:27 ET]). See J. H. Eaton, Kingship in the Psalms (SBT), 182-84.
n2305513: tn Heb вАЬa nation,вАЭ but the singular is collective here, as the plural verbs in the next line indicate (note that both вАЬknowвАЭ and вАЬrunвАЭ are third plural forms).
n2305514: tn Heb вАЬa nation,вАЭ but the singular is collective here, as the plural verbs that follow indicate.
n2305515: sn See the note on the phrase вАЬthe Holy One of IsraelвАЭ in 1:4.
n2305516: tn Heb вАЬwhile he allows himself to be found.вАЭ The Niphal form has a tolerative force here.
n2305517: tn Heb вАЬLet the wicked one abandon his way.вАЭ The singular is collective.
n2305518: tn Heb вАЬand the man of evil his thoughts.вАЭ The singular is collective.
n2305519: tn Heb вАЬlet him return.вАЭ The singular is collective, meaning вАЬlet them.вАЭ
n2305520: tn The imperfect with vav (вАП„ХвАО) conjunctive after the jussive indicates purpose/result.
n2305521: sn The appeal and promise of vv. 6-7 echoes the language of Deut 4:25-31; 30:1-10; and 1 Kgs 8:46-53, all of which anticipate the exile and speak of the prerequisites for restoration.
n2305524: tn Heb вАЬare not.вАЭ вАЬLikeвАЭ is interpretive, but v. 9 indicates that a comparison is in view.
n2305526: tn Heb вАЬare not.вАЭ вАЬLikeвАЭ is interpretive, but v. 9 indicates that a comparison is in view.
n2305527: tn Or вАЬthe heavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheavensвАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n2305531: tn This verse begins in the Hebrew text with вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ „Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Р÷≤„©„Б÷ґ„®вАО (ki ka Њasher, вАЬfor, just asвАЭ), which is completed by вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ken, вАЬso, in the same wayвАЭ) at the beginning of v. 11. For stylistic reasons, this lengthy sentence is divided up into separate sentences in the translation.
n2305532: tn Heb вАЬso is the word which goes out from my mouth, it does not return to me empty.вАЭ вАЬWordвАЭ refers here to divine promises, like the ones made just prior to and after this (see vv. 7b, 12-13).
n2305533:
tn Heb вАЬbut it accomplishes what I desire, and succeeds in what I commission it with.вАЭ
sn Verses 8-11 focus on the reliability of the divine word and support the promises before (vv. 3-5, 7b) and after (vv. 12-13) this. Israel can be certain that repentance will bring forgiveness and a new covenantal relationship because GodвАЩs promises are reliable. In contrast to human plans (or вАЬthoughtsвАЭ), which are destined to fail (Ps 94:11) apart from divine approval (Prov 19:21), and human deeds (or вАЬwaysвАЭ), which are evil and lead to destruction (Prov 1:15-19; 3:31-33; 4:19), GodвАЩs plans are realized, and his deeds accomplish something positive.
n2305534: tn Heb вАЬto the Lord for a name.вАЭ For вАП„©„Б÷µ„ЭвАО (shem) used in the sense of вАЬmonument,вАЭ see also 56:5, where it stands parallel to вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО (yad).
n2305535: tn Or, more literally, вАЬa permanent sign that will not be cut off.вАЭ
n230561: tn Heb вАЬguardвАЭ; KJV вАЬKeepвАЭ; NAB вАЬObserveвАЭ; NASB вАЬPreserveвАЭ; NIV, NRSV вАЬMaintain.вАЭ
n230562: tn Heb вАЬfor near is my deliverance to enter, and my vindication [or вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ] to be revealed.вАЭ
n230563: tn Heb вАЬblessed is the man who does this.вАЭ
n230564: tn Heb вАЬthe son of mankind who takes hold of it.вАЭ
n230565: tn Heb and who keeps his hand from doing any evil.вАЭ
n230567: tn The infinitive absolute precedes the finite verb for emphasis.
n230568: tn Heb вАЬand take hold ofвАЭ (so KJV); NASB вАЬhold fast.вАЭ
n230569: tn Heb вАЬa hand and a name.вАЭ For other examples where вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО (yad) refers to a monument, see HALOT 388 s.v.
n2305612: tn Heb вАЬand take hold ofвАЭ; NAB вАЬhold toвАЭ; NIV, NRSV вАЬhold fast.вАЭ
n2305613: tn Heb вАЬin the house of my prayer.вАЭ
n2305614: tn Heb вАЬfor my house will be called a house of prayer for all the nations.вАЭ
n2305615: tn The meaning of the statement is unclear. The text reads literally, вАЬStill I will gather upon him to his gathered ones.вАЭ Perhaps the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) before вАЬgathered onesвАЭ introduces the object of the verb, as in Jer 49:5. The third masculine singular suffix on both вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„Щ„ХвАО ( њalayv) and вАП„†÷і„І÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„¶÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (niqbatsayv) probably refers to вАЬIsrael.вАЭ In this case one can translate literally, вАЬStill I will gather to him his gathered ones.вАЭ
n2305616: sn The вАЬwatchmenвАЭ are probably spiritual leaders, most likely prophets and priests, responsible for giving the people moral direction.
n2305617: tn Heb вАЬthey do not knowвАЭ; KJV вАЬthey are all ignorantвАЭ; NIV вАЬthey all lack knowledge.вАЭ
n2305618: tn The Hebrew text has вАП„Ф÷є„Ц÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (hozim), which appears to be derived from an otherwise unattested verbal root вАП„Ф÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО (hazah). On the basis of alleged cognates, BDB 223 s.v. вАП„Ф÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО offers the definition вАЬdream, raveвАЭ while HALOT 243 s.v. вАП„Ф„Ц„ФвАО lists вАЬpant.вАЭ In this case the dog metaphor of the preceding lines continues. The reference to dogs at the beginning of v. 11 favors the extension of the metaphor. The Qumran scroll 1QIsaa has вАП„Ч„Ц„Щ„ЭвАО (вАЬseersвАЭ) here. In this case the вАЬwatchmenвАЭ are directly identified as prophets and depicted as lazy.
n2305619: sn The phrase never full alludes to the greed of the leaders.
n2305620: tn Heb вАЬfor his gain from his end.вАЭ
n2305621: tn The words вАЬeach one saysвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2305622: tn Heb вАЬgreat, [in] abundance, very much,вАЭ i.e., вАЬvery great indeed.вАЭ See HALOT 452 s.v. вАП„Щ÷ґ„™÷ґ„®вАО.
n230571: tn Or вАЬrighteousвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB вАЬthe just manвАЭ; TEV вАЬGood people.вАЭ
n230572: tn Or perhaps, вАЬunderstands.вАЭ Heb вАЬand there is no man who sets [it] upon [his] heart.вАЭ
n230573: tn Heb вАЬMen of loyalty are taken away.вАЭ The Niphal of вАП„Р÷Є„°÷Ј„£вАО ( Њasaf) here means вАЬto die.вАЭ
n230574: tn The Hebrew term вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Р÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО (be Њen) often has the nuance вАЬwhen there is no.вАЭ See Prov 8:24; 11; 14; 14:4; 15:22; 26:20; 29:18.
n230575: tn Or вАЬrealizesвАЭ; Heb вАЬunderstandsвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n230576: tn Or вАЬrighteousвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB вАЬthe just man.вАЭ
n230577: tn Heb вАЬare taken away.вАЭ The Niphal of вАП„Р÷Є„°÷Ј„£вАО ( Њasaf) here means вАЬto die.вАЭ
n230578: tn The term вАП„Ю÷і„§÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (mippene, вАЬfrom the face ofвАЭ) often has a causal nuance. It also appears with the Niphal of вАП„Р÷Є„°÷Ј„£вАО ( Њasaph, вАЬgatherвАЭ) in 2 Chr 12:5: вАП„Р÷≤„©„Б÷ґ„®÷Њ„†÷ґ„Р÷ґ„°÷∞„§„Х÷Љ „Р÷ґ„Ь÷Њ„Щ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Э÷і „Ю÷і„§÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„Щ „©„Б÷і„Щ„©„Б÷Є„ІвАО ( Њasher-ne Њespu  Њel-yerushalaim mippene shishaq, вАЬwho had gathered at Jerusalem because of [i.e., due to fear of] ShishakвАЭ).
n230579: tn The translation assumes that this verse, in proverbial fashion, laments societyвАЩs apathy over the persecution of the godly. The second half of the verse observes that such apathy results in more widespread oppression. Since the next verse pictures the godly being taken to a place of rest, some interpret the second half of v. 1 in a more positive vein. According to proponents of this view, God removes the godly so that they might be spared suffering and calamity, a fact which the general populace fails to realize.
n2305710: tn Heb вАЬhe enters peace, they rest on their beds, the one who walks straight ahead of himself.вАЭ The tomb is here viewed in a fairly positive way as a place where the dead are at peace and sleep undisturbed.
n2305711: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬoffspring of an adulterer [masculine] and [one who] has committed adultery.вАЭ Perhaps the text has suffered from transposition of vav (вАП„ХвАО) and tav (вАП„™вАО) and вАП„Ю÷∞„†÷Є„Р÷µ„£ „Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„†÷ґ„ФвАО (mena Њef vattizneh) should be emended to вАП„Ю÷∞„†÷Є„Р÷ґ„§÷ґ„™ „Х÷∞„Ц÷є„†÷Є„ФвАО (mena Њefet vezonah, вАЬan adulteress and a prostituteвАЭ). Both singular nouns would be understood in a collective sense. Most modern English versions render both forms as nouns.
n2305712: tn Heb вАЬAre you not children of rebellion, offspring of a lie?вАЭ The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, вАЬOf course you are!вАЭ
n2305713: tn Heb вАЬinflame yourselvesвАЭ; NRSV вАЬburn with lust.вАЭ This verse alludes to the practice of ritual sex that accompanied pagan fertility rites.
n2305714: tn The term вАП„Р÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њelim) may be from a root meaning вАЬmighty ones,вАЭ referring to mighty trees. The form may also refer to вАЬgods,вАЭ a less common masculine plural of ( Њel). This would fit the context of idolatry (lusting after gods).
n2305715: sn This apparently alludes to the practice of child sacrifice (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).
n2305716: tn Heb вАЬamong the smooth stones of the stream [is] your portion, they, they [are] your lot.вАЭ The next line indicates idols are in view.
n2305717: tn The text reads literally as a question, вАЬBecause of these am I relenting?вАЭ However, the initial letter he may be dittographic (note the final he [вАП„ФвАО] on the preceding word). In this case one could understand the verb in the sense of вАЬBecause of these I will seek vengeance,вАЭ as in 1:24. If the prefixed interrogative particle is retained at the beginning of the sentence, then the question is rhetorical, with the Niphal of вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Є„ЭвАО (nakham) probably being used in the sense of вАЬrelent, change oneвАЩs mind.вАЭ
n2305718: tn The precise referent of вАП„Ц÷і„Ы÷Љ÷Є„®„Х÷є„ЯвАО (zikkaron) in this context is uncertain. Elsewhere the word refers to a memorial or commemorative sign. Here it likely refers to some type of idolatrous symbol.
n2305720: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, вАЬfrom me you uncover.вАЭ The translation assumes an emendation of the Piel form вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Ь÷Љ÷і„Щ„™вАО (gillit, вАЬyou uncoverвАЭ), which has no object expressed here, to the Qal вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„™вАО (galit, вАЬyou departвАЭ).
n2305721: tn Heb вАЬyou make wide your bedвАЭ (NASB similar).
n2305722: tc Heb вАЬand you [second masculine singular, unless the form be taken as third feminine singular] cut for yourself [feminine singular] from them.вАЭ Most English translations retain the MT reading in spite of at least three problems. This section makes significant use of feminine verbs and noun suffixes because of the sexual imagery. The verb in question is likely a second person masculine singular verb. Nevertheless, this kind of fluctuation in gender appears elsewhere (GKC 127-28 ¬І47.k and 462 ¬І144.p; cf. Jer 3:5; Ezek 22:4; 23:32; cf. J. N. Oswalt, Isaiah [NICOT], 2:473, n. 13). Secondly, when this verbal root signifies establishing a covenant, it is normally accompanied by the noun for вАЬcovenantвАЭ (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷і„Щ„™вАО, berit).
